topics.title: Pollutants: Sources, Formation, and Effects on Environment and Humans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pollutants: Sources, Formation, and Effects on Environment and Humans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Automotive Pollutants?

An **automotive pollutant** (or vehicular emission) is any gaseous, liquid, or solid by-product released into the atmosphere by an internal combustion engine (ICE) or hybrid/electric vehicle sub-system that adversely alters the natural chemical, physical, or biological composition of air, soil, or water, and causes harm to living organisms, ecosystems, or built infrastructure.

The four regulated criteria pollutants (set by the US EPA, Euro VI, and Bharat Stage VI / BS-VI norms) emitted by a typical vehicle are:

1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** – a colourless, odourless toxic gas.
2. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** – products of incomplete combustion.
3. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)** – NO + NO₂, formed at high in-cylinder temperatures.
4. **Particulate Matter (PM)** – soot, aerosols, and metallic ash.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Regulated pollutants also include **CO₂** (greenhouse gas), **SOx** (sulphur oxides), **Lead (Pb)** (from leaded petrol, now banned), **Ozone (O₃) precursors** (evaporative HC), and **Ammonia (NH₃)** from SCR systems. The KTU board expects students to list sources, explain the formation chemistry, and describe health/environmental impacts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dirty Kitchen" Model

Imagine a kitchen stove (the engine cylinder) where you cook food (burn fuel). In a perfect kitchen, the gas burns cleanly and only water vapour and CO₂ leave through the chimney. In a real car engine, the "kitchen" is far messier:

- **Open door, poor stirring** → fuel-air mixture is non-uniform → some droplets of fuel escape unburnt (**HC**).
- **Too little oxygen** → carbon cannot fully oxidise → **CO** is produced.
- **Stove too hot (>1500 °C)** → nitrogen in the air itself splits and reacts with oxygen → **NOx**.
- **Very heavy pots and oil splatter** → tiny solid carbon clusters form smoke and soot → **PM**.
- **Leaking gas pipe (fuel tank)** → petrol evaporates before reaching the burner → **evaporative HC emissions**.

So just as a chef can reduce smoke by managing heat, air, and stirring, an automotive engineer minimises pollutants by controlling the **Air-Fuel (A/F) ratio, combustion temperature, residence time, and after-treatment catalysts**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Must Remember)

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (Petrol) | **14.7 : 1** | Mass of air per mass of fuel for complete combustion |
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (Diesel) | **14.5 : 1** | Slightly leaner chemistry |
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (CNG) | **17.2 : 1** | Methane requires more oxygen |
| Auto-ignition temperature (Petrol) | **~250 °C** | Below this, fuel does not self-ignite |
| Peak flame temperature (SI) | **~2300 K** | Drives thermal NOx formation |
| Stoichiometric lambda (λ) | **1.0** | λ < 1 = rich; λ > 1 = lean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for the exam:** The KTU valuation key explicitly awards marks for correctly stating the stoichiometric A/F ratios of petrol, diesel, and CNG. Memorise **14.7 : 1** for petrol — it appears in almost every Part-A paper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Automotive Pollutant Sources

Automotive pollutants are released through **four principal pathways**. The KTU 2024 module explicitly tests whether students can separate *combustion* sources from *non-combustion* sources.

### A. Tailpipe (Exhaust) Emissions — ~70 % of total pollution
By-products of incomplete or high-temperature combustion leaving via the exhaust valve.

### B. Evaporative Emissions — ~20 %
- **Diurnal:** Fuel tank breathing due to ambient temperature swings (HC).
- **Running losses:** Heat soak-back to the tank during driving.
- **Hot-soak:** Engine-off evaporation from a hot fuel system.
- **Refuelling:** Vapour displacement from the filler neck.

### C. Crankcase Emissions (Blow-by) — ~5 %
Combustion gases leak past piston rings into the sump. Modern vehicles use a **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** valve to route these back to the intake manifold.

### D. Refuelling & Fuel Permeation — ~5 %
Vapour loss from storage tanks and permeation through polymer fuel lines.

## 2.2 The Four Primary Pollutants — Formation Chemistry

### 2.2.1 Carbon Monoxide (CO)
CO is formed when **carbon in the fuel is only partially oxidised** because of a **lack of local oxygen** or **insufficient residence time** at peak temperature.

$$2 C + O_2 \longrightarrow 2 CO \quad \text{(incomplete oxidation)}$$

$$2 CO + O_2 \longrightarrow 2 CO_2 \quad \text{(complete oxidation — favoured at high T, long residence time)}$$

> **Why CO increases at idle and rich mixtures:** At low engine speed the combustion chamber is cool, the flame quenches near the cold cylinder walls (within ~0.5 mm), and the A/F mixture is rich to ensure drivability. All three factors favour CO survival.

### 2.2.2 Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)
HC is fuel that escapes combustion entirely. Three classic mechanisms:

1. **Quench layer:** A thin film of flame (~0.1–0.3 mm thick) adjacent to the cold walls where temperature drops below the reaction threshold.
2. **Crevice volumes:** Fuel trapped in the piston-ring grooves, head-gasket crevices, and spark-plug threads.
3. **Oil-layer absorption:** Fuel dissolves into the lubricating oil film on the cylinder wall and desorbs later.

### 2.2.3 Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)
NOx is predominantly **Nitric Oxide (NO)**, which later oxidises in the atmosphere to **NO₂**. The dominant formation pathway is the **Extended Zeldovich Mechanism**:

$$O + N_2 \longrightarrow NO + N \quad (k_1)$$

$$N + O_2 \longrightarrow NO + O \quad (k_2)$$

$$N + OH \longrightarrow NO + H \quad (k_3)$$

Key facts:
- **Activation energy** is huge (~318 kJ/mol for the first step) → requires **> 1500 K**.
- **Doubling temperature** roughly **doubles** NO formation rate (Arrhenius behaviour).
- The rate is also **proportional to O₂ concentration**, so NOx peaks slightly lean of stoichiometric.

### 2.2.4 Particulate Matter (PM)
PM in diesel engines consists of:
- **Elemental Carbon (soot)** nuclei (~0.01–0.05 µm).
- **Volatile Organic Fraction (VOF)** — condensed HC and SOF.
- **Sulphates (SO₄²⁻)** — from fuel sulphur content.
- **Ash** — metallic additives from lubricating oil (Ca, Zn, Mg).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The PM trade-off:** Reducing NOx (by lowering temperature with EGR) *increases* PM, because lower temperatures inhibit soot oxidation. This is the famous **NOx–PM trade-off in diesels**, and the KTU board regularly asks how BS-VI systems (SCR + DPF) resolve it.

## 2.3 Greenhouse Gases and Other Emissions

| Pollutant | Symbol | Origin | GWP (100-yr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Complete combustion of hydrocarbons | **1** (reference) |
| Methane | CH₄ | Unburnt HC, natural-gas vehicles | **28** |
| Nitrous Oxide | N₂O | Three-way catalysts at rich mixtures | **265** |
| Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | Sulphur in diesel fuel | — |

> **GWP (Global Warming Potential)** measures the heat trapped by 1 tonne of gas versus 1 tonne of CO₂ over 100 years. Methane is 28× more potent than CO₂.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Lambda — relative air-fuel ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $A/F = 14.7$ (petrol), $14.5$ (diesel), $17.2$ (CNG) | Stoichiometric A/F ratios | kg air / kg fuel |
| 3 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{[CO] + [HC]}{[CO_2] + [CO] + [HC]}$ | Combustion efficiency (carbon basis) | — |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} = A \, e^{-E_a/RT} [O_2]^{1/2} [N_2]$ | Zel'dovich NO rate (simplified) | mol/(m³·s) |
| 5 | $E_{a,NO} \approx 318$ kJ/mol | Activation energy for NO formation | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $GWP_x = \dfrac{\int_0^{100} a_x \, F_x(t)\, dt}{\int_0^{100} a_{CO_2}\, F_{CO_2}(t)\, dt}$ | Global Warming Potential of gas $x$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $PM_{total} = PM_{10} + PM_{2.5}$ | Sum of coarse and fine particulates | µg/m³ |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{HC,evap} = K \, A \, (P_{vap} - P_{amb})$ | Evaporative HC mass flow | g/s |
| 9 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake-Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kW·h |
| 10 | $BSCO = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CO}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake-Specific CO emission | g/kW·h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam tip:** In every derivation, examiners look for the **activation energy** of NO formation and the **λ < 1 / λ > 1** behaviour of the four regulated pollutants. Always draw a small sketch in your answer: CO peaks rich, NOx peaks slightly lean, HC peaks on both extremes, PM (diesel) increases as mixture goes rich.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding pollutant formation is the foundation of every modern after-treatment system:

- **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC):** Reduces CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously — but **only when λ is held within ±1 % of 1.0**, hence the need for closed-loop lambda control with an O₂ sensor.
- **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):** Oxidises CO and HC to CO₂ and H₂O.
- **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** Traps PM and periodically regenerates by burning it off at ~600 °C.
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** Injects AdBlue (urea) to convert NOx into N₂ and H₂O.
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Lowers in-cylinder temperature to suppress thermal NOx — the primary lever for meeting BS-VI NOx limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Petrol (Octane C₈H₁₈)

This is the **single most-asked derivation** in the KTU Module-1 exam. The examiner gives full marks only if every step is shown.

**Step 1 — Write the complete combustion equation of iso-octane with air.**

The chemical formula of iso-octane is **C₈H₁₈**. Combustion with pure O₂ first:

$$C_8H_{18} + a\, O_2 \longrightarrow b\, CO_2 + c\, H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Balance carbon atoms.**

Carbon balance: $b = 8$ atoms of C on the right must equal 8 on the left.

**Step 3 — Balance hydrogen atoms.**

Hydrogen balance: $2c = 18$, therefore $c = 9$.

**Step 4 — Balance oxygen atoms.**

Oxygen atoms on the right = $2b + c = 2(8) + 9 = 25$. So we need $a = 12.5$ moles of O₂.

**Step 5 — Convert O₂ demand to air demand.**

Air is approximately 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume (and 23 % O₂, 77 % N₂ by mass). On a molar basis:

$$\text{Air moles required} = \dfrac{12.5}{0.21} = 59.52 \text{ mol air}$$

**Step 6 — Compute mass-based A/F ratio.**

- Molar mass of C₈H₁₈ = $8(12) + 18(1) = 114$ kg/kmol.
- Molar mass of air = **28.97 kg/kmol**.
- Molar mass of O₂ = 32 kg/kmol.

Mass of fuel: $1 \times 114 = 114$ kg.

Mass of air: $59.52 \times 28.97 = 1724.3$ kg.

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{1724.3}{114} = 15.13$$

**Step 7 — Refine using the molecular-weight ratio trick (the KTU-shortcut).**

For a generic hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ the theoretical O₂ demand is:

$$a = x + \dfrac{y}{4}$$

For iso-octane: $a = 8 + 18/4 = 12.5$ ✓

Mass-based A/F using the constant $M_{air}/M_{O_2} = 28.97/32 = 0.9053$:

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{a \times M_{O_2} \times (M_{air}/M_{O_2})}{M_{fuel}} = \dfrac{a \times 28.97}{M_{fuel}}$$

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 28.97}{114} = \mathbf{15.13}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU uses 14.7 in the exam table:** When the fuel is a *blend* representative of commercial petrol (≈ 86 % C, 14 % H by mass, plus trace additives), the stoichiometric A/F drops to **14.7 : 1**. The KTU board accepts both 14.7 (blend) and 15.13 (pure octane) — but the **safe answer is 14.7** because the question almost always says "petrol" not "octane".

## 3.2 Derivation — Extended Zeldovich NOx Formation Rate

The three reactions of the Extended Zeldovich mechanism are:

$$R_1: \quad O + N_2 \xrightarrow{k_1} NO + N$$

$$R_2: \quad N + O_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} NO + O$$

$$R_3: \quad N + OH \xrightarrow{k_3} NO + H$$

Assuming steady-state for the radical N (its production rate equals its consumption rate) and that $k_3$ is small compared to $k_2$:

$$\dfrac{d[N]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] - k_2 [N][O_2] - k_3 [N][OH] = 0$$

Solving for [N]:

$$[N] = \dfrac{k_1 [O][N_2]}{k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH]}$$

The net NO formation rate (from the original Lavoie & Heywood formulation) is:

$$\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 k_1 [O][N_2] \left[\dfrac{1 - \dfrac{k_1 k_2 [N_2][O]}{J_1}}{1 + \dfrac{k_1 [N_2]}{J_1}}\right]$$

where the term $J_1 = k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH]$ bundles the recombination kinetics.

**Simplified Arrhenius form (the one to memorise):**

$$\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} = A \, e^{-E_a/RT} [N_2] [O_2]^{1/2}$$

Substituting the empirical constants from Lavoie, Heywood & Keck:

$$A = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \quad \text{and} \quad E_a = 38370 \text{ cal/mol}$$

**Numerical check at T = 2300 K (peak SI flame temperature):**

$$\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \cdot e^{-38370 / (1.987 \times 2300)}$$

$$= 1.8 \times 10^{11} \cdot e^{-8.394} = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \cdot 2.27 \times 10^{-4} = 4.08 \times 10^{7}$$

This shows why NO is produced rapidly only above ~2000 K — a 100 K increase multiplies the rate by a factor of ~3 (consistent with the **doubling** rule of thumb).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Air-Fuel Ratio on Pollutant Formation

| Engine Regime | λ | CO | HC | NOx | PM (Diesel) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Very Rich | 0.7 – 0.85 | **Very High** ↑↑ | High ↑ | Low ↓ | High ↑ |
| Rich | 0.85 – 0.95 | High ↑ | Moderate | Moderate | **Very High** ↑↑ |
| Stoichiometric | 1.0 | Low | Low | **Peak** ↑↑ | Moderate |
| Lean | 1.05 – 1.20 | Very Low ↓ | Moderate | Falling | Low ↓ |
| Very Lean | > 1.20 | Negligible | High ↑ (misfire) | Low | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Answering Trick:** Sketch this matrix in your answer booklet during every 14-mark question. KTU examiners actively award a 2-mark "presentation" credit for a well-drawn A/F vs emissions curve.

## 3.4 Worked Example — BSFC to BSCO Conversion

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine develops **50 kW** at the crank. It consumes **15 kg/h** of fuel and emits **60 g/h** of CO. Calculate the **brake-specific CO emission (BSCO)**.

**Solution:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{15}{3600} = 4.167 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$BSFC = \dfrac{4.167 \times 10^{-3}}{50} = 8.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(kW·s)} = 300 \text{ g/kW·h}$$

For CO:

$$\dot{m}_{CO} = \dfrac{60}{3600} = 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$BSCO = \dfrac{1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{50} = 3.33 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/(kW·s)} = 1.2 \text{ g/kW·h}$$

**Answer:** **BSCO = 1.2 g/kW·h**, comfortably below the BS-VI limit of ~2.5 g/kW·h for the corresponding engine class.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Sources of Automotive Emissions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Automotive Pollution Sources]:::root
    A1[1 Tailpipe Exhaust]:::branch
    A2[2 Evaporative Loss]:::branch
    A3[3 Crankcase Blow-by]:::branch
    A4[4 Refuelling Loss]:::branch

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    A0 --> A4

    A1a[CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO2, SO2]:::leaf
    A1 --> A1a

    A2a[Diurnal Tank Breathing]:::leaf
    A2b[Running Losses Heat Soak]:::leaf
    A2c[Hot Soak Post-Shutdown]:::leaf
    A2d[Permeation through hoses]:::leaf
    A2 --> A2a
    A2 --> A2b
    A2 --> A2c
    A2 --> A2d

    A3a[Combustion gases past piston rings]:::leaf
    A3b[Controlled by PCV valve]:::leaf
    A3 --> A3a
    A3 --> A3b

    A4a[Vapour displaced from filler neck]:::leaf
    A4b[Stage 1 and 2 vapour recovery]:::leaf
    A4 --> A4a
    A4 --> A4b

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef branch fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Formation Pathways of the Four Regulated Pollutants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[In-Cylinder Zone]
        F[Fuel Air Mixture]:::input
        F --> Q1[Quench Layer near Walls]
        F --> Q2[Crevice Volumes Piston Rings]
        F --> F3[Incomplete Combustion]
        F --> F4[High Temperature > 1500 K]
        Q1 --> HC[HC Unburnt Hydrocarbons]:::pol
        Q2 --> HC
        F3 --> CO[CO Carbon Monoxide]:::pol
        F4 --> NO[NO Nitric Oxide]:::pol
        NO --> NOX[NOx = NO plus NO2]:::pol
        F5[High Pressure Pyrolysis of HC] --> PM[PM Soot and VOF]:::pol
    end

    A --> OUT[Tailpipe Exhaust to After-treatment]
    OUT --> CAT[Three Way Catalyst or SCR plus DPF]
    CAT --> AIR[Atmosphere]

    classDef input fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#064e3b
    classDef pol fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#450a0a,font-weight:bold
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Effects on Environment and Humans

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Automobile Pollutants]:::src

    P0 --> E1[Environment]:::env
    P0 --> H1[Human Health]:::hum

    E1 --> E1a[Photochemical Smog NOx plus HC plus Sunlight]
    E1 --> E1b[Acid Rain SO2 plus NOx plus H2O]
    E1 --> E1c[Global Warming CO2 plus CH4 plus N2O]
    E1 --> E1d[Particulate Haze Reduced Visibility]
    E1 --> E1e[Eutrophication NOx deposition on soils]

    H1 --> H1a[CO binds haemoglobin > 200 ppm lethal]
    H1 --> H1b[NO2 causes bronchitis and asthma]
    H1 --> H1c[PM 2.5 penetrates alveoli > cardiovascular risk]
    H1 --> H1d[Benzene and PAH from unburnt HC > carcinogenic]
    H1 --> H1e[Pb neurotoxin > child development]
    H1 --> H1f[O3 ground level > lung inflammation]

    classDef src fill:#1f2937,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef env fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#052e16
    classDef hum fill:#fda4af,stroke:#881337,color:#4c0519
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block Architecture of a BS-VI After-Treatment System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Cylinder]:::unit --> DOC[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst DOC]:::unit
    DOC --> DPF[Diesel Particulate Filter DPF]:::unit
    DPF --> SCR[Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR]:::unit
    ADB[AdBlue Urea Injection Module]:::supp --> SCR
    SCR --> ASC[Ammonia Slip Catalyst]:::unit
    ASC --> TAIL[Tailpipe to Atmosphere]:::unit

    LAMBDA[Lambda O2 Sensor]:::ctrl --> ECU[Engine ECU]:::ctrl
    ECU --> ENG
    NOXSENS[NOx Sensor Downstream]:::ctrl --> ECU
    PMTEMP[DPF Pressure and Temperature Sensor]:::ctrl --> ECU

    classDef unit fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#0c1f4a
    classDef supp fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#451a03
    classDef ctrl fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#500724
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** The examiner expects you to label **arrows** (chemical species / process) and **boxes** (components). A simple box-and-arrow drawing gets 70 % of the marks; adding 1–2 equations on the arrows (e.g., "NO → NO₂") gets you full marks.
