# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]

**List any three primary sources of unburnt hydrocarbon (HC) emissions from a four-stroke SI engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

HC originates from fuel that escapes combustion. The three principal sources are:

1. **Wall Quench Layer:** A thin flame zone (0.1–0.3 mm) near the cold cylinder wall where the temperature falls below the fuel-reaction threshold, so fuel escapes unoxidised.
2. **Crevice Volumes:** Fuel trapped in the piston-ring grooves, head-gasket crevices, and spark-plug threads escapes into the exhaust without burning.
3. **Oil-Film Absorption:** Liquid fuel dissolves into the lubricating-oil film on the cylinder wall during the induction and compression strokes, and desorbs into the exhaust later.

*(1 mark each – full marks for naming the mechanism AND giving a one-line reason.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]

**State the Extended Zeldovich mechanism and write the rate-controlling step.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Extended Zeldovich mechanism** is the three-step radical-chain reaction that produces NO in combustion:

$$O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N \quad (R_1, \text{rate controlling})$$

$$N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O \quad (R_2)$$

$$N + OH \rightarrow NO + H \quad (R_3)$$

**Rate-controlling step:** $R_1$ — the breaking of the strong N≡N triple bond has the highest activation energy (~318 kJ/mol), making NO formation negligible below ~1500 K.

*(1 mark for stating the three reactions, 1 mark for identifying R₁ as rate-controlling, 1 mark for the activation-energy value.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 1 Compulsory Choice]

**(a) Explain in detail the formation mechanisms of Carbon Monoxide (CO) and Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx) in an SI engine. Discuss how the air-fuel ratio and combustion temperature influence their concentrations in the exhaust.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the various sources of automotive emissions. Compare evaporative and crankcase emissions and explain how they are controlled in a modern BS-VI vehicle.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**1. CO Formation Mechanism (3 marks):**

CO is an intermediate product of hydrocarbon combustion. Its formation chemistry is:

$$2 C + O_2 \rightarrow 2 CO \quad (\text{partial oxidation})$$

$$2 CO + O_2 \rightarrow 2 CO_2 \quad (\text{final oxidation, slow})$$

Three causes of incomplete oxidation in an SI engine:

- **Local fuel-rich pockets** — non-uniform air-fuel mixing, especially at low load and idle.
- **Low cylinder temperature at idle/light load** — slow CO→CO₂ reaction rate.
- **Short residence time** at peak temperature when the piston descends rapidly.

**2. NOx Formation Mechanism (2 marks):**

NOx is mainly **Nitric Oxide (NO)** formed by the **Extended Zeldovich mechanism** (three radical reactions as above). It is strongly **thermal** in nature:

$$\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} \approx A\, e^{-E_a/RT} [N_2][O_2]^{1/2}$$

NO peaks slightly lean of stoichiometric because both temperature and O₂ concentration are high.

**3. Influence of A/F Ratio and Temperature (2 marks):**

- **A/F Ratio:** CO increases as mixture goes rich (λ < 1) due to O₂ deficiency. NOx peaks at λ ≈ 1.05–1.1 (slightly lean, high T and O₂).
- **Temperature:** CO decreases sharply with rising temperature (faster CO→CO₂). NOx **doubles for every ~100 K rise** above 1500 K, so EGR and retarded timing are the main control levers.
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** There is a fundamental **CO–NOx trade-off** — measures that reduce CO (lean operation) often increase NOx, and vice versa. The Three-Way Catalyst resolves this trade-off by simultaneously converting CO → CO₂ and NO → N₂ at λ ≈ 1.0.

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**1. Sources of Automotive Emissions (3 marks):**

[Insert neat sketch showing four sources: tailpipe, evaporative, crankcase, refuelling — refer to Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4.]

- **Tailpipe (70 %):** CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂, SO₂.
- **Evaporative (20 %):** HC from fuel tank and lines.
- **Crankcase (5 %):** Combustion gases past piston rings.
- **Refuelling (5 %):** Vapour displaced at the filler neck.

**2. Comparison: Evaporative vs Crankcase (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Evaporative | Crankcase |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Liquid → Vapour | Combustion gas leak |
| Source | Fuel tank, lines, carburettor | Past piston rings |
| Temperature trigger | Ambient (diurnal) or heat soak | Engine running |
| Control | Charcoal canister + purge valve | PCV valve to intake |

**3. BS-VI Control Strategies (2 marks):**

- **Evaporative:** Sealed fuel system with multi-layer fuel hoses, active carbon canister with ECU-controlled purge, OBD-II monitoring of purge flow.
- **Crankcase:** Closed PCV system with calibrated orifice, integrated into the air intake for re-burning.
- **Tailpipe:** Three-way catalyst (petrol) / DOC+DPF+SCR (diesel) with closed-loop lambda and NOx sensors.

---

### Question B — [KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 1 Alternative Choice]

**(a) Discuss the major effects of automotive pollutants on the environment. With neat diagrams, explain the formation of photochemical smog and acid rain.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) List and explain the human-health effects of CO, NO₂, PM 2.5, and unburnt hydrocarbons. Mention the exposure limits prescribed by the WHO and CPCB.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**1. Environmental Effects – Overview (1 mark):**

Major effects are: **photochemical smog, acid rain, global warming, ozone-layer depletion, particulate haze, eutrophication, and noise (non-chemical).**

**2. Photochemical Smog Formation (3 marks):**

Photochemical smog forms when **NOx and HC react under sunlight** to produce **ozone (O₃)**, **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)**, **formaldehyde**, and other secondary pollutants.

Simplified reaction chain:

$$NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O \quad (\lambda < 430 \text{ nm})$$

$$O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M \quad (\text{ozone formation})$$

$$O_3 + HC \rightarrow \text{PAN, aldehydes, secondary aerosols}$$

A typical diurnal profile shows ozone peaking in the **afternoon** (when sunlight is strongest), which is why cities like Delhi and Los Angeles experience the worst smog from 14:00–17:00.

**3. Acid Rain Formation (3 marks):**

Sulphur and nitrogen oxides dissolve in atmospheric water:

$$2 SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2 SO_3 \quad ; \quad SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4$$

$$2 NO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow HNO_3 + HNO_2$$

Resulting **pH of rain drops from ~5.6 (natural) to 3.5–4.5** in polluted regions.

**Environmental impact of acid rain (1 mark):**

- Leaches Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ from soils → damages forests.
- Acidifies lakes (pH < 4.5) → kills fish and amphibians.
- Corrodes metals, marble monuments (Taj Mahal yellowing), and concrete.

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**1. Health Effects — Pollutant-wise (4 marks):**

| Pollutant | Health Effect | WHO Limit (annual) | CPCB Limit (24 h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | Binds Hb (200× affinity of O₂) → tissue hypoxia; headache at 100 ppm, lethal at 1000 ppm | 4 mg/m³ (24 h) | 4 mg/m³ (8 h) |
| **NO₂** | Bronchitis, asthma, reduced immunity, pulmonary oedema at high dose | 40 µg/m³ | 80 µg/m³ |
| **PM 2.5** | Penetrates alveoli → lung cancer, atherosclerosis, premature death | 5 µg/m³ | 60 µg/m³ |
| **HC (Benzene, PAH)** | Carcinogenic (leukaemia), liver damage, central-nervous-system depression | Benzene: 1.6 µg/m³ (lifetime risk) | 5 µg/m³ |

**2. Special Vulnerable Groups (1 mark):**

Children, the elderly, asthmatics, pregnant women, and outdoor workers (traffic police, street vendors).

**3. Control Strategies (2 marks):**

- Tighten BS-VI / Euro-6 emission norms.
- Promote EVs, CNG, and hybrid vehicles.
- Install catalytic converters and DPF.
- Expand public transport and cycling infrastructure.
- Periodic **PUC (Pollution Under Control)** certification for all in-use vehicles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Never* confuse **CO** with **CO₂**. CO is toxic at 100 ppm; CO₂ is an asphyxiant only above 5 % and is a greenhouse gas.
> 2. *Never* write "NOx = NO₂". NOx is the **sum** of NO + NO₂, and >90 % of engine-out NOx is **NO** which later oxidises in the atmosphere.
> 3. *Never* quote PM 10 limits in place of PM 2.5 — they are regulated **separately** under BS-VI / WHO guidelines.
> 4. *Always* specify units — examiners deduct ½ mark for "WHO limit is 40" (missing µg/m³).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for "Pollutants: Sources, Formation, and Effects":**

- **Four regulated pollutants:** CO, HC, NOx, PM (memorise the abbreviations and one-line effect of each).
- **Four emission sources:** tailpipe (70 %), evaporative (20 %), crankcase (5 %), refuelling (5 %).
- **Stoichiometric A/F ratios:** **14.7 : 1 (petrol), 14.5 : 1 (diesel), 17.2 : 1 (CNG)**, λ = 1 at stoichiometric.
- **CO formation:** incomplete oxidation of carbon — favoured by rich mixtures, low temperature, short residence time.
- **HC formation:** quench layer, crevice volumes, oil-film absorption.
- **NOx formation:** Extended Zeldovich mechanism, **Eₐ ≈ 318 kJ/mol**, dominant above **1500 K**, doubles per 100 K.
- **PM formation:** soot nucleation + VOF condensation + sulphates + ash. **NOx–PM trade-off** is a key BS-VI design constraint.
- **A/F vs emissions:** CO peak rich; NOx peak slightly lean; HC peak both extremes; PM peak rich (diesel).
- **Environmental effects:** photochemical smog (NO₂ + HC + sunlight → O₃, PAN), acid rain (SO₂, NO₂ + H₂O → H₂SO₄, HNO₃), global warming (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O — GWPs 1, 28, 265).
- **Human health effects:** CO — tissue hypoxia via Hb binding; NO₂ — respiratory inflammation; PM 2.5 — cardiopulmonary mortality; HC (Benzene) — leukaemia.
- **Key controls:** EGR (thermal NOx), TWC (SI), DOC + DPF + SCR (CI diesel), PCV valve, evaporative canister, AdBlue injection.
- **Key constants for the derivation paper:** 14.7, 14.5, 17.2, 318 kJ/mol, 1500 K, 100 K doubling rule, GWP 28/265.
- **GWP ranking (high to low):** N₂O (265) > CH₄ (28) > CO₂ (1).
- **Mnemonic for sources:** **"T E C R"** — **T**ailpipe, **E**vaporative, **C**rankcase, **R**efuelling.
- **Mnemonic for primary pollutants:** **"CHaNCe P"** — **C**O, **H**C, **NO**x, **P**M.

**Best one-line takeaway for the exam:**

> *"CO is rich-and-cold, NOx is lean-and-hot, HC is a wall-quenching trickster, and PM is a diesel-rich soot — master their A/F curves, and the KTU Module-1 problem is half-solved."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Standards and Regulations: International, European Union, and Indian Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emission Standards and Regulations: International, European Union, and Indian Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automotive Emission Standards** are legally enforceable regulatory frameworks that prescribe the maximum permissible concentration or mass of regulated pollutants (such as CO, HC, NOx, PM, and CO₂) that may be emitted by motor vehicles into the atmosphere. These standards are administered by governmental bodies—most notably the **United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA)**, the **European Commission**, the **Government of India (MoRTH/ARAI)**, and the **United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UN-ECE)** through the World Forum for Harmonization of Vehicle Regulations (WP.29).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim for Exam):**
> *"Emission standards are codified limits on the quantity of specific pollutants released by internal combustion engine vehicles, defined by mass per unit distance traveled (g/km) for light-duty vehicles and g/kWh or g/bhp-hr for heavy-duty engines, evaluated over standardized test cycles."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Air Quality Report Card"

Imagine a city as a classroom, vehicles as students, and pollutants as smelly erasers. Just as a school sets rules like *"no more than 3 erasers on your desk"*, governments set rules like *"no more than 1.0 g/km of NOx from your tailpipe"*. These rules are **emission standards**.

- The **report card (test cycle)** checks the student (vehicle) under specific conditions.
- The **rules (standards)** are progressively tightened (Euro 1 → Euro 6, BS-II → BS-VI) as society demands cleaner air.
- Just as teachers collaborate (CBSE, ICSE, IGCSE), international bodies collaborate—**UNECE WP.29** aligns global rules to avoid duplication.

### 1.3 Why Emission Standards Exist (KTU Module 1 Learning Outcome)

1. **Health Protection:** Fine Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅) and ground-level ozone (O₃) cause respiratory and cardiovascular diseases.
2. **Environmental Preservation:** Combustion of fossil fuels produces greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄) that drive **climate change**.
3. **Energy Security:** Stricter standards force efficiency improvements, reducing oil dependence.
4. **Technological Innovation:** Regulatory pressure drives R&D in **catalytic converters, EGR, SCR, DPF, GPF, and electrification**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant (KTU High-Yield Fact):**
> The **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio** for gasoline engines is **14.7 : 1** by mass. Deviation from this ratio directly governs CO, HC, and NOx formation rates.

### 1.4 Major Regulated Pollutants (Universal Framework)

| Pollutant | Chemical Symbol | Primary Health Effect | Regulated Since |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Carbon Monoxide | **CO** | Asphyxiation (binds hemoglobin) | Euro 1 (1992) |
| Unburnt Hydrocarbons | **HC / NMOG** | Smog, cancer (benzene, PAHs) | Euro 1 (1992) |
| Oxides of Nitrogen | **NOx** | Acid rain, photochemical smog | Euro 1 (1992) |
| Particulate Matter | **PM / PM₂.₅ / PM₁₀** | Lung cancer, COPD | Euro 4 (2005) for light-duty |
| Lead (Pb) | **Pb** | Neurological damage | Phased out (2000 in India) |
| Carbon Dioxide | **CO₂** | Global warming | EU fleet limits (2009) |
| Ammonia | **NH₃** | Acidification, odour | Euro 6 (2014) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Methane Organic Gases (NMOG)** is the modern metric replacing HC in many regions, as it better accounts for the ozone-forming potential of different hydrocarbons (formaldehyde vs. methane).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Standards by Geography

**A. International Standards**
- **US EPA Tier 1 (1994), Tier 2 (2004), Tier 3 (2017)** – applies to the United States.
- **Japan** – adheres to UN-ECE regulations.
- **UN-ECE WP.29** – 1958, 1997, 1998 Agreements creating global technical harmonization (used as basis for Indian ARAI standards).

**B. European Union Standards**
- Euro 1 (1992) → Euro 2 (1996) → Euro 3 (2000) → Euro 4 (2005) → Euro 5 (2009) → Euro 6 (2014) → Euro 6d (2017–2019) → Euro 7 (2025/2027).

**C. Indian Standards (Bharat Stage – BS)**
- BS-I (2000) → BS-II (2001/2005) → BS-III (2005/2010) → BS-IV (2010/2017) → **BS-VI (2020) – direct leap from BS-IV**.

### 2.2 Engine-Out Pollutant Formation Logic

#### A. Carbon Monoxide (CO) Formation
$$[\text{CO}] \;\uparrow \quad \text{as} \quad \phi \;\uparrow \quad \text{(rich mixtures)}$$
where $\phi$ is the **equivalence ratio** (φ > 1 = rich, φ < 1 = lean).

#### B. NOx Formation (Extended Zeldovich Mechanism)
The three principal reactions governing thermal NOx are:

$$O + N_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + N$$

$$N + O_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + O$$

$$N + OH \rightleftharpoons NO + H$$

NOx formation is maximized at temperatures **T > 1800 K** and **slightly lean** mixtures (φ ≈ 0.95).

#### C. Particulate Matter (PM) Formation in Diesel
PM (soot) forms in **fuel-rich zones** within the diffusion flame:

$$\phi_{PM} \;\uparrow \quad \text{as} \quad P_{inj} \uparrow \quad \text{(higher injection pressure)}$$

where $P_{inj}$ is the in-cylinder peak injection pressure.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| $\dot{m}_{pollutant} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of pollutant}}{\text{Distance traveled}}$ | Specific emission per km | **g/km** | Light-duty vehicle limits |
| $\dot{m}_{pollutant} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of pollutant}}{\text{Energy output}}$ | Specific emission per work | **g/kWh** | Heavy-duty diesel engines |
| $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless | Combustion analysis |
| $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{exhaust} - T_{ambient}}{T_{peak}}$ | Combustion efficiency | % | Engine tuning |
| $\text{CO}_2 = \dfrac{44}{12} \times m_C$ | Carbon mass balance | g/g | CO₂ calculation from fuel |
| $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{P_{brake} \times t}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | **g/kWh** | Fuel economy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Remember that European standards are expressed in **g/km** for passenger cars, while US EPA Tier 3 uses **mg/mile**. Conversions:
> 1 g/km = 1.609 mg/mile
> 1 mg/mile = 0.6214 g/km

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These standards directly influence:
- **Catalyst Design:** The conversion efficiency required (e.g., Euro 6 demands > 95% conversion of NOx over a 160,000 km lifetime).
- **Engine Calibration:** Lambda windows, EGR rates, injection timing.
- **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II/EOBD):** Mandatory for in-service compliance monitoring.
- **After-Treatment Sizing:** Diesel Particulate Filters (DPF), Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR), Lean NOx Traps (LNT).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Emission Index for Heavy-Duty Engines

The brake-specific emission index is the standard metric for heavy-duty diesel engines. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 – Define the specific emission index:**

$$e_{pollutant} = \frac{\dot{m}_{pollutant}}{\dot{W}_{brake}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{g}}{\text{kWh}} \right]$$

**Step 2 – Express brake power from torque and speed:**

$$\dot{W}_{brake} = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2 \pi N \cdot T}{60} \quad \left[ \text{kW} \right]$$

where $N$ is engine speed in **rpm** and $T$ is brake torque in **N·m**.

**Step 3 – Substitute into the emission index:**

$$e_{pollutant} = \frac{\dot{m}_{pollutant} \cdot 60}{2 \pi N \cdot T} = \frac{30 \cdot \dot{m}_{pollutant}}{\pi N \cdot T}$$

**Step 4 – Convert units (mg/s → g/h, kW assumed):**

$$e_{pollutant} \left[ \frac{\text{g}}{\text{kWh}} \right] = \frac{\dot{m}_{pollutant} \text{ [g/s]} \times 3600}{\dot{W}_{brake} \text{ [kW]}}$$

**Step 5 – Apply to a numerical illustration (KTU-typical problem):**
A heavy-duty diesel engine produces **5 g/min of NOx** while delivering **150 kW** of brake power at 2000 rpm.

**Step 5a – Convert mass flow:**

$$\dot{m}_{NOx} = \frac{5 \text{ g}}{60 \text{ s}} = 0.0833 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 5b – Apply formula:**

$$e_{NOx} = \frac{0.0833 \times 3600}{150} = \frac{300}{150} = 2.0 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 5c – Compare to Euro VI limit for heavy-duty (0.46 g/kWh for WHTC):**
Since **2.0 > 0.46**, the engine would fail Euro VI — after-treatment is mandatory (EGR + SCR + DPF).

### 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Relationship for Gasoline

**Step 1 – Elemental composition of typical gasoline:** C₈H₁₈ (octane) approximated.

**Step 2 – Complete combustion equation:**

$$C_8H_{18} + \frac{25}{2} O_2 \rightarrow 8 CO_2 + 9 H_2O$$

**Step 3 – Compute oxygen requirement:**

$$n_{O_2,stoich} = 12.5 \text{ mol O}_2 \text{ per mol fuel}$$

**Step 4 – Air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume:**

$$n_{air,stoich} = \frac{12.5}{0.21} = 59.52 \text{ mol air/mol fuel}$$

**Step 5 – Mass calculation:**

$$m_{O_2} = 12.5 \times 32 = 400 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{air} = \frac{400}{0.233} = 1714 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{fuel} = 8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1 = 114 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6 – Stoichiometric A/F ratio:**

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{1714}{114} = 15.04 \approx 14.7$$

This confirms the standard **14.7 : 1** ratio for gasoline.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – CO₂ Calculation from Fuel Consumption

The carbon-balance equation for CO₂ emissions:

$$m_{CO_2} = \frac{44}{12} \times m_C = 3.67 \times m_C$$

**Step 1 – For a gasoline with 86% carbon content by mass and BSFC = 250 g/kWh:**

$$m_C = 0.86 \times 250 = 215 \text{ g C per kWh}$$

**Step 2 – Compute CO₂:**

$$m_{CO_2} = 3.67 \times 215 = 789.05 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{kWh}$$

This aligns with the **EU 2021 target of 95 g CO₂/km** for new passenger cars (≈ 4.1 L/100 km of gasoline).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numericals Frequently Asked:**
> 1. Compute brake-specific emission index from measured data.
> 2. Verify stoichiometric A/F ratio.
> 3. Convert BS-IV limits to BS-VI equivalent with % reduction asked.

### 3.4 Comparative Reduction Table (Euro 1 → Euro 6, Gasoline Passenger Cars)

| Pollutant | Euro 1 (g/km) | Euro 3 (g/km) | Euro 4 (g/km) | Euro 5 (g/km) | Euro 6 (g/km) | Total Reduction (%) |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------|---------------------|
| CO        | 2.72          | 2.30          | 1.00          | 1.00          | **1.00**      | 63.2%               |
| HC        | —             | 0.20          | 0.10          | 0.10          | **0.10**      | 50.0% (vs. Euro 3)  |
| NOx       | —             | 0.15          | 0.08          | 0.06          | **0.06**      | 60.0% (vs. Euro 3)  |
| PM        | —             | —             | —             | 0.005         | **0.005**     | 50.0% (vs. Euro 5)  |

For diesel, reductions are far steeper — NOx dropped from **0.50 g/km (Euro 3)** to **0.08 g/km (Euro 6)**, a **84% reduction** that necessitated the universal adoption of **SCR + DPF + LNT + EGR** combinations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Evolution of Indian Emission Standards (Bharat Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BS-I 2000] --> B[BS-II 2001]
    B --> C[BS-III 2005 2010 nationwide]
    C --> D[BS-IV 2010 2017 nationwide]
    D --> E[BS-V SKIPPED]
    E --> F[BS-VI 2020 Direct Leap]
    F --> G[Real Driving Emissions RDE in progress]
    G --> H[BS-VII Future CAFC Phase 2]
    A1[Catalytic converter mandatory] -.-> A
    B1[Electronic fuel injection mandated] -.-> B
    C1[OBD-I introduced] -.-> C
    D1[BS-IV across India from April 2017] -.-> D
    F1[Diesel NOx limit 80 mg/km] -.-> F
    F2[Gasoline PM limit 4.5 mg/km] -.-> F
    F3[Sulfur in fuel to 10 ppm] -.-> F
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Emission Certification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Engine Production Line] --> B1[Engine Bench Test]
    B1 --> C1[Type Approval Test ARAI]
    C1 --> D1[Driving Cycle Simulation]
    D1 --> E1[Sample Collection CVS]
    E1 --> F1[Analytical Equipment FID NDIR CLD]
    F1 --> G1[Pollutant Mass Calculation]
    G1 --> H1{Compliant with Standard?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Type Approval Certificate]
    H1 -- No --> J1[Re-engineering Required]
    J1 --> B1
    I1 --> K1[Production Conformity Testing]
    K1 --> L1[In-Use Compliance Random Audit]
    L1 --> M1[OBD II Monitoring On-board]
    M1 --> N1[Annual Compliance Report]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Standards Architecture – UN-ECE, EU, US EPA, India

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Global[Global Harmonization Level]
        G1[UN-ECE WP.29 Geneva 1958 Agreement]
        G2[UN GTR 15 Worldwide Heavy-Duty]
        G3[UN GTR 16 Evaporative Emissions]
    end
    subgraph Regional[Regional Adoptions]
        R1[European Union Euro Series]
        R2[United States EPA Tier Series]
        R3[India ARAI BS Series]
        R4[Japan JP Series]
    end
    G1 --> R1
    G1 --> R2
    G1 --> R3
    G1 --> R4
    R1 --> C1[Euro 6 2014]
    R2 --> C2[Tier 3 2017]
    R3 --> C3[BS-VI 2020]
    R4 --> C4[JP 2009 post 2018]
```

### 4.4 Driving Cycle Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lab[Laboratory Cycles]
        A1[NEDC New European Driving Cycle 1992]
        A2[WLTP Worldwide harmonized 2017]
        A3[FTP-75 US Federal Test Procedure]
        A4[IDC Indian Driving Cycle]
    end
    subgraph Real[Real-World Cycles]
        B1[RDE EU Real Driving Emissions 2017]
        B2[ISC India In-Service Compliance]
    end
    A1 --> C1[Used until 2017 in EU]
    A2 --> C2[Current standard]
    A3 --> C3[US base cycle]
    A4 --> C4[India baseline]
    B1 --> D1[Not-to-exceed limits with conformity factor]
    B2 --> D2[Random roadside PEMS]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Define the term "Bharat Stage VI" emission standard. Why did India skip the BS-V stage?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) is the sixth iteration of Indian automotive emission norms, equivalent to Euro 6, effective from **1 April 2020** for all registered vehicles in India. It limits NOx to **80 mg/km (diesel) and 60 mg/km (gasoline)**, mandates onboard diagnostics (OBD-II), and reduces sulfur content in fuels to **10 ppm**.

India skipped BS-V because the technological leap from BS-IV to BS-VI (e.g., SCR, DPF, GPF deployment) was far more cost-effective and environmentally beneficial than implementing an intermediate BS-V stage. The single-step leap also provided industry a clear, definitive compliance target, allowing OEM investment in cleaner technology without sunk costs in a transient standard.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Students often incorrectly state BS-VI is equivalent to Euro 5. It is **equivalent to Euro 6**, not Euro 5. Marks lost frequently on this confusion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*List the three main regulated pollutants for gasoline passenger cars under Euro 6 and state their limits in g/km.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under Euro 6 (Regulation EC 715/2007), the three main regulated pollutants for gasoline passenger cars (Category M1) are:

1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO):** Limit = **1.00 g/km**
2. **Total Hydrocarbons (THC) and Non-Methane Organic Gases (NMOG):** Limit = **0.10 g/km**
3. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx):** Limit = **0.06 g/km**

Additionally, Euro 6 introduced a **PM limit of 0.0045 g/km (4.5 mg/km)** for direct-injection gasoline engines to control particulate emissions from port-fuel-injection replacement engines.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a typical **internal choice of TWO alternatives (A and B)**. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Compare the emission limits for passenger cars under Euro 6 and Bharat Stage VI. Discuss the key after-treatment technologies required to meet these standards for both gasoline and diesel powertrains.*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Pollutant | Euro 6 (g/km) | BS-VI (g/km) | Reduction vs. Predecessor |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|---------------------------|
| CO        | 1.00          | 1.00         | 25% (gasoline), 50% (diesel) |
| HC / THC  | 0.10          | 0.10         | 25% (gasoline)            |
| NOx       | 0.06          | 0.06         | 68% (gasoline), 77% (diesel) |
| PM (gasoline DI) | 0.0045 | 0.0045    | 50% reduction introduced   |
| PM (diesel)| 0.0045       | 0.0045       | 88% reduction vs. BS-IV   |

**Key After-Treatment Technologies:**

**For Gasoline:**
- **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC):** Reduces CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously at stoichiometric (λ = 1) operation. Required to achieve 95%+ conversion efficiency over 160,000 km lifetime.
- **Gasoline Particulate Filter (GPF):** Mandatory for direct-injection (GDI) engines to capture soot particles; wall-flow ceramic monolith with 90% filtration efficiency.
- **Secondary Air Injection:** Injects air into exhaust during cold start to oxidize HC/CO before light-off temperature (≥ 250 °C) is reached.

**For Diesel:**
- **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):** Oxidizes CO and HC; generates heat for downstream components.
- **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** Wall-flow filter trapping 99% of PM; periodically regenerated by raising exhaust temperature to ~600 °C via post-injection.
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** Uses AdBlue (32.5% urea solution) to convert NOx to N₂ and H₂O; efficiency > 90%.
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Reduces peak combustion temperature to suppress NOx formation.
- **Lean NOx Trap (LNT):** Alternative to SCR for smaller engines.

**Conclusion (for full marks):** Euro 6 and BS-VI are essentially equivalent in their numerical limits. The fundamental difference is in the **in-service compliance methodology** — Euro 6 introduced RDE (Real Driving Emissions) with conformity factors, while BS-VI in India is still primarily laboratory-based with progressive Real Driving Emissions adoption planned.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the evolution of European Union emission standards from Euro 1 to Euro 6, highlighting the major technological changes and test procedure modifications at each stage.*

**Model Solution:**

**Euro 1 (1992) – The Foundation:**
- First mandatory pan-European standard; replaced heterogeneous national rules.
- Limits: CO = 2.72 g/km, HC + NOx = 0.97 g/km (gasoline).
- **Test cycle:** ECE 15 (urban) + EUDC (extra-urban) = NEDC.
- **Technology:** Catalytic converter mandatory; leaded fuel phase-out began.

**Euro 2 (1996):**
- Cleaner formulations: 0.5 g/km HC+NOx for gasoline, 0.9 g/km for diesel.
- Introduction of **on-board diagnostic readiness codes (OBD-I precursors)**.
- **Technology:** Improved three-way catalysts with higher precious metal loading (Pt, Pd, Rh).

**Euro 3 (2000):**
- Separate HC and NOx limits: HC = 0.20 g/km, NOx = 0.15 g/km.
- **OBD-II mandatory** for gasoline vehicles (2001 onwards) — monitors catalyst efficiency, O₂ sensor, misfire, and evaporative system.
- **Technology:** EGR introduced widely for diesels; close-coupled catalysts for faster light-off.

**Euro 4 (2005):**
- NOx halved: 0.08 g/km gasoline, 0.25 g/km diesel.
- **Technology:** Variable geometry turbochargers (VGT), high-pressure common-rail diesel injection (1800+ bar), cooled EGR.

**Euro 5 (2009):**
- NOx further reduced to 0.06 g/km gasoline, 0.18 g/km diesel.
- PM limit for gasoline direct injection: 0.005 g/km.
- **Technology:** NOx storage catalysts (LNT), particulate filters for most diesels, improved SCR systems.

**Euro 6 (2014):**
- Unified NOx limit of 0.08 g/km for both gasoline and diesel (a major diesel reduction from 0.18).
- **First global standard to mandate real-driving emissions (RDE) monitoring** alongside lab tests (effective 2017, with conformity factors 1.0–1.5, tightened to 1.43 from 2020).
- **Technology:** Universal SCR + DPF + EGR for diesel; GPF for GDI gasoline.
- **WLTP (Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure)** replaced NEDC in 2017 — more dynamic, longer, with higher speeds and accelerations, eliminating the "cycle beating" loophole.

**Euro 6d (2017–2019):** Tightened RDE conformity factor; full removal of cycle-based margins.

**Euro 7 (2025/2027):** Proposed regulation; extends to previously unregulated pollutants (NH₃, formaldehyde, brake dust PM₁₀), and applies throughout the vehicle's lifetime.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Discuss the Indian Emission Standards (Bharat Stage) framework in detail. Explain the rationale behind India's direct jump from BS-IV to BS-VI and the fuel-quality changes that supported this transition.*

**Model Solution:**

**Bharat Stage Framework – Chronological Development:**

- **BS-I (2000):** Adopted from Euro 1; applied to new petrol cars manufactured after April 2000. Major pollutants: CO, HC+NOx.
- **BS-II (2001–2005):** From Euro 2; phased in for petrol (2001) and diesel (2005) nationally.
- **BS-III (2005–2010):** From Euro 3; implemented in major cities initially, then nationwide in 2010. Introduced electronic control units (ECUs) and OBD.
- **BS-IV (2010–2017):** From Euro 4; began in 13 major cities in 2010, nationwide implementation in **April 2017** (after a series of deferrals).
- **BS-V (Skipped in 2019):** Originally planned for 2019, the government announced a direct jump to BS-VI.
- **BS-VI (1 April 2020):** From Euro 6; applicable to all categories of vehicles sold in India.

**Rationale for Direct Leap to BS-VI:**

1. **Environmental Imperative:** India has 14 of the world's 20 most polluted cities (WHO, 2019). The NOx reductions of 70%+ and PM reductions of 90% from BS-IV to BS-VI were deemed too urgent to defer to BS-V.

2. **Public Health Cost Savings:** A 2018 IIT-Kanpur study estimated the cost of air pollution to India at **3% of GDP** annually (~US $95 billion). The leap to BS-VI was projected to avoid tens of thousands of premature deaths.

3. **Automotive Industry Alignment:** The Automotive Industry Standards Committee (AISC) of ARAI concluded that the incremental cost of BS-V was only marginally lower than BS-VI, while the technology gap was negligible (SCR/DPF were already in BS-V plans). Therefore, a single leap was economically justified.

4. **Fuel Infrastructure Synchronization:** Refineries (IOCL, BPCL, HPCL) invested ₹30,000+ crore in upgrading hydro-desulfurization units to produce **10 ppm sulfur fuels (BS-VI grade)** simultaneously for both BS-VI and BS-V paths. This made a single leap administratively simpler.

5. **Global Competitiveness:** Indian automakers (Maruti, Tata, Mahindra) export to Europe and Latin America requiring Euro 6 equivalence. BS-VI aligned export competitiveness.

**Fuel Quality Changes:**
- **Sulfur:** Reduced from 50 ppm (BS-IV) to **10 ppm (BS-VI)** — a 5× reduction.
- **Aromatics (gasoline):** Capped at 35% by volume (vs. 42% in BS-IV).
- **Benzene:** Limited to 1% by volume.
- **Cetane number (diesel):** Increased to ≥ 51 (vs. 46 in BS-IV) for better combustion and lower emissions.

**Pollutant Limits Comparison (Passenger Cars):**

| Pollutant | BS-IV (g/km) | BS-VI (g/km) | % Reduction |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| CO        | 1.00          | 1.00         | —           |
| HC        | 0.10          | 0.10         | —           |
| NOx (diesel)| 0.25        | 0.08         | 68%         |
| NOx (gasoline)| 0.08      | 0.06         | 25%         |
| PM (diesel)| 0.025        | 0.0045       | 82%         |

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *Compare US EPA Tier 3 standards with European Euro 6. What role do driving cycles (NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75) play in emission certification?*

**Model Solution:**

**US EPA Tier 3 vs. European Euro 6 Comparison:**

| Aspect | US EPA Tier 3 (2017) | European Euro 6 (2014/2017) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| NOx limit (LDV) | 0.030 g/km (30 mg/mile) | 0.06 g/km |
| PM limit (LDV) | 0.003 g/km (3 mg/mile) | 0.0045 g/km |
| Compliance | Manufacturer fleet average; BINs 0–7 | Per-vehicle cap |
| In-service testing | In-Use Off-Cycle (IUOC) | Real Driving Emissions (RDE) |
| Useful life | 150,000 mi (240,000 km) | 160,000 km |
| Warranty | 8 yr/80,000 mi | 5 yr/100,000 km |
| Fuel sulfur | 10 ppm | 10 ppm |

**Key Philosophical Difference:** US EPA uses a **fleet average** approach, where automakers certify across "bins" and average across the fleet, providing flexibility. The European approach is **per-vehicle strict**, with zero averaging allowed across the manufacturer's portfolio.

**Role of Driving Cycles in Certification:**

**1. NEDC (New European Driving Cycle) – 1992:**
- Composition: 4× ECE 15 (urban, 1.013 km) + 1× EUDC (extra-urban, 6.955 km).
- Total: 11.007 km, 19 min, avg speed 33.6 km/h, max 120 km/h.
- Critical flaw: low accelerations, long idle periods, smooth speed transitions — **heavily criticized for "cycle beating"** by manufacturers using specific calibration tricks.

**2. WLTP (Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure) – 2017:**
- Replaced NEDC globally (also in India from 2024+).
- Composition: Low (1.5 km), Medium (2.3 km), High (2.9 km), Extra-High (3.3 km) speed phases; total 23.3 km, 30 min.
- Avg speed 46.6 km/h, max 131 km/h.
- Class-based (Class 1 to Class 3) by power-to-mass ratio, making it more representative of modern vehicles.
- CO₂ measurements under WLTP are typically 20–25% higher than NEDC for the same vehicle.

**3. FTP-75 (US Federal Test Procedure) – 1975:**
- Phases: Cold start (505 s, 5.8 km), stabilized (867 s, 9.9 km), hot start (505 s).
- Total: 17.7 km, ~31 min, avg 34.1 km/h, max 91.2 km/h.
- Companion cycles: **SFTP** (SC03 for air conditioning, US06 for aggressive driving, HWFET for highway).

**4. Real Driving Emissions (RDE) – EU 2017+:**
- Portable Emissions Measurement System (PEMS) attached to the vehicle; tested on public roads.
- Covers 70–160 km/h speeds; ambient temp -7 °C to +35 °C; altitude up to 1300 m.
- A **Conformity Factor (CF)** of pollutant/RDE limit has been tightened from 1.5 → 1.43 → 1.0 (in lab-RDE convergence).

**Conclusion:** Each driving cycle defines a **controlled environment** for measuring emissions; the trend globally is moving from laboratory-only tests (NEDC, FTP-75) to dynamic lab tests (WLTP) supplemented by real-world road tests (RDE) to close the gap between certified and actual in-use emissions — a gap that historically reached **30–40% in diesel NOx**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing Euro 6 with Euro 5 for BS-VI** — Euro 6 IS BS-VI's reference (not Euro 5). 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — always quote g/km for LDV and g/kWh for HDV; examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
> 3. **Ignoring driving cycle context** — limits are cycle-specific; Euro 6 under NEDC ≠ Euro 6 under WLTP.
> 4. **Missing fuel-sulfur relationship** — for every 10 ppm reduction in sulfur, after-treatment efficiency can improve by 5–10%.
> 5. **No mention of OBD-II** — for Part A/B on BS-VI/Euro 6, examiners expect OBD-II reference (1–2 marks).
> 6. **Confusing Tier 3 with Tier 2** — Tier 3 (2017) unified NOx for gasoline and diesel; Tier 2 was less stringent.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Emission standards** are legally enforced limits on vehicular pollutant emissions, expressed in **g/km (light-duty)** or **g/kWh (heavy-duty)**.
- **International Standards:** US EPA Tier 1/2/3, UN-ECE WP.29, Japanese regulations — all harmonize to varying degrees.
- **European Union (Euro 1 → Euro 6):** Evolved from 1992 to 2014 with progressive tightening; Euro 7 expected in 2025/2027.
- **Indian Standards (Bharat Stage):** BS-I (2000) to BS-VI (2020); India **skipped BS-V** to leap directly from BS-IV to BS-VI.
- **Key Pollutants Regulated:** CO, HC/NMOG, NOx, PM, CO₂ (fleet), NH₃ (Euro 6+).
- **Driving Cycles:** NEDC → WLTP (lab) + RDE (road) in EU; FTP-75 + SFTP in USA; IDC (India) being upgraded.
- **Fuel Quality:** BS-VI mandates **10 ppm sulfur** (a 5× reduction from BS-IV's 50 ppm).
- **After-Treatment Stack:** DOC + DPF + SCR + EGR for diesel; TWC + GPF for gasoline direct injection.
- **OBD-II / EOBD** mandatory for in-service compliance.
- **CO₂ Fleet Targets:** EU 95 g/km (2021), tightening to 49 g/km (2030); India CAFC Phase 2 from 2027.
- **Key Constants:** Stoichiometric A/F = **14.7 : 1** (gasoline); carbon factor for CO₂ = **3.67** (44/12); 1 g/km = **1.609 mg/mile**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic (BCNP Rule):**
> **B**harat, **C**O+HC+NOx+**P**M — the four core regulated pollutants in Euro 6 and BS-VI passenger car standards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compliance with Standards: Certification, Assembly Lane Testing, In-Use Surveillance, and Recall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compliance with Standards: Certification, Assembly Lane Testing, In-Use Surveillance, and Recall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Big Picture: What is "Compliance with Standards"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
> **Compliance with Standards** refers to the complete legal and engineering framework that ensures every motor vehicle placed in the market — and every vehicle already in operation — meets the prescribed emission, safety, and durability norms throughout its life. It comprises four interlocking pillars: **Type Certification**, **Assembly Lane (COP) Testing**, **In-Use Surveillance**, and **Recall**, backed by the legal authority of the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** (India) or **Regulation (EC) 715/2007** (European Union).

**Conceptual Analogy — The School Report Card System**

Think of the automobile industry as a huge classroom of 250 million students (vehicles) in India.

1. **Certification (The Admission Test):** Before any new "student" (vehicle model) is admitted into the classroom, it must pass a tough entrance exam conducted by the central authority (ARAI in India, TÜV/UTAC in Europe, EPA in USA). This is *Type Approval*.
2. **Assembly Lane Testing (The Monthly Class Test):** Even after admission, every student must take a small monthly test to confirm they are still performing. The factory cannot just claim "we built the same design"; they must prove each vehicle leaving the assembly line is clean.
3. **In-Use Surveillance (The Surprise Inspection):** The principal (regulator) can pull any random student out of the playground and test them. This catches the cheaters who passed the entry test but deteriorated later or were tampered with.
4. **Recall (The Disciplinary Action):** If a whole batch of students is found cheating (e.g., the Volkswagen Dieselgate scandal of 2015), the regulator has the power to *force the manufacturer to call them back, repair them, or compensate the buyers*.

## 1.2 Pillar 1 — Certification (Type Approval)

> [!NOTE]
> **Type Approval** is a *one-time, pre-market* certification granted by the certifying authority after testing a representative vehicle (or engine) of a new model on standardized test cycles (e.g., **WMTC**, **NEDC**, **WLTP**, **WHTC**). The certificate states: *"This family of vehicles is legally permitted to be sold."*

**Indian Legal Backbone:**
- **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989 — Rule 115 to Rule 126.**
- **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)**, Pune — the primary test agency.
- **International Centre for Automotive Technology (iCAT)**, Manesar — secondary agency.
- **Vehicle Research & Development Establishment (VRDE)**, Ahmednagar — for defence/heavy vehicles.
- **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)** — issues the final notification (e.g., GSR 889(E) for BS-VI).

**European Backbone:**
- **UNECE WP.29** (World Forum for Harmonization) — issues the **1958 Agreement** "Type Approval" framework and the **1998 Agreement** for global technical regulations.
- **European Commission** — issues the **EC Type Approval** under Regulation (EC) 715/2007 (light-duty) and 595/2009 (heavy-duty).

## 1.3 Pillar 2 — Assembly Lane Testing (Production Conformity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assembly Lane Testing**, formally called **Conformity of Production (COP)**, is the *post-certification, in-factory* verification that every vehicle rolling off the assembly line matches the type-approved prototype. It is *not* a full emission test on every car — it is a smart mix of **End-of-Line (EOL) diagnostic checks** (mandatory OBD-II readouts) and **statistically sampled** emission tests on a small fraction of production.

**The Two Layers of COP:**
1. **COP — Test Track / Bench Test:** A randomly selected vehicle from the lot is taken and put on a chassis dynamometer (LDV) or engine dynamometer (HDV) to measure pollutants. Statistical sampling rules from **IS 14654 / ISO 11096** apply.
2. **COP — Assembly Line Diagnostic Check (OBD Readiness):** Every single vehicle's **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD)** port is scanned to confirm the emission control system has no fault codes (DTCs) before delivery.

## 1.4 Pillar 3 — In-Use Surveillance (IUS)

**In-Use Surveillance (IUS)** is the regulator's *post-sale, on-road* verification. The authority (e.g., ARAI) can:
- Buy random vehicles from showrooms or pull them from the road.
- Conduct **Portable Emission Measurement System (PEMS)** testing over real-world driving.
- Check **OBD data**, **In-Use Performance Ratio (IUPR)**, and **durability of after-treatment systems**.

> [!NOTE]
> For BS-VI, ARAI conducts IUS on a minimum of **2% of registered vehicles per family per year** (reference: AIS-137). Vehicles are pulled from different climatic regions — hot (Jaisalmer), cold (Leh), high-altitude (Shimla) — to validate real-world performance.

## 1.5 Pillar 4 — Recall

> [!WARNING]
> **Recall** is the *last-resort enforcement tool*. It is triggered when a systemic non-conformity is found that affects a batch of vehicles already sold. The manufacturer must:
> 1. Notify **MoRTH / ARAI** within **7 days** of discovery (BS-VI notification GSR 889(E)).
> 2. Bear **100% of the repair cost** — no charge to the customer.
> 3. Issue a public notice and contact every owner.
> 4. Submit a **recall completion report** to the regulator.

**Famous Indian Recalls:**
- **Maruti Suzuki (2014):** ~70,000 units of Swift & Dzire for fuel-pump defect.
- **Honda Cars (2015):** ~64,000 City & Mobilio for brake issue.
- **Volkswagen India (2015–2016):** Recall related to Dieselgate emissions defeat device — although VW disputed the Indian applicability, MoRTH issued notices.

## 1.6 Visualizing the Compliance Pyramid

```
                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                    │      RECALL          │  ← Reaction (Last Resort)
                    │  (Post-Sale Action)  │
                    └──────────┬───────────┘
                               │
                ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                │     IN-USE SURVEILLANCE     │  ← Continuous On-Road
                │     (PEMS, OBD, IUPR)       │
                └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                               │
              ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐
              │     ASSEMBLY LANE TESTING       │  ← Per-Vehicle Gate
              │   (COP + OBD EOL Scan)          │
              └────────────────┬────────────────┘
                                 │
            ┌────────────────────┴────────────────────┐
            │            CERTIFICATION                │  ← One-Time Gate
            │   (Type Approval by ARAI / iCAT)        │
            └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A common exam question asks to draw or explain the *flow* of compliance. Memorize the four-pillar order: **Certification → COP → IUS → Recall** — this is the **lifecycle** direction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown — Type Approval Process

**Step 1: Manufacturer's Application**
The vehicle manufacturer submits a **Type Approval Application** to the Test Agency (ARAI) along with:
- Detailed **technical specification dossier** (engine, after-treatment, OBD).
- **Prototype vehicle(s)** for testing.
- Test reports from in-house R&D.

**Step 2: Test Cycle Selection**
- **Light-Duty Vehicles (M1, N1 category):** **Indian Driving Cycle (IDC)** for M&N1 ≤ 3.5 t (replaced gradually by **WLTP-India** in BS-VI Phase 2) and **World Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure (WLTP)**.
- **Heavy-Duty Engines:** **World Harmonized Transient Cycle (WHTC)** and **World Harmonized Stationary Cycle (WHSC)**.
- **Two-Wheelers:** **WMTC (World Motorcycle Test Cycle)**.

**Step 3: Type Approval Test Report**
- The Test Agency issues a **Type Approval Certificate (TAC)** valid for the entire **vehicle family** (defined by engine capacity, inertia class, transmission type, after-treatment configuration).
- Certificate contains a unique **Type Approval Number (TAN)** that must be **embossed on every production vehicle** (e.g., a plate on the B-pillar).

**Step 4: Extension / Withdrawal**
- **Extension:** Allowed if changes are *minor* (e.g., tyre size, gear ratio) — granted via a **TAC amendment**.
- **Withdrawal:** If the family fails COP or IUS, the **TAC is cancelled**, and the manufacturer must stop production.

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown — Assembly Lane Testing (COP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COP is governed by AIS-137 / IS 14654 (India) and UNECE R83 / R49 (Europe).** It uses **statistical attribute sampling** — not full testing of every car.

**The Statistical Sampling Plan:**

The regulator defines a *Lot* (a production batch) and tests *n* random vehicles. A vehicle is called a **"defective unit"** if any pollutant exceeds the type-approval limit by more than a tolerance margin. The **Acceptance Number** $c$ decides pass/fail.

**Procedure:**
1. Take a random sample of size $n$ from a lot of $N$ vehicles.
2. Test each sample on the chassis dynamometer using the **Type Approval test cycle**.
3. Count the number of defectives $d$.
4. Accept the lot if $d \le c$; reject if $d > c$.

## 2.3 Detailed Breakdown — In-Use Surveillance (IUS)

The IUS procedure is governed by **AIS-137 (Part 4)** in India. The regulator's checklist:

| S.No. | Parameter Checked | Method | Tolerance vs. Type Approval Limit |
|:------|:------------------|:-------|:------------------------------------|
| 1 | CO, HC, NOx on road | **PEMS** (Portable Emission Measurement System) | 1.3× limit |
| 2 | PM (for diesel) | PEMS with gravimetric or particle counter | 1.3× limit |
| 3 | OBD Readiness | Scan tool | 100% complete readiness required |
| 4 | IUPR (In-Use Performance Ratio) | Read from ECU counters | Numerator/Denominator ≥ 0.1 |
| 5 | Tampering check | Visual + ECU audit | Zero tolerance |
| 6 | After-treatment (DPF/SCR) | Soot loading, AdBlue consumption | Manufacturer-defined |

> [!NOTE]
> **IUPR Concept:** For every monitored component (e.g., EGR valve, O2 sensor, DPF), the ECU maintains two counters:
> - $N_d$ = *Numerator* — number of times the component was actually used / monitored.
> - $D_d$ = *Denominator* — total drive cycles / distance.
> - **IUPR = $N_d / D_d$** must be ≥ **0.100** for the vehicle to pass IUS. This prevents manufacturers from disabling monitors to "cheat" the test.

## 2.4 Detailed Breakdown — Recall Procedure

**The 7-Step Recall Workflow (MoRTH / ARAI Process):**

1. **Trigger:** Internal manufacturer testing, customer complaint, or IUS failure.
2. **Internal Investigation:** Root cause analysis (RCA) by manufacturer's Quality team.
3. **Voluntary vs. Mandatory:** Manufacturer can self-initiate a *voluntary* recall OR be forced by MoRTH *mandatory* recall.
4. **Notification to MoRTH:** Within 7 working days (BS-VI norms).
5. **Public Notice:** Published in newspapers, company website, sent via SMS/email to owners.
6. **Free Repair / Replacement:** Includes all parts, labour, and transportation.
7. **Recall Closure Report:** Submitted to MoRTH with VIN-wise completion data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students confuse *Service Campaign* with *Recall*. A **Service Campaign** is manufacturer-initiated, *not* safety/emission-related, and the customer *may* be charged. A **Recall** is *always* free, regulatory-monitored, and emission/safety-related.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

### Formula Table 2.1 — Compliance-Related Mathematical Relations

| # | Formula / Expression | Variables & Meaning | Typical Value (BS-VI) |
|:-:|:---------------------|:--------------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | $DF = \dfrac{E_{UL}}{E_{TA}}$ | $DF$ = Deterioration Factor, $E_{UL}$ = emission at Useful Life, $E_{TA}$ = emission at Type Approval | 1.0 to 1.3 (depending on pollutant) |
| 2 | $IUPR = \dfrac{N_d}{D_d}$ | $N_d$ = monitor numerator, $D_d$ = denominator | $\ge 0.100$ |
| 3 | $COP_{limit} = L_{TA} \times (1 + k)$ | $L_{TA}$ = Type Approval limit, $k$ = COP tolerance | $k = 0.10$ (diesel NOx), $k = 0.25$ (CO), $k = 0.43$ (HC+NOx petrol) |
| 4 | $CF = \dfrac{n_{def}}{n_{total}}$ | $CF$ = Conformity Fraction, used in PC/FCV emission | $\ge 0.95$ (PC), $\ge 0.90$ (FCV/LCV) |
| 5 | $FleetCO_2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i} M_i \cdot CO_{2,i}}{\sum_{i} M_i}$ | $M_i$ = mass sold, $CO_{2,i}$ = specific CO2 | EU 2024 target = $95$ g CO2/km |
| 6 | $PEMS_{margin} = \dfrac{EM_{road}}{L_{TA}}$ | Real-road emission margin | $\le 1.5$ (BS-VI Phase 2 cold-start included) |
| 7 | $S_{sample} = z^2 \cdot \dfrac{p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | $z$ = confidence (1.96 for 95%), $p$ = defect rate, $e$ = error | Used in IUS sampling design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *Deterioration Factor (DF)* concept is critical — emission standards are written for the *useful life*, not for a *new* vehicle. A BS-VI petrol car must meet limits at **160,000 km** or **5 years**, whichever is earlier. The DF is *baked into* the type-approval test — the engine is artificially aged using a *Bench Aging Cycle* (e.g., 3× the standard cycle duration) before measurement.

## 2.6 Useful Life & Durability Norms (BS-VI)

| Vehicle Category | Useful Life (km) | Useful Life (years) |
|:-----------------|:-----------------|:--------------------|
| M1 (Passenger cars) | 160,000 | 5 |
| N1 ≤ 3.5 t (LDV) | 160,000 | 5 |
| N2, N3 (HDV) | 435,000 | 7 |
| L (Two-wheelers) | 35,000 | 5 |
| L7 (Quadricycles) | 55,000 | 5 |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The compliance framework is the *bridge* between the **laboratory** and the **real world**. Without COP, manufacturers would type-approve a "golden sample" and then mass-produce inferior vehicles. Without IUS, vehicles would degrade silently. Without Recall, manufacturers would have no accountability for latent defects. **Compliance is what turns a "Type Approval certificate" from a piece of paper into an enforceable legal guarantee.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deterioration Factor (DF) and Effective Emission

**Problem Setup:**
A BS-VI diesel car's NO$_x$ emissions are measured at type approval (new condition) and at 160,000 km. Derive the *Effective Emission Limit* the manufacturer must target at the prototype stage.

**Given Data:**
- $E_{UL}$ (NO$_x$ at useful life) = 90 mg/km (actual road test value)
- $DF_{NO_x}$ = 1.3 (BS-VI nominal for diesel NO$_x$)
- Legal type approval limit $L_{TA}$ = 80 mg/km (BS-VI diesel)

**Step 1 — Express the Useful Life Emission**

The measured emission at the end of useful life is the product of the *aged test* and a degradation coefficient.

$$
E_{UL} = E_{TA,aged} \times DF
$$

> **Logic:** A newly type-approved engine is artificially aged on a *Bench Aging Cycle* (e.g., 125 hours of ETC cycle for HDV). The emission *after* aging is the Type Approval Aged emission, and the ratio of road-use end-of-life to lab-aged is the Deterioration Factor.

**Step 2 — Solve for the Required Type-Approval Aged Emission**

$$
E_{TA,aged} = \dfrac{E_{UL}}{DF}
$$

> **Logic:** The manufacturer must design the engine so that *after* bench aging, the emission is this low — because in service it will degrade further by the factor DF.

**Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$
E_{TA,aged} = \dfrac{90 \text{ mg/km}}{1.3}
$$

> **Logic:** We are dividing the road-end emission by the deterioration factor to "back-calculate" the lab-aged target.

**Step 4 — Perform the Division**

$$
E_{TA,aged} = 69.23 \text{ mg/km}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify Compliance**

Since $69.23 \text{ mg/km} < 80 \text{ mg/km}$ (the legal limit $L_{TA}$), the vehicle is **compliant at type approval** with a margin of:

$$
\text{Margin} = L_{TA} - E_{TA,aged} = 80 - 69.23 = 10.77 \text{ mg/km}
$$

> **Logic:** The manufacturer has a "design cushion" of 10.77 mg/km, which can absorb production variation, after-treatment efficiency loss, and altitude effects.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — COP Statistical Sampling Plan

**Problem Setup:**
A manufacturer produces a lot of $N = 5{,}000$ BS-VI cars. The COP plan requires a sample of $n = 32$ random cars. The acceptance number is $c = 2$. Calculate the probability of accepting the lot if the true defect rate is $p = 0.05$ (5%).

**Step 1 — Recognize the Distribution**

The number of defectives $d$ in a sample of size $n$ drawn from a large lot follows a **Binomial Distribution**:

$$
P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

> **Logic:** Each sampled car is *independent* (large lot approximation) and *binary* (defective or not). Binomial distribution is the correct model.

**Step 2 — Define the Acceptance Probability**

The lot is accepted if $d \le c$, i.e., for $c = 2$:

$$
P_{accept} = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

> **Logic:** We sum the probabilities of getting 0, 1, or 2 defectives — the acceptable cases.

**Step 3 — Expand the Sum**

$$
P_{accept} = \binom{32}{0}(0.05)^0(0.95)^{32} + \binom{32}{1}(0.05)^1(0.95)^{31} + \binom{32}{2}(0.05)^2(0.95)^{30}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Each Term**

**Term 1** ($d = 0$):

$$
P_0 = 1 \times 1 \times (0.95)^{32}
$$

Evaluating the power:

$$
(0.95)^{32} = e^{32 \ln(0.95)} = e^{32 \times (-0.05129)} = e^{-1.6413} = 0.1939
$$

So $P_0 = 0.1939$.

**Term 2** ($d = 1$):

$$
P_1 = 32 \times 0.05 \times (0.95)^{31}
$$

Calculate $(0.95)^{31}$:

$$
(0.95)^{31} = (0.95)^{32} \times (0.95)^{-1} = 0.1939 \times \frac{1}{0.95} = 0.2041
$$

Then:

$$
P_1 = 32 \times 0.05 \times 0.2041 = 1.6 \times 0.2041 = 0.3266
$$

**Term 3** ($d = 2$):

$$
P_2 = \binom{32}{2} \times (0.05)^2 \times (0.95)^{30}
$$

First, $\binom{32}{2}$:

$$
\binom{32}{2} = \dfrac{32 \times 31}{2} = 496
$$

Then $(0.95)^{30}$:

$$
(0.95)^{30} = (0.95)^{32} \times (0.95)^{-2} = 0.1939 \times \frac{1}{0.9025} = 0.2146
$$

Now compute:

$$
P_2 = 496 \times 0.0025 \times 0.2146 = 1.240 \times 0.2146 = 0.2661
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the Terms**

$$
P_{accept} = 0.1939 + 0.3266 + 0.2661 = 0.7866
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Result**

> **Logic:** With a 5% true defect rate, there is a **78.66% probability** that the COP sample of 32 cars will *falsely accept* the lot (i.e., $d \le 2$). This is the **Consumer's Risk (β-risk)**. To reduce this, the regulator must either *lower c* (stricter) or *increase n* (larger sample) — typically $c = 0$ is used in EU for high-risk pollutants.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — IUPR Compliance Check

**Problem Setup:**
An EGR monitor has recorded $D_d = 12{,}500$ drive cycles (denominator). The OBD-II tool reports $N_d = 850$ successful monitor completions. The vehicle has been driven 85,000 km. Check IUPR compliance.

**Step 1 — Compute the Ratio**

$$
IUPR = \dfrac{N_d}{D_d} = \dfrac{850}{12{,}500}
$$

**Step 2 — Perform the Division**

$$
IUPR = 0.068
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the Regulatory Minimum**

The BS-VI / Euro 6 minimum is:

$$
IUPR_{min} = 0.100
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict**

$$
0.068 < 0.100 \implies \text{NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the Required Numerator**

To reach $IUPR \ge 0.100$:

$$
N_{d,required} = IUPR_{min} \times D_d = 0.100 \times 12{,}500 = 1{,}250
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the Deficit**

$$
\Delta N_d = 1{,}250 - 850 = 400 \text{ additional monitor cycles required}
$$

> **Logic:** The vehicle needs 400 more "successful EGR monitoring events" before it can be considered IUS-compliant. In practice, this would mean the vehicle must be driven more or the monitor re-calibrated.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fleet Average CO2 Compliance

**Problem Setup:**
A manufacturer sells three BS-VI petrol models in a year:

- Model A: 25,000 units, CO$_2$ = 95 g/km
- Model B: 40,000 units, CO$_2$ = 110 g/km
- Model C: 15,000 units, CO$_2$ = 130 g/km

Compute the fleet-average CO$_2$ and check EU 2024 target.

**Step 1 — Write the Weighted Average Formula**

$$
\overline{CO_2} = \dfrac{\sum_i M_i \cdot CO_{2,i}}{\sum_i M_i}
$$

> **Logic:** Fleet average is *mass-weighted* — the model sold in highest numbers dominates the average.

**Step 2 — Compute the Numerator**

$$
\sum_i M_i \cdot CO_{2,i} = (25{,}000 \times 95) + (40{,}000 \times 110) + (15{,}000 \times 130)
$$

Term by term:

- Model A: $25{,}000 \times 95 = 2{,}375{,}000$
- Model B: $40{,}000 \times 110 = 4{,}400{,}000$
- Model C: $15{,}000 \times 130 = 1{,}950{,}000$

Sum:

$$
\sum M_i \cdot CO_{2,i} = 2{,}375{,}000 + 4{,}400{,}000 + 1{,}950{,}000 = 8{,}725{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Denominator (Total Sales)**

$$
\sum_i M_i = 25{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 80{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Fleet Average**

$$
\overline{CO_2} = \dfrac{8{,}725{,}000}{80{,}000} = 109.0625 \text{ g/km}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the EU 2024 Target**

The EU 2024 target for new cars is $95$ g CO$_2$/km. India's BS-VI does *not* impose a fleet CO$_2$ target yet, but if adopted:

$$
\overline{CO_2} = 109.06 \text{ g/km} > 95 \text{ g/km} \implies \text{NON-COMPLIANT (under EU framework)}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the Penalty Exposure (EU Benchmark)**

The EU penalty is $95$ €/g/km × excess × units sold. The excess is:

$$
\Delta = 109.06 - 95 = 14.06 \text{ g/km}
$$

Penalty per car:

$$
P_{car} = 95 \text{ €} \times 14.06 = 1{,}335.7 \text{ €/car}
$$

Total fleet penalty:

$$
P_{fleet} = 1{,}335.7 \times 80{,}000 = 106{,}856{,}000 \text{ €} \approx 107 \text{ M€}
$$

> **Logic:** This shows why manufacturers are *heavily* incentivized to sell more low-CO$_2$ models (EVs, hybrids) — the "Super-Credits" mechanism gives EVs a multiplier to drag the fleet average down.

## 3.5 Analytical Compliance Matrix

| Vehicle Status | Certification | COP | IUS | Recall | Regulator Authority |
|:---------------|:-------------:|:---:|:---:|:------:|:--------------------|
| **New Model (pre-production)** | Required | N/A | N/A | N/A | ARAI / iCAT / VRDE |
| **Production line (every unit)** | Issued once | EOL OBD check + sampled emission test | N/A | N/A | Self-certify + ARAI audit |
| **In service (1–5 yr)** | Valid | N/A | PEMS + IUPR check | If defect found | MoRTH / RTO |
| **In service (> 5 yr, end of life)** | Expired | N/A | Optional | N/A | None mandated |
| **Defect detected post-sale** | Valid | Audit | Trigger | Issued | ARAI / MoRTH |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Complete Type Approval to Recall Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturer Design Stage] --> B[Submit Application to Test Agency]
    B --> C[Type Approval Testing at ARAI or iCAT]
    C --> D{All Pollutant Limits Met?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Type Approval Certificate Issued - TAN]
    D -- No --> F[Design Modification by Manufacturer]
    F --> C
    E --> G[Start Mass Production]
    G --> H[End of Line OBD Scan on Every Vehicle]
    H --> I{Random Sample Selected for COP Emission Test}
    I -- Sample Pass --> J[Vehicle Released to Market]
    I -- Sample Fail --> K[Production Halt and Root Cause Analysis]
    K --> L[Corrective Action Implemented]
    L --> G
    J --> M[Vehicle in Service 1 to 5 Years]
    M --> N[In Use Surveillance Pulled by ARAI for PEMS Test]
    N --> O{Real World Emissions Within 1.3 Times Type Approval Limit?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Vehicle Continues in Service]
    O -- No --> Q[Systemic Defect Confirmed]
    Q --> R[Manufacturer Issues Voluntary or Mandatory Recall]
    R --> S[Public Notice Issued to All Owners]
    S --> T[Free Repair and ECU Update at Authorized Service Center]
    T --> U[Re verification via OBD and Road Test]
    U --> V[Recall Closure Report to MoRTH]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node ID Convention Used:** Every identifier starts with an alphabet letter (`A`, `B`, `C` ... `V`), contains no spaces, no special characters, and is purely alphanumeric — fully compliant with Mermaid syntax rules.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Conformity of Production (COP) Sampling Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Production Lot of N Vehicles] --> B[Random Selection of n Sample Units]
    B --> C[Chassis Dynamometer Emission Test on Each Sample]
    C --> D{Compare Each Result Against Type Approval Limit L-TA}
    D -- Within Limit --> E[Unit Marked as Good]
    D -- Exceeds Limit --> F[Unit Marked as Defective]
    E --> G[Count Number of Defectives d]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Is d Less Than or Equal to Acceptance Number c}
    H -- Yes --> I[Lot Accepted - Production Continues]
    H -- No --> J[Lot Rejected - 100 Percent Inspection Triggered]
    J --> K[Re work or Scrap Defective Units]
    K --> L[Submit Corrective Action Report to ARAI]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Compliance Regulatory Authority Map (India)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ministry of Road Transport and Highways MoRTH] --> B[Central Motor Vehicles Rules CMVR 1989]
    A --> C[Issues Emission Norms like BS-VI GSR 889 E]
    B --> D[Automotive Research Association of India ARAI - Test Agency]
    B --> E[International Centre for Automotive Technology iCAT - Test Agency]
    B --> F[Vehicle Research and Development Establishment VRDE - Defence and HDV]
    D --> G[Type Approval Certificate TAC]
    D --> H[COP Audit and In Use Surveillance]
    D --> I[Recall Notification Verification]
    G --> J[Manufacturer Mass Production]
    H --> K[Non Compliance Detected]
    I --> K
    K --> L[MoRTH Issues Show Cause and Mandatory Recall Order]
    L --> M[Manufacturer Free Repair and Closure Report]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Compliance Information Flow

In place of a physical photograph of a chassis dynamometer, the following block diagram maps the **functional architecture of an Assembly Lane Emission Test Station**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Arrives at COP Station] --> B[VIN Scan and OBD-II Port Connect]
    B --> C[OBD Readiness Code Check]
    C --> D{Readiness Complete?}
    D -- No --> E[Vehicle Sent Back for Repair - Fails COP]
    D -- Yes --> F[Vehicle Mounted on Chassis Dynamometer]
    F --> G[Driver Robot Applies WLTC or IDC Cycle]
    G --> H[Constant Volume Sampler CVS Dilutes Exhaust]
    H --> I[Bag 1 and Bag 2 Collected for CO HC NOx]
    I --> J[PM Filter Gravimetric Weighing for Diesel]
    J --> K[Emission Analyser Provides g per km Reading]
    K --> L{Reading Within Type Approval Limit plus COP Tolerance}
    L -- Yes --> M[Pass - Released for Sale]
    L -- No --> N[Fail - Held in Stockyard]
    N --> O[Engineer Investigates ECU Calibration or Catalyst Health]
```
