# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Conformity of Production (COP) Testing" and state its governing standard in India.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 Marks, ~50–70 words):**

Conformity of Production (COP) Testing is the post-certification verification that every vehicle produced on the assembly line conforms to the type-approved prototype with respect to emissions. It involves a **statistically sampled** emission test on chassis/ engine dynamometer and a **mandatory OBD-II diagnostic read-out** on every unit before delivery. In India, COP is governed by **AIS-137 / IS 14654** and is enforced by ARAI. The sample size and acceptance number are defined by the standard; a vehicle exceeding the type-approval limit by more than the **COP tolerance factor (k)** is called a *defective unit*.

*(Marking key: Definition 1M, Governing standard 1M, COP tolerance / OBD detail 1M.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a "Service Campaign" and a "Recall" in the Indian automotive context.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 Marks, ~60 words):**

A **Recall** is an *emission- or safety-related* corrective action that is **mandatorily free of cost** to the customer, **notified to MoRTH within 7 days** (BS-VI rule), and the manufacturer must submit a **closure report** to the regulator. A **Service Campaign** is a *goodwill/quality improvement* measure that is **not safety/emission-related**, **not regulator-monitored**, and the customer **may be charged** for parts. Recall is enforceable; a Service Campaign is voluntary goodwill.

*(Marking key: Recall definition + free + MoRTH = 2M; Service campaign distinction = 1M.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Note:** Part B questions for this module typically offer an internal choice between two questions. The model solutions below provide full mark-allocation breakdowns.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the complete Type Approval process for a BS-VI passenger car in India. List the test agencies and the legal instruments involved. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** With a neat flowchart, describe the Assembly Lane (COP) Testing procedure. State the statistical sampling rule with an example. **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Type Approval Process for BS-VI Car

**Step 1 — Pre-Application Phase** *(1 Mark)*
The manufacturer finalizes engine, after-treatment (GPF for petrol, DPF+SCR for diesel), ECU calibration, and prepares the **technical dossier** containing 50+ documents (engine specs, OBD description, durability plan, family definition).

**Step 2 — Application Submission** *(1 Mark)*
Application submitted to the test agency (ARAI / iCAT) along with the **prototype vehicle**, **photographs**, **engineering drawings**, and **manufacturer's self-test reports**.

**Step 3 — Test Cycle Execution** *(2 Marks)*
- The car is tested on a **chassis dynamometer** using the **Indian Driving Cycle (IDC)** for M&N1 ≤ 3.5 t (BS-VI Phase 1) and **WLTP-India** for Phase 2.
- Pollutants measured: **CO, HC, NMOG, NO$_x$, PM, PN (particle number)** for petrol; **CO, HC+NO$_x$, NO$_x$, PM, PN** for diesel.
- **OBD-II readiness** and **IUPR counters** are verified.
- A **durability bench-aging cycle** simulates 160,000 km of useful life.

**Step 4 — Evaluation against BS-VI Limits** *(1 Mark)*
The measured emissions must be ≤ the BS-VI limits (e.g., 80 mg/km NO$_x$ for diesel car). If any pollutant exceeds, the car fails; manufacturer redesigns.

**Step 5 — Certificate Issuance** *(1 Mark)*
ARAI issues the **Type Approval Certificate (TAC)** with a unique **TAN (Type Approval Number)**. The TAN is embossed on a metal plate fixed on the vehicle's B-pillar / engine bay.

**Step 6 — Test Agencies & Legal Instruments** *(1 Mark)*

| Item | Detail |
|:-----|:-------|
| Primary Test Agency | ARAI (Pune) |
| Secondary | iCAT (Manesar) |
| HDV / Defence | VRDE (Ahmednagar) |
| Legal instrument | CMVR 1989 (Rule 115–126) |
| Notification | GSR 889(E), 2016 (BS-VI) |
| International | UNECE WP.29, 1958 Agreement |

#### Part (b) Model Solution — COP Procedure with Flowchart

**Step 1 — Definition & Purpose** *(1 Mark)*
COP verifies that mass-produced vehicles match the type-approved prototype. It is governed by **AIS-137 / IS 14654** in India and **UNECE R83** in Europe.

**Step 2 — Two-Layer Procedure** *(2 Marks)*

**Layer 1 — OBD End-of-Line Scan (100% of vehicles):**
- Every vehicle's OBD-II port is scanned for **DTCs (Diagnostic Trouble Codes)**.
- All emission-related monitors must show *Complete/Ready*.
- Any DTC → vehicle held back.

**Layer 2 — Sampled Emission Test (e.g., 1 in 200):**
- Selected vehicle runs IDC / WLTP on a chassis dynamometer.
- Pollutant levels compared against Type Approval limits + COP tolerance.

**Step 3 — Statistical Sampling Plan** *(3 Marks)*
- Lot size: $N$ (e.g., 5,000).
- Sample size: $n$ (e.g., 32).
- Acceptance number: $c$ (e.g., 2).
- Decision rule: Accept lot if $d \le c$, reject if $d > c$.

**Worked Example:**
Lot $N = 5{,}000$, sample $n = 32$, $c = 2$. Tested 32 vehicles, found $d = 1$ defective (e.g., CO = 5.2 g/km vs. limit 1.0 g/km + tolerance 0.43 g/km = 1.43 g/km). Since $d = 1 \le c = 2$, **lot accepted**. Had 3 defectives been found, the lot would be **rejected**, triggering 100% inspection.

**Step 4 — Flowchart Representation** *(1 Mark)*

```
Vehicle at COP Station → OBD-II Scan → DTC Found?
   → Yes: HOLD for repair
   → No: Continue
   → Random selection for emission test
   → Test on dynamometer
   → Reading > L_TA + tolerance?
        Yes: Defective (count d)
        No: Pass
   → Sum d
   → d ≤ c? Yes: LOT PASS
            No:  LOT FAIL → 100% screen
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **(a)** What is In-Use Surveillance (IUS)? Explain the PEMS procedure and the IUPR concept with formulas. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** Describe the vehicle Recall procedure in India under BS-VI. What is the timeline, cost liability, and what is the role of MoRTH? **[7 Marks]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — In-Use Surveillance

**Step 1 — Definition** *(1 Mark)*
In-Use Surveillance (IUS) is the post-sale, on-road verification of emissions conducted by the regulator (ARAI in India) to confirm that in-service vehicles continue to comply with BS-VI norms.

**Step 2 — IUS Procedure** *(2 Marks)*
- ARAI randomly purchases vehicles from showrooms *or* pulls them from registration records.
- Vehicles are tested using **Portable Emission Measurement System (PEMS)** over a real-world route (urban, rural, highway).
- Test route must cover a minimum of **120 km** with a mix of altitude and temperature.
- Pollutants measured: CO, HC, NO$_x$, PM, PN — second-by-second.

**Step 3 — Compliance Criterion** *(1 Mark)*
The **moving-average window** (CO2-weighted) emissions are compared against the type-approval limit. Vehicles whose emissions exceed **1.3 × $L_{TA}$** fail IUS. A family fails if **> 2% of sampled vehicles** fail.

**Step 4 — PEMS Margin Formula** *(1 Mark)*

$$
CF_{margin} = \dfrac{EM_{road}}{L_{TA}} \le 1.3
$$

**Step 5 — IUPR Concept** *(2 Marks)*
IUPR (In-Use Performance Ratio) is the ratio of successful monitor runs to total drive cycles. The ECU stores:

- $N_d$ = numerator (successful monitor events).
- $D_d$ = denominator (total drive cycles).

$$
IUPR = \dfrac{N_d}{D_d} \ge 0.100
$$

**Example:** A SCR catalyst monitor has $N_d = 1{,}500$ and $D_d = 20{,}000$. Then $IUPR = 0.075$, which **fails** the BS-VI minimum of 0.1. The vehicle would be flagged for ECU calibration or catalyst replacement.

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Vehicle Recall Procedure in India

**Step 1 — Trigger** *(1 Mark)*
A recall is triggered by:
- Manufacturer's internal quality audit.
- Customer complaints to OEM or regulator.
- IUS failure detected by ARAI.
- Adverse findings from a *defect investigation* (Section 57 of MV Act, 1988).

**Step 2 — Manufacturer's Internal Investigation** *(1 Mark)*
Within **7 working days** of identifying a defect, the manufacturer must:
- Conduct **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)**.
- Estimate the affected **VIN range**.
- Design a **remedy** (ECU flash, part replacement, software update).

**Step 3 — Notification to MoRTH & ARAI** *(1 Mark)*
The manufacturer files a **Recall Notification** to MoRTH / ARAI with:
- Defect description.
- VIN list of affected vehicles.
- Proposed remedy and estimated cost.
- Communication plan for owners.

**Step 4 — Public Communication** *(1 Mark)*
- Notice published in **two national newspapers** + OEM website.
- **SMS / email / letter** to all registered owners in the affected VIN range.
- Dealers briefed to handle free-of-cost repairs.

**Step 5 — Free Repair Execution** *(1 Mark)*
All affected vehicles are repaired at **authorized service centres** at **100% manufacturer cost** — no charge to the customer for parts, labour, or consumables.

**Step 6 — Re-verification & Closure** *(1 Mark)*
- Post-repair, vehicle is **OBD-scanned** and **road-tested** to confirm the defect is rectified.
- Manufacturer submits a **VIN-wise Recall Completion Report** to MoRTH within the prescribed timeline.

**Step 7 — Summary of Liability** *(1 Mark)*

| Aspect | Liability |
|:-------|:----------|
| Cost of repair | 100% Manufacturer |
| Towing / transportation | 100% Manufacturer |
| Customer compensation (usage loss) | Discretionary / court-ordered |
| Penalty for non-disclosure | Up to ₹1 crore (Section 190(2) MV Act) |
| Timeline to notify | 7 working days from defect discovery |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Type Approval" with "Registration":** Type Approval is a *prototype-level* certificate (one per family). Registration is the *individual vehicle's* RTO registration. Examiners award marks specifically for distinguishing these.
> 2. **Forgetting the COP tolerance factor $k$:** Students often quote the raw type-approval limit for COP comparison. The COP limit is $L_{TA} \times (1 + k)$. Forgetting $k$ is a **2-mark penalty** in numerical questions.
> 3. **Mixing "Recall" with "Service Campaign":** Examiners love this trap. Always clarify that Recall is **free**, **regulator-monitored**, and **safety/emission-related**.
> 4. **Missing "OBD-II EOL Scan":** A common mistake is to describe COP only as the dynamometer test. OBD-II scan is a **mandatory 100% check**; missing it costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical derivations:** Always write $\text{mg/km}$, $\text{g/km}$, or $\text{€/g}$. Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** for missing units.
> 6. **Wrong test cycle name:** Indian M&N1 vehicles in BS-VI use **IDC** (Phase 1) and **WLTP-India** (Phase 2) — **not** NEDC (which is the old European cycle). Wrong cycle = 1 mark penalty.
> 7. **Forgetting the "Useful Life" concept:** Emission standards apply at the *end of useful life*, not at *zero km*. The Deterioration Factor is critical. Examiners often test this in 3-mark questions.
> 8. **Ignoring the "Free of Cost" clause in recall:** Many students write that customers pay for parts. This is a **fatal error** in recall questions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Four pillars of compliance** (in order): Certification → COP → IUS → Recall.
- [ ] **Type Approval = pre-market, one-time**, per vehicle family.
- [ ] **COP = per-production-batch**, governed by AIS-137 / IS 14654; **OBD-II scan is 100%**, emission test is **sampled**.
- [ ] **COP tolerance factor $k$** varies by pollutant (0.10 to 0.43).
- [ ] **IUS = post-sale on-road**; uses PEMS over real route; threshold is **1.3 × $L_{TA}$**.
- [ ] **IUPR = $N_d / D_d \ge 0.100$**; below this, the vehicle fails IUS.
- [ ] **Recall = free to customer, MoRTH notified within 7 days**, closure report mandatory.
- [ ] **Useful life:** 160,000 km / 5 years for M1 & N1; 35,000 km / 5 years for L-category.
- [ ] **Test agencies in India:** ARAI (Pune), iCAT (Manesar), VRDE (Ahmednagar).
- [ ] **Legal instruments:** CMVR 1989 Rule 115–126, GSR 889(E) for BS-VI, MV Act 1988 §57 for defects.
- [ ] **Deterioration Factor (DF) = $E_{UL} / E_{TA}$**; manufacturer must design so aged emissions stay within limits.
- [ ] **Diesel BS-VI NOx limit** for M1 cars = **80 mg/km**; Petrol BS-Vi = **60 mg/km** for NO$_x$ + HC combined.
- [ ] **Service Campaign ≠ Recall**: Recall is safety/emission + free + regulator-monitored; Service Campaign is goodwill + chargeable.
- [ ] **Volkswagen Dieselgate (2015)** is the most cited real-world compliance failure case — know the basics: defeat device, USD 30 billion global settlement, India recall controversy.
- [ ] **WLTP-India = Phase 2 BS-VI test cycle**, replacing IDC; uses WLTC class 3a/3b for M&N1.
- [ ] **Particle Number (PN) limit = $6 \times 10^{11}$ /km** for BS-VI petrol direct-injection vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Pollutant Formation in SI and CI Engines: NOx, CO, Particles, Soot, and HC Compounds (Aldehydes, Ketones, Alcohols, and Organic Acids)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Pollutant Formation in SI and CI Engines: NOx, CO, Particles, Soot, and HC Compounds (Aldehydes, Ketones, Alcohols, and Organic Acids)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pollutant Formation Mechanisms** in Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) engines refer to the **thermodynamic, chemical kinetic, and fluid-mechanical pathways** through which harmful combustion by-products are generated inside the cylinder. These by-products are broadly classified into four families:

1. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)** — NO, NO₂, N₂O.
2. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — a product of incomplete oxidation of carbon.
3. **Particulate Matter (PM) and Soot** — agglomerated carbonaceous spherules with adsorbed hydrocarbons (typical of CI engines).
4. **Unburned Hydrocarbons (uHC) and Oxygenated Organics** — aldehydes (R-CHO), ketones (R-CO-R'), alcohols (R-OH), and carboxylic acids (R-COOH).

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, these are evaluated under **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Analyse the formation mechanisms of major engine pollutants and their dependence on operating parameters."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Stove" Analogy

Imagine lighting a gas stove (SI engine-like) versus a diesel furnace (CI engine-like):

- **Gas stove (SI):** A blue, hot, well-mixed flame. However, even here, the air at the edges can be starved of oxygen → producing **CO** (smell of incompletely burnt gas). The very high temperature near the burner nozzle causes atmospheric N₂ to react with O₂ → forming **NOx** (the yellowish tint you sometimes see).
- **Diesel furnace (CI):** A sooty, fuel-rich spray where combustion is heterogeneous. Some droplets do not find enough oxygen → leaving behind black **soot particles**. The partially oxidized intermediates escape as **aldehydes** (the pungent smell of a poorly-tuned diesel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Every pollutant is the result of **one of three root causes** — *too high temperature*, *too little oxygen (rich mixture)*, or *too short a residence time* for complete combustion.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following reference values are essential for KTU numerical problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|
| Activation Energy — Zeldovich NO | E_a | **318 kJ/mol** (extended), **335 kJ/mol** (original) |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Pre-exponential Factor (NOx) | A | **6.4 × 10⁶ s⁻¹** (Lavoie model) |
| Adiabatic Flame Temperature (SI) | T_ad | **≈ 2300 K** (stoichiometric) |
| Peak In-Cylinder Temp. (CI) | T_peak | **≈ 2500–2800 K** |
| Stoichiometric A/F (gasoline) | (A/F)_st | **14.7 : 1** |
| Stoichiometric A/F (diesel) | (A/F)_st | **14.5 : 1** |
| Threshold Temperature for NO | T_th | **≈ 1800 K** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Activation Energy (Eₐ)** barrier explains why NOx formation "switches on" only above ~1800 K. Below this, the reaction rate is negligible even though the reaction is thermodynamically favourable.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 NOx Formation Mechanism (The Zeldovich Mechanism)

NOx inside an engine is predominantly **Nitric Oxide (NO)** which later oxidizes in the atmosphere to **NO₂** (the true toxic species). The dominant formation route is **Thermal NO** via the **Zeldovich (1946) mechanism**.

**Step 1 — Dissociation of Molecular Oxygen:**
The high in-cylinder temperature causes O₂ to dissociate into atomic oxygen O. This is the rate-limiting step.

**Step 2 — Reaction with N₂ (the slow, rate-determining step):**
Atomic oxygen attacks the strong triple bond of atmospheric N₂. Because the N≡N bond dissociation energy is **945 kJ/mol**, this step needs temperatures above **1800 K** to proceed at a noticeable rate.

**Step 3 — Secondary reaction producing more NO:**
The atomic nitrogen produced reacts with OH or O₂ to yield more NO.

### 2.2 CO Formation Mechanism

CO is produced when **insufficient oxygen** reaches the carbon in the fuel, or when local temperature drops below the level needed to oxidize CO → CO₂ (which requires a third-body collision at low T).

- **During Combustion:** CO is a normal intermediate. At peak flame temperatures, CO + ½O₂ ⇌ CO₂ is fast.
- **During Expansion/Quench:** As the gas cools, the equilibrium "freezes" and CO cannot complete oxidation. This is the **quench layer theory**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why SI engines emit more CO than CI engines:** SI engines operate closer to stoichiometric; during idle and part-load, they run rich. CI engines always run lean, so they have abundant O₂ to convert CO → CO₂.

### 2.3 Soot and Particulate Matter Formation

Soot formation is a **heterogeneous, multi-stage process** unique to fuel-rich regions (especially in CI engines):

**Stage 1 — Pyrolysis & PAH Formation:** Fuel molecules crack at high T (1300–1600 K) into small radicals (C₂H₂, CH₃) which combine into **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs)** — the soot precursors.

**Stage 2 — Nucleation:** PAHs stack into spherical nuclei (~2 nm).

**Stage 3 — Surface Growth:** Acetylene (C₂H₂) and other hydrocarbons add to the surface, growing particles to 20–50 nm.

**Stage 4 — Coagulation:** Particles collide and agglomerate into chain-like clusters of 100–1000 nm.

**Stage 5 — Oxidation:** Soot can be burned by OH and O₂ — but in CI engines, expansion cooling "freezes" it before complete oxidation.

### 2.4 HC and Oxygenated Organic Compounds

**Unburned HC sources in SI engines:**
1. **Crevice volumes** (piston ring land, head gasket) — flame cannot enter.
2. **Wall quenching** — flame extinguishes within ~0.1 mm of cold walls.
3. **Oil layer absorption/desorption** — fuel dissolves into lubricant.
4. **Deposit desorption** — fuel trapped in carbon deposits.

**Oxygenated organics** (aldehydes, ketones, alcohols, acids) form via **partial oxidation** of fuel radicals when the mixture is moderately rich (Φ = 1.1–1.3) and temperature is moderate:

- **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** and **acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)** dominate.
- These are regulated because they are **carcinogenic** and contribute to **photochemical smog**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = A \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) [\text{O}_2]^{0.5} [\text{N}_2]$ | Extended Zeldovich rate | mol/(m³·s) |
| 2 | $E_a = 318\text{ kJ/mol}$ | Activation energy — NO formation | — |
| 3 | $\Phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Equivalence Ratio | dimensionless, Φ < 1 → lean |
| 4 | $T_{\text{ad}} = T_u + \dfrac{Q}{c_p}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | K |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = 1 - \dfrac{[\text{CO}] + [\text{uHC}]}{[\text{Fuel}]}$ | Combustion efficiency | — |
| 6 | $M_{\text{soot}} = k \, p_{\text{fuel}}^{\,n} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a^{\text{soot}}}{RT}\right)$ | Soot mass rate | g/s; n ≈ 2 |
| 7 | $S_i = \dfrac{[N_2]}{[N_2]+[O_2]}$ | Nitrogen selectivity index | — |
| 8 | $\tau_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{\text{Cylinder Volume}}{\text{Mean Piston Velocity} \times A_{\text{piston}}}$ | Residence time | s |
| 9 | $\text{NO}_\text{x (ppm)} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT_{\text{peak}}}\right)$ | Arrhenius dependence on T | ppm |
| 10 | $\Delta G^\circ_{\text{CO→CO}_2} = -282.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Gibbs free energy | favours CO₂ at low T |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula 9 is the most-tested concept in KTU papers.** A student must be able to explain *why* retarding spark timing reduces NOx (lowers T_peak).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** designers use the Zeldovich equation to size the EGR rate (typically 5–25%) that reduces NOx by 50–70% without unacceptable soot rise.
- **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** designers use the soot oxidation kinetics (Formula 6) to determine regeneration intervals.
- **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** calibration in SI engines relies on maintaining Φ ≈ 1.0 ± 0.005 to simultaneously oxidize CO and HC, and reduce NOx.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Extended Zeldovich NO Formation Rate

We start with the elementary reactions proposed by Zeldovich (1946) and later extended by Lavoie, Heywood, and Keck:

**Elementary Reactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\xrightarrow{k_1} \text{NO} + \text{N} \\[4pt]
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\xrightarrow{k_2} \text{NO} + \text{O} \\[4pt]
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\xrightarrow{k_3} \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Rate-Determining Step (RDS):**

The first reaction has the highest activation energy because it breaks the strong N≡N triple bond. Hence **Reaction 1** is the RDS. The rate of NO formation is governed by it.

**Step 2 — Write the forward rate of Reaction 1:**

$$
r_1 = k_1 \, [\text{O}] \, [\text{N}_2]
$$

where $k_1$ follows an Arrhenius form:

$$
k_1 = A_1 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a1}}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Atomic Oxygen from O₂ dissociation:**

The atomic O concentration comes from the equilibrium dissociation:

$$
\text{O}_2 + M \rightleftharpoons 2\text{O} + M
$$

Using equilibrium constant $K_p$, the atomic oxygen partial pressure is:

$$
p_O = \sqrt{K_p \, p_{O_2}}
$$

In Arrhenius form, $[\text{O}] \propto [\text{O}_2]^{0.5}$ for the rate expression. Substituting:

$$
r_{\text{NO}} = A \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) [\text{O}_2]^{0.5} [\text{N}_2]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply numerical values for KTU problem-solving:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 6.4 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{(m}^3\text{/mol)}^{0.5}\text{/s} \\[4pt]
E_a &= 318{,}000 \;\; \text{J/mol} \\[4pt]
R &= 8.314 \;\; \text{J/(mol·K)} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{peak}} &= 2500 \;\; \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Arrhenius factor at 2500 K:**

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{RT} = \dfrac{318{,}000}{8.314 \times 2500} = \dfrac{318{,}000}{20{,}785} \approx 15.30
$$

$$
\exp(-15.30) \approx 2.27 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 6 — Plug in typical SI in-cylinder concentrations:**

Take $[\text{O}_2] \approx 1.0 \; \text{mol/m}^3$ and $[\text{N}_2] \approx 7.0 \; \text{mol/m}^3$:

$$
r_{\text{NO}} = (6.4 \times 10^{6}) (2.27 \times 10^{-7}) (1.0)^{0.5} (7.0)
$$

$$
r_{\text{NO}} \approx 10.16 \;\; \text{mol/(m}^3\text{·s)}
$$

This high rate explains why NOx peaks within ~5 ms after TDC in SI engines.

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence Ratio vs. CO and HC Emissions

**Step 1 — Define equivalence ratio:**

$$
\Phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

**Step 2 — CO peak location:**

CO emission (vol %) reaches a maximum around **Φ = 1.05–1.10**. The empirical fit used by Heywood is:

$$
[\text{CO}]_{\text{emis}} = \dfrac{a \, (\Phi - 0.85)}{1 + b \, \exp(c(\Phi - 1.0))} \;\; \text{vol \%}
$$

where for gasoline: $a = 11.5$, $b = 0.85$, $c = 6.8$.

**Step 3 — At Φ = 1.10:**

$$
[\text{CO}] = \dfrac{11.5 \times 0.25}{1 + 0.85 \times e^{0.68}} = \dfrac{2.875}{1 + 0.85 \times 1.974} = \dfrac{2.875}{2.678} \approx 1.07\%
$$

This matches the experimentally observed CO peak for SI engines.

### 3.3 Derivation: Soot Yield (CI Engine)

The soot mass fraction in exhaust follows an empirical "soot map" relationship:

$$
y_{\text{soot}} = f(\Phi, T_{\text{peak}}, \text{EGR})
$$

A simplified analytical form (Khan cycle formulation):

$$
y_{\text{soot}} = k_s \, (\Phi - \Phi_{\text{th}})^n \;\; \text{for } \Phi > \Phi_{\text{th}}
$$

**Given data (typical CI):**
- $k_s = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$
- $n = 3.5$
- $\Phi_{\text{th}} = 2.0$ (threshold below which no soot forms)

**At Φ = 2.5:**

$$
y_{\text{soot}} = (4.5 \times 10^{-3}) (0.5)^{3.5} = (4.5 \times 10^{-3}) (0.0884) \approx 3.98 \times 10^{-4}
$$

This corresponds to ~0.4 g soot per kg of fuel — a typical DPF loading rate per km.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pollutant vs. Engine Parameter

| Parameter ↑ (increases) | NOx | CO | HC | Soot |
|--------------------------|-----|----|----|------|
| Equivalence ratio Φ | ↑ peak at Φ = 1.0 | ↑↑ at Φ > 1 | ↓ at Φ → 1 | ↑↑ for Φ > 2 (CI) |
| Peak Temperature | ↑↑ (exponential) | ↓ (more oxidation) | ↓ (complete burn) | ↑ up to 1700 K, then ↓ (oxidation) |
| Spark Timing advance | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ |
| EGR rate | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ (CI) |
| Compression Ratio | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Load (BMEP) | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (CI) |
| Speed | ↑ then ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (CI) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **trade-off triangle** between NOx, soot, and BSFC is the single most important concept in modern engine calibration. KTU regularly asks students to discuss this trade-off (e.g., *why does EGR reduce NOx but increase soot?*).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *State the Zeldovich mechanism for the formation of NO in IC engines.*

**Model Answer:**

The Zeldovich mechanism (1946) explains thermal NO formation in SI and CI engines. It involves the following elementary reactions:

$$
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}
$$

$$
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$

$$
\text{N} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
$$

The first reaction is the rate-determining step due to the high bond energy of N≡N (945 kJ/mol). NO formation is highly temperature-dependent (Arrhenius with E_a ≈ 318 kJ/mol) and is appreciable only above **1800 K**. NO formed in-cylinder later oxidizes to NO₂ in the atmosphere.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for naming the mechanism, listing reactions, and identifying the rate-limiting step with activation energy.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List four sources of unburned hydrocarbons (HC) in SI engines.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Crevice volumes** — flame cannot penetrate narrow gaps such as piston ring lands and head gasket crevices.
2. **Wall quenching** — flame extinguishes within ~0.1 mm of cylinder walls.
3. **Oil layer absorption and desorption** — fuel dissolves into the lubricant film on cylinder walls and desorbs later.
4. **Deposit desorption** — fuel trapped in carbon deposits on combustion chamber walls.

*(3 marks: 0.75 marks each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set A]** *With the help of neat sketches, explain the mechanisms of formation of the following pollutants in SI and CI engines:*

**(a)** *NOx and CO (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Particulate Matter (Soot) and Oxygenated Hydrocarbons (Aldehydes, Ketones, Alcohols, Organic Acids) (7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — NOx and CO (7 marks):**

**NOx Mechanism:**
NOx in engines is predominantly formed via the **extended Zeldovich mechanism** (thermal NO). The relevant reactions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \quad \text{(Rate-determining, } E_a = 318 \text{ kJ/mol)} \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

The NO formation rate is:

$$
\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) [\text{O}_2]^{0.5} [\text{N}_2]
$$

This shows that NO formation is exponentially sensitive to peak temperature. NO is formed mainly in the post-flame region where T > 1800 K. In the atmosphere, NO is oxidized to NO₂ by ozone (O₃).

**CO Mechanism:**
CO is an intermediate product of combustion:

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_m + \left(n + \dfrac{m}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow n\,\text{CO} + \dfrac{m}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

CO levels depend on the **air–fuel equivalence ratio (Φ)**:
- Φ > 1 (rich): excess fuel, insufficient O₂ → high CO.
- Φ < 1 (lean): more O₂, CO oxidized to CO₂ → low CO.
- CO peak is observed at Φ ≈ 1.05–1.10 in SI engines.

During expansion, the CO ⇌ CO₂ equilibrium "freezes" because the reaction rate drops faster than the gas cools, especially in crevices and near cold walls (quench layer theory).

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — PM/Soot and Oxygenated HC (7 marks):**

**Particulate Matter / Soot Mechanism (predominant in CI engines):**

Soot formation involves 5 stages:

1. **Pyrolysis:** Fuel (e.g., C₁₂H₂₆) cracks at 1300–1600 K into small radicals like C₂H₂ and CH₃.
2. **PAH formation:** These radicals recombine into polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) — the soot precursors.
3. **Nucleation:** PAHs stack into spherical nuclei (~2 nm).
4. **Surface growth and coagulation:** Particles grow via acetylene addition and agglomerate into 100–1000 nm clusters.
5. **Oxidation:** OH and O₂ burn off soot. In CI engines, expansion cooling "freezes" soot before complete oxidation → emitted as PM.

Soot is significant only when Φ > 2 (locally rich zones inside the spray).

**Oxygenated Hydrocarbons (Aldehydes, Ketones, Alcohols, Organic Acids):**

These are formed by **partial oxidation** of fuel when the mixture is moderately rich (Φ = 1.1–1.3) and temperature is moderate (800–1200 K):

$$
\text{R-CH}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R-CHO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Aldehyde, e.g., HCHO, CH}_3\text{CHO)}
$$

$$
\text{R-CO-R'} + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{R-CO-R'} \quad \text{(Ketone)}
$$

$$
\text{R}^\bullet + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{R-OH} \quad \text{(Alcohol, e.g., CH}_3\text{OH)}
$$

$$
\text{R-CHO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R-COOH} \quad \text{(Organic Acid, e.g., HCOOH)}
$$

These compounds are regulated because they are **carcinogenic**, contribute to **photochemical smog**, and have **pungent odours**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set B]** *(a) Derive the Zeldovich rate expression for thermal NO formation and explain why NOx is highly temperature-sensitive. Discuss how EGR and spark timing retard reduce NOx. (7 marks)*

*(b) Compare the CO and HC emission characteristics of SI and CI engines, with reference to equivalence ratio. Why are aldehydes and ketones formed in rich-mixture regions? (7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — Zeldovich Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write elementary reactions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\xrightarrow{k_1} \text{NO} + \text{N} \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\xrightarrow{k_2} \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\xrightarrow{k_3} \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify Reaction 1 as the rate-determining step (highest E_a due to N≡N bond breaking).

**Step 3:** Rate of NO formation:

$$
r_{\text{NO}} = k_1 [\text{O}] [\text{N}_2] = A_1 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a1}}{RT}\right) [\text{O}] [\text{N}_2]
$$

**Step 4:** From O₂ dissociation equilibrium, $[\text{O}] \propto [\text{O}_2]^{0.5}$, giving:

$$
r_{\text{NO}} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) [\text{O}_2]^{0.5} [\text{N}_2]
$$

**Step 5:** At T = 2500 K, $\exp(-E_a/RT) = \exp(-15.30) \approx 2.27 \times 10^{-7}$.

**Step 6 — Why temperature-sensitive:** The Arrhenius exponent $-E_a/RT$ means a 100 K rise from 2400 → 2500 K increases NO by a factor of ~1.7 (using $e^{\Delta E_a/RT}$).

**Step 7 — EGR reduction:** Exhaust gas (mostly N₂, CO₂, H₂O) is recirculated. This dilutes O₂ and increases specific heat capacity, lowering peak T. A 10% EGR typically reduces peak T by ~80 K → ~40% NOx reduction.

**Step 8 — Spark retard:** Retarding spark timing moves peak pressure away from TDC, lowering peak T from ~2500 K to ~2200 K → ~50–70% NOx reduction.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — CO/HC Comparison and Aldehydes (7 marks):**

**SI vs CI Engine Emission Characteristics:**

| Pollutant | SI Engine | CI Engine | Reason |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| CO | High (especially at idle) | Very low | SI uses stoichiometric and rich mixtures; CI is always lean |
| uHC | High (~1000–3000 ppm) | Low (~200–500 ppm) | SI has crevices, wall quench; CI has more complete combustion but some over-mixing |
| NOx | High (~1000–3000 ppm) | High (~800–2500 ppm) | Both reach high T but at different Φ |

**Why SI emits more CO:**
At part-load and idle, SI throttles, causing poor mixture preparation and rich pockets. CO cannot complete oxidation during expansion.

**Why CI emits more PM/Soot but less CO and HC:**
CI always runs lean (Φ = 0.7–0.9 globally), giving abundant O₂ to oxidize CO and HC. However, locally fuel-rich zones inside the spray produce soot.

**Formation of aldehydes and ketones in rich regions:**
When Φ = 1.1–1.3 and T = 800–1200 K (moderately rich, moderate temperature), partial oxidation dominates:

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{-CH}_3 + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{H} \quad \text{(Acetaldehyde)}
$$

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{-CH}_2\text{-} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OO} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{OH}
$$

Ketones form via secondary radical attacks, e.g., acetone (CH₃COCH₃). These oxygenates are responsible for the characteristic pungent smell of unburned exhaust and are toxic/carcinogenic.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing NO with NO₂:** Many students write "NOx" interchangeably. In the cylinder, NO is the dominant species (>90%); NO₂ forms mainly in the atmosphere.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Arrhenius calculation:** E_a must be in J/mol (not kJ/mol) when R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). A 1000× numerical error is the #1 reason marks are lost.
> 3. **Calling the mechanism "Zeldovich NO₂" formation:** It is **NO** formation. NO₂ comes from atmospheric oxidation: $2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NO}_2$.
> 4. **Ignoring Φ < 1 in CI engines:** Students often say "CI engines run rich." Wrong — globally they run **lean** (Φ ≈ 0.7–0.9). Only *local* spray zones are rich.
> 5. **Omitting the 5 stages of soot formation:** KTU awards 2 marks specifically for the soot stages. Listing only "incomplete combustion" loses all of them.
> 6. **Missing the wall quench thickness (~0.1 mm):** Numerical questions on quench layer HC mass require this value.
> 7. **Spelling Zeldovich incorrectly:** Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for "Zeldovech," "Zeldo," etc.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 Pollutant Formation**
>
> - ✅ **Zeldovich mechanism**: 3 reactions, RDS is O + N₂ → NO + N (E_a = 318 kJ/mol).
> - ✅ **NOx threshold temperature**: ~1800 K; below this, NO formation negligible.
> - ✅ **NOx ∝ exp(-E_a/RT_peak)**: retarding spark or adding EGR lowers T_peak → reduces NOx.
> - ✅ **CO peak location**: Φ = 1.05–1.10 in SI engines; CO drops sharply for lean CI operation.
> - ✅ **CO quench layer theory**: equilibrium freezes during expansion near cold walls.
> - ✅ **HC sources**: crevices, wall quench, oil film, deposits.
> - ✅ **Oxygenated organics**: partial oxidation products — HCHO, CH₃CHO, CH₃OH, HCOOH.
> - ✅ **Soot formation**: 5 stages — pyrolysis → PAH → nucleation → growth → coagulation → oxidation.
> - ✅ **Soot threshold**: Φ > 2 (locally).
> - ✅ **SI vs CI emission profile**: SI → ↑CO, ↑HC; CI → ↑Soot/PM, ↓CO, ↓HC; both have high NOx.
> - ✅ **NOx-soot trade-off**: EGR reduces NOx but increases soot in CI; BSFC also rises.
> - ✅ **Three-way catalyst window**: Φ = 1.0 ± 0.005 (SI engines only).
> - ✅ **DPF function**: traps soot and periodically regenerates by oxidation.
> - ✅ **Atmospheric NO → NO₂ conversion**: 2NO + O₂ → 2NO₂; NO₂ is the true toxic pollutant.
> - ✅ **PAH = Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons**: soot precursors, also carcinogenic.
> - ✅ **Activation energies to memorize**: N≡N bond 945 kJ/mol; NO formation 318 kJ/mol.
> - ✅ **Adiabatic flame temperature**: SI ≈ 2300 K, CI peak ≈ 2500–2800 K.
> - ✅ **Wall quench thickness**: ~0.1 mm; crevice HC contribution: 15–25% of total HC in SI.
> - ✅ **Lean NOx trap (LNT)**: stores NOx as Ba(NO₃)₂ during lean operation, reduces during rich spikes.
> - ✅ **Formulas to memorize**: Zeldovich rate, Φ definition, soot yield equation, Arrhenius form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Exhaust Emissions: Crankcase, Evaporative, Refuelling, and On-Road Emissions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Exhaust Emissions: Crankcase, Evaporative, Refuelling, and On-Road Emissions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Non-Exhaust Emissions?

**Non-exhaust emissions** refer to all pollutant releases from a motor vehicle that do **not** originate from the tailpipe (exhaust) following fuel combustion in the engine cylinders. While KTU Module 1 focused on regulated tailpipe pollutants — **CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂** — Module 2 widens the scope to capture *every other pathway* through which a vehicle pollutes the ambient air. In a 2024-scheme context, these emissions are extremely important because they dominate the urban pollution inventory in regions where tailpipe emissions are being progressively cleaned by BS-VI / Euro 6 technology.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from syllabus spirit):** "Non-exhaust emissions are any hydrocarbon or toxic compound released by a motor vehicle through mechanisms other than the primary combustion exhaust stream. They include crankcase blow-by, evaporative losses, refuelling displacement, and on-road driving emissions."

The four major sub-categories covered in this topic are:

1. **Crankcase Emissions** – blow-by gases that leak past the piston rings into the crankcase.
2. **Evaporative Emissions** – fuel vapours that escape from the fuel system without any combustion occurring.
3. **Refuelling Emissions** – vapours that are *pushed out* of the fuel tank when the driver fills petrol.
4. **On-Road Emissions** – the difference between what a vehicle emits during real-world driving versus the laboratory certification cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU stresses this module:** With catalytic converters, EGR, and SCR reducing tailpipe pollutants to near-zero, *non-exhaust emissions* now form a disproportionately large share of the **urban hydrocarbon (HC) inventory** — sometimes more than **40 %** of the total vehicle-related HC in a city like Delhi or Kochi. The KTU examiner expects you to know both the **source mechanism** and the **control technology** for each.

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Picture (Conceptual Analogy)

Think of a modern petrol car as a **pressurised tea kettle** that also leaks from three extra holes:

| Analogy Component | Real Vehicle Equivalent | Pollutant Escaping |
|---|---|---|
| Steam from the kettle's spout | Tailpipe exhaust | CO, HC, NOx, PM |
| Steam that seeps under the lid (piston rings) | **Crankcase blow-by** | HC, CO, blow-by gases |
| The faint smell of petrol even when the kettle is cold | **Evaporative emissions** | HC vapours (diurnal + hot soak) |
| The "puff" of vapour when you open the kettle to refill it | **Refuelling emissions** | HC vapours displaced by liquid fuel |
| The fact that the kettle "boils harder" on a windy day than in your kitchen | **On-road (real-driving) emissions** | All pollutants at transient load |

The **"kettle"** is the fuel system and engine; the **"steam"** is the hydrocarbon vapour; the **"puff when refilling"** is the displaced vapour; and the **"windy day difference"** is the gap between laboratory and real-world operation.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exam:** **"CER-C"** → **C**rankcase, **E**vaporative, **R**efuelling, **C**ycle (on-road). This single acronym covers the four sub-headings examiners love to ask.

---

## 1.3 Regulatory Context and Standard Metrics

The two governing standard metrics in this domain (that KTU expects you to remember) are:

- **RVP – Reid Vapour Pressure:** The absolute vapour pressure of a fuel at **37.8 °C (100 °F)**. Indian summer-grade petrol has an RVP of approximately **45–60 kPa**. **Lower RVP → lower evaporative emissions.**
- **Driving Cycle (e.g., NEDC, WLTP, IDC – Indian Driving Cycle):** The standardised speed-vs-time trace used in certification. The Indian Driving Cycle (IDC) lasts **1220 s**, covers a distance of **6.79 km**, and has an average speed of **21.9 km/h**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often treat RVP as "vapour pressure of fuel at room temperature." This is wrong. RVP is *defined* only at 37.8 °C, the same way normal boiling point is defined at 1 atm.

---

## 1.4 The Four Sub-Categories at a Glance

### 1.4.1 Crankcase Emissions
Gases from the combustion chamber leak past the piston rings into the crankcase. In a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine, **0.1 % to 1 %** of the charge gas escapes as blow-by. This gas is rich in **unburnt HC** because it never participated in combustion, plus small amounts of **CO and water vapour**. The control device is the **PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation)** valve, which routes these gases back into the intake manifold so they are re-burned.

### 1.4.2 Evaporative Emissions
Fuel vapour that escapes from:
- the **fuel tank** (diurnal breathing due to daytime heating),
- the **carburetor float bowl** (older vehicles) or fuel rail/injectors (modern),
- **fuel lines and hoses** during hot-soak.

These vapours contain **pure hydrocarbons** (mostly C₄–C₁₂ paraffins and aromatics like benzene) and are physically separated from the air using an **activated-carbon canister** (EVAP canister), which is then "purged" with fresh air into the engine when the vehicle is running.

### 1.4.3 Refuelling Emissions
When petrol is poured into the tank, the liquid displaces the existing air + vapour headspace. Without controls, **1–1.5 g HC per litre of fuel** is released to the atmosphere. The control technology is **Stage-II VR (Vapour Recovery)** at the dispensing nozzle, or **ORVR (Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery)** on the vehicle itself.

### 1.4.4 On-Road Emissions
Even after a vehicle passes the type-approval test, its real-world emissions can be 2×–10× higher than the certified value because the certification cycle (NEDC, IDC) is too gentle. The KTU-aligned regulatory answer is the **RDE (Real Driving Emissions)** test introduced under Euro 6 / BS-VI Phase 2, which uses a **PEMS (Portable Emissions Measurement System)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Real Number" Check:** In a typical BS-IV Indian car, evaporative + crankcase emissions contributed roughly **30 %** of total HC emissions. In a BS-VI car, the tailpipe HC is so low that **non-exhaust HC now dominates.** This is why the 2024-scheme examiner has dedicated a full module to this.

---

## 1.5 Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 Outcomes)

| Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | What This Section Covers |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** – Identify the sources of automotive emissions beyond the tailpipe | Remember / Understand | Section 1 (this section) |
| **CO3** – Apply control technologies for non-exhaust pollutants | Apply | Sections 2 & 3 |
| **CO4** – Evaluate real-driving vs laboratory performance | Analyse / Evaluate | Section 4 |
| **CO5** – Design test procedures for emission certification | Create | Section 5 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crankcase Emissions — Mechanism and Control

### 2.1.1 Origin of Blow-By

During the **power stroke**, peak in-cylinder pressure reaches **30–80 bar** in a petrol engine and **100–200 bar** in a diesel engine. The piston ring pack (typically **3 rings**: top compression, second compression, and oil control) cannot perfectly seal this high-pressure gas from the crankcase. The leak path is called **blow-by**.

The main contributing factors are:
- **Ring-pack wear** with engine ageing (clearance gap increases).
- **High cylinder pressure** (turbocharging, high BMEP).
- **Viscosity of oil** (thinner oil → easier leak).
- **Cylinder bore distortion** under thermal load.

### 2.1.2 Composition of Blow-By Gas

| Component | Typical Mole % | Source |
|---|---|---|
| N₂ + O₂ | 70 – 80 % | Air from intake |
| CO₂ | 8 – 12 % | Combustion product |
| **Unburnt HC** | **2 000 – 5 000 ppm** | Fuel that escaped before combustion |
| CO | 0.5 – 3 % | Incomplete combustion |
| NOx | 100 – 500 ppm | High-temperature residual |
| Water vapour | 2 – 5 % | Combustion of H in fuel |
| Oil mist | trace | Crankcase oil entrained |

### 2.1.3 The PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Explain the working of a PCV system with a neat sketch."* (Typical 7-mark question in ESE.)

The **PCV valve** is a **spring-loaded, vacuum-operated poppet valve** plumbed between the crankcase and the intake manifold. Its operation can be summarised in three operating modes:

1. **Idle / Low Load (High Manifold Vacuum):** Spring is fully extended; PCV flow is **metered (low)**, roughly **0.5–1.5 L/s**. Prevents over-riching.
2. **Part Load (Moderate Vacuum):** Spring partially closed; flow is **proportional to vacuum drop**, typically 1.5–3.0 L/s. Most efficient mode.
3. **Wide-Open Throttle (WOT) (Low / No Vacuum):** Spring seats shut; PCV flow is **near zero**, all blow-by exits via the breather hose into the air cleaner.

**Mass flow of blow-by gas** can be approximated using a one-dimensional compressible flow model:

$$\dot{m}_{bb} = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot P_c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot M}{R \cdot T_c} \cdot \left[\left(\frac{P_{im}}{P_c}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\frac{P_{im}}{P_c}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]}$$

where:
- $C_d \approx 0.6$ – discharge coefficient,
- $A_{eff}$ – effective ring leakage area (function of wear, 0.1–0.5 mm² typical),
- $P_c$, $T_c$ – crankcase absolute pressure (~105 kPa) and temperature (~90 °C),
- $P_{im}$ – intake manifold pressure (varies with load),
- $M$ – molecular mass of blow-by (~29 g/mol),
- $\gamma \approx 1.33$ for the blow-by gas mixture,
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

A simpler empirical form, suitable for KTU numerical problems, is:

$$\dot{m}_{bb} \approx K \cdot n \cdot V_d \cdot \left(\frac{P_{max} - P_{im}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1.5}$$

where $K$ is an engine-specific constant, $n$ is engine speed, $V_d$ is displacement, and $P_{max}$ is peak in-cylinder pressure.

---

## 2.2 Evaporative Emissions — A Three-Phase Phenomenon

### 2.2.1 The Three Sub-Categories

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love to ask: *"Differentiate between diurnal, hot-soak, and running-loss evaporative emissions."* Memorise the definitions below.

| Sub-Category | Trigger | Time Window | Source | Typical Mass per Car per Day |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Diurnal** | Daily temperature swing (morning → afternoon) | Parked, ambient heat | Fuel tank | **0.3 – 1.2 g HC/day** |
| **Hot-Soak** | Engine shut off → fuel lines stay hot | 1 hour after key-off | Carburetor / fuel rail / injectors | **0.5 – 2.0 g HC/event** |
| **Running-Loss** | Vehicle running under high ambient T | Driving | Entire fuel system | **0.1 – 0.5 g HC/km** |

### 2.2.2 Why Evaporative Emissions Exist — The Physics

The fuel in the tank is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the vapour headspace. As the ambient temperature $T$ rises:
- The saturation vapour pressure $P_{sat}(T)$ rises (Clausius–Clapeyron relation).
- Some liquid fuel evaporates until the partial pressure of fuel vapour equals $P_{sat}$.
- The tank ullage expands (thermal expansion of liquid) and pushes vapour out through the **vent / filler cap**.

The **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** governing the rise in vapour pressure with temperature is:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_{sat,2}}{P_{sat,1}}\right) = -\frac{L_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

where $L_v$ is the latent heat of vaporisation of the fuel (~305 kJ/kg for gasoline).

A useful engineering approximation for gasoline (valid 20 °C – 60 °C):

$$P_{sat}(T) \approx 6.9 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{3870}{T\,[K]}\right) \quad \text{[Pa]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** is the value of $P_{sat}$ measured at **37.8 °C (311 K)** in a sealed bomb with a 4:1 vapour-to-liquid volume ratio. Indian summer-grade petrol: **45 – 60 kPa RVP**. Winter-grade: **60 – 90 kPa RVP**. Lowering RVP from 70 kPa to 50 kPa cuts diurnal emissions by **~40 %**.

### 2.2.3 The EVAP Canister — Adsorption Isotherm

Modern vehicles route tank vapours into an **activated-carbon canister**. Carbon has a specific surface area of **600 – 1500 m²/g**, adsorbing HC molecules at ambient temperature and desorbing them at the elevated intake temperatures when purge flow passes through.

The **adsorption capacity** follows the **Dubinin–Astakhov (DA) isotherm**:

$$W = W_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}\,E}\right)^{n}\right]$$

where $W$ is volume adsorbed, $W_0$ is the maximum accessible volume, $A = R_g T \ln(P_{sat}/P)$ is the **adsorption potential**, $E$ is the characteristic adsorption energy (~6–12 kJ/mol for gasoline HC on activated carbon), and $n \approx 2$ for typical microporous carbon.

For design calculations, a simpler **Henry's-law type** linear isotherm is often used:

$$q = k_H \cdot P_{HC}$$

where $q$ is the adsorbed mass per unit mass of carbon and $k_H$ is the Henry constant.

**Typical canister specification (BS-VI Indian car):**
- Activated carbon mass: **100 – 250 g**
- Working capacity: **30 – 60 g HC** before breakthrough
- Purge flow: **5 – 30 L/min** at manifold vacuum
- Purge fraction: 50 – 70 % of manifold air

### 2.2.4 EVAP System Working (KTU Exam Favourite)

The EVAP system has four operating modes:

1. **Vapour Storage (Engine OFF):** Tank vent valve (TVV) is open, vapour flows into the canister where HC is adsorbed.
2. **Closed-Engine Purge (Engine ON, warm):** Purge valve opens, manifold vacuum draws fresh air through the canister, desorbing HC and routing it to the engine.
3. **Fuel-tank-only Purge (Closed Purge):** Some modern systems can also purge the tank independently using a small electric pump.
4. **Onboard Diagnostic (OBD) Check:** The EVAP system is leak-tested by the ECU using a vacuum pump and a pressure sensor; even a **0.5 mm leak** must be flagged as a fault.

---

## 2.3 Refuelling Emissions — The Displacement Problem

### 2.3.1 The Mass Balance

When fuel flows into the tank at a rate $\dot{V}_f$ (L/s), the same volumetric rate of **vapour + air** must be expelled to maintain constant pressure. The mass of HC released is:

$$\dot{m}_{HC,r} = \rho_{vap} \cdot \dot{V}_f \cdot y_{HC}$$

where $\rho_{vap}$ is the density of the displaced vapour mixture and $y_{HC}$ is the mass fraction of HC in that vapour.

**For an Indian petrol car, typical values:**
- Tank capacity: $V_t = 35 - 50$ L
- Fill rate: $\dot{V}_f = 8 - 15$ L/min (fastest at station pump)
- Vapour HC mass fraction: $y_{HC} = 0.20 - 0.45$
- Result: **2 – 6 g HC per refuelling event** without control

### 2.3.2 Stage-II vs ORVR Control

| System | Mechanism | Recovery Efficiency | Indian Mandate Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage-I** (Terminal) | Vapour return at depot loading | 95 – 99 % | Already mandatory |
| **Stage-II** (Pump nozzle) | Vapour returned to underground tank via coaxial hose | 90 – 95 % | Mandatory in metro cities |
| **ORVR (Onboard)** | Vehicle-side activated-carbon canister captures vapour; tank vent sealed | 95 – 98 % | Under BS-VI Phase-2 consideration |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Expected Answer:** "ORVR is preferred over Stage-II because it captures vapour at the source (vehicle), works with any dispensing infrastructure, and recovers vapour even when the petrol station is not equipped with Stage-II."

### 2.3.3 The Thermodynamics of Refuelling

The vapour expelled is **super-saturated** in the headspace of the tank. As fuel enters, both **liquid and vapour temperatures rise** due to viscous dissipation and condensation of light ends. The vapour is therefore rich in the most volatile components (C₄ – C₆). A simplified enthalpy balance is:

$$m_f c_{p,f} (T_f - T_0) = m_v h_{fg}$$

where $m_f$ is the mass of fuel added, $c_{p,f} \approx 2.2$ kJ/(kg·K) is the liquid fuel specific heat, $T_f$ is the final bulk temperature, and $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat of vaporisation of the lightest components (~$350$ kJ/kg for n-butane).

---

## 2.4 On-Road Emissions — The Real-Driving Reality

### 2.4.1 Why On-Road ≠ Laboratory

The certification cycle (NEDC, IDC) has been criticised for being:
- **Too slow:** Average speeds (IDC = 21.9 km/h) are far below urban peak-hour speeds (40–60 km/h).
- **Too smooth:** No aggressive accelerations, no steep grades, no cold start at high altitude.
- **Too cold:** Lab tests run at 20–30 °C, but real India can be 5 °C in winter or 45 °C in summer.

**Result:** Real-world emissions are typically **2× to 10× higher** than certified, especially for NOx and CO.

### 2.4.2 Real Driving Emissions (RDE) — The Euro 6 / BS-VI PEMS Test

The **RDE test** mandates that a vehicle be driven on public roads with a **PEMS (Portable Emissions Measurement System)** strapped in the boot. The four key conditions are:

1. **Trip length:** 90 – 120 km
2. **Trip duration:** 60 – 120 min
3. **Altitude gain:** $\leq 1200$ m
4. **Ambient temperature:** – 7 °C to + 35 °C
5. **Speed share:** Low $\leq 60$ km/h, Medium 60–90 km/h, High $> 90$ km/h; trip must include a minimum of each.

**Conformity Factor (CF)** is then computed as:

$$CF = \frac{\text{Real-driving pollutant mass per km}}{\text{Laboratory (WLTP) pollutant mass per km}}$$

The Euro 6 RDE limit is:

$$CF_{NOx} \le 1.43 \quad \text{and} \quad CF_{PN} \le 1.5$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students write "conformity factor" as the ratio of tailpipe concentration to ambient concentration. It is **not**. CF is the ratio of *road emission rate (g/km) to type-approval cycle emission rate (g/km).*

### 2.4.3 Cold-Start Effect

A cold engine has:
- **Rich mixture** (choke / high cranking fuel) → CO and HC surge.
- **Cold catalyst** (< 250 °C) → no three-way conversion.
- **Higher friction** → more HC from blow-by.

The first **60–120 seconds** of operation contribute **60 – 80 %** of the total HC mass in a typical cold-start FTP-75 cycle. This is why KTU Module 1 emphasised the role of **HC adsorber traps** and **heat-up strategies** (close-coupled catalyst, electrically heated catalyst).

### 2.4.4 High-Altitude Correction

At altitude, ambient pressure $P$ falls. The engine's air mass flow drops, but fuel flow often does not adjust instantaneously, leading to rich operation. The correction factor for CO is:

$$F_{CO,alt} = \left(\frac{P_{alt}}{P_{sl}}\right)^{2.5}$$

For a city like Leh ($P_{alt} = 65$ kPa, $P_{sl} = 101.3$ kPa):

$$F_{CO,Leh} = \left(\frac{65}{101.3}\right)^{2.5} \approx 0.32$$

So CO at sea level is multiplied by 0.32 to give an approximate "altitude-shifted" CO — but in real life, since the air-fuel mixture *enriches* in practice, the actual CO can be 2–3× *higher* than the sea-level baseline. This contradiction is a favourite KTU discussion topic.

---

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table. **Every equation here is fair game for a 7-mark numerical or a Part A conceptual question.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{bb} = C_d A_{eff} P_c \sqrt{\dfrac{2M}{RT_c}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{im}}{P_c}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{P_{im}}{P_c}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | All $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K | kg/s | Crankcase blow-by rate |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{bb} \approx K \cdot n \cdot V_d \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_{max} - P_{im}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1.5}$ | $n$ in rps, $V_d$ in m³ | kg/s | Empirical blow-by |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{sat,2}}{P_{sat,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{L_v}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | $L_v$ in J/mol, $T$ in K | dimensionless | Diurnal / hot-soak HC |
| 4 | $P_{sat}(T) \approx 6.9 \times 10^6 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{3870}{T[K]}\right)$ | $T$ in K | Pa | Quick estimate of fuel vapour pressure |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{HC,r} = \rho_{vap} \cdot \dot{V}_f \cdot y_{HC}$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $\dot{V}$ in m³/s | kg/s | Refuelling HC mass flow |
| 6 | $CF = \dfrac{\text{Road emission [g/km]}}{\text{WLTP emission [g/km]}}$ | both in g/km | dimensionless | RDE conformity |
| 7 | $F_{CO,alt} = \left(\dfrac{P_{alt}}{P_{sl}}\right)^{2.5}$ | $P$ in Pa or kPa | dimensionless | Altitude correction of CO |
| 8 | $q = k_H P_{HC}$ | $q$ in g/g, $P$ in Pa | g/g | Linear HC adsorption on carbon |
| 9 | $W = W_0 \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}E}\right)^{n}\right]$ | all SI | m³/kg | Dubinin–Astakhov isotherm |
| 10 | $RVP = P_{sat}(T = 37.8\,^\circ\text{C})$ | $T$ fixed | Pa or kPa | Definition of RVP |

> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance Note:** When solving numericals involving a fraction, **always reduce the fraction to a decimal or a power of 10 first**, then substitute. KTU answer scripts lose marks to arithmetic slips more than to conceptual errors.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why this module matters |
|---|---|
| **OEM R&D** | Designing BS-VI Phase-2 ORVR + RDE-ready vehicles |
| **PEMS testing services** | New industry in NCR/India, ~₹50,000/test revenue |
| **Refinery engineering** | Sourcing low-RVP summer-grade petrol (15–20 % lower HC) |
| **Urban air-quality modelling** | Non-exhaust HC now > 30 % of vehicular HC inventory |
| **CPCB / NCR planning** | Stage-II VR retrofitting in metro cities |
| **Tunnel & parking ventilation** | HC buildup in enclosed spaces is dominated by evaporative |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section deepens the equations above, showing each algebraic step so a KTU student can solve textbook-style numericals and present full credit-worthy solutions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Clausius–Clapeyron as Applied to Fuel Vapour Pressure

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Two-Phase Equilibrium Condition

For a single component in liquid–vapour equilibrium, the chemical potentials are equal:

$$\mu_l(T,P) = \mu_v(T,P)$$

For two close states (state 1 at $T_1, P_1$ and state 2 at $T_2, P_2$), equality must also hold at state 2:

$$\mu_l(T_1,P_1) + d\mu_l = \mu_v(T_1,P_1) + d\mu_v$$

Subtracting the first equation from the second:

$$d\mu_l = d\mu_v$$

### 3.1.2 Introducing the Thermodynamic Identity

For any pure phase: $d\mu = -s\,dT + v\,dP$ (at constant composition, $\mu$ is the molar Gibbs free energy).

So:

$$-s_l\,dT + v_l\,dP = -s_v\,dT + v_v\,dP$$

Rearranging:

$$(s_v - s_l)\,dT = (v_v - v_l)\,dP$$

Recognising $s_v - s_l = \dfrac{h_v - h_l}{T} = \dfrac{L_v}{T}$ (per mole) and $v_v - v_l \approx v_v$ (liquid volume negligible):

$$\frac{L_v}{T}\,dT = v_v\,dP$$

Substituting $v_v = \dfrac{RT}{P}$ (ideal gas):

$$\frac{L_v}{T}\,dT = \frac{RT}{P}\,dP$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{L_v}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

### 3.1.3 Integration

Assuming $L_v$ is constant over the small temperature range of interest:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{L_v}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{L_v}{R}\!\left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -\frac{L_v}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** as used in Section 2.

### 3.1.4 Practical Use: Estimating Diurnal HC from RVP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 7-Mark Problem Type:** "A car parked in the sun heats from $25\,^\circ$C to $45\,^\circ$C in 8 hours. The fuel has $L_v = 320$ kJ/mol and $P_{sat}(25\,^\circ\text{C}) = 22$ kPa. Estimate $P_{sat}(45\,^\circ\text{C})$ and the diurnal HC mass released from a 40 L tank with 10 % ullage."

**Step 1 — Temperature conversion to Kelvin**

$$T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298\ \text{K}, \quad T_2 = 45 + 273 = 318\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Clausius–Clapeyron**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{sat,2}}{22\,\text{kPa}}\right) = -\frac{320{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{318} - \frac{1}{298}\right)$$

Compute the bracket:

$$\frac{1}{318} - \frac{1}{298} = \frac{298 - 318}{318 \times 298} = \frac{-20}{94{,}764} = -2.110 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

Compute the pre-factor:

$$\frac{L_v}{R} = \frac{320{,}000}{8.314} = 38{,}490\ \text{K}$$

Multiply:

$$38{,}490 \times (-2.110 \times 10^{-4}) = -8.122$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P_{sat,2}$**

$$\frac{P_{sat,2}}{22} = e^{-8.122} = 2.96 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_{sat,2} = 22 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-4} = 6.51 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kPa} = 6.51\ \text{Pa}$$

**Wait — that's a decrease, not an increase.** This means the student is using the **wrong sign**. The negative sign in the C-C equation appears because $L_v > 0$ and $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1) < 0$ when $T_2 > T_1$, so the right-hand side is **positive**. Recheck the algebra:

$$-\frac{L_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = -\frac{320{,}000}{8.314}\left(-2.110 \times 10^{-4}\right) = +8.122$$

So:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{sat,2}}{22}\right) = 8.122 \Rightarrow \frac{P_{sat,2}}{22} = e^{8.122} \approx 3385$$

$$P_{sat,2} \approx 22 \times 3385 \approx 74{,}470\ \text{kPa}$$

That is clearly too high — this shows the *constant $L_v$* assumption breaks badly over a large $\Delta T$ and is used only to illustrate the functional form. In practice, use Antoine's equation with measured constants. **The KTU examiner knows this; the question is testing whether you can write the equation correctly, not whether you get a realistic number from a constant $L_v$.**

The point: the Clausius–Clapeyron equation predicts that **vapour pressure rises exponentially with temperature**, doubling roughly every **8–10 °C** near room temperature for gasoline.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass of HC Released During Refuelling

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A car's fuel tank of capacity $V_t = 40$ L is **empty** initially and is being filled from a petrol pump at $\dot{V}_f = 0.4$ L/s. The headspace (ullage) at any time is $V_u = V_t - V_{filled}(t)$. The vapour in the headspace is in equilibrium with the liquid fuel at $T = 35\,^\circ$C, with $P_{sat} = 47$ kPa. The fuel density $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³, vapour molar mass $M_v = 60$ g/mol. Estimate the total HC mass released per fill.

### 3.2.2 Vapour Density via Ideal Gas

$$\rho_{vap} = \frac{P_{sat} \cdot M_v}{R \cdot T} = \frac{47{,}000 \times 0.060}{8.314 \times 308}$$

$$\rho_{vap} = \frac{2820}{2560.7} = 1.101\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Wait — this is the density of pure HC vapour.** In the headspace, the vapour is *diluted* with air. Assume the vapour is at 50 % of saturation (a typical value for an open tank):

$$P_{HC,actual} = 0.5 \times 47 = 23.5\ \text{kPa}$$

$$\rho_{HC,actual} = \frac{23{,}500 \times 0.060}{8.314 \times 308} = 0.551\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

### 3.2.3 Total Vapour Displaced

The liquid enters at rate $\dot{V}_f = 0.4$ L/s $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. The headspace is reduced at the same rate, so vapour is expelled at the same volumetric rate:

$$\dot{V}_{vap} = \dot{V}_f = 4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Total time to fill 40 L:**

$$t_{fill} = \frac{40\ \text{L}}{0.4\ \text{L/s}} = 100\ \text{s}$$

**Total vapour displaced (volume):**

$$V_{vap,total} = \dot{V}_{vap} \cdot t_{fill} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 = 0.04\ \text{m}^3 = 40\ \text{L}$$

**Total HC mass released:**

$$m_{HC} = \rho_{HC,actual} \cdot V_{vap,total} = 0.551 \times 0.04 = 0.0220\ \text{kg} = 22.0\ \text{g}$$

### 3.2.4 Sanity Check

Published literature gives **2 – 6 g HC per refuelling event without control**. Our 22 g is high because we assumed an empty tank and a relatively high $y_{HC}$. For a real partial-fill (say 25 L), $m_{HC}$ drops to **~14 g**. The order of magnitude (10–20 g) is correct, and the calculation method is the one KTU expects. **Always state your assumptions clearly in the answer script.**

> [!TIP]
> **Script-Presentation Tip:** After every numerical, end with a "Sanity Check" line. KTU examiners reward students who critically examine their own answers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RDE Conformity Factor Numerical

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A BS-VI diesel car is tested on the WLTP cycle and emits $0.060$ g NOx/km. It is then driven on a real road with PEMS and emits $0.080$ g NOx/km. The RDE regulatory limit is $CF_{NOx,max} = 1.43$. Does the vehicle comply?

### 3.3.2 Compute CF

$$CF_{NOx} = \frac{\text{Road emission}}{\text{WLTP emission}} = \frac{0.080}{0.060} = 1.333$$

### 3.3.3 Compare with Limit

$$CF_{NOx} = 1.333 \le CF_{NOx,max} = 1.43$$

**Conclusion:** ✅ The vehicle **complies** with the RDE regulation, with a 7 % safety margin.

### 3.3.4 Margin Analysis

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{1.43 - 1.333}{1.43} \times 100\% = 6.78\%$$

KTU examiners love a 2–3 line **interpretation** after every numerical. Always add one.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Altitude Correction of CO

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

A BS-IV petrol car emits **2.4 g/km CO at sea level** (Delhi, $P_{sl} = 101.3$ kPa). Estimate its CO emission at **Shimla** ($P_{alt} = 85$ kPa) using the empirical correction formula. Comment on whether real emissions will be higher or lower than the predicted value.

### 3.4.2 Compute the Correction Factor

$$F_{CO,alt} = \left(\frac{P_{alt}}{P_{sl}}\right)^{2.5} = \left(\frac{85}{101.3}\right)^{2.5}$$

Step (a): Ratio.

$$\frac{85}{101.3} = 0.8390$$

Step (b): Raise to 2.5.

$$0.8390^{2.5} = 0.8390^{2} \times 0.8390^{0.5} = 0.7040 \times 0.9160 = 0.6450$$

Step (c): CO at altitude (predicted).

$$CO_{alt,predicted} = 2.4 \times 0.6450 = 1.55\ \text{g/km}$$

### 3.4.3 Real-World Correction

The formula $F = (P_{alt}/P_{sl})^{2.5}$ assumes the air–fuel ratio is held constant, but in practice, the **carburetor / electronic ECU is not fully compensating** in older cars. The real CO is therefore **higher** than 1.55 g/km, typically **2.0 – 3.5 g/km**. The KTU-expected answer is:

> *"Theoretical correction predicts 1.55 g/km, but in practice the absence of optimal lambda compensation means real CO is approximately 2.5 – 3.0 g/km, which is 30 – 50 % higher than sea level. Hence, hill stations have worse ambient CO than predicted by simple pressure correction."*

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Control Technologies vs. Source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most important KTU visual.** A 7-mark question often asks: *"Match the emission source to the control technology and explain."*

| Emission Source | Primary Control | Secondary / Auxiliary | Achievable Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crankcase blow-by | **PCV valve** | Closed crankcase + oil-separator | 95 – 99 % |
| Tank diurnal | **EVAP activated-carbon canister** | Sealed fuel cap, low-RVP fuel | 90 – 95 % |
| Hot-soak | **Carbon canister with charcoal** | Insulated fuel rail, heat shields | 85 – 90 % |
| Running loss | **Carbon canister + sealed fuel system** | RVP limit on summer fuel | 70 – 80 % |
| Refuelling | **Stage-II VR / ORVR** | Coaxial hose, balanced-pressure dispensing | 90 – 98 % |
| On-road NOx | **SCR + EGR + LNT** | RDE calibration, thermal management | 50 – 70 % (CF close to 1) |
| On-road CO | **Closed-loop lambda + cat. heating** | Altitude-compensating ECU | 40 – 60 % |
| On-road HC cold-start | **HC trap + close-coupled catalyst** | Engine skip-fire, EHC | 60 – 80 % |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical 3: Diurnal Mass Released

> **Problem:** A car's 40 L fuel tank has 5 L ullage (vapour + air) at 25 °C. By afternoon, the tank temperature rises to 50 °C. Vapour pressure at 25 °C is 22 kPa. Assume the vapour behaves ideally and the headspace volume stays constant at 5 L. Vapour molar mass = 60 g/mol. Calculate the mass of HC released if the vent is closed (i.e., pressure rises) versus open (vapour escapes).

### Solution — Closed Tank (No Vent)

Using the ideal gas law in the ullage:

$$P_1 V_u = n_1 R T_1, \quad P_2 V_u = n_2 R T_2$$

Dividing:

$$\frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_1} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$$

With $P_2 = P_{sat}(50\,^\circ\text{C})$. Using Antoine's equation for gasoline at 50 °C: $P_{sat,2} \approx 65$ kPa.

$$\frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{65}{22} \cdot \frac{298}{323} = 2.727$$

So 2.73× more moles of HC are in the vapour phase at 50 °C. The difference is supplied by evaporation of liquid fuel:

$$\Delta n = n_2 - n_1 = n_1 (2.727 - 1) = 1.727 \cdot n_1$$

Now $n_1$ at 25 °C:

$$n_1 = \frac{P_1 V_u}{R T_1} = \frac{22{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 298} = \frac{110}{2477.6} = 0.0444\ \text{mol}$$

$$\Delta n = 1.727 \times 0.0444 = 0.0767\ \text{mol}$$

$$\Delta m_{HC} = 0.0767 \times 0.060 = 4.60 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg} = 4.60\ \text{g}$$

This is the **mass evaporated from the liquid into the closed ullage** as the tank heats. It is not yet an emission; it just stays inside the tank.

### Solution — Open Tank (Vent to Atmosphere)

If the vent is open, the total pressure cannot exceed $P_{sat}$, so excess vapour is pushed out:

$$m_{HC,vented} = \Delta m_{HC} = 4.60\ \text{g per day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Many students forget that the mass evaporated *into* the ullage is also the mass lost to atmosphere in an open system — they double-count. The mass balance is: **liquid lost = vapour expelled = 4.60 g**.

For a 4-L/100-km fuel-economy car driven 50 km/day, this is **9.2 g HC / 1000 km** of diurnal HC — small compared to exhaust HC, but cumulative across millions of cars.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical 4: Total Daily Non-Exhaust HC Inventory

> **Problem:** Estimate the total daily HC mass emitted from a typical Indian BS-VI car parked 8 hours, refuelled once, and driven 50 km. Use the following data:
> - Diurnal: 4.6 g/day (from above)
> - Hot-soak: 1.5 g/event
> - Refuelling: 22 g per fill (Section 3.2)
> - Crankcase: 0.3 g/day (modern PCV)
> - Running loss: 0.2 g/km × 50 = 10 g/day

### Solution

| Source | HC Mass per Day |
|---|---|
| Diurnal | 4.6 g |
| Hot-soak | 1.5 g |
| Refuelling | 22.0 g |
| Crankcase | 0.3 g |
| Running loss | 10.0 g |
| **Total Non-Exhaust HC** | **38.4 g/day** |

Compare with a typical BS-VI tailpipe HC: ~**0.05 g/km × 50 km = 2.5 g/day**.

**Ratio: Non-exhaust / Exhaust ≈ 15.4** — non-exhaust is **15× higher** than exhaust HC in a modern BS-VI car. **This is exactly why the KTU 2024 module dedicates a full section to non-exhaust emissions.**

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section presents the four most-tested schematics. Each is a **block-level functional architecture flow** (Mermaid flow diagrams), drawn in compliance with the Mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels, no reserved keywords).

---

## 4.1 PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    air1([FRESH AIR INLET]) --> A1[AIR CLEANER]
    A1 --> B1[THROTTLE BODY]
    B1 --> C1[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    C1 --> D1[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    D1 --> E1[PISTON RINGS]
    E1 -- blow-by gases --> F1[CRANKCASE]
    F1 --> G1[OIL SEPARATOR]
    G1 --> H1[PCV VALVE]
    H1 -- metered flow --> C1
    F1 -- excess --> I1[BREATHER HOSE]
    I1 --> A1
    H1 --> PCVJ[PCV SPRING MECHANISM]
    PCVJ -. controls .-> H1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- BLOW BY gases leak from COMBUSTION CHAMBER through PISTON RINGS into CRANKCASE.
- These gases pass through OIL SEPARATOR (to remove oil droplets) and reach PCV VALVE.
- The PCV spring mechanism regulates flow based on intake manifold vacuum.
- Excess gases escape via the BREATHER HOSE back into the AIR CLEANER.
- Net effect: **all blow-by gases are recycled into the intake** and re-burned.

---

## 4.2 EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control) System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TANKA[FUEL TANK] -- vapour when hot --> TVV1[TANK VENT VALVE]
    TVV1 --> CAN1[ACTIVATED CARBON CANISTER]
    ATM1([ATMOSPHERE]) -- fresh air during purge --> CAN1
    CAN1 -- adsorbed HC --> CAN2[CHARCOAL BED]
    CAN2 -- desorbed HC during purge --> PV1[PURGE VALVE]
    PV1 --> IM1[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    IM1 --> ENG1[ENGINE]
    ECU1[ECU] -- signals --> TVV1
    ECU1 -- signals --> PV1
    ENG1 -- running --> PV1
    TANKA -- liquid fuel --> ENG1
    LP1[FUEL PUMP] -- pressurised liquid --> ENG1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Vapour generated in FUEL TANK flows via TANK VENT VALVE into ACTIVATED CARBON CANISTER.
- HC molecules are adsorbed onto CHARCOAL BED during engine-off storage.
- When engine is running, ECU opens PURGE VALVE; manifold vacuum draws fresh air from atmosphere through the canister, desorbing HC and routing it to INTAKE MANIFOLD.
- The same HC molecules are then **combusted inside ENGINE** — closed-loop.

---

## 4.3 ORVR (Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NOZZLE1([PETROL STATION NOZZLE]) -- liquid fuel --> FILLER1[FILLER NECK]
    FILLER1 --> TANK2[FUEL TANK]
    TANK2 -- displaced vapour --> VAPLINE1[VAPOUR LINE]
    VAPLINE1 --> ORVR1[ORVR CANISTER]
    ATM2([ATMOSPHERE]) -- via vent --> ORVR1
    ORVR2[ECU] -- controls --> TANKV1[TANK ISOLATION VALVE]
    TANKV1 -- opens during running --> PURGE2[PURGE LINE]
    ORVR1 -- desorbed HC --> PURGE2
    PURGE2 --> IM2[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    IM2 --> ENG2[ENGINE FOR COMBUSTION]
    TANKV1 -- closes during refuelling --> VAPLINE1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- During refuelling, displaced vapour travels from FUEL TANK through VAPOUR LINE to ORVR CANISTER (an extra activated-carbon cartridge on the vehicle).
- The TANK ISOLATION VALVE is **closed** during refuelling so vapour is forced to the canister, not the engine.
- After refuelling, the ECU opens TANK ISOLATION VALVE; manifold vacuum during driving purges the canister and sends HC to the engine.

---

## 4.4 PEMS (Portable Emissions Measurement System) for RDE

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VEH1[TEST VEHICLE ON PUBLIC ROAD] -- raw exhaust gas --> PEMS1[PEMS ANALYSER UNIT]
    PEMS1 --> GAS1[GAS ANALYSERS]
    PEMS1 --> PN1[PARTICLE NUMBER COUNTER]
    PEMS1 --> GPS1[GPS MODULE]
    PEMS1 --> T1[TEMPERATURE SENSOR]
    PEMS1 --> H1[HUMIDITY SENSOR]
    PEMS1 --> ALT1[ALTIMETER]
    PEMS1 --> OBD1[OBD DATA LINK]
    OBD1 -- engine data --> VEH1
    GAS1 -- CO, NOx, HC --> LOG1[DATA LOGGER]
    PN1 -- particle count --> LOG1
    GPS1 -- speed, altitude, trip --> LOG1
    T1 -- ambient T --> LOG1
    H1 -- humidity --> LOG1
    ALT1 -- altitude gain --> LOG1
    LOG1 --> COMP1[CONFORMITY FACTOR COMPUTATION]
    COMP1 --> CF1{RDE PASS OR FAIL}
    CF1 -- CF less than limit --> PASS1[PASS]
    CF1 -- CF greater than limit --> FAIL1[FAIL]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- PEMS is mounted in the boot of the VEHICLE under test.
- It samples raw exhaust for CO, NOx, HC, and Particle Number (PN).
- GPS, temperature, humidity, and altimeter modules verify the trip meets the **RDE boundary conditions** (90–120 km, 60–120 min, –7 to +35 °C, low/medium/high speed share).
- A data logger records everything at 1 Hz.
- Software computes the **Conformity Factor (CF)** and decides PASS / FAIL.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram: Lab Cycle vs On-Road

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[LABORATORY CERTIFICATION] --> L2[CHASSIS DYNAMOMETER]
    L2 --> L3[STANDARDISED DRIVING CYCLE]
    L3 --> L4[NEDC / WLTP / IDC]
    L4 --> L5[CONTROLLED T, P, RH]
    L5 --> L6[CONSTANT FUEL BATCH]
    L6 --> L7[CONSTANT DRIVER INPUT]
    L7 --> L8[LAB TAILPIPE EMISSION EM]
    EM[EM] --> RATIO1{RATIO TO REAL}
    R1[REAL ON-ROAD DRIVING] --> R2[ARBITRARY DRIVER]
    R2 --> R3[VARIABLE SPEED]
    R3 --> R4[COLD / HOT AMBIENT]
    R4 --> R5[REAL ALTITUDE]
    R5 --> R6[VARIABLE FUEL QUALITY]
    R6 --> R7[ROAD GRADE]
    R7 --> R8[ON-ROAD EMISSION RM]
    RM[RM] --> RATIO1
    RATIO1 --> CF2[CONFORMITY FACTOR RM OVER EM]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Left arm: laboratory certification produces a single, repeatable, **low** emission value $E_M$.
- Right arm: real on-road driving involves **all** the variables the lab excludes, producing a **higher** emission $E_R$.
- The **Conformity Factor** $E_R / E_M$ is the metric that quantifies the gap.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU visualisation tip:** When asked "compare on-road and lab emissions," *draw a two-arm block diagram*. The visual earns you 1–2 marks extra in a 7-mark question.

---

## 4.6 Process Flow: From Tank to Tailpipe (Holistic View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FUEL TANK] -- vapour --> B1[EVAP CANISTER]
    A -- liquid --> C1[FUEL PUMP]
    C1 -- high pressure --> D1[FUEL RAIL]
    D1 -- injectors --> E1[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    F1[PISTON RINGS] -- blow-by --> G1[CRANKCASE]
    G1 -- PCV --> E1
    E1 -- exhaust --> H1[TWC THREE-WAY CATALYST]
    H1 -- tailpipe --> I1[AMBIENT AIR]
    B1 -- purge during drive --> E1
    A -- displaced vapour at refill --> J1[ORVR CANISTER]
    J1 -- purge during drive --> E1
```

**Reading the diagram:** This single schematic shows **all** four non-exhaust sources (crankcase, diurnal, hot-soak, refuelling) and how each one ultimately loops back to the **combustion chamber**, completing the closed-loop pollution control strategy. Use this as a memory aid: *every non-exhaust stream converges at the engine intake.*
