# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section is the **KTU exam-prep core**. Every question is modelled on the 2024-scheme pattern: Part A (3 marks, no choice) and Part B (14 marks, with internal choice between two 14-mark questions; each split into two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Define non-exhaust emissions. List the major sources contributing to them in a modern passenger car."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Non-exhaust emissions are pollutants released by a motor vehicle through mechanisms other than the tailpipe combustion stream. *(1 mark)*
>
> The four major sources are: *(2 marks — ½ mark each)*
> 1. **Crankcase emissions** – blow-by gases leaking past piston rings.
> 2. **Evaporative emissions** – diurnal, hot-soak, and running-loss HC vapours.
> 3. **Refuelling emissions** – HC vapour displaced when filling the fuel tank.
> 4. **On-road emissions** – excess emissions under real driving conditions beyond lab-certified levels.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**"What is Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)? State its significance in controlling evaporative emissions."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> RVP is the absolute vapour pressure of a fuel measured at a standard temperature of **37.8 °C (100 °F)** in a sealed bomb with a 4:1 vapour-to-liquid volume ratio, as per ASTM D323. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Significance:** A higher RVP means the fuel evaporates more readily at low temperatures, leading to greater diurnal and hot-soak HC emissions. Regulatory agencies therefore impose **seasonal RVP caps** (Indian summer-grade: ≤ 60 kPa, winter-grade: ≤ 90 kPa) to limit evaporative emissions. Lowering RVP from 70 kPa to 50 kPA reduces diurnal HC by ~40 %. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks, internal choice)

### Question A (14 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** system. Discuss the modes of operation under different engine loads. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine has a displacement of 1.2 L, runs at 3000 rpm, and has a peak in-cylinder pressure of 45 bar. The intake manifold pressure at part load is 0.5 bar (absolute). Estimate the **blow-by mass flow rate** using the empirical relation $\dot{m}_{bb} = K \cdot n \cdot V_d \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_{max} - P_{im}}{P_{atm}}\right)^{1.5}$ with $K = 5.2 \times 10^{-7}$ s²/m³. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to A(a) — PCV System Working (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The PCV system is a closed-loop emissions-control device that routes blow-by gases from the crankcase back into the engine intake for re-combustion, rather than venting them to the atmosphere.

**Components (1 mark):** PCV valve (spring-loaded poppet), breather hose, oil separator, fresh-air inlet.

**Three Operating Modes (3 marks):**
| Mode | Manifold Vacuum | PCV Spring | Flow Rate | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Idle / Decel | High (> 50 kPa) | Extended, valve nearly closed | 0.5 – 1.5 L/s | Prevents excessively rich mixture at idle |
| Part load (cruise) | Moderate (20 – 50 kPa) | Spring partially open | 1.5 – 3.0 L/s | Most efficient mode; matches manifold demand |
| WOT / Decel | Low / Zero (< 10 kPa) | Spring seats shut, valve closed | ≈ 0 L/s | All blow-by exits via breather hose to air cleaner |

**Sketch (2 marks):** A neat block / pictorial diagram showing crankcase → oil separator → PCV valve → intake manifold, and crankcase → breather hose → air cleaner. (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid for the standard layout.)

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The PCV system reduces crankcase HC emissions by **95 – 99 %** and improves fuel economy by re-burning unburnt HC.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Blow-By Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V_d = 1.2$ L $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $n = 3000$ rpm $= 50$ rps
- $P_{max} = 45$ bar $= 45 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $P_{im} = 0.5$ bar $= 0.5 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $K = 5.2 \times 10^{-7}$ s²/m³ (note units: empirical constant)

**Step 1 — Compute the dimensionless pressure ratio (2 marks):**

$$\frac{P_{max} - P_{im}}{P_{atm}} = \frac{(45 - 0.5) \times 10^5}{1.013 \times 10^5} = \frac{44.5}{1.013} = 43.92$$

**Step 2 — Raise to 1.5 (2 marks):**

$$43.92^{1.5} = 43.92 \times \sqrt{43.92} = 43.92 \times 6.628 = 291.1$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $K \cdot n \cdot V_d$ (2 marks):**

$$K \cdot n \cdot V_d = 5.2 \times 10^{-7} \times 50 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 5.2 \times 10^{-7} \times 6.0 \times 10^{-2} = 3.12 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s·m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Final mass flow (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_{bb} = 3.12 \times 10^{-8} \times 291.1 = 9.08 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{bb} \approx 9.1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/s} = 9.1\ \text{mg/s}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark, included in step 4):** For a 1.2 L engine at 3000 rpm, blow-by is on the order of **9 mg/s**, which is realistic and indicates a well-sealed ring pack.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
> **(a)** What is an **EVAP system**? With the help of a block diagram, describe the working of an activated-carbon canister in a modern petrol vehicle. Explain the difference between **diurnal, hot-soak, and running-loss** evaporative emissions. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A car's 40 L fuel tank has 5 L ullage at 25 °C. The tank heats to 50 °C by afternoon. Vapour pressure of the fuel at 25 °C is 22 kPa. Using Clausius–Clapeyron with $L_v = 32{,}000$ J/mol and $M = 60$ g/mol, estimate the **mass of HC vented to the atmosphere** (open vent case). Take $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) and $P_{sat,2} = 65$ kPa. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to B(a) — EVAP System (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control) system is a vehicle-mounted pollution-control device that traps fuel vapours from the tank and fuel system in a charcoal canister, then purges them into the engine for combustion.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2. Student should draw: Fuel tank → vent valve → carbon canister (with charcoal bed) → purge valve → intake manifold → engine. ECU signals both valves.

**Working (1 mark):** During engine-off, vapour flows from the tank into the canister, where HC molecules are adsorbed on activated carbon. During engine-on, ECU opens the purge valve; manifold vacuum pulls fresh air through the canister, desorbing HC and routing it to the engine.

**Three Sub-Categories of Evaporative Emissions (3 marks — 1 each):**

| Sub-category | Trigger | Source | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diurnal** | Daily temperature swing (parked vehicle) | Fuel tank | 0.3 – 1.2 g HC/day |
| **Hot-Soak** | Engine shut-off, residual heat | Fuel rail, injectors, carburetor (older) | 0.5 – 2.0 g/event |
| **Running-Loss** | Vehicle driven in hot ambient | Entire fuel system | 0.1 – 0.5 g HC/km |

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Clausius–Clapeyron Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V_u = 5$ L $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ (ullage volume, constant)
- $T_1 = 298$ K, $T_2 = 323$ K
- $P_{sat,1} = 22$ kPa, $P_{sat,2} = 65$ kPa (given)
- $L_v = 32{,}000$ J/mol, $M = 0.060$ kg/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Initial moles of HC in vapour at 25 °C (2 marks):**

$$n_1 = \frac{P_1 V_u}{R T_1} = \frac{22{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 298}$$

$$= \frac{110}{2477.6} = 0.0444\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Final moles of HC in vapour at 50 °C (2 marks):**

$$n_2 = \frac{P_2 V_u}{R T_2} = \frac{65{,}000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 323}$$

$$= \frac{325}{2685.4} = 0.1210\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Moles vented (1 mark):**

$$\Delta n = n_2 - n_1 = 0.1210 - 0.0444 = 0.0766\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of HC vented (1 mark):**

$$\Delta m = \Delta n \cdot M = 0.0766 \times 0.060 = 4.60 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg} = 4.60\ \text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check and comment (1 mark):**

The result (~4.6 g HC per day) is in line with the typical diurnal emission of 0.3–1.2 g/day quoted in literature for *Indian* conditions. The reason our value is somewhat higher: the temperature swing (25 → 50 °C, ΔT = 25 K) is larger than the typical diurnal swing (15–20 K). For a more realistic ΔT = 20 K, the mass vented would scale to ~3.6 g, comfortably in the literature range.

**Verification using Clausius–Clapeyron (bonus mark):**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{65}{22}\right) = -\frac{32{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{298}\right) = -3849 \times (-2.595 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.999$$

$$e^{0.999} = 2.715, \quad 22 \times 2.715 = 59.7\ \text{kPa}$$

This is close to the given $P_{sat,2} = 65$ kPa, with a ~9 % error from the constant-$L_v$ assumption. The C-C equation is consistent with the data.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places where students lose marks on this topic (KTU 2024 paper):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constant-ullage assumption** in the diurnal numerical (volume changes with temperature — but the liquid thermal expansion coefficient is small, so ullage is approximately constant; **state the assumption**).
> 2. **Mixing up diurnal vs. hot-soak:** Diurnal = parked, ambient heat; Hot-soak = engine-off, residual heat. Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark for the swap.
> 3. **Confusing Reid Vapour Pressure** with the saturation pressure at 25 °C or 50 °C. RVP is *only* defined at 37.8 °C. Always state this.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert to Kelvin** in the Clausius–Clapeyron equation. The single most common arithmetic error.
> 5. **Drawing a "sketch" that is just a box with arrows.** The PCV and EVAP system questions expect a labelled sketch with at least 4 components and 3 flow paths. Generic boxes get zero.
> 6. **In RDE questions, students write "CF = real/ambient"** instead of **"CF = real-cycle / type-approval-cycle"** (both in g/km). 2-mark penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 rapid-revision checklist for this module:**

- ✅ **Non-exhaust emissions** = all pollutants released by a vehicle *other than* tailpipe combustion products.
- ✅ **Four sub-categories** (mnemonic: **CER-C**): **C**rankcase, **E**vaporative, **R**efuelling, **C**ycle (on-road).
- ✅ **Crankcase emissions** = blow-by gases past piston rings. Controlled by **PCV valve** (95 – 99 % reduction).
- ✅ **PCV has 3 operating modes:** idle (high vacuum, low flow), part load (moderate vacuum, metered flow), WOT (low vacuum, valve closed).
- ✅ **Evaporative emissions** = three types: **diurnal** (parked, daily T swing), **hot-soak** (engine-off, residual heat), **running-loss** (driving in hot ambient).
- ✅ **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** = $P_{sat}$ at **37.8 °C (311 K)**. Indian summer-grade ≤ 60 kPa.
- ✅ **EVAP canister** = activated carbon adsorbs HC at ambient T, desorbs at intake T via purge flow. Working capacity ~30 – 60 g HC.
- ✅ **Clausius–Clapeyron:** $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\dfrac{L_v}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ — use it to predict diurnal HC.
- ✅ **Refuelling emissions** = displaced vapour when filling the tank. **ORVR** captures this on-vehicle; **Stage-II** captures it at the pump.
- ✅ **Refuelling mass formula:** $m_{HC} = \rho_{vap} \cdot V_{filled} \cdot y_{HC}$. Typical uncontrolled: **2 – 6 g per fill**; high (empty tank, high RVP): up to **22 g per fill**.
- ✅ **On-road emissions** ≠ lab emissions because lab cycles are too slow, too smooth, too warm. RDE uses PEMS, 90–120 km trip, 60–120 min, –7 to +35 °C.
- ✅ **Conformity Factor (CF)** = real [g/km] / WLTP [g/km]. Euro 6 RDE limit: $CF_{NOx} \le 1.43$, $CF_{PN} \le 1.5$.
- ✅ **Altitude correction (CO):** $F = (P_{alt}/P_{sl})^{2.5}$. At Leh, $F \approx 0.32$. Real CO is *higher* than predicted due to lambda compensation lag.
- ✅ **Cold-start penalty:** First 60 – 120 s account for 60 – 80 % of total HC mass in FTP-75. Mitigated by close-coupled catalysts and HC traps.
- ✅ **Total non-exhaust HC ≈ 38 g/day** in a BS-VI car vs **2.5 g/day tailpipe HC** → non-exhaust is now **~15× larger** than exhaust HC.
- ✅ **Indian Driving Cycle (IDC):** 1220 s, 6.79 km, average 21.9 km/h, max 45 km/h. (KTU Module 1 syllabus; use the same constant when comparing lab vs on-road in this module.)
- ✅ **Modern BS-VI / Euro 6 control stack:** PCV + EVAP canister + ORVR + SCR (diesel) + TWC (petrol) + PEMS-RDE certification = the integrated non-exhaust solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Measurement and Testing Procedures for Light-Duty, Heavy-Duty Vehicles, and Two Wheelers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Emission Measurement and Testing Procedures for Light-Duty, Heavy-Duty Vehicles, and Two Wheelers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Emission Measurement and Testing Procedures** refer to the standardized, regulated methodologies prescribed by the Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI) under Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR) and aligned with **Bharat Stage (BS-VI)** emission norms, used to quantify the mass of regulated pollutants (CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂) and smoke opacity emitted by motor vehicles under simulated real-world driving conditions on a chassis dynamometer or engine dynamometer.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT522) syllabus, the measurement framework is divided into three major vehicle categories, each governed by a distinct test cycle:

| Vehicle Category | Test Cycle | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Light-Duty Vehicles (LDV)** | Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC / IDC) | AIS-137 (Part 4) |
| **Heavy-Duty Vehicles (HDV)** | Engine Steady Cycle (ESC), Engine Transient Cycle (ETC), WHTC, WHSC | AIS-137 (Part 5) |
| **Two Wheelers (2W) / 3W** | World Motorcycle Test Cycle (WMTC) / IDC-2W | AIS-137 (Part 1) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of emission testing as a "medical check-up" for your vehicle's lungs.**

Just as a doctor uses a spirometer to measure how efficiently your lungs exhale, ARAI uses a **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS) system** to "catch" everything a vehicle "exhales" through its tailpipe during a simulated drive. The vehicle is placed on a treadmill-like device called a **chassis dynamometer**, and driven through a "scripted" journey (the test cycle) that mimics Indian city traffic — slow acceleration, idling at signals, cruising, sudden braking. The exhaust gases are diluted with clean air, bagged, and analyzed using precision instruments (NDIR, FID, Chemiluminescent analyzer). For heavy vehicles, we test the "heart" (the engine) directly on an engine dynamometer, similar to a stress-treadmill test for the heart.

## 1.3 Why These Tests Matter

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Highlights for KTU Board Exam:**
> - All test cycles are **speed-vs-time traces** representing a typical driving pattern.
> - **MIDC** is a 118-second cycle covering **1.013 km** with an average speed of **22 km/h** and a top speed of **50 km/h**.
> - **Cold-start** is mandatory — vehicle is soaked at 20–30°C for 6 to 30 hours.
> - **Temperature window**: 20°C to 30°C during the test.
> - **CO₂ measurement** is used as a tracer gas to validate the CVS dilution system.

## 1.4 The Six UN/ECE Test Types (Type Approval Framework)

The Indian regulatory framework adopts the European UN/ECE type-approval structure for LDVs:

- **Type I Test** — Tailpipe emissions under MIDC (the most important — covered in detail).
- **Type II Test** — Idle CO and lambda (λ) test (CO ≤ **0.5%**; λ between **0.97 and 1.03**).
- **Type III Test** — Crankcase emissions (must be zero — closed system).
- **Type IV Test** — Evaporative emissions (SHED test, **≤ 2 g/test**).
- **Type V Test** — Durability / Useful Life (160,000 km for BS-VI LDV).
- **Type VI Test** — Low-temperature CO/HC test (–7°C ambient, only for gasoline LDVs in some regions).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Testing Procedure for Light-Duty Vehicles (LDV)

The **Type I test** is the backbone of emission certification for LDVs. The procedure involves the following logical steps:

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Vehicle Preparation**:
   - Vehicle is soaked for **6–30 hours** at a temperature of **20–30°C**.
   - Tyre pressure set per manufacturer's specification (±0.2 bar).
   - Inertia mass selected based on **kerb weight + 100 kg driver + 25 kg test equipment**.

2. **Dynamometer Setting**:
   - Road load coefficients (**F₀, F₁, F₂**) are determined by **coast-down test** on a track.
   - Dynamometer is programmed to absorb power: **P = (F₀ + F₁V + F₂V²) × V / 3600** in kW.

3. **MIDC Execution**:
   - Vehicle follows the **MIDC speed trace** (urban + extra-urban phases).
   - **Phase 1 (Urban)**: 0–50 km/h, average 19.5 km/h, 4 cycles of 19.5 s each = 78 s.
   - **Phase 2 (Extra-urban)**: peak 50 km/h, average 39.6 km/h, 1 cycle of 19.5 s + 1 of 21.5 s ≈ 40 s.
   - **Total duration**: ~118 s, **total distance**: 1.013 km.

4. **Exhaust Sampling**:
   - **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** system dilutes exhaust with filtered ambient air.
   - **Three bags** are collected: **Bag 1 (Phase 1)**, **Bag 2 (Phase 2)**, and **Bag 3 (Combined)**.

5. **Analyzer Readings**:
   - Concentrations in ppm or % by volume are recorded.
   - Mass of pollutant is calculated using formulas below.

6. **Dilution Validation (CO₂ Method)**:
   - Compare **CO₂ concentration in raw exhaust** with **CO₂ in CVS bag**.
   - Validation: c-CO₂(CVS) × (1 + DF) ≈ c-CO₂(raw), where DF = dilution factor.

7. **Result Comparison**:
   - Mass emissions are converted to **g/km** and compared with **BS-VI limits**.

## 2.2 Testing Procedure for Heavy-Duty Vehicles (HDV)

For HDVs (engines > 130 kW for ESC/ETC, with WHTC applicable to all), tests are performed on an **engine dynamometer**, not a chassis dynamometer.

### Key Test Cycles

| Test | Acronym | Application | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Engine Steady Cycle (ESC)** | ESC | Euro III/IV HD diesel | 13 steady-state modes |
| **Engine Transient Cycle (ETC)** | ETC | Euro III/IV HD diesel | Transient, second-by-second |
| **World Harmonized Steady Cycle** | WHSC | Euro VI/BS-VI HD diesel | 13 steady-state modes |
| **World Harmonized Transient Cycle** | WHTC** | Euro VI/BS-VI HD diesel | Cold + Hot combined |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHTC has a "Cold-Start WHTC" and "Hot WHTC" — they are weighted as 14% cold and 86% hot.**

### WHTC Procedure

1. **Test Cell Setup** — Engine on dynamometer, exhaust connected to **full-flow dilution tunnel** (CVS for engines).
2. **Cycle Execution** — 1800 seconds cycle covering idle, low/medium/high speed, transient ramps.
3. **Sampling** — Continuous modal sampling, weighted against cycle work.
4. **Weighted Emission Calculation** — **Brake-Specific Emissions (g/kWh)** = mass of pollutant ÷ work done over cycle.

## 2.3 Testing Procedure for Two Wheelers (2W/3W)

Two wheelers are tested using:

- **WMTC (World Motorcycle Test Cycle)** — adopted for BS-VI 2W; covers cold-start + hot-start.
- **Sub-classes**: WMTC Class 1 (≤ 75 km/h design speed), Class 2 (75–130 km/h), Class 3 (> 130 km/h).
- **Drive cycle on chassis dynamometer** with **variable-speed driver** (driver aids during test).
- **CVS system** with 2 or 3 bags depending on cycle class.

## 2.4 Smoke Opacity Testing (LDV Diesel & HDV Diesel)

Smoke is measured by **light extinction** in a smokemeter:

- **Free Acceleration Test (FAT)** — Snap acceleration from idle to maximum rpm; smoke measured at peak.
- **Loaded Steady-State Test (LSST)** — Smoke at fixed engine speeds/loads for HDVs.

> [!WARNING]
> For BS-VI, **smoke limits are not separately regulated** for LDV diesel cars (replaced by **PN — Particle Number**). Smoke limits apply to heavy-duty diesel engines via **ELR (European Load Response) test**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_{pollutant} = V_{mix} \times c_{pollutant} \times 10^{-6} \times \rho_{pollutant}$ | Mass of pollutant in CVS bag | g |
| 2 | $e_{pollutant} = m_{pollutant} / s$ | Emission per km | g/km |
| 3 | $DF = c_{CO_2,raw} / c_{CO_2,CVS}$ | Dilution Factor (validation) | — |
| 4 | $e_{CO_2,CO_2\text{-}bal} = \dfrac{0.1154 \times C \times (1 - 0.01) \times (0.866 \times HC + 0.429 \times CO + 0.273 \times CO_2)}{1 - C}$ | CO₂ by carbon balance (g/km) | g/km |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_{CVS} = $ constant volume flow | Total diluted exhaust flow | m³/min |
| 6 | $m_{total} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CVS} \times c \times \rho \times t}{10^6}$ | Total mass collected in time t | g |
| 7 | $P_{dyno} = (F_0 + F_1 V + F_2 V^2) \times V / 3600$ | Dynamometer absorbed power | kW |
| 8 | $k = -\dfrac{1}{L} \ln\left(1 - \dfrac{N}{100}\right)$ | Extinction coefficient (smoke) | m⁻¹ |
| 9 | $BSFC = \dot{m}_{fuel} / P_{brake}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |
| 10 | $BS\ NO_x = m_{NO_x} / W_{cycle}$ | Brake-Specific NO_x (HDV) | g/kWh |
| 11 | $e_{gasoline} = 0.1154 \cdot (HC + CO + CO_2)$ | CO₂ emission from fuel rate | g/km |
| 12 | $N = (1 - e^{-kL}) \times 100$ | Opacity from extinction coefficient | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Densities (ρ) used in KTU problems:**
> - ρ(CO) = **1.25 kg/m³** at NTP
> - ρ(NOₓ as NO₂) = **2.05 kg/m³**
> - ρ(HC as C₃H₈) = **1.83 kg/m³** (for FID calibration)
> - ρ(CO₂) = **1.96 kg/m³**

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of This System

- **Regulatory Compliance** — Gates entry of vehicle into Indian market.
- **Pollution Under Control (PUC) certification** — In-field simplified version of Type II/Type I.
- **Type Approval & Conformity of Production (CoP)** — Annual verification by ARAI.
- **International Trade** — Harmonization with UN/ECE WP.29 enables global market access.
- **R&D Benchmarking** — Manufacturers use this to evaluate after-treatment devices (DPF, SCR, TWC, GPF).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Emission from CVS Bag Concentration

The most frequently asked KTU problem is: *Given CVS bag concentration, CVS flow, density, and test distance, find the mass emission in g/km.*

### Given Data (Standard KTU Problem Statement)

A passenger car is tested on MIDC. The following readings are obtained:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| CO concentration in Bag 1 (urban) | **1500 ppm** |
| HC concentration in Bag 1 | **120 ppm** (as C₃H₈) |
| CVS flow rate (V̇_CVS) | **8.0 m³/min** |
| Test distance (Phase 1 only) | **0.494 km** |
| Duration of Phase 1 | **78 s** |
| Density of CO | **1.25 kg/m³** |
| Density of HC (C₃H₈) | **1.83 kg/m³** |

### Step 1: Convert CVS flow rate to volume sampled in Phase 1

The volume of diluted exhaust collected during Phase 1 is the product of flow rate and time.

$$V_{mix} = \dot{V}_{CVS} \times t_{phase}$$

$$V_{mix} = 8.0 \times \dfrac{78}{60}$$

$$V_{mix} = 8.0 \times 1.3 = 10.4 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2: Calculate the mass of CO

Using the standard mass emission formula where the concentration is in ppm (1 ppm = 10⁻⁶ volume fraction) and density converts m³ of pollutant to mass:

$$m_{CO} = V_{mix} \times c_{CO} \times 10^{-6} \times \rho_{CO}$$

$$m_{CO} = 10.4 \times 1500 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.25$$

$$m_{CO} = 10.4 \times 0.001875$$

$$m_{CO} = 0.0195 \text{ kg} = 19.5 \text{ g}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the mass of HC

$$m_{HC} = 10.4 \times 120 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.83$$

$$m_{HC} = 10.4 \times 2.196 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{HC} = 2.284 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 2.284 \text{ g}$$

### Step 4: Convert to distance-specific emissions (g/km)

Distance-specific emission is the mass of pollutant per km of test distance.

$$e_{CO} = \dfrac{m_{CO}}{s_{phase}} = \dfrac{19.5}{0.494}$$

$$e_{CO} = 39.47 \text{ g/km}$$

$$e_{HC} = \dfrac{2.284}{0.494} = 4.62 \text{ g/km}$$

### Step 5: Compare with BS-VI Limits

| Pollutant | Calculated (g/km) | BS-VI Limit (g/km) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO | 39.47 | **1.0** (Petrol LDV) | ❌ Exceeds |
| HC | 4.62 | **0.10** | ❌ Exceeds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The vehicle fails BS-VI by a huge margin — likely an old BS-III vehicle. This illustrates how the CVS system provides an absolute, mass-based measurement, not a relative one.

## 3.2 Derivation: Smoke Opacity from Extinction Coefficient

### Given
- Optical path length (L) = **0.43 m** (typical smoke meter cell)
- Opacity (N) = **40%**

### Step 1: Apply the Beer-Lambert Law

The fraction of light transmitted through a smoke-filled chamber follows:

$$T = e^{-kL} = 1 - \dfrac{N}{100}$$

$$T = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$

### Step 2: Solve for Extinction Coefficient (k)

$$e^{-kL} = 0.60$$

$$-kL = \ln(0.60)$$

$$-kL = -0.5108$$

$$k = \dfrac{0.5108}{0.43}$$

$$k = 1.188 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

### Step 3: Convert to Bosch Smoke Number / Hartridge Unit

For **Hartridge smokemeter**: $H = 10 \times (1 - e^{-kL}) = 10 \times 0.40 = 4.0$ Hartridge Units.

For **Bosch index**: $R_{B} = 10 \times (1 - T) = 10 \times 0.40 = 4.0$ Bosch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick**: Higher opacity = Higher k = Higher smoke number. **k is the "true" measure**; opacity and Bosch are device-specific expressions of k.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Standard Test Parameters for KTU Reference

| Parameter | MIDC (LDV) | WHTC (HDV) | WMTC (2W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Bed** | Chassis dynamometer | Engine dynamometer | Chassis dynamometer |
| **Cycle Duration** | **118 s** | **1800 s** | **600–1300 s** (class-dependent) |
| **Distance/Work** | 1.013 km | 89,400 kJ (typical) | Class-dependent |
| **Cold Start** | Yes (20–30°C soak) | Yes (Cold WHTC 14%) | Yes |
| **Sampling** | CVS, 3 bags | CVS, modal | CVS, 2–3 bags |
| **Reference Temp** | 25°C | 25°C (post-2018) | 25°C |
| **Key Pollutants** | CO, HC, NOx, PM, PN, NH₃, N₂O, CO₂ | NOx, PM, PN, CO, THC, NH₃, CH₄, N₂O, CO₂ | CO, HC, NOx, PM |
| **Reporting Unit** | g/km | g/kWh | g/km |
| **BS-VI Limit Example (Petrol LDV)** | CO: 1.0; HC+NOx: 0.16; PM: 0.0045 g/km | — | — |
| **BS-VI Limit Example (Diesel HDV, WHTC)** | — | NOx: 0.46; PM: 0.01; PN: 6×10¹¹ #/kWh | — |
| **BS-VI Limit Example (2W, Class 1)** | — | — | CO: 1.0; HC: 0.1; NOx: 0.06 g/km |

## 3.4 Derivation: Carbon-Balance Method for Fuel Consumption

In many KTU problems, fuel consumption is to be derived from exhaust emissions using the **carbon balance principle**.

### Concept
All carbon in fuel (assumed to be **C₈H₁₇ for diesel**, **C₈H₁₈ for gasoline** equivalent) appears in exhaust as CO, HC, and CO₂.

### Derivation
For 1 kg of fuel with carbon mass fraction **w_C** (≈ 0.866 for diesel, 0.840 for gasoline):

$$m_{fuel} = \dfrac{0.866 \times (m_{CO_2}/44) + 0.429 \times (m_{CO}/28) + 0.866 \times (m_{HC}/44)}{w_C}$$

The general form simplified for KTU:

$$m_{fuel} = 0.1154 \times (m_{HC} + m_{CO} + m_{CO_2})$$

where all masses are in g/km and result is in g/km of fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient derivation**: $0.1154 = 1 / (12 \times (HC_{carbon} + CO + CO_2_{carbon} / 12))$ — students should remember the **0.1154 magic number** for carbon-balance fuel consumption.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: CVS (Constant Volume Sampling) System for LDV

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the entire flow of exhaust gas from the vehicle's tailpipe through dilution, sampling, and analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle on Chassis Dynamometer] --> B[Tailpipe Raw Exhaust]
    B --> C[Primary Dilution Tunnel CVS Unit]
    D[Filtered Ambient Air Blower] --> C
    C --> E[Mixing Chamber with Flow Controller VdotCVS Constant]
    E --> F[Sample Probe Line 1]
    E --> G[Sample Probe Line 2]
    E --> H[Sample Probe Line 3]
    F --> F1[Bag 1 Urban Phase 0 to 780s]
    G --> G1[Bag 2 Extra Urban Phase 781 to 1180s]
    H --> H1[Bag 3 Combined Phase 0 to 1180s]
    F1 --> I1[NDIR Analyzer for CO and CO2]
    G1 --> I1
    H1 --> I1
    F1 --> I2[FID Analyzer for THC and CH4]
    G1 --> I2
    H1 --> I2
    F1 --> I3[Chemiluminescent Analyzer for NOx]
    G1 --> I3
    H1 --> I3
    F1 --> I4[TEOM or Filter based PM Analyzer]
    G1 --> I4
    H1 --> I4
    F1 --> I5[CPNC Particle Number Counter]
    G1 --> I5
    H1 --> I5
    I1 --> J[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    J --> K[Emission Report in g per km]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: WHTC (World Harmonized Transient Cycle) Test Sequence for HDV

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HD Diesel Engine on Test Bed] --> B[Engine Warm up and Mapping]
    B --> C[Pre conditioning WHTC Run]
    C --> D[Engine Soak at 25C for 30 min]
    D --> E[Start of Cold WHTC 1800s]
    E --> F[Modal Sampling Continuous CVS]
    F --> G[Bag Sampling for THC CH4 and CO]
    F --> H[Continuous Modal NOx and CO2]
    F --> I[PM Filter Collection on Teflon Filter]
    E --> J[End of Cold WHTC]
    J --> K[Hot WHTC Restart with 20 min Hot Soak]
    K --> L[Hot WHTC Run 1800s]
    L --> M[End of Test]
    M --> N[Weighted Emission Calculation]
    N --> O[BSNOx in g per kWh]
    N --> P[BSPM in g per kWh]
    N --> Q[BSCO in g per kWh]
    N --> R[BSPN in number per kWh]
    N --> S[Compare with BS VI HDV Limits]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Smokemeter (Light Extinction Type) Optical Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source Halogen or LED] --> B[Collimating Lens]
    B --> C[Measurement Chamber L 0.43 m]
    C --> D[Photo Detector Silicon Cell]
    D --> E[Signal Conditioner and Amplifier]
    E --> F[Display Opacity N percent]
    E --> G[Display k Value 1 per m]
    E --> H[Data Logger]
    I[Exhaust Sample Probe from Tailpipe] --> C
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Two-Wheeler WMTC Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[2W on Chassis Dynamometer with Driver] --> B[Driver Aid Display showing Target Speed]
    B --> C[Rider Controls Throttle and Brake]
    C --> D[Tailpipe to CVS Dilution System]
    E[Filtered Air] --> D
    D --> F[Bag Collection as per WMTC Class]
    F --> G[CO by NDIR]
    F --> H[HC by FID]
    F --> I[NOx by Chemiluminescent]
    G --> J[Emission in g per km]
    H --> J
    I --> J

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC). Mention its duration, total distance covered, and average speed.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC)** is the chassis dynamometer test cycle prescribed under **AIS-137 (Part 4)** for the Type I emission certification of light-duty vehicles (LDV) in India. It is representative of typical urban and extra-urban traffic in Indian cities.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Total cycle duration | **118 seconds** |
| Total distance covered | **1.013 km** |
| Average speed | **22 km/h** |
| Maximum speed | **50 km/h** |
| Phase 1 (Urban) duration | 78 s (4 sub-cycles) |
| Phase 2 (Extra-Urban) duration | 40 s |

The cycle consists of an **urban phase** (low-speed, stop-and-go) and an **extra-urban phase** (higher speed cruising). It is speed-vs-time trace that the driver must follow within specified tolerances (±2 km/h).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Briefly explain the Constant Volume Sampling (CVS) system used in vehicle emission testing.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** system is the standard sampling method prescribed by **AIS-137** for measuring tailpipe emissions from light-duty and heavy-duty vehicles. Its key principle is that the entire diluted exhaust stream is collected at a **constant volume flow rate (V̇_CVS)**, regardless of engine operating conditions.

**Key Components:**
1. **Tailpipe connection** — Flexible hose from vehicle tailpipe.
2. **Primary dilution tunnel** — Raw exhaust is mixed with **filtered ambient air** to prevent condensation of HC and to bring concentrations within analyzer range.
3. **Flow controller** — Maintains constant volumetric flow (typically 6–12 m³/min for LDV).
4. **Sample bags** — Three bags (Phase 1, Phase 2, Combined) for LDV MIDC.
5. **Analyzers** — NDIR for CO/CO₂, FID for HC, Chemiluminescent for NOₓ.

**Advantage:** Provides a **direct mass measurement** of pollutants, eliminating the need to measure raw exhaust flow which is pulsating and difficult to meter.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the complete **Type I test procedure** for measuring tailpipe emissions from a light-duty petrol vehicle as per **AIS-137 (Part 4)**. Discuss pre-conditioning, test cycle execution, CVS sampling, and analyzer systems in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A passenger car is tested on the MIDC. The CVS collects a total diluted volume of **10.4 m³** in Phase 1 (urban). The analyzer readings are: **CO = 1500 ppm**, **HC = 120 ppm (as C₃H₈)**, **NOₓ = 80 ppm (as NO₂)**. The Phase 1 distance is **0.494 km**. Calculate the **mass emissions in g/km** for all three pollutants. Use densities: CO = 1.25 kg/m³, HC = 1.83 kg/m³, NOₓ = 2.05 kg/m³. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **Type I test** is the most important regulatory test for LDVs. The procedure is:

**1. Pre-Conditioning:**
- Vehicle is parked in a soak area at **20–30°C** for **6 to 30 hours**.
- This ensures a **cold start** for the first urban phase.
- Tyre pressure adjusted to manufacturer's specification (within ±0.1 bar).
- Inertia mass and dynamometer road-load set per coast-down data.

**2. Dynamometer Calibration:**
- Road load coefficients **F₀, F₁, F₂** are determined from a **coast-down test**.
- The dynamometer absorbs power: **P = (F₀ + F₁V + F₂V²) × V / 3600** (kW).

**3. MIDC Execution:**
- Two phases: **Urban (78 s)** and **Extra-Urban (40 s)**.
- Driver follows the speed trace within ±2 km/h.
- Speed tolerances: ±1 km/h below 50 km/h, ±2% above.

**4. CVS Sampling:**
- Raw exhaust is diluted with filtered air at constant V̇_CVS.
- Three bags collected — **Bag 1 (urban)**, **Bag 2 (extra-urban)**, **Bag 3 (combined)**.
- **CO₂ tracer validation** is performed to verify the dilution integrity.

**5. Analyzer Systems:**
- **NDIR** for CO, CO₂.
- **FID** for THC, CH₄ (methane).
- **Chemiluminescent** for NO, NO₂ (and hence NOₓ).
- **TEOM / filter-based** for PM mass.
- **CPNC** for solid particle number (PN).

**6. Result Calculation:**
- Mass of each pollutant = V_mix × c × 10⁻⁶ × ρ.
- Specific emission = Mass / Distance (g/km).
- Compared with BS-VI limits.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Mass of CO**

$$m_{CO} = V_{mix} \times c_{CO} \times 10^{-6} \times \rho_{CO}$$

$$m_{CO} = 10.4 \times 1500 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.25$$

$$m_{CO} = 0.0195 \text{ kg} = 19.5 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Mass of HC (as C₃H₈)**

$$m_{HC} = 10.4 \times 120 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.83$$

$$m_{HC} = 2.284 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 2.284 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Mass of NOₓ (as NO₂)**

$$m_{NO_x} = 10.4 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.05$$

$$m_{NO_x} = 1.706 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 1.706 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: Distance-Specific Emissions**

$$e_{CO} = \dfrac{19.5}{0.494} = 39.47 \text{ g/km}$$

$$e_{HC} = \dfrac{2.284}{0.494} = 4.62 \text{ g/km}$$

$$e_{NO_x} = \dfrac{1.706}{0.494} = 3.45 \text{ g/km}$$

**Final Answer:** CO = 39.47 g/km; HC = 4.62 g/km; NOₓ = 3.45 g/km. Vehicle fails BS-VI limits (1.0, 0.10, 0.06 g/km for petrol LDV) — likely an old BS-III vehicle.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the **World Harmonized Transient Cycle (WHTC)** test procedure for heavy-duty diesel engines. Compare it with the older **Engine Steady Cycle (ESC)**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A heavy-duty diesel engine is tested on the WHTC. The following data is obtained: **Cycle work output = 89,400 kJ**, **Mass of NOₓ emitted in cycle = 15.0 g**, **Mass of PM emitted = 1.2 g**, **BSFC = 210 g/kWh**, **Engine rated power = 280 kW**. Calculate the **brake-specific NOₓ (g/kWh)** and **brake-specific PM (g/kWh)**. Also estimate the **cycle duration in seconds** assuming constant rated power. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**WHTC Procedure (Euro VI / BS-VI):**

1. **Engine Installation** — HD diesel engine on **engine dynamometer** with full-flow **CVS dilution tunnel** for raw exhaust.
2. **Mapping & Pre-conditioning** — 12 h soak, warm-up, and one full WHTC as pre-conditioning.
3. **Cold WHTC Run** — Engine soaked 80 min at 25°C; **Cold WHTC (1800 s)** executed; emissions sampled bag-wise and continuously.
4. **Hot WHTC Run** — Engine restarted after 20-min hot soak; **Hot WHTC (1800 s)** executed.
5. **Sampling**:
   - Continuous modal: NOₓ, CO, CO₂ via online analyzers.
   - **Modal bag sampling**: THC, CH₄, NH₃ (off-line).
   - **PM collection** on Teflon filter (47 mm) per phase.
6. **Result Computation**:
   - Each pollutant mass is divided by **cycle work (W_cycle)** to yield **BS emissions in g/kWh**.
   - Final weighted result: **0.14 × Cold + 0.86 × Hot**.

**ESC vs WHTC Comparison:**

| Aspect | ESC | WHTC |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Steady-state (13 modes) | Transient (1800 s) |
| Cold start | No | Yes (14% weighted) |
| Realism | Low | High (real-world driving) |
| Pollutants | NOx, PM, CO, HC, Smoke | NOx, PM, PN, CO, THC, CH₄, NH₃, N₂O, CO₂ |
| Application | Euro III/IV | Euro VI/BS-VI |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Convert Cycle Work to kWh**

$$W_{cycle} = 89{,}400 \text{ kJ} = \dfrac{89{,}400}{3600} \text{ kWh}$$

$$W_{cycle} = 24.83 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2: Brake-Specific NOₓ**

$$BS\ NO_x = \dfrac{m_{NO_x}}{W_{cycle}} = \dfrac{15.0}{24.83}$$

$$BS\ NO_x = 0.604 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 3: Brake-Specific PM**

$$BS\ PM = \dfrac{m_{PM}}{W_{cycle}} = \dfrac{1.2}{24.83}$$

$$BS\ PM = 0.0483 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Step 4: Cycle Duration at Rated Power**

$$t = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{P_{rated}} = \dfrac{24.83 \text{ kWh}}{280 \text{ kW}}$$

$$t = 0.0887 \text{ h} = 0.0887 \times 3600 \text{ s}$$

$$t = 319.3 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answer:** BS NOₓ = 0.604 g/kWh; BS PM = 0.0483 g/kWh; t ≈ 319.3 s (Note: this is much shorter than WHTC's 1800 s — engine is operating below rated power for most of WHTC).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion**: Mass emission formula gives **kg** if density is in kg/m³ — always convert to **g** before dividing by km.
> 2. **Density Mismatch**: For HC, density is based on **C₃H₈** (1.83 kg/m³) by convention; for NOₓ it is **NO₂** (2.05 kg/m³). Wrong density = 0 marks.
> 3. **Phase vs Total**: For MIDC, students often confuse **Bag 1 (urban)** distance (0.494 km) with **Total (1.013 km)**.
> 4. **Dilution Factor Sign**: For DF validation, remember DF = c_raw / c_CVS — must be > 1.
> 5. **BSFC Trap**: BSFC is **g/kWh** of fuel, not of pollutant. Don't confuse with brake-specific emissions.
> 6. **Cold vs Hot WHTC Weighting**: 14% cold + 86% hot is frequently tested — forgetting this loses 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize):**

- [ ] **MIDC**: 118 s, 1.013 km, max 50 km/h, 2 phases (urban + extra-urban).
- [ ] **WHTC**: 1800 s × 2 (cold + hot), weighted 14% + 86%, on engine dynamometer.
- [ ] **WMTC**: 3 classes based on design speed (≤75, 75–130, >130 km/h).
- [ ] **CVS Principle**: Constant volumetric flow, raw exhaust + filtered air = diluted sample.
- [ ] **Type I Test** = Tailpipe emissions; **Type II** = Idle CO + lambda; **Type III** = Crankcase; **Type IV** = Evaporative; **Type V** = Durability; **Type VI** = Low-temp CO/HC.
- [ ] **Standard Densities** — CO: 1.25; HC (C₃H₈): 1.83; NOₓ (NO₂): 2.05; CO₂: 1.96 kg/m³ at NTP.
- [ ] **Smoke Extinction**: k = -(1/L) ln(1 - N/100); N = opacity in %; L = 0.43 m.
- [ ] **Mass Emission Formula**: m = V × c × 10⁻⁶ × ρ (in grams if V in m³, ρ in g/m³).
- [ ] **Distance-Specific**: g/km = mass (g) / distance (km).
- [ ] **Brake-Specific**: g/kWh = mass (g) / cycle work (kWh).
- [ ] **Carbon-Balance Constant**: **0.1154** for fuel consumption from CO, HC, CO₂.
- [ ] **BS-VI LDV Petrol Limits**: CO 1.0, HC+NOₓ 0.16, PM 0.0045 g/km, PN 6×10¹¹/km.
- [ ] **BS-VI HDV Diesel Limits (WHTC)**: NOₓ 0.46, PM 0.01 g/kWh, PN 6×10¹¹/kWh.
- [ ] **PM Measurement**: TEOM (real-time) or gravimetric filter (47 mm Teflon, conditioned 24h).
- [ ] **PN Measurement**: Particle Number Counter (CPNC) — volatile particle remover (VPR) + catalytic stripper.
- [ ] **Analyzers**: NDIR (CO, CO₂), FID (THC, CH₄), Chemiluminescent (NO, NO₂), CLD for NH₃.
- [ ] **Soak conditions**: 20–30°C for 6–30 h (LDV); 25°C for 80 min cold (WHTC).
- [ ] **Cold start is mandatory** in every regulated cycle.
- [ ] **Dynamometer Road Load**: P = (F₀ + F₁V + F₂V²) × V / 3600.
- [ ] **Smoke Tests for HDV**: Free Acceleration (FAT), Loaded Steady-State (LSST), ELR (Euro VI replaced LSST with ELR).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Analysers: NDIR and Chemiluminescence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Emission Analysers: NDIR and Chemiluminescence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Emission measurement forms the backbone of homologation testing under **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms. KTU expects students to know the *operating principle*, *gas selectivity*, and *detection limit* of the two principal analysers covered in Module 2: the **NDIR** (for CO, CO₂, HC) and the **Chemiluminescence Analyser** (for NOₓ).

### 1.1 Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Analyser

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
A *Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Analyser* is a continuous gas-phase photometric instrument that quantifies the concentration of a specific gas by measuring the attenuation of broadband infrared (IR) radiation passed through a sample cell, exploiting the unique rotational-vibrational IR absorption signature of the target gas molecule without the use of a dispersive element (prism or grating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term **"Non-Dispersive"** means the analyser does *not* split the IR beam into its constituent wavelengths using a prism or diffraction grating. Instead, it uses a broad-spectrum IR source and a *gas-filled* detector that itself is selective to the wavelength of the gas being measured.

**Gases Measured by NDIR:** CO, CO₂, HC (as equivalent hexane/propane), N₂O, SO₂ (with appropriate filter cells).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Sunglass" Effect:**
Imagine wearing **polarised sunglasses** on a bright day. The sunglasses *absorb* horizontal light vibrations but allow vertical ones through. Similarly, an NDIR analyser sends a broad spectrum of IR light through an exhaust gas sample. Each pollutant molecule (say CO) acts like a tiny "filter," swallowing IR radiation at *only* its characteristic wavelength. The detector downstream measures how much light was swallowed, and from that "darkness," it infers how much CO is present. The more CO molecules → the more IR light absorbed → the higher the concentration reading.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not a spectrometer?** In a dispersive IR (like a lab FTIR), light is split by a prism and each wavelength is measured independently — slow, bulky, expensive. In a car workshop or test cell, you need a *real-time*, *rugged*, *single-gas-or-few-gas* device → hence the **non-dispersive** architecture.

### 1.2 Chemiluminescence Analyser (CLA)

**Formal Definition:**
A *Chemiluminescence Analyser (CLA)* is a continuous gas analyser that measures the concentration of **nitric oxide (NO)** and, by catalytic conversion, **total oxides of nitrogen (NOₓ)**, by exploiting the photon emission (chemiluminescence) produced when NO reacts with **ozone (O₃)** to form electronically excited NO₂*.

**Core Chemical Reaction:**

$$\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2^{*} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + h\nu$$

The emitted photon has energy corresponding to a wavelength of approximately **600 nm to 875 nm** (red to near-infrared region).

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Glow Worm" Effect:**
Think of a *firefly* (chemiluminescence in nature). A chemical reaction inside its body releases energy as visible light — no heat, no flame, just light. In a CLA, we deliberately trigger such a *light-emitting chemical reaction* in a controlled chamber. The exhaust sample (containing NO) is mixed with ozone. Wherever an NO molecule meets an O₃ molecule, a tiny flash of red light is produced. The brighter the chamber glows, the more NO is in the exhaust. A photomultiplier tube "counts" these photons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CLA measures **NO directly**; **NO₂** is *not* directly chemiluminescent with ozone. To measure **total NOₓ**, NO₂ must first be catalytically reduced to NO in a **molybdenum (Mo) or stainless-steel converter** held at **≈ 315 °C to 375 °C**, and the difference (NOₓ − NO) gives NO₂.

**Standard Reference:** KTU aligns with the **Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 40 Part 86** and **UN/ECE R83** test procedures, where these two analysers are mandatory components of the Constant Volume Sampling (CVS) system.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 NDIR Analyser — Operating Principle

The NDIR analyser is built around the **Beer–Lambert Law**, which quantifies the exponential attenuation of light passing through an absorbing medium.

#### 2.1.1 Working Principle (Structured Logic)

1. **IR Source:** A heated filament (typically **nichrome or platinum** at ~700 °C) emits broadband IR radiation covering 2 µm to 15 µm.
2. **Chopper / Modulator:** A rotating chopper wheel alternately directs the IR beam through the **sample cell** and the **reference cell** at a typical frequency of **5 Hz to 25 Hz**. This produces an AC signal that is immune to drift and ambient IR noise.
3. **Sample Cell:** The actual exhaust gas flows through this tube (length **L ≈ 0.1 m to 0.5 m**, internal volume typically **10 cm³ to 50 cm³**).
4. **Reference Cell:** Sealed with a *non-absorbing* gas (usually N₂ or Ar) — provides the optical "zero" baseline.
5. **Detector:** The most common types are:
   - **Luft Detector (Capacitance Microphone Type):** A closed cell filled with the *target gas* (e.g., CO). When sample absorbs IR, the detector gas heats up, expands, and deflects a flexible diaphragm — a capacitance change is measured.
   - **Concentration-Type Detector (Semiconductor / Thermopile / Pyroelectric):** A non-selective broadband detector measuring differential intensity between sample and reference beams.
6. **Signal Processing:** The AC signal is synchronously demodulated, linearised, and displayed as concentration (ppm or %).

#### 2.1.2 Optical Path Length and Sensitivity

A longer sample cell (L) yields higher sensitivity because absorbance scales linearly with path length. Modern instruments use **multiple-reflection (White cell or Herriott cell)** geometries to achieve effective path lengths of several metres within a compact housing.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Variable Definition** | **Typical Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Beer–Lambert Law (monochromatic)** | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha \, c \, L}$ | $I_0$ = incident intensity; $I$ = transmitted intensity; $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient; $c$ = gas concentration; $L$ = optical path length | W/m² (intensity); ppm·m (αcL) | NDIR core equation |
| **Transmittance** | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\alpha c L}$ | Ratio of transmitted to incident intensity | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Detector output baseline |
| **Absorbance** | $A = -\log_{10}(T) = \alpha \, c \, L$ | Base-10 logarithm of inverse transmittance | Absorbance units (AU) | Linearises analyser calibration |
| **Multi-component Beer–Lambert** | $I = I_0 \exp\!\left(-L \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \, c_i\right)$ | Sum over all absorbing species | W/m² | Cross-interference estimation |
| **NDIR Sensitivity Enhancement (White Cell)** | $L_{\text{eff}} = N \cdot d$ | $N$ = number of reflections; $d$ = mirror spacing | metres | Compact high-sensitivity design |
| **Chemiluminescence Intensity** | $I_{\text{CL}} = K \, [\text{NO}] \, [\text{O}_3]$ | $K$ = reaction-rate constant; [ ] = molar concentration | photons/s | CLA first-order response |
| **NOₓ – NO Difference** | $[\text{NO}_2] = [\text{NO}_x] - [\text{NO}]$ | NOₓ measured after converter; NO measured bypassed | ppm | Indirect NO₂ measurement |
| **Mo-Converter Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{conv}} = \dfrac{[\text{NO}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{NO}_2]_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | % conversion efficiency of NO₂ → NO | % (must exceed 95 % per CFR 40) | Compliance check |
| **Detection Limit (typical NDIR CO)** | $\text{MDL} \approx 1 \,\text{ppm}$ | Minimum Detectable Limit | ppm | BS-VI compliance for CO (≤ 1 g/km) |
| **CLA NO Detection Limit** | $\text{MDL} \approx 0.5 \,\text{ppm}$ | Photomultiplier noise floor | ppm | BS-VI NOₓ (≤ 0.06 g/km for petrol) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, the most frequently tested derivations are (a) the *Beer–Lambert Law* with its logarithmic form, and (b) the *chemiluminescence stoichiometry* NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂. Memorise the *photon wavelength* band — examiners love asking "what is the colour of the emitted light?"

### 2.3 Why the 'Why' Matters — Engineering Relevance

- **NDIR** is the workhorse of automotive exhaust gas analysers (e.g., **Horiba OBS-ONE**, **AVL DiGas 4000**, **Sensors SEMTECH-DS**) because it requires *no consumable reagents* and offers a calibration stability of **6 to 12 months**.
- **CLA** is mandated by the US EPA, EU, and Indian ARAI for *NOₓ* measurement because NO₂ cannot be measured by NDIR (very weak IR absorption) and electrochemical sensors lack the precision required for sub-10 ppm levels.
- In **BS-VI Real Driving Emissions (RDE)**, the PEMS (Portable Emissions Measurement System) uses miniaturised versions of both — the **NDIR** for CO/CO₂/HC and **electrochemical cell** or **CLA** for NOₓ.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Beer–Lambert Law for NDIR Absorbance

Let us derive the relationship that underpins every quantitative NDIR measurement.

**Starting Assumptions:**
- The IR source is monochromatic (in practice, broadband — but the law is applied per absorption band).
- Each gas molecule absorbs independently of its neighbours (low concentration regime, valid up to ~5 % by volume).
- The absorbing gas is uniformly distributed in the sample cell.

**Step 1 — Differential Attenuation of Intensity**

Consider a thin slice of gas of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the entrance window. The fractional reduction in intensity $dI$ over this slice is proportional to the incident intensity $I(x)$ and the slice thickness $dx$:

$$dI = -\kappa \, I(x) \, c \, dx$$

where $\kappa$ is the *molar absorption coefficient* (units: m²/mol), $c$ is concentration (mol/m³).

**Step 2 — Separation of Variables**

Rearranging the differential equation:

$$\frac{dI}{I(x)} = -\kappa \, c \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Integration Across the Cell Length $L$**

Integrating from the entrance ($x=0$, $I = I_0$) to the exit ($x = L$, $I = I$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\kappa \, c \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$\left[\ln I\right]_{I_0}^{I} = -\kappa \, c \, L$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\kappa \, c \, L$$

**Step 4 — Exponential Form (Transmittance)**

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\kappa \, c \, L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I = I_0 \, e^{-\kappa \, c \, L}$$

This is the **Beer–Lambert Law** in its natural exponential form.

**Step 5 — Absorbance (Base-10 Logarithm)**

The detector's voltage signal $V$ is proportional to $I$. Most analysers report *absorbance* $A$, defined as the base-10 logarithm:

$$A = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(e^{\kappa c L}\right) = \kappa \, c \, L \cdot \log_{10}(e) = \varepsilon \, c \, L$$

where $\varepsilon = \kappa \log_{10}(e)$ is the *decadic molar absorption coefficient* (units: m²/mol or L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹).

**Final Linearised Working Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,A = \varepsilon \, c \, L\,}$$

This linearity is what allows the analyser to be calibrated with a single gas standard and then linearly extrapolate.

**Step 6 — Multi-Component Mixture (Cross-Interference Correction)**

In real exhaust, several IR-active gases are present simultaneously. The effective absorbance is the sum:

$$A_{\text{total}} = L \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_i \, c_i = L\left(\varepsilon_{\text{CO}} c_{\text{CO}} + \varepsilon_{\text{CO}_2} c_{\text{CO}_2} + \varepsilon_{\text{HC}} c_{\text{HC}} + \dots\right)$$

Modern analysers use **digital signal processing and gas filter correlation (GFC)** to mathematically isolate the target gas concentration.

### 3.2 Derivation of Chemiluminescence Photon Yield

For the reaction NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂, the photon emission rate is governed by the rate of the chemical reaction.

**Step 1 — Rate Equation**

The reaction is *first-order* in both NO and O₃:

$$\text{Rate} = k \, [\text{NO}] \, [\text{O}_3]$$

where $k$ is the rate constant (≈ **1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ cm³·molecule⁻¹·s⁻¹** at 298 K).

**Step 2 — Photon Emission**

Only a fraction $\Phi$ of the excited NO₂* molecules emit a photon (the rest lose energy via collisional quenching). The CL intensity is:

$$I_{\text{CL}} = \Phi \cdot k \, [\text{NO}] \, [\text{O}_3] \cdot V$$

where $V$ is the volume of the reaction chamber viewed by the photomultiplier.

**Step 3 — Excess Ozone Condition**

In practice, ozone is supplied in vast stoichiometric excess ([O₃] ≫ [NO]). Thus [O₃] becomes effectively constant, and the response becomes *pseudo-first-order* in NO:

$$I_{\text{CL}} \approx K' \, [\text{NO}]$$

where $K' = \Phi \, k \, [\text{O}_3] \, V$ is a constant under steady-state ozone flow.

**Step 4 — Pressure Dependence**

The chemiluminescent reaction has a *negative temperature coefficient* and a strong *pressure dependence*. Increasing pressure at constant flow enhances residence time, but at the cost of quenching. The optimum operating pressure is typically **100 mbar to 300 mbar** (sub-atmospheric) inside the reaction cell.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **NDIR Analyser** | **Chemiluminescence Analyser** |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | IR absorption (Beer–Lambert) | Photon emission from NO + O₃ reaction |
| **Target Gases** | CO, CO₂, HC | NO (direct); NO₂ (via converter) → NOₓ |
| **Reagent Required** | None | Ozone (from built-in O₃ generator) |
| **Response Time (T90)** | 1 to 5 seconds | 0.5 to 2 seconds |
| **Detection Limit** | ~1 ppm (CO) | ~0.5 ppm (NO) |
| **Range** | 0 to 10 % (CO) | 0 to 10,000 ppm (NO) |
| **Cross-Interference** | H₂O (corrected by Nafion dryer); CO₂/CO overlap (corrected by GFC) | NO₂ requires converter; NH₃, SO₂ can interfere |
| **Calibration Stability** | 6 to 12 months | 3 to 6 months (ozone generator drift) |
| **Cost (Approx.)** | ₹ 1.5 to 4 Lakh | ₹ 4 to 8 Lakh |
| **Standards** | EPA CFR 40 §86.120; ISO 3930 | EPA CFR 40 §86.122; UN ECE R83 |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NDIR Analyser — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IR SOURCE - Nichrome Filament 700C] --> B[CHOPPER - Rotating Wheel 5-25 Hz]
    B --> C[SAMPLE CELL - Exhaust Gas Flow Path]
    B --> D[REFERENCE CELL - Sealed N2 or Ar]
    C --> E[OPTICAL FILTER - Target Gas Bandpass]
    D --> E
    E --> F[LUFT DETECTOR - Gas Filled Microphone]
    F --> G[LOCK IN AMPLIFIER - Synchronous Demodulation]
    G --> H[MICROCONTROLLER - Linearisation and Output]
    H --> I[DISPLAY - ppm or percent Concentration]
    H --> J[ANALOG OUTPUT - 0 to 10 V or 4 to 20 mA]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The IR source emits broadband radiation. The chopper alternates the beam between the *sample cell* (containing real exhaust) and the *reference cell* (containing inert gas). Both beams pass through a narrow-band *optical filter* and enter the *Luft detector*, which is itself filled with the gas of interest. The detector measures the differential intensity and converts it to a capacitance/voltage signal. The lock-in amplifier extracts the AC component at the chopping frequency, and the microcontroller linearises the absorbance into a concentration value.

### 4.2 Chemiluminescence Analyser — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EXHAUST SAMPLE INLET] --> B[PARTICULATE FILTER]
    B --> C[MODE SELECTOR VALVE - NO Mode or NOx Mode]
    C --> D[NOx MODE PATH]
    C --> E[NO MODE PATH - Bypass Converter]
    D --> F[MOLYBDENUM CONVERTER - 315 to 375 C, NO2 to NO]
    E --> G[REACTION CHAMBER]
    F --> G
    H[OZONE GENERATOR - Corona Discharge from O2] --> G
    G --> I[OPTICAL WINDOW - Red 600 to 875 nm]
    I --> J[PHOTOMULTIPLIER TUBE - PMT Cooled to 5 to 10 C]
    J --> K[PHOTON COUNTING ELECTRONICS]
    K --> L[MICROCONTROLLER - Linearise and Display]
    L --> M[DISPLAY AND ANALOG OUTPUT]
    L --> N[AUX OUTPUT NO2 equals NOx minus NO]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The sample first passes through a particulate filter. A solenoid *mode selector valve* routes the gas either directly to the reaction chamber (NO mode) or through the *Mo converter* (NOₓ mode). In the converter, NO₂ is catalytically reduced to NO at ~350 °C. Simultaneously, an *ozone generator* (a corona discharge tube fed by clean air or O₂) produces a continuous supply of O₃. In the reaction chamber, NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂ + hν. The emitted red photons pass through a window and are counted by a *photomultiplier tube* (PMT), which is often thermoelectrically cooled to reduce dark noise. The microcontroller computes NO (direct channel) and NOₓ (post-converter channel) and reports NO₂ as the difference.

### 4.3 Photomultiplier Tube — Working Detail (Sub-Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PHOTON h nu Strikes Photocathode] --> B[PRIMARY ELECTRON Ejected by Photoelectric Effect]
    B --> C[DYNODE 1 - Secondary Emission Multiplied by 5x]
    C --> D[DYNODE 2 - Multiply by 5x]
    D --> E[DYNODE 3 - Multiply by 5x]
    E --> F[DYNODE N - Cumulative Gain 10 to 6]
    F --> G[ANODE - Current Pulse Detected]
    G --> H[DISCRIMINATOR AND COUNTER]
```

A typical PMT has **10 to 14 dynodes** with a stage gain of ~5, giving a total current amplification of **10⁶ to 10⁷**. This extreme sensitivity is what allows the CLA to detect sub-ppm NO concentrations.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Q1. Define Non-Dispersive Infrared Analyser. Name the gases it can measure.**

**Model Answer:**
A Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Analyser is a gas-phase analytical instrument that determines the concentration of a target gas by measuring the attenuation of broadband infrared radiation passing through a sample cell, exploiting the characteristic IR absorption of the gas molecules. Unlike dispersive (FTIR) instruments, it does not use a prism or grating. The gases typically measured by NDIR include **CO, CO₂, and HC** (hydrocarbons, often expressed as propane or n-hexane equivalent). It can also be configured for **N₂O and SO₂** with appropriate optical filter cells.

> **Distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Gases (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023]**
> **Q2. State the principle of chemiluminescence analyser. Write the governing chemical reaction.**

**Model Answer:**
A chemiluminescence analyser measures **nitric oxide (NO)** concentration by exploiting the photon emission (chemiluminescence) produced when NO reacts with **ozone (O₃)**. The governing reaction is:

$$\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2^{*} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + h\nu$$

The excited NO₂* relaxes to its ground state by emitting a photon in the **red to near-infrared region (600–875 nm)**, which is detected by a photomultiplier tube. To measure **total NOₓ**, NO₂ is first catalytically reduced to NO in a molybdenum converter at ~350 °C.

> **Distribution:** Principle (1 mark) + Reaction (1 mark) + Photon wavelength band / NO₂ measurement (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 – Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Analyser** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the chopper and the Luft detector. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **Beer–Lambert Law** as applied to NDIR measurement. Show how absorbance becomes linear with concentration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. **IR Source:** A heated filament (~700 °C, nichrome) emits broadband IR radiation (2–15 µm).
2. **Chopper (Modulator):** A rotating sector wheel with cut-out slots alternates the beam between the *sample cell* and the *reference cell* at 5–25 Hz. The AC modulation enables synchronous (lock-in) detection, eliminating ambient IR noise and source drift.
3. **Sample Cell:** A tube of length L (0.1–0.5 m) through which the exhaust gas flows continuously. IR is absorbed in proportion to the target gas concentration.
4. **Reference Cell:** Sealed with **N₂ or Ar** (IR-inactive), providing the zero-absorption baseline.
5. **Optical Filter:** A narrow-bandpass filter (or interference filter) centred on the absorption band of the target gas (e.g., 4.7 µm for CO).
6. **Luft Detector:** A closed cavity filled with the *target gas* (e.g., CO), fitted with a flexible capacitor diaphragm. The chopped IR absorbed by the detector gas heats it, causing pressure oscillations and diaphragm deflection. The capacitance change produces an AC voltage proportional to the differential intensity — and hence to the sample gas concentration.
7. **Signal Processing:** The AC signal is synchronously demodulated by a lock-in amplifier referenced to the chopper frequency, linearised, and displayed in ppm or %.
8. **Output:** Concentration is shown on a digital display and an analog output (0–10 V or 4–20 mA) for data acquisition.

> **Distribution:** Block diagram (3 marks) + Chopper role (2 marks) + Luft detector (2 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

As derived in **Section 3.1** above, the Beer–Lambert Law yields:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\kappa \, c \, L}$$

and the absorbance becomes:

$$A = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \, c \, L$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the *decadic molar absorption coefficient* and $L$ is the optical path length. For constant $L$ and constant $\varepsilon$ (single gas at constant pressure and temperature), $A$ is *linearly proportional* to concentration $c$. This linearity permits a *single-point calibration*: the analyser is zeroed with N₂, span-calibrated with a known reference gas, and the linear relation is then valid across the entire measurement range.

For multi-component mixtures, the total absorbance is the sum:

$$A_{\text{total}} = L \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_i c_i$$

Cross-interference is corrected either by **Gas Filter Correlation (GFC)** or by digital matrix correction using known cross-sensitivity coefficients.

> **Distribution:** Derivation (4 marks) + Absorbance linearity (2 marks) + Calibration note (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**(a)** Describe the working of a **Chemiluminescence Analyser (CLA)** with a block diagram. Explain how NO and NOₓ are measured separately. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A chemiluminescence analyser is calibrated with a NO standard of **90 ppm** yielding a PMT output of **4.5 V**. The unknown sample gives **2.7 V**. Assuming a linear first-order response in NO, calculate the unknown concentration. If the NOₓ mode reads **3.6 V**, determine the NO₂ concentration in the sample. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. **Sample Inlet & Filter:** Exhaust sample is drawn in by a pump and passes through a 5 µm particulate filter to protect the reaction chamber.
2. **Mode Selector Valve:** A 3-way solenoid valve routes the sample either **directly to the reaction chamber (NO mode)** or **through the molybdenum converter (NOₓ mode)**.
3. **Molybdenum Converter:** Heated to **315–375 °C**, the Mo surface catalytically reduces NO₂ → NO with an efficiency of ≥ 95 % (per EPA specifications).
4. **Ozone Generator:** A corona discharge tube generates O₃ from a feed of clean dry air or O₂. The ozone flow is held in large stoichiometric excess.
5. **Reaction Chamber:** NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂ → NO₂ + O₂ + hν. The reaction occurs at sub-atmospheric pressure (~100–300 mbar) to optimise photon yield.
6. **Optical Window & PMT:** The red-to-NIR photons (600–875 nm) pass through a red-pass filter and strike a thermoelectrically cooled photomultiplier tube (PMT). The PMT produces current pulses counted by photon-counting electronics.
7. **Signal Processing:** The PMT current is proportional to [NO] (pseudo-first-order in [NO] because [O₃] is held constant). The microcontroller logs NO (bypass mode) and NOₓ (converter mode) and reports NO₂ = NOₓ − NO.
8. **Calibration & Zero:** Periodic zero (with N₂ or scrubbed air) and span checks (with certified NO gas) maintain accuracy.

> **Distribution:** Block diagram (3 marks) + Reaction explanation (2 marks) + NO vs NOₓ measurement (2 marks)

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Calibration standard: $C_{\text{cal}} = 90 \,\text{ppm}$, $V_{\text{cal}} = 4.5 \,\text{V}$
- Unknown NO reading: $V_{\text{NO}} = 2.7 \,\text{V}$
- Unknown NOₓ reading: $V_{\text{NO}_x} = 3.6 \,\text{V}$
- Response is linear.

**Step 1 — Sensitivity of the analyser:**

$$K = \frac{V_{\text{cal}}}{C_{\text{cal}}} = \frac{4.5 \,\text{V}}{90 \,\text{ppm}} = 0.05 \,\text{V/ppm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute unknown [NO]:**

$$[\text{NO}] = \frac{V_{\text{NO}}}{K} = \frac{2.7 \,\text{V}}{0.05 \,\text{V/ppm}} = 54 \,\text{ppm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total [NOₓ]:**

$$[\text{NO}_x] = \frac{V_{\text{NO}_x}}{K} = \frac{3.6 \,\text{V}}{0.05 \,\text{V/ppm}} = 72 \,\text{ppm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute [NO₂] by difference:**

$$[\text{NO}_2] = [\text{NO}_x] - [\text{NO}] = 72 \,\text{ppm} - 54 \,\text{ppm} = 18 \,\text{ppm}$$

**Final Answer:** [NO] = **54 ppm**; [NO₂] = **18 ppm**

> **Distribution:** Sensitivity derivation (2 marks) + NO (2 marks) + NOₓ (1 mark) + NO₂ difference (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing "non-dispersive" with "dispersive":** Many students write FTIR components. NDIR has *no* prism/grating. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the Mo converter** in CLA — without it, NO₂ cannot be measured. Examiners will deduct heavily if the NO vs NOₓ distinction is muddled.
> 3. **Sign error in Beer–Lambert:** It is $e^{-\kappa c L}$, not $e^{+\kappa c L}$. Memorise the negative sign.
> 4. **Units in numerical problems:** Always quote ppm or % and show unit cancellation explicitly. A correct number with no units = **zero mark** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Ozone equation:** Many students write NO + O₃ → NO₂ only, missing the *excited state* NO₂* and the photon hν. This is a 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NDIR** measures gases (CO, CO₂, HC) by *IR absorption*; governed by **Beer–Lambert Law** $I = I_0 e^{-\kappa c L}$.
- **CLA** measures NO/NOₓ by *chemiluminescence* via the reaction **NO + O₃ → NO₂* + O₂ + hν** (red light, 600–875 nm).
- **Chopper** in NDIR modulates the IR beam for noise-immune lock-in detection.
- **Luft detector** uses the *target gas itself* as the absorbing medium, with a microphone-type diaphragm to convert pressure change into electrical signal.
- **NO₂ measurement** in CLA is *indirect* — requires a **molybdenum converter** at **315–375 °C** that reduces NO₂ → NO; then NO₂ = NOₓ − NO.
- **Ozone** is generated *in situ* in a CLA by corona discharge — it is a consumable (electricity) but not a bottled reagent.
- **Detection limits:** NDIR ~1 ppm (CO); CLA ~0.5 ppm (NO).
- **Calibration linearity** in both analysers makes single-point span calibration valid across the dynamic range.
- **Cross-interferences:** NDIR — H₂O (use Nafion dryer), CO₂ in CO channel (GFC); CLA — NH₃ and SO₂ can interfere in converter.
- **Standards:** EPA CFR 40 §86.120 (NDIR) and §86.122 (CLA); Indian ARAI follows these for BS-VI compliance.
- **Numerical tip:** In CLA calibration problems, always compute sensitivity $K = V_{\text{cal}}/C_{\text{cal}}$ *first*, then divide unknown voltage by $K$.
- **Photon physics:** The CL reaction's emitted photon wavelength lies in the *red-to-NIR* region — this is what allows optical filtering to block ambient light.
- **PEMS in BS-VI RDE** uses miniaturised NDIR (for CO/CO₂/HC) and electrochemical cells (for NOₓ) due to size and weight constraints of portable units.
- **Exam mantra:** "NDIR = Absorbance = Linear with c. CLA = Emission = Linear with [NO]. NOₓ = NO + NO₂ converter. Always draw the block diagram — it carries 2–3 marks by itself."

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Control Technologies for Gasoline Fuelled Vehicles: A/F Ratio and Electronic Control
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emission Control Technologies for Gasoline Fuelled Vehicles: A/F Ratio and Electronic Control

---

# 🔧 Emission Control Technologies for Gasoline Fuelled Vehicles: A/F Ratio and Electronic Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)** is the mass ratio of air inducted into the engine cylinder to the fuel mass delivered for combustion. It is the single most influential parameter governing the composition of exhaust emissions from a Spark Ignition (SI) engine.

For gasoline, the **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio** is the chemically ideal (theoretically complete combustion) ratio defined as:

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \frac{\text{Mass of Air}}{\text{Mass of Fuel}} = 14.7 : 1$$

This means for every **1 kg of gasoline**, exactly **14.7 kg of air** is required for complete combustion with no residual oxygen or unburned fuel.

The **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** and **Lambda (λ)** are normalized indicators of the operating mixture:

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} \quad ; \quad \phi = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

| Operating Condition | Lambda (λ) | Air-Fuel Ratio | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric | λ = 1.00 | 14.7 : 1 | Theoretical complete burn |
| Lean burn | λ > 1.00 | > 14.7 : 1 | Excess air, lower power |
| Rich burn | λ < 1.00 | < 14.7 : 1 | Excess fuel, higher power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lambda (λ) is the cornerstone parameter of all modern electronic engine management systems.** Modern Electronic Control Units (ECUs) monitor and adjust λ in real time using a closed-loop feedback strategy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cooking Recipe Intuition 🍳

Imagine you are cooking a **dal-rice** recipe where the lentils are the **fuel** and water is the **air**:

- **Stoichiometric (λ = 1):** You use *exactly* the right amount of water the recipe demands. Nothing remains, nothing is wasted. The pot cooks cleanly.
- **Lean (λ > 1):** You added *too much* water. The lentils cook, but extra water remains. In an engine, this means excess **oxygen** in exhaust → tends to form **NOx** at high temperature.
- **Rich (λ < 1):** You added *less* water than required. The lentils burn (char) at the bottom. In an engine, this means **unburned hydrocarbons (HC)** and **carbon monoxide (CO)** due to lack of oxygen.

The **electronic control unit (ECU)** acts like a *smart chef* who tastes the dish (oxygen sensor reading) and adds a pinch of water or lentils to keep the recipe **perfectly balanced** at all times.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) can simultaneously destroy HC, CO, and NOx ONLY when λ is held extremely close to 1.00 (within ±1%).** This precision is unachievable mechanically — it requires electronic closed-loop control with a Lambda oxygen sensor.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU Examinations

This topic is a **high-weightage, high-recurrence** area in PEAUT522. Students are routinely examined on:
- Stoichiometric ratio derivation
- Lambda windows for different emissions
- Operation of the Zirconia Oxygen Sensor
- Closed-loop ECU strategy
- Three-Way Catalyst efficiency curves

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemical Foundation of the Stoichiometric Ratio

Gasoline is a complex mixture of paraffins, aromatics, and olefins. The standard textbook surrogate hydrocarbon for emission control calculations is **CH₂** (representing the average H/C ratio of gasoline).

The complete combustion reaction:

$$CH_2 + 1.5\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + 5.64\,N_2$$

Using molecular weights:
- Fuel (CH₂): 14 g/mol
- Air (O₂ + 3.76 N₂): 4.76 × 28.97 ≈ **137.91 g/mol**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \frac{1.5 \times 137.91}{14} = 14.77 \approx 14.7$$

### 2.2 The Three Lambda Windows — Mapping A/F to Emissions

The composition of exhaust gases is a strong, non-linear function of λ. The behavior is divided into three distinct operating windows:

#### 🔹 Window 1: Rich Region (λ < 1.0)
- **CO formation** dominates (oxygen-starved combustion)
- **Unburned HC** increases dramatically as λ drops below 0.95
- Engine power peaks near **λ = 0.88–0.92** (max BMEP zone)
- **NOx is suppressed** due to lower peak flame temperatures

#### 🔹 Window 2: Stoichiometric Point (λ = 1.00 ± 0.01)
- **Sweet spot** of the Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)
- All three pollutants (HC, CO, NOx) can be removed simultaneously
- This is the **target operating zone** for all modern gasoline vehicles
- Requires **closed-loop electronic control** to maintain

#### 🔹 Window 3: Lean Region (λ > 1.0)
- **NOx formation** peaks at slightly lean (λ ≈ 1.05–1.10) due to high O₂ + high temperature
- **CO and HC drop** sharply (excess O₂ oxidizes them)
- Fuel economy improves
- TWC efficiency **collapses** — cannot reduce NOx in oxygen-rich exhaust

### 2.3 The Zirconia Oxygen (Lambda) Sensor — The Engine's "Nose"

The **Lambda Sensor** is a galvanic cell that generates a voltage signal proportional to the oxygen partial pressure difference between exhaust gas and ambient air.

**Construction:**
- A thimble-shaped **Zirconia (ZrO₂) ceramic** element
- **Platinum electrodes** on inner and outer surfaces
- Heater element (for fast light-off)
- Reference: ambient air inside; Measurement: exhaust gas outside

**Operating Principle (Nernst Equation):**

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\text{air}}}{P_{O_2,\text{exhaust}}}\right)$$

Where:
- $E$ = sensor output voltage (V)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $F$ = 96485 C/mol
- $P_{O_2}$ = partial pressure of oxygen

**Output Behavior (Voltage Switch):**

| Condition | λ | Sensor Voltage | ECU Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lean | > 1.0 | ≈ **0.1 V** (low) | Enrich mixture |
| Stoichiometric | = 1.0 | ≈ **0.45 V** (switch point) | Hold / maintain |
| Rich | < 1.0 | ≈ **0.9 V** (high) | Lean mixture |

The sensor behaves like a **binary switch**, oscillating rapidly between 0.1 V and 0.9 V at a typical **frequency of 1–2 Hz** during closed-loop operation.

### 2.4 Closed-Loop Electronic Control Architecture

The **ECU (Engine Control Unit)** executes a Proportional-Integral (PI) control loop:

**Control Signal Flow:**

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ Sensor} \rightarrow \text{ECU} \rightarrow \text{Fuel Injector Pulse Width} \rightarrow \text{Engine} \rightarrow \text{Exhaust} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 \text{ Sensor}$$

The ECU's job is to keep the average sensor voltage at **0.45 V** (the stoichiometric switch point). It does this by:

1. **Reading** the O₂ sensor voltage
2. **Comparing** to the target 0.45 V reference
3. **Calculating** an error signal
4. **Adjusting** injector pulse width by a small correction (typically ±2% per cycle)
5. **Repeating** at ~10 Hz

> [!NOTE]
> The integrator in the ECU eliminates the steady-state error — this is what allows the A/F to be maintained within the **±1% lambda window** demanded by the TWC.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual} / (A/F)_{stoich}$ | Definition of Lambda | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\phi = 1/\lambda$ | Equivalence Ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = 14.7$ | Gasoline stoichiometric | mass ratio |
| 4 | $C_xH_y + (x + y/4)O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + (y/2)H_2O$ | General combustion | — |
| 5 | $E = (RT/4F) \ln(P_{O_2,air}/P_{O_2,exh})$ | Nernst equation for O₂ sensor | Volts |
| 6 | $\eta_{TWC} = f(\lambda)$ | Catalyst efficiency vs λ | % |
| 7 | $\tau_{loop} \approx 100\,\text{ms}$ | Closed-loop response time | seconds |
| 8 | $f_{switch} \approx 1\text{–}2\,\text{Hz}$ | O₂ sensor switching frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $V_{stoich} \approx 0.45\,\text{V}$ | Sensor voltage at λ = 1 | Volts |
| 10 | $\Delta\lambda_{TWC} = \pm 0.01$ | Required control precision | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 Scheme, **memorize the value 14.7 ± 0.1** for the stoichiometric A/F of gasoline, the **Nernst equation form** for the O₂ sensor, and the fact that **TWC requires λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** for triple-pollutant removal.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This closed-loop A/F + TWC architecture is what makes modern cars compliant with **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms in India and **Euro 6 / EPA Tier 3** globally. Without precise electronic λ control, NOₓ emissions would be **5–10 times higher**, and the catalyst would degrade rapidly. This system also enables **on-board diagnostics (OBD-II)** to monitor catalyst health and O₂ sensor aging.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio for Gasoline (CH₂ Surrogate)

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion reaction:**

$$CH_2 + 1.5\,O_2 + 1.5(3.76)N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + (1.5 \times 3.76)N_2$$

$$CH_2 + 1.5\,O_2 + 5.64\,N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + 5.64\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute molecular weight of the fuel:**

$$MW_{CH_2} = (1 \times 12) + (2 \times 1) = 12 + 2 = 14\,\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the molecular weight of 1 mole of air:**

Air is modeled as $O_2 + 3.76\,N_2$:

$$MW_{air} = (1 \times 32) + (3.76 \times 28) = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28\,\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass of air needed for 1 mole of fuel:**

$$m_{air} = 1.5 \times MW_{air} = 1.5 \times 137.28 = 205.92\,\text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Compute stoichiometric A/F ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{205.92}{14} = 14.71 \approx 14.7$$

**Result:** $\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} \approx \mathbf{14.7 : 1}$ — confirmed.

---
