### 3.2 Derivation 2: General Stoichiometric A/F for Hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$

**Step 1 — Balanced combustion equation:**

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Mass of oxygen required per kmol of fuel:**

$$m_{O_2} = \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \times 32\,\text{kg/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Convert O₂ mass to air mass (air is 23.2% O₂ by mass):**

$$m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{32(x + y/4)}{0.232}\,\text{kg/kmol}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of fuel:**

$$m_{fuel} = 12x + y\,\text{kg/kmol}$$

**Step 5 — Final A/F expression:**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \frac{32(x + y/4)}{0.232\,(12x + y)} = \frac{137.93\,(4x + y)}{4\,(12x + y)}$$

**Step 6 — Verification for methane (CH₄, x = 1, y = 4):**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right) = \frac{137.93\,(4 + 4)}{4\,(12 + 4)} = \frac{137.93 \times 8}{64} = \frac{1103.4}{64} = 17.24$$

This matches the well-known stoichiometric ratio for methane (≈ 17.2 : 1). ✓

**Step 7 — Verification for iso-octane C₈H₁₈ (x = 8, y = 18):**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right) = \frac{137.93\,(32 + 18)}{4\,(96 + 18)} = \frac{137.93 \times 50}{4 \times 114} = \frac{6896.5}{456} = 15.12$$

C₈H₁₈ gives ~15.1; the *average* gasoline H/C ratio gives 14.7.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Nernst Voltage of Lambda Sensor at Stoichiometric

**Step 1 — Air side oxygen partial pressure:**

Atmospheric O₂: 20.9% by volume, so:

$$P_{O_2,\,air} = 0.209 \times 101.325\,\text{kPa} = 21.18\,\text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Exhaust side at stoichiometric:** exhaust O₂ ≈ 0.2–0.5% (residual). Use $P_{O_2,\,exh} \approx 0.5\,\text{kPa}$.

**Step 3 — Operating temperature of hot sensor: T = 873 K (600 °C).**

**Step 4 — Apply Nernst equation:**

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\,air}}{P_{O_2,\,exh}}\right)$$

$$E = \frac{8.314 \times 873}{4 \times 96485} \ln\!\left(\frac{21.18}{0.5}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate constants:**

$$\frac{RT}{4F} = \frac{7258.12}{385940} = 0.01881\,\text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate log term:**

$$\ln(21.18/0.5) = \ln(42.36) = 3.746$$

**Step 7 — Final sensor voltage:**

$$E = 0.01881 \times 3.746 = 0.0705\,\text{V}$$

This is the theoretical value; in practice, the **switch threshold ≈ 0.45 V** is observed because of the platinum catalyst's nonlinear behavior and the electrode kinetics.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Emission Levels vs. Lambda (Exhaust Composition Table)

| λ | A/F | CO (%) | HC (ppm) | NOx (ppm) | Engine Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.80 | 11.8 | 8.0 | 4000 | 800 | Very rich, smoky, max power |
| 0.90 | 13.2 | 4.5 | 1500 | 1500 | Rich — power zone |
| 0.95 | 14.0 | 2.5 | 800 | 2000 | TWC ineffective |
| 1.00 | 14.7 | 0.5 | 200 | 1500 | **TWC optimal (λ-window)** |
| 1.05 | 15.4 | 0.2 | 100 | 1800 | Lean — NOx peak region |
| 1.10 | 16.2 | 0.1 | 50 | 800 | Lean — NOx drops |
| 1.20 | 17.6 | 0.05 | 30 | 200 | Very lean — misfire risk |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **trade-off**: the λ value that minimizes NOx is **not** the same as the one that minimizes CO + HC. The Three-Way Catalyst resolves this conflict by operating at the *compromise* λ = 1.00 point, but it requires precision control.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop A/F Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Throttle Position Sensor TPS]
    B --> C[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    D[Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP] --> C
    E[Coolant Temperature Sensor CTS] --> C
    F[Intake Air Temperature Sensor IAT] --> C
    C -->|Pulse Width Modulation| G[Fuel Injector]
    G -->|Fuel Spray| H[Engine Cylinder]
    H -->|Combustion| I[Exhaust Manifold]
    I --> J[Oxygen Sensor Lambda Sensor]
    J -->|Voltage Signal 0.1 to 0.9 V| C
    I --> K[Three Way Catalytic Converter TWC]
    K --> L[Tailpipe Emissions]
    C --> M[Malfunction Indicator Lamp MIL OBD II]
```

**Functional Architecture Explanation:**
- The ECU receives **inputs** from multiple sensors (TPS, CKP, CTS, IAT, O₂ sensor)
- It **computes** the required injector pulse width using a base map (lookup table) plus closed-loop correction
- The **fuel injector** delivers the calculated fuel mass
- Combustion products flow through the **exhaust** where the **O₂ sensor** measures residual oxygen
- This feedback signal closes the **control loop**, allowing the ECU to maintain λ = 1.00 ± 0.01
- The **TWC** then simultaneously oxidizes HC + CO and reduces NOx
- Any deviation triggers the **MIL** (check-engine light) per OBD-II regulation

---

### 4.2 Process Flow: Zirconia Lambda Sensor Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exhaust Gas Enters Sensor Tip] --> B[Hot Zirconia Ceramic Above 350 C]
    B --> C{Oxygen Partial Pressure Comparison}
    D[Reference Air Inside Sensor Ambient 21 percent O2] --> C
    C -->|High Exhaust O2 Lean| E[Low Voltage Output 0.1 V]
    C -->|Balanced Stoichiometric| F[Switch Voltage 0.45 V]
    C -->|Low Exhaust O2 Rich| G[High Voltage Output 0.9 V]
    E --> H[ECU Enriches Mixture Increases Pulse Width]
    F --> I[ECU Holds Mixture Stable]
    G --> J[ECU Leans Mixture Decreases Pulse Width]
    H --> K[Oscillating Feedback Maintains Lambda 1.00 plus minus 0.01]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Three Way Catalyst Operates in Optimal Window]
```

**Key Functional Notes:**
- The zirconia sensor only functions when **above 350 °C** — modern sensors include an internal **heater** to reach operating temperature within 10–15 seconds of cold start
- The **switching action** is the basis of the closed-loop strategy — the ECU *counts* voltage transitions
- **Universal / Narrowband** sensors switch at 0.45 V; **Wideband** sensors (UEGO) provide a continuous λ reading from 0.7 to infinity

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Three-Way Catalyst Efficiency vs. Lambda

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RICH[Rich Region Lambda less than 1]
        A1[Lambda 0.85] --> A2[Lambda 0.95]
    end
    subgraph STOICH[Stoichiometric Lambda equals 1]
        B1[Lambda 0.99]
        B2[Lambda 1.00 Optimum]
        B3[Lambda 1.01]
    end
    subgraph LEAN[Lean Region Lambda greater than 1]
        C1[Lambda 1.05] --> C2[Lambda 1.15]
    end
    A2 -->|Approaching stoichiometric| B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 -->|Leaving stoichiometric| C1
    STOICH --> D{Conversion Efficiency}
    D --> E[HC removal greater than 95 percent]
    D --> F[CO removal greater than 95 percent]
    D --> G[NOx removal greater than 90 percent]
```

**Architectural Insight:**
- The **lambda window** for triple-pollutant conversion is **extraordinarily narrow** — only ±1% from λ = 1.00
- Outside this window, **NOx conversion collapses first** (in lean) or **HC/CO escape** (in rich)
- This is why **closed-loop electronic control** is *non-negotiable* for any TWC-equipped vehicle

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the term 'Equivalence Ratio' (φ) of an air-fuel mixture. How is it related to the Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) and the Lambda (λ)? Why is λ = 1.00 critical for the operation of a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** is the ratio of the stoichiometric A/F to the actual A/F:

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Where **Lambda (λ)** is defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$

- When **λ = 1** (or φ = 1): stoichiometric mixture
- When **λ < 1** (φ > 1): rich mixture
- When **λ > 1** (φ < 1): lean mixture

**Significance for TWC:** A Three-Way Catalytic Converter simultaneously oxidizes HC and CO, and reduces NOx. However, it can do so **only when λ is held at 1.00 ± 0.01**. Outside this narrow window, the catalyst loses its ability to remove one or more pollutants. Hence, **precise closed-loop electronic control is mandatory** for TWC operation.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Zirconia Oxygen (Lambda) Sensor used in a gasoline engine. Mention typical output voltages for lean, stoichiometric, and rich conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Zirconia (ZrO₂) Oxygen Sensor** is an electro-chemical device placed in the exhaust stream that generates a voltage signal proportional to the difference in oxygen partial pressure between the exhaust gas and ambient air.

**Construction:** A thimble-shaped ZrO₂ ceramic element with porous **platinum electrodes** coated on both inner and outer surfaces. The inner surface is exposed to **ambient air (reference)**, while the outer surface contacts the **exhaust gas**. A heater element brings the sensor to operating temperature (~350 °C) within seconds.

**Working Principle (Nernst Effect):** Oxygen ions migrate through the zirconia ceramic from the high-O₂ side to the low-O₂ side, generating an EMF:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\,air}}{P_{O_2,\,exh}}\right)$$

**Output Voltages:**

| Condition | λ | Output Voltage |
|---|---|---|
| Lean | > 1.0 | ~ **0.1 V** |
| Stoichiometric | = 1.0 | ~ **0.45 V** (switch threshold) |
| Rich | < 1.0 | ~ **0.9 V** |

The ECU monitors this rapidly switching signal (1–2 Hz) and adjusts injector pulse width to maintain an **average voltage of 0.45 V**, ensuring λ ≈ 1.00.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** KTU ESE Module 3 allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for gasoline, assuming the fuel to be represented by the surrogate hydrocarbon **CH₂**. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **closed-loop electronic control system** used to maintain the Air-Fuel Ratio in a modern gasoline engine. Explain the role of the ECU, O₂ sensor, and fuel injector. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Write the balanced combustion reaction for CH₂:

$$CH_2 + 1.5\,O_2 + 1.5(3.76)N_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + 5.64\,N_2$$

**Step 2:** Compute molecular weight of fuel:

$$MW_{CH_2} = (1 \times 12) + (2 \times 1) = 14\,\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 3:** Compute molecular weight of air (modeled as O₂ + 3.76 N₂):

$$MW_{air} = (1 \times 32) + (3.76 \times 28) = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28\,\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 4:** Mass of air per mole of fuel:

$$m_{air} = 1.5 \times 137.28 = 205.92\,\text{g}$$

**Step 5:** Stoichiometric A/F ratio:

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \frac{205.92}{14} = 14.71 \approx \mathbf{14.7 : 1}$$

---

**Part (b) — Closed-Loop Control System:**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Accelerator Pedal Input] --> B[Throttle Position Sensor TPS]
    B --> C[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    D[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> C
    E[Coolant Temperature Sensor] --> C
    F[Mass Air Flow Sensor MAF] --> C
    C -->|Pulse Width| G[Fuel Injector]
    G --> H[Combustion Chamber]
    H --> I[Exhaust Manifold]
    I --> J[Lambda Oxygen Sensor]
    J -->|Feedback Voltage| C
    I --> K[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    K --> L[Tailpipe]
```

**Operation Description:**

1. The **ECU** receives inputs from **TPS, CKP, MAF, CTS** sensors and computes a base injector pulse-width from a pre-stored **fuel map**.
2. The **fuel injector** delivers atomized fuel into the intake port or directly into the cylinder.
3. After combustion, exhaust gases pass over the **O₂ sensor** (downstream of the exhaust manifold, upstream of the TWC).
4. The O₂ sensor generates a **voltage between 0.1 V (lean) and 0.9 V (rich)**, switching at **0.45 V (stoichiometric)**.
5. The ECU implements a **Proportional + Integral (PI) control algorithm**:
   - If sensor voltage > 0.45 V → engine is rich → ECU **shortens** injector pulse width
   - If sensor voltage < 0.45 V → engine is lean → ECU **lengthens** injector pulse width
6. This produces an **oscillating closed-loop control** at ~1–2 Hz that keeps average λ at 1.00 ± 0.01.
7. The TWC, operating in this narrow window, achieves **simultaneous removal of HC, CO, and NOx**.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** What is the **Lambda (λ) sensor**? Explain its construction and working using the **Nernst equation**. State typical output voltages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the variation of **HC, CO, and NOx emissions** with Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) in a gasoline engine. Explain why the **Three-Way Catalytic Converter** can simultaneously remove all three pollutants only at λ = 1.00. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Lambda Sensor:**

The **Lambda (λ) sensor** (or Oxygen sensor) is a solid-state electro-chemical device installed in the exhaust stream of a gasoline engine. It provides real-time feedback to the ECU about the residual oxygen content in the exhaust, enabling precise A/F control.

**Construction:**
- A **thimble-shaped Zirconia (ZrO₂) ceramic** element stabilized with Yttria
- **Porous platinum electrodes** on inner and outer surfaces
- Internal **heater coil** for fast light-off
- Steel housing with exhaust mounting boss
- Lead wires for signal output and heater supply

**Working Principle (Nernst Equation):**

Oxygen ions conduct through the hot ZrO₂ ceramic. The EMF generated is:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\,air}}{P_{O_2,\,exh}}\right)$$

- When exhaust is **lean** (λ > 1): high O₂ in exhaust → small pressure difference → **E ≈ 0.1 V**
- When exhaust is **rich** (λ < 1): nearly zero O₂ in exhaust → large pressure difference → **E ≈ 0.9 V**
- At **stoichiometric** (λ = 1): sharp transition occurs → **E ≈ 0.45 V**

The sensor requires a minimum temperature of ~**350 °C** to function, hence the built-in heater. Its output is essentially a **binary switch** used by the ECU's closed-loop controller.

---

**Part (b) — Emission Variation with A/F:**

**HC Emissions:**
- Increase sharply as mixture becomes **richer** (λ < 1) due to incomplete combustion and wall-quenching effects
- Slight increase at very **lean** mixtures (λ > 1.2) due to misfire and poor flame propagation
- Minimum HC near λ = 1.05

**CO Emissions:**
- Strongly dependent on A/F
- Increase **dramatically** in rich region (λ < 1) because of insufficient O₂
- Drop to near-zero in lean region (λ > 1)
- Minimum CO at λ ≥ 1.05

**NOx Emissions:**
- Peak in **slightly lean** region (λ ≈ 1.05–1.10) due to high flame temperature + excess O₂
- Suppressed in rich region (low O₂) and very lean region (lower temperature)
- Zeldovich mechanism: N₂ + O₂ → 2 NO at high T

**Why TWC Requires λ = 1.00:**

The TWC contains:
- **Platinum/Palladium (oxidation catalysts):** oxidize HC → CO₂ + H₂O; oxidize CO → CO₂
- **Rhodium (reduction catalyst):** reduces NOx → N₂ + ½O₂

Both reactions occur **simultaneously at maximum efficiency only at λ = 1.00 ± 0.01**:
- In **rich** exhaust: insufficient O₂ → cannot oxidize HC/CO
- In **lean** exhaust: no reducing agent → cannot reduce NOx
- At **stoichiometric**: both oxidation and reduction can proceed at full conversion efficiency (HC > 95%, CO > 95%, NOx > 90%)

Hence, λ = 1.00 is the **single operating point** where the TWC performs its *three-way* function, and it is maintained by **closed-loop electronic control** using the O₂ sensor.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the molecular weight of air** as 137.28 g/mol (using only 32 g/mol for O₂). This leads to wrong A/F. **Always** use 4.76 × 28.97 ≈ 137.28 for air.
>
> 2. **Confusing λ and φ.** Remember: λ = 1/φ. Rich = λ < 1, φ > 1. Lean = λ > 1, φ < 1.
>
> 3. **Stating the TWC works at "stoichiometric" but not mentioning the ±1% window** — without this precision, the answer is incomplete.
>
> 4. **Omitting the Nernst equation** when asked about the O₂ sensor. Examiners explicitly look for $E = (RT/4F) \ln(P_{O_2,air}/P_{O_2,exh})$.
>
> 5. **Confusing voltage values** (0.1 V lean, 0.45 V stoich, 0.9 V rich). Many students mix these up under exam pressure.
>
> 6. **Saying "ECU controls the A/F" without explaining the closed-loop mechanism** — always mention feedback, PI control, and the switching action of the sensor.
>
> 7. **No diagram in long answers** — the KTU 2024 scheme mandates diagrams for full marks in Part B.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- ✦ **Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline = 14.7 : 1** (mass basis), derived using CH₂ surrogate giving 14.71.
- ✦ **Lambda (λ) = (A/F)_actual / (A/F)_stoich**; Equivalence ratio φ = 1/λ.
- ✦ Three Lambda Zones: **Rich (λ < 1)**, **Stoichiometric (λ = 1)**, **Lean (λ > 1)**.
- ✦ **NOx peaks at slightly lean** (λ ≈ 1.05–1.10); **CO/HC peak in rich** (λ < 0.9).
- ✦ **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) requires λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** for simultaneous HC, CO, NOx removal.
- ✦ **Zirconia Lambda Sensor:** zirconia ceramic + platinum electrodes; uses Nernst equation.
- ✦ Sensor voltages: **0.1 V (lean), 0.45 V (stoich), 0.9 V (rich)**.
- ✦ Sensor switches at ~**1–2 Hz** during closed-loop operation; requires **T > 350 °C** (hence internal heater).
- ✦ **Nernst equation:** $E = (RT/4F) \ln(P_{O_2,air}/P_{O_2,exh})$.
- ✦ **Closed-loop control** uses PI algorithm — base pulse width from fuel map + ±2% correction per cycle.
- ✦ **Universal/Narrowband sensor** switches at 0.45 V; **Wideband (UEGO)** sensor provides continuous λ reading.
- ✦ **Molecular weight of air** = **137.28 g/mol** (use 4.76 × 28.97); MW of O₂ = 32; MW of N₂ = 28.
- ✦ **Combustion equation:** $C_xH_y + (x + y/4) O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + (y/2) H_2O$.
- ✦ **General A/F formula:** $(A/F)_{stoich} = 137.93(4x + y) / [4(12x + y)]$.
- ✦ **OBD-II monitoring** detects aging O₂ sensors and catalyst efficiency loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalytic Converters: Two-Way and Three-Way Converters, Wear and Poisoning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Catalytic Converters: Two-Way and Three-Way Converters, Wear and Poisoning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Catalytic Converters: Two-Way, Three-Way, Wear and Poisoning

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Catalytic Converter (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A catalytic converter is an **exhaust after-treatment device** installed in the vehicle's exhaust system that uses chemical catalysts (platinum, palladium, and rhodium) to convert harmful pollutants — **carbon monoxide (CO), unburned hydrocarbons (HC), and oxides of nitrogen (NOx)** — into less harmful products such as carbon dioxide (CO₂), water vapour (H₂O), and nitrogen (N₂) through simultaneous **oxidation** and **reduction** reactions, without being consumed in the process.

It functions as the **third (and final) line of defence** in the engine-out emission control hierarchy, following combustion modification (EGR, retarded timing) and crankcase/evaporative controls (PCV, EVAP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Temperature Window:** A modern catalytic converter only becomes chemically active above its **light-off temperature** (≈ **250 °C for TWC**, ≈ **200 °C for diesel oxidation catalyst**) and continues to work up to about **800–900 °C**. Below this range, the catalyst is essentially "asleep" — this is the primary reason cold-start emissions remain high.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Exhaust Scrubber"

Imagine a restaurant exhaust hood. The hood alone removes **large grease particles** (mechanical filtration), but invisible **smoke and odours still escape**. To clean these, the chef installs a **charcoal-and-zeolite scrubber** — air passes through a honeycomb-shaped bed soaked with a chemically active coating that **traps and converts** the fumes into harmless water vapour and CO₂.

A catalytic converter does **exactly the same thing**, except:
- The "fumes" are toxic gases (CO, HC, NOx).
- The "charcoal" is replaced by a **ceramic honeycomb** washcoated with **alumina (Al₂O₃)** carrying **precious metal crystallites**.
- The "scrubbing chemistry" happens at the **atomic surface** of Pt, Pd, and Rh.

Just as the restaurant scrubber has a **finite life** before its charcoal becomes saturated, the catalytic converter has a finite life before its active sites become **worn** (thermally sintered) or **poisoned** (chemically blocked by Pb, S, P, Zn). The "filter" is no longer effective after this — the same way a saturated charcoal filter no longer absorbs odours.

---

## 1.3 Why Catalytic Converters? — The Regulatory Trigger

| Pollutant | Health/Environmental Effect | Source in Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** | Binds haemoglobin; colourless, odourless poison | Incomplete combustion (rich mixtures) |
| **HC (Hydrocarbons)** | Smog, carcinogenic benzene species | Wall quench, crevice volumes, misfire |
| **NOx (NO + NO₂)** | Acid rain, photochemical smog, respiratory irritant | High in-cylinder temperatures (Zeldovich mechanism) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Thermal-NOx Dilemma:** Increasing combustion temperature improves fuel economy but **exponentially increases NOx formation**. Conversely, lowering temperature (EGR, retarded timing) reduces NOx but raises HC and CO. The catalytic converter is the **engine-out → tailpipe-out** clean-up device that allows the engine to be tuned for **best fuel economy** without violating emissions norms.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Catalytic Converters

1. **Two-Way (Oxidation) Catalytic Converter** — oxidises CO and HC; cannot reduce NOx.
2. **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** — simultaneously oxidises CO/HC **and** reduces NOx.
3. **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** — special oxidation catalyst for lean-burn diesels.
4. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** — uses urea injection to reduce NOx (covered in Module 4).
5. **Lean NOx Trap (LNT)** — adsorbs NOx during lean operation, regenerates during rich purge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Two-Way vs Three-Way Converters

## 2.1 Two-Way Catalytic Converter (Oxidation Catalyst)

### Operating Principle
A two-way converter (also called **oxidation catalyst**, OC) performs **only oxidation reactions**. It is positioned in the exhaust stream after the manifold (or close-coupled) where exhaust temperatures are sufficiently high.

### Reactions Promoted

$$
2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO}_2
$$

$$
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Pre-Requisites
- **Excess oxygen must be present** in the exhaust — therefore it is **ideal for diesel engines** (always operate lean) and for **lean-burn gasoline engines**.
- **Cannot reduce NOx** because the chemistry is purely oxidative. In fact, the excess O₂ required would *re-oxidise* any reduced nitrogen back to NOx.

### Catalyst Composition
- **Platinum (Pt)** — primary oxidation activity.
- **Palladium (Pd)** — secondary oxidation, more sulphur-tolerant, lower cost.
- **NO Pt/Rh** — rhodium is not used in a pure oxidation catalyst.

### Applications
- Pre-1990s gasoline vehicles (before TWC dominated).
- **Modern diesel engines** as part of the after-treatment train (DOC + DPF + SCR).

---

## 2.2 Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)

The TWC is the **workhorse of modern gasoline-engine emission control**. It performs **oxidation AND reduction simultaneously** in a single monolith.

### Reactions Promoted

#### Oxidation Reactions (Reduction Catalyst + Air Injection if Rich)
$$
2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO}_2
$$

$$
2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
2\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 25\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 16\text{CO}_2 + 18\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

#### Reduction Reactions
$$
2\text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

$$
2\text{NO} + 2\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{CO}_2
$$

$$
2\text{NO} + 2\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
5\text{H}_2 + 2\text{NO} \longrightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(side reaction, usually suppressed)}
$$

### Catalyst Composition
- **Platinum (Pt)** — oxidation of CO and HC.
- **Palladium (Pd)** — oxidation of HC; thermally robust; modern cost-effective substitute for Pt.
- **Rhodium (Rh)** — **the only practical catalyst that breaks the N–O bond** to release N₂; absolutely essential for NOx reduction. (Rh is the **rarest and most expensive** of the three.)

### The Stoichiometric Requirement — The "Lambda Window"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TWC's Achilles' Heel:** A three-way converter only works efficiently when the engine operates within a **very narrow air-fuel ratio band** centred on the stoichiometric point (A/F = 14.7 for gasoline; λ = 1.0). The acceptable efficiency band is approximately **λ = 0.99 to 1.01** (often called the **"lambda window"**). Outside this band:
> - **Rich (λ < 1):** NOx efficiency is excellent, but CO and HC *increase* because of insufficient O₂.
> - **Lean (λ > 1):** CO and HC efficiency is excellent, but NOx conversion *collapses* because of excess O₂ driving the Le Chatelier equilibrium back toward NO.

This is why **closed-loop air-fuel control with an oxygen (lambda) sensor** is mandatory for any vehicle equipped with a TWC.

---

## 2.3 Physical Construction of a Catalytic Converter

A modern catalytic converter is constructed in three layers:

| Layer | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Substrate (Core)** | Cordierite ceramic honeycomb (2MgO·2Al₂O₃·5SiO₂) OR stainless-steel foil | Provides structural rigidity; vast internal surface area through parallel channels (typical cell density: **400 cpsi** — cells per square inch — can go up to **900 cpsi**) |
| **Washcoat** | γ-Alumina (γ-Al₂O₃) impregnated with **CeO₂, ZrO₂, La₂O₃** (oxygen storage promoters) | High surface area (~**50,000 m² per litre of catalyst volume**); stabilises precious metal particles; **Cerium oxide (CeO₂)** acts as an **oxygen storage buffer** by switching between Ce³⁺ ↔ Ce⁴⁺ |
| **Active Metals (Impregnated)** | Pt, Pd, Rh (total loading ~**1–3 grams per converter**) | Provide active catalytic sites |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total precious metal per converter is typically only 1–3 grams** spread as nano-crystallites over the washcoat. Despite this microscopic quantity, the conversion efficiency exceeds **95%** once the converter is hot.

---

## 2.4 Wear of Catalytic Converters

"Wear" refers to **permanent, irreversible degradation** of catalyst activity.

### 2.4.1 Thermal Degradation (Sintering)
- **Mechanism:** At sustained high temperatures (> **850–900 °C**), the dispersed precious-metal nano-particles **migrate and coalesce** into larger crystallites.
- **Consequence:** Total surface area of active metal drops → activity drops irreversibly.
- **Cause:** Long-duration high-load driving, misfire (raw fuel burns in the catalyst), lean burn before light-off, aftermarket engine modifications.
- **Mathematical description:** Active surface area loss follows an Arrhenius-type relation:

$$
S(t) = S_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\cdot t\right)
$$

where $E_a$ is the sintering activation energy, $R$ the gas constant, $T$ absolute temperature, and $t$ time of exposure.

### 2.4.2 Mechanical Degradation
- Vibrations, thermal cycling fatigue, road debris impact, corrosion of metallic substrate.
- Substrate cracking or washcoat spallation reduces the active area.

### 2.4.3 Chemical Attack
- Sulphur can form stable sulphates (e.g., Al₂(SO₄)₃) that block washcoat pores.

---

## 2.5 Poisoning of Catalytic Converters

A "poison" is a **chemical species that binds strongly and irreversibly to the active metal sites**, rendering them catalytically dead. The converter is poisoned when **a single monolayer** of poison covers the active surface.

### Common Poisons

| Poison | Source | Effect | Reversibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lead (Pb)** | Leaded petrol (now banned in India from 2000) | Forms alloy with Pt; permanently destroys active sites | **Irreversible** |
| **Sulphur (S)** | Sulphur in fuel / lube oil | Competes with reactants for active sites; forms surface sulphates | **Partially reversible** — high-temperature desulphation can recover some activity |
| **Phosphorus (P)** | **Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate (ZDDP)** anti-wear additive in engine oil | Forms glassy phosphate deposits on washcoat; blocks pores | **Largely irreversible** |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | Same ZDDP additive | Co-poisons with P; reduces washcoat porosity | **Irreversible** |
| **Manganese (Mn)** | Older fuel additives (MMT — Methylcyclopentadienyl Manganese Tricarbonyl) | Deposits as MnO₂ on washcoat | **Irreversible** |
| **Silicon (Si)** | Silicone-containing antifreeze leak into combustion chamber | Coats active sites with silica glaze | **Irreversible** |
| **Carbon (C)** | Rich operation, misfire | Cokes the catalyst | **Reversible** — burnt off at high temperature in O₂ |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Leaded-Petrol Legacy:** A single tank of leaded petrol can permanently destroy a modern TWC. The leaded-petrol ban in India (April 2000) is the single most important reason TWC-equipped vehicles became viable in this country. KTU examiners love to test this historical fact.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | $>95\%$ once hot |
| 2 | **Stoichiometric A/F (gasoline)** | $A/F_{\text{stoich}}$ | **14.7 : 1** |
| 3 | **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | φ = 1 stoichiometric |
| 4 | **Lambda (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | λ = 1 stoichiometric; >1 lean; <1 rich |
| 5 | **Lambda Window (TWC)** | $0.99 \leq \lambda \leq 1.01$ | **± 1 % tolerance** |
| 6 | **Space Velocity (SV)** | $SV = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{exhaust}}}{V_{\text{catalyst}}}$ | **30,000 – 100,000 hr⁻¹** |
| 7 | **Light-Off Temperature** | $T_{\text{light-off}}$ | **≈ 250 °C** (TWC); **≈ 200 °C** (DOC) |
| 8 | **Maximum Sustainable Temperature** | $T_{\text{max}}$ | **≈ 800–900 °C** |
| 9 | **Sintering Active Surface Loss** | $S(t) = S_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\cdot t\right)$ | Exponential decay |
| 10 | **Oxygen Storage Capacity (OSC)** | $\Delta n_{\text{O}_2}$ per gram of CeO₂ | Cerium content controls transient λ buffering |
| 11 | **Cell Density (Substrate)** | $N_{\text{cells}}$ per sq. inch | **400 cpsi** (older) → **600–900 cpsi** (modern) |
| 12 | **Precious Metal Loading** | $m_{\text{PM}}$ per converter | **1 – 3 grams** total |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Significance

1. **Closed-Loop ECU Control:** The lambda sensor in the exhaust stream tells the ECU whether the mixture is rich or lean. The ECU then trims the injector pulse width by ± a few percent to keep the engine inside the lambda window. Without this, the TWC is useless.
2. **OBD-II Monitoring:** Since 1996 (India: 2010s for BS-IV), OBD-II monitors the **secondary (post-cat) lambda sensor** to detect catalyst degradation. If the post-cat signal mirrors the pre-cat signal, the catalyst is "dead" and a Check Engine Light is set (P0420).
3. **Unleaded Petrol Mandate:** India's Bharat Stage emission norms are practically impossible to meet with TWC unless **lead-free fuel** is enforced. Lead fouls the catalyst permanently.
4. **Global Warming Trade-off:** Although a TWC reduces toxic CO, HC, and NOx, it increases CO₂ (a greenhouse gas) because the reactions *complete* the combustion of HC and CO that would otherwise leave the engine.

# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Iso-Octane

The reference fuel for stoichiometric calculations is **iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)**, which approximates gasoline. The complete combustion reaction is:

$$
2\,\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 25\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 16\,\text{CO}_2 + 18\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify mass of each reactant per mole of fuel.

Molar mass of C₈H₁₈ = $8(12) + 18(1) = 114$ g/mol.

For 1 kmol of fuel: mass of fuel = **114 kg**.

### Step 2 — Identify moles of O₂ required per mole of fuel.

From the balanced equation, 2 kmol C₈H₁₈ require 25 kmol O₂.

For 1 kmol of C₈H₁₈: moles of O₂ = $25/2 = 12.5$ kmol.

Mass of O₂ = $12.5 \times 32 = 400$ kg.

### Step 3 — Convert to air (air contains ~ 23.2 % O₂ by mass).

Mass of air required = $\dfrac{400}{0.232} = 1724.14$ kg.

### Step 4 — Compute A/F ratio.

$$
(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{1724.14}{114} = 15.12 \;\; (\text{theoretical, pure O}_2\text{-only basis})
$$

Adjusting for the fact that combustion air contains ~ 21 % O₂ by **volume** (not mass) and using molar concentrations correctly gives the accepted textbook value:

$$
(A/F)_{\text{stoich,gasoline}} \approx \mathbf{14.7 : 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slight numerical mismatch arises because of whether mass-fraction or mole-fraction of O₂ in air is used. **Always use 14.7 : 1** in KTU answers; 15.1 is acceptable if the derivation above is shown.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion Efficiency of a Catalytic Converter

The standard measure of catalyst performance is the **conversion efficiency** for each pollutant.

$$
\eta_i = \frac{C_{i,\text{in}} - C_{i,\text{out}}}{C_{i,\text{in}}} \times 100\%
$$

where the subscript $i$ denotes the specific pollutant (CO, HC, or NOx).

### Worked Example
A four-cylinder gasoline engine running on a chassis dynamometer produces the following exhaust concentrations at the inlet and outlet of the TWC:

| Pollutant | Inlet (ppm) | Outlet (ppm) |
|---|---|---|
| CO | 25,000 | 1,250 |
| HC (as C₁) | 1,500 | 75 |
| NOx (as NO) | 1,200 | 60 |

**Solution:**

For CO:
$$
\eta_{\text{CO}} = \frac{25000 - 1250}{25000} \times 100\% = \frac{23750}{25000} \times 100\% = \mathbf{95.0\,\%}
$$

For HC:
$$
\eta_{\text{HC}} = \frac{1500 - 75}{1500} \times 100\% = \frac{1425}{1500} \times 100\% = \mathbf{95.0\,\%}
$$

For NOx:
$$
\eta_{\text{NOx}} = \frac{1200 - 60}{1200} \times 100\% = \frac{1140}{1200} \times 100\% = \mathbf{95.0\,\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **healthy modern TWC** operating at stoichiometric and above light-off temperature should achieve **≥ 95 % conversion** for **all three pollutants simultaneously**. This is the hallmark of a "three-way" catalyst — a two-way converter would show 0 % for NOx.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lambda (λ) Calculation from Exhaust Gas Analysis

Lambda is defined as:

$$
\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

The Bosch LSU 4.9 wide-band lambda sensor outputs a voltage proportional to the **pump current** needed to maintain a stoichiometric equilibrium in the sensor cell. The ECU computes the actual λ from this current.

### Worked Example
An engine is measured to have an actual A/F ratio of 13.0 : 1.

**Solution:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{13.0}{14.7} = 0.884
$$

Since $\lambda < 1$, the mixture is **rich** (excess fuel, deficient air). This means:
- CO and HC will be **higher** at the converter inlet (some fuel did not combust).
- NOx formation in the cylinder will be **lower** (lower peak temperature).
- TWC will reduce NOx well but struggle with CO/HC.

### Inverse Case
If A/F = 16.5 : 1:

$$
\lambda = \frac{16.5}{14.7} = 1.122
$$

Since $\lambda > 1$, the mixture is **lean** (excess air):
- CO and HC at the inlet are **low**.
- NOx formation is **high** (high O₂ and high temperature).
- TWC will oxidise CO/HC well but fail to reduce NOx.

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly why **λ must remain in 0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01** — outside this window, at least one pollutant's conversion efficiency collapses.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Space Velocity and Catalyst Sizing

The **space velocity (SV)** defines how much exhaust gas flows per unit volume of catalyst per hour. It is the primary parameter used by catalyst engineers to size the converter.

$$
SV = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{exhaust}}}{V_{\text{catalyst}}} \quad [\text{hr}^{-1}]
$$

### Worked Example
A 2.0 L gasoline engine produces an exhaust mass flow of $\dot{m}_{\text{ex}} = 0.10$ kg/s. Exhaust density at the converter inlet (≈ 600 °C, 1 atm) is $\rho \approx 0.5$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert mass flow to volumetric flow.**

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.10}{0.5} = 0.20 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 0.20 \times 3600 = 720 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose target space velocity.**

For a TWC, automotive designers target $SV \approx 60{,}000$ hr⁻¹.

**Step 3 — Compute required catalyst volume.**

$$
V_{\text{catalyst}} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{SV} = \frac{720}{60{,}000} = 0.012 \;\; \text{m}^3 = \mathbf{12\; litres}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **typical full-size TWC volume** for a 2.0 L engine. The corresponding converter is roughly a **cylinder 15 cm diameter × 70 cm long**, which fits in the underbody exhaust system.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Rate of Oxygen Storage Release by CeO₂

During a transient rich spike, the cerium oxide stores oxygen as:

$$
2\text{CeO}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Ce}_2\text{O}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2
$$

During a subsequent transient lean spike, it releases oxygen:

$$
\text{Ce}_2\text{O}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CeO}_2
$$

The mass of O₂ released per kg of CeO₂ is:

$$
\frac{\Delta m_{\text{O}_2}}{m_{\text{CeO}_2}} = \frac{0.5 \times 32}{2 \times 172.11} = \frac{16}{344.22} = 0.0465 \;\;\frac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg CeO}_2}
$$

This **4.65 % OSC by mass** is what allows the catalyst to "ride out" brief λ excursions without the tailpipe emissions spiking.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Two-Way vs Three-Way

| Parameter | Two-Way (Oxidation) | Three-Way (TWC) |
|---|---|---|
| Pollutants Treated | CO, HC | CO, HC, **NOx** |
| NOx Reduction Capability | **None** | **Yes (≥ 95 %)** |
| Required Air-Fuel Condition | Lean (excess O₂) | Stoichiometric (λ ≈ 1) |
| Lambda Sensor Required? | No | **Yes (essential)** |
| Engine Application | Diesel, lean-burn petrol | All stoichiometric petrol engines |
| Precious Metals Used | Pt, Pd | Pt, Pd, **+ Rh** |
| Cost (Indicative) | Lower | Higher (Rh is expensive) |
| Light-Off Temperature | ≈ 200 °C | ≈ 250 °C |
| Typical Operating Band | λ ≥ 1.05 | **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01** |
| Reaction Type | Oxidation only | Oxidation + Reduction |

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
**Define a three-way catalytic converter. List the pollutants it controls and the precious metals used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A three-way catalytic converter (TWC) is an exhaust after-treatment device that simultaneously oxidises carbon monoxide (CO) and unburned hydrocarbons (HC), and reduces oxides of nitrogen (NOx) into non-toxic products. It controls **three pollutants**: CO, HC, and NOx. The three precious metals impregnated in the washcoat are **platinum (Pt)** for oxidation of CO and HC, **palladium (Pd)** for HC oxidation, and **rhodium (Rh)** for NOx reduction. Total precious-metal loading is typically 1–3 g per converter, and conversion efficiency exceeds 95 % at operating temperature.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing pollutants, 1 mark for listing metals.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**  
**List any three poisoning agents for catalytic converters and state whether each poisoning is reversible.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Poisoning Agent | Source | Reversibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lead (Pb)** | Leaded petrol | **Irreversible** — forms Pt–Pb alloy |
| 2 | **Sulphur (S)** | Fuel / lubricant | **Partially reversible** — high-temperature desulphation possible |
| 3 | **Phosphorus (P)** | ZDDP anti-wear additive in engine oil | **Largely irreversible** — phosphate deposits block washcoat pores |

*(Mark split: 1 mark per correctly described poison with reversibility statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** *In the End Semester Examination, every Module carries an internal choice — the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each long-answer question is divided into two sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks  
**Explain the construction and working of a three-way catalytic converter with neat sketches. Discuss the role of platinum, palladium, and rhodium in the catalytic reactions.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (3 marks):**

A modern TWC consists of three concentric layers:

- **Stainless-steel shell** — houses the assembly; resists corrosion and thermal shock.
- **Cordierite ceramic honeycomb substrate** (or metallic foil substrate) — provides parallel flow channels with high cell density (400–900 cpsi), giving a large geometric surface area per unit volume.
- **Washcoat** — a porous layer (20–50 µm thick) of γ-alumina (γ-Al₂O₃) impregnated with **cerium oxide (CeO₂)**, **zirconium oxide (ZrO₂)**, and **lanthanum oxide (La₂O₃)**. This layer provides a high surface area (~50,000 m² per litre of catalyst) and an **oxygen-storage buffer** (OSC).
- **Active metal sites** — finely dispersed nano-crystallites of Pt, Pd, and Rh (1–3 nm size, 1–3 g total loading).

**2. Working (2 marks):**

Exhaust gases at 400–800 °C pass through the monolith. Pollutants adsorb onto the active metal sites, undergo oxidation (CO, HC) or reduction (NOx), and desorb as CO₂, H₂O, and N₂. The CeO₂ ↔ Ce₂O₃ redox couple buffers short-term λ excursions.

**3. Role of precious metals (2 marks):**

- **Platinum (Pt):** Highly active for **oxidation** of CO and HC at light-off; fast light-off characteristic.
- **Palladium (Pd):** Excellent for **HC oxidation**; more thermally robust than Pt; cheaper. Often replaces Pt in modern loadings.
- **Rhodium (Rh):** The **only practical metal** for breaking the **N–O bond** to release N₂; essential for NOx reduction. Rare and expensive.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks  
**What is the 'lambda window' of a TWC? Why is closed-loop air-fuel control with a lambda sensor mandatory for TWC-equipped vehicles? Sketch the typical conversion-efficiency vs λ curve.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Lambda window (2 marks):**

The lambda window is the narrow air-fuel-ratio band within which the TWC achieves **simultaneously high conversion (> 95 %)** of CO, HC, and NOx. It is centred on **λ = 1.0** and extends approximately from **λ = 0.99 to λ = 1.01** (i.e., A/F between 14.55 and 14.85).

**2. Why closed-loop control is mandatory (3 marks):**

Without feedback, engine A/F drifts with load, speed, ambient temperature, and altitude, easily leaving the lambda window. Outside this window:

- **Rich (λ < 0.99):** Insufficient O₂ → CO and HC conversion drops sharply; the reactions:
$$
2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO}_2
$$
become oxygen-starved. NOx efficiency remains high.
- **Lean (λ > 1.01):** Excess O₂ favours oxidation but **NOx reduction is poisoned** because the equilibrium favours NO reformation.

Therefore, a **lambda (oxygen) sensor** in the exhaust stream continuously measures λ. The ECU uses this signal to **trim injector pulse width** by ± few percent, holding the engine within the lambda window. Without this, the TWC fails emission norms even though it is mechanically intact.

**3. Sketch of conversion-efficiency vs λ curve (2 marks):**

```
Efficiency (%)
100 |           ___________
    |         /             \
 95 |        /               \
    |       /                 \
  0 |______/___________________\__________
          0.99   1.00   1.01    λ →
              ↑
          Lambda window
   (CO, HC, NOx all > 95 %)
```

A bell-shaped (Gaussian-like) curve with peak at λ = 1.0; sharp drop on either side. The three curves for CO, HC, and NOx overlap only inside the lambda window — this is the entire reason TWC requires stoichiometric operation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks  
**Compare a two-way catalytic converter with a three-way catalytic converter under the following heads: (i) pollutants controlled, (ii) reactions performed, (iii) catalyst composition, (iv) lambda range of operation, (v) typical application, (vi) need for lambda sensor, (vii) cost.**

**Model Answer (tabular format, 7 × 1 = 7 marks):**

| Sl. | Parameter | Two-Way (Oxidation) | Three-Way (TWC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Pollutants controlled | CO, HC only | **CO, HC, and NOx** |
| (ii) | Reactions performed | Oxidation only | Oxidation + Reduction |
| (iii) | Catalyst composition | Pt, Pd | **Pt, Pd, + Rh** |
| (iv) | Lambda range | λ > 1 (lean operation) | **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01** (stoichiometric) |
| (v) | Typical application | Diesel engines, old petrol vehicles | **All modern stoichiometric petrol engines** |
| (vi) | Lambda sensor needed? | **No** | **Yes — mandatory** |
| (vii) | Cost | Lower | Higher (Rh is rare) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks  
**Discuss the various mechanisms by which a catalytic converter loses its activity over time. Include thermal degradation, chemical poisoning, and mechanical failure in your answer.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Thermal Degradation (3 marks):**

- Above 850–900 °C, Pt/Pd/Rh nano-crystallites **sinter** — surface migration and coalescence of particles.
- Active surface area $S(t)$ decreases as $S(t) = S_0 \exp(-E_a t / RT)$.
- Causes: prolonged high-load operation, misfire (raw fuel burns in the catalyst), poor engine tuning.
- **Result:** Permanent loss of catalytic activity.

**2. Chemical Poisoning (3 marks):**

- **Lead (Pb):** Forms Pt–Pb alloy — **irreversible**.
- **Sulphur (S):** Forms surface sulphates; **partially reversible** via high-temperature desulphation.
- **Phosphorus & Zinc (P, Zn):** From ZDDP in engine oil; form phosphate glaze; **irreversible**.
- **Manganese (Mn):** From MMT fuel additive; coats washcoat as MnO₂; **irreversible**.
- **Silicon (Si):** From coolant leak; **irreversible**.
- **Carbon (C):** From rich operation; **reversible** (burns off in O₂ at high T).

**3. Mechanical Failure (1 mark):**

- Substrate cracking from thermal cycling, vibration, road-debris impact.
- Washcoat spallation (peeling off) due to thermal expansion mismatch.
- Resulting in loss of active area and physical disintegration of the monolith.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark-Loss Points**
> 
> 1. **Do NOT confuse equivalence ratio φ with lambda λ.** They are **reciprocals**: $\lambda = 1/\phi$. Examiners deduct marks if you state "λ = (F/A)/(F/A)_stoich" without clarifying that **λ > 1 means lean**.
> 2. **Do NOT claim a two-way converter can reduce NOx** — it cannot, by design. Many students lose 1–2 marks by stating that an oxidation catalyst "treats all three pollutants."
> 3. **Always specify the catalyst metals with their role.** Writing "platinum is used" without explaining *what it catalyses* loses a mark.
> 4. **When discussing poisoning, distinguish reversible vs irreversible.** Examiners specifically check this. Carbon and sulphur poisoning are reversible; lead, phosphorus, and zinc are not.
> 5. **Always draw the bell-shaped λ vs efficiency curve** when asked — a verbal description without a sketch loses up to 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 6. **Numerical problems on conversion efficiency** — show the formula *before* substituting numbers, and clearly state units. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction per occurrence.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ A **catalytic converter** is an exhaust after-treatment device; it is the **third line of defence** after combustion modification and crankcase controls.
- ✅ A **two-way (oxidation) converter** treats only **CO and HC** by oxidation; **cannot reduce NOx**; used in diesels and old/lean-burn petrol engines.
- ✅ A **three-way converter (TWC)** simultaneously oxidises **CO & HC** and reduces **NOx**; uses **Pt + Pd + Rh** on an **alumina + CeO₂ washcoat** over a **cordierite honeycomb substrate**.
- ✅ The **stoichiometric A/F for gasoline is 14.7 : 1**; **λ = 1.0**.
- ✅ **Rhodium is the only practical catalyst** for breaking the N–O bond.
- ✅ The **lambda window** is **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01** — outside this band, at least one pollutant's conversion collapses.
- ✅ **Closed-loop control** with a **lambda sensor** is **mandatory** for TWC operation.
- ✅ **Conversion efficiency** $\eta_i = (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}) / C_{\text{in}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Space velocity** $SV = \dot{Q} / V_{\text{catalyst}}$; typical TWC $SV \approx 60{,}000$ hr⁻¹.
- ✅ **Light-off temperature** ≈ **250 °C** for TWC, **200 °C** for DOC.
- ✅ **CeO₂ / Ce₂O₃** redox couple provides **oxygen-storage capacity (OSC)**, buffering transient λ excursions.
- ✅ **Wear mechanisms:** thermal sintering (irreversible), mechanical fracture, chemical attack.
- ✅ **Poisoning agents:** **Pb** (irreversible), **P & Zn** from ZDDP (irreversible), **S** (reversible), **Mn** (irreversible), **Si** from coolant leak (irreversible), **C** (reversible).
- ✅ **Leaded petrol was banned in India in 2000** because lead permanently destroys TWC.
- ✅ **OBD-II** monitors the post-cat lambda sensor; **P0420** code indicates catalyst efficiency below threshold.
- ✅ Modern TWCs achieve **≥ 95 % conversion** of all three regulated pollutants simultaneously when hot and within the lambda window.
- ✅ Total **precious-metal loading** is just **1–3 grams** per converter — distributed as nano-crystallites over a washcoat of enormous surface area (~50,000 m²/L).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Control for Diesel Fuelled Vehicles: Engine Design, EGR, Crankcase, Evaporative, and Fuel Distribution Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emission Control for Diesel Fuelled Vehicles: Engine Design, EGR, Crankcase, Evaporative, and Fuel Distribution Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Diesel Emission Control

Diesel engines, despite their superior thermal efficiency (typically **35–45%** in modern common-rail units) and lower CO₂ output per kilometre, are inherent sources of **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)** and **Particulate Matter (PM)**. Unlike petrol engines, diesel exhaust contains excess oxygen (lean combustion), making traditional three-way catalytic conversion of NOx far less efficient. Emission control in diesel vehicles therefore requires a multi-pronged **in-cylinder + after-treatment + evaporative** strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Highlight):**
> *Emission control for diesel-fuelled vehicles involves in-cylinder techniques (combustion chamber design, retarded injection timing, EGR), and out-of-cylinder systems (crankcase blow-by control, evaporative emission control, and refuelling/fuel distribution control).*

## 1.2 The Five Pillars of Diesel Emission Control (Module 3 Scope)

| Pillar | Primary Pollutant Targeted | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| Engine Design Modifications | NOx, PM, HC | High (14-mark favourites) |
| Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) | NOx | Very High |
| Crankcase Emission Control | HC (Blow-by) | Moderate |
| Evaporative Emission Control | HC (Fuel vapours) | Moderate |
| Fuel Distribution Control | HC (Refuelling vapours) | Moderate |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Pressure Cooker" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a pressure cooker (your diesel engine) burning fuel on a high flame. The flame is lean and efficient, but it gets VERY hot (high peak cylinder temperature ~2000 K), producing NOx just like a hot stovetop produces burnt smells. To reduce the "burnt smell" (NOx), you (1) modify the burner shape (engine design), (2) mix some already-cooked steam back into the pot (EGR), (3) seal the lid tighter so vapour cannot escape (crankcase/evaporative control), and (4) carefully pour the fuel (fuel distribution control). Each strategy attacks a different escape route of the pollutants.

## 1.4 Key Pollutants from Diesel Engines

- **NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen):** Formed via the Zeldovich mechanism when peak flame temperatures exceed ~1800 K. Diesel NOx is predominantly **NO (90–95%)** with minor NO₂.
- **PM (Particulate Matter / Soot):** Comprises elemental carbon (soot cores), soluble organic fraction (SOF), and sulphates. Modern limit: **4.5 mg/km** (BS-VI).
- **HC (Unburnt Hydrocarbons):** Mainly from over-rich regions, wall-quenching, and crevice volumes.
- **CO:** Naturally low in diesel due to lean operation, but spikes in cold-start and idling.
- **Blow-by:** Combustion gases that leak past piston rings into the crankcase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Trade-off (KTU's most asked concept):**
> *NOx and PM in a diesel engine are inversely related — measures that reduce NOx (e.g., retarded timing) typically increase PM, and vice-versa. Modern BS-VI/Euro-VI systems use EGR + SCR + DPF in a layered strategy to break this trade-off.*

---

## 1.5 Standards & Limits (BS-VI / Euro-VI Reference)

| Pollutant | Diesel Car Limit (BS-VI) | Test Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| NOx | **80 mg/km** | WLTP |
| PM | **4.5 mg/km** | WLTP |
| HC + NOx | **170 mg/km** | WLTP |
| CO | **500 mg/km** | WLTP |
| Evaporative (SHED test) | **1.5 g/test** | SHED |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PILLAR 1: Engine Design Modifications for Diesel Emissions

### 2.1.1 Combustion Chamber Design

**a) Direct Injection (DI) Diesel — Open Combustion Chamber**
- Fuel injected directly into a single chamber formed between the piston crown and cylinder head.
- Fuel–air mixing relies on in-cylinder air motion (swirl, squish, tumble).
- Higher thermal efficiency, lower HC and CO.
- **Higher peak temperatures → higher NOx and noise (mechanical).**

**b) Indirect Injection (IDI) Diesel — Pre-Combustion / Swirl / Pre-Chamber**
- Fuel injected into a separate small chamber connected to the main chamber by a throat.
- Combustion begins in the small chamber and spreads into the main chamber.
- Lower peak temperatures, smoother combustion, **lower NOx**, lower noise.
- Disadvantage: higher fuel consumption, higher HC due to wall quenching.

**c) M-Combustion Chamber (Hiroyasu design)**
- A re-entrant bowl in the piston crown optimised for fuel-air mixing using strong swirl.
- Adopted in almost all modern light-duty direct-injection diesels.

### 2.1.2 Injection System Modifications

- **Higher Injection Pressure (1800–2500 bar in Common Rail):** Atomises fuel into finer droplets → better air entrainment → lower PM, lower soot, but slight NOx rise.
- **Retarded Injection Timing:** Shifts combustion later so peak temperature occurs closer to TDC expansion stroke → reduces peak flame temperature → **reduces NOx by 30–60%**, but increases PM, HC, BSFC, and exhaust smoke.
- **Pilot + Main Injection (Multi-Pulse):** A small pilot dose pre-mixes air before the main combustion → smoother pressure rise → lower noise and lower NOx.
- **Rate Shaped Injection:** Square or "boot"-shaped injection rate profile optimises heat release.

### 2.1.3 Compression Ratio Considerations

Diesel compression ratio is set by combustion requirements (typically **14:1 to 22:1**). Reducing CR lowers peak temperatures and NOx, but raises HC, CO, and BSFC and makes cold starting harder. KTU focus: trade-off question.

### 2.1.4 Turbocharging & Charge Air Cooling

- **Turbocharging** increases mass of air inducted → enables more fuel and power without smoke.
- **Intercooling / Charge Air Cooling (CAC):** Reduces temperature of compressed intake air → lowers peak flame temperature → **reduces NOx (10–20%)**.

### 2.1.5 After-Cooling

Cooling the intake charge after the compressor before the inlet manifold.

---

## 2.2 PILLAR 2: Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)

### 2.2.1 Definition
EGR is the technique of routing a portion of the exhaust gas back into the engine's intake (combustion) air to dilute the fresh charge.

### 2.2.2 Mechanism of NOx Reduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physics of EGR:**
> The exhaust gas contains ~80% inert N₂ and CO₂. When mixed with intake air, this inert gas:
> 1. **Reduces the peak flame temperature** (specific heat capacity of CO₂ ≈ 37 J/mol·K vs air ≈ 29 J/mol·K).
> 2. **Reduces O₂ partial pressure**, slowing the Zeldovich reactions.
> 3. **Increases the specific heat of the charge**, absorbing more energy at peak combustion.

### 2.2.3 Types of EGR

**a) Internal EGR (IEGR)**
- Achieved by leaving the exhaust valve open longer (overlap) so a fraction of exhaust gas re-enters the cylinder during valve overlap.
- No external hardware; cost-effective.
- Limited in control; used as supplementary.

**b) External EGR (EEGR)**
- Dedicated pipe, control valve, and cooler route exhaust gas from the exhaust manifold to the intake manifold.
- **Hot EGR:** No cooler — simple, but raises intake temperature, partially offsetting NOx benefit.
- **Cooled EGR (most common in modern BS-VI diesels):** EGR gas passed through a water-cooled heat exchanger before re-entry. **Reduces NOx by 40–60%** without major PM penalty.

### 2.2.4 EGR Rate (Formula)

The EGR rate is defined as the mass fraction of exhaust gas in the inducted charge:

$$
\text{EGR Rate} = \frac{m_{EGR}}{m_{EGR} + m_{air}} \times 100\%
$$

Typical diesel values:
- Light load: 5–10%
- Medium load: 15–25%
- High load: < 5% (to avoid excessive smoke/PM)

### 2.2.5 EGR Trade-offs
- ↑ EGR → ↓ NOx, but ↑ PM, ↑ HC, ↑ BSFC, ↑ cold-start smoke.
- Excessive EGR can cause misfire-like combustion instability.

---

## 2.3 PILLAR 3: Crankcase Emission Control (Blow-by)

### 2.3.1 Source of Blow-by
Combustion gases (combustion products + unburnt HC) leak past the piston ring-pack into the crankcase. Diesel blow-by is significantly higher than petrol due to higher peak cylinder pressures (80–200 bar).

### 2.3.2 Control System — Closed Crankcase Ventilation (CCV)

**Components:**
1. **Breather / Oil Separator:** Centrifugal + impingement type — removes oil mist from blow-by.
2. **PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) Valve:** Pressure-regulating valve in petrol engines.
3. **Vacuum line to intake manifold:** Draws the filtered blow-by back into the intake for re-combustion.

**Why not vent to atmosphere?**
- Venting releases HC directly to the atmosphere — illegal under BS-VI.
- Re-intake to engine oxidises HC and reduces crankcase pressure.

---

## 2.4 PILLAR 4: Evaporative Emission Control (Diesel-Specific)

### 2.4.1 Source of Diesel Evaporative Emissions
- **Hot-soak losses:** Fuel evaporation from a hot engine after shut-off (highest contribution).
- **Running losses:** Vapour generation during operation (less in diesel than petrol due to lower volatility, but still significant for bio-diesel blends).
- **Diurnal losses:** Temperature swing of the fuel tank over a day (10–15% of total).

### 2.4.2 EVAP System Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Activated Carbon Canister | Adsorbs HC vapours; ~200–500 g carbon |
| Purge Valve | Periodically opens under ECU control during engine run to draw vapours into intake |
| Canister Purge Solenoid | ECU PWM control of purge flow |
| Vapour/Liquid Separator | Prevents liquid fuel reaching the canister |
| Pressure-Vacuum Relief Valve (on tank) | Maintains slight pressure/vacuum to limit vapour escape |
| Fuel Tank Filler Cap | Sealed; small bore to allow refuelling air displacement |

### 2.4.3 SHED Test (Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination)
The vehicle is sealed in a temperature-controlled chamber; mass of HC emitted over a diurnal + hot-soak cycle is measured. BS-VI limit: **1.5 g/test** for diesel cars.

---

## 2.5 PILLAR 5: Fuel Distribution Control (Refuelling / Displacement Losses)

### 2.5.1 The Refuelling Vapour Problem
During refuelling, the incoming liquid fuel displaces an equal volume of saturated fuel vapour from the tank. Without control, these vapours are pushed out through the filler neck and vent — a significant source of HC.

### 2.5.2 Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery (ORVR)

**In ORVR-equipped vehicles:**
- The displaced vapour flows back into the fuel tank.
- The tank is connected to the **carbon canister**.
- The vapour is adsorbed onto activated carbon.
- Once the engine runs, the canister is purged into the intake manifold.

**Key Components:**
- Sealed filler cap with integral valve.
- Vapour return line integrated into the filler neck.
- Large-capacity activated carbon canister.
- ECU-controlled purge solenoid.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on ORVR:**
> ORVR is mandatory under Indian BS-VI for diesel passenger cars and recovers ~95% of refuelling vapours. Stage-II vapour recovery at the fuel station is an alternative/complementary system used in countries without vehicle ORVR.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{EGR Rate} = \dfrac{m_{EGR}}{m_{EGR} + m_{air}} \times 100\%$ | Fraction of exhaust gas in charge |
| 2 | $\text{NOx} \propto \sqrt{P_3 \cdot e^{\frac{-E_a}{R \cdot T_3}}}$ | Zeldovich dependency on peak T₃ and pressure P₃ |
| 3 | $T_3 \propto \dfrac{Q_{in}}{m_{charge} \cdot c_{p,charge}}$ | EGR effect: ↑ cₚ (via CO₂) → ↓ T₃ |
| 4 | $\eta_{th,Brake} = \dfrac{W_b}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | Thermal efficiency of diesel |
| 5 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{W_b}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{blow-by} = f(P_{cyl}, \text{Ring condition}, \Delta P)$ | Blow-by rate; ↑ with cylinder pressure |
| 7 | $m_{HC,evap} = V_{tank} \cdot \rho_{vap}(T) - V_{tank}\cdot \rho_{vap}(T_{ref})$ | Diurnal loss from thermal swing |
| 8 | $\rho_{vap} = \dfrac{P_{sat}(T) \cdot M_{vap}}{R \cdot T}$ | Saturated vapour density (Antoine equation input) |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{refuel,displaced} = \rho_{vap}(T) \cdot \dot{V}_{fuel}$ | Refuelling displacement loss rate |
| 10 | $\text{Smoke Opacity (BSU)} = 100 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Bosch Smoke Units; Hartridge scale |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EGR + SCR + DPF (BS-VI):** All three are *complementary* — EGR controls mid-load NOx, SCR controls high-load NOx, DPF captures PM. No single system can meet BS-VI alone.
- **ORVR adoption** reduces urban refuelling-station HC exposure (benzene, a carcinogen).
- **CCV with multi-stage oil separation** extends oil life and prevents intake-manifold sludge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of EGR on Adiabatic Flame Temperature

**Objective:** Show mathematically why adding EGR reduces peak flame temperature and hence NOx.

**Starting Point:** The Zeldovich thermal NOx formation rate is highly temperature-dependent (Arrhenius term). Therefore the most effective way to reduce NOx is to lower the peak adiabatic flame temperature T_ad.

**Assumption:** A simplified model treats the post-combustion mixture as an ideal gas at constant pressure (approximating the post-flame gases in a diesel at expansion onset).

### Step 1: Energy balance on the charge

The total enthalpy of reactants at the start of combustion equals the total enthalpy of products:

$$
\sum_i n_i h_i^{react}(T_i) = \sum_j n_j h_j^{prod}(T_{ad})
$$

### Step 2: Introducing an inert exhaust-gas diluent (EGR)

Let:
- $n_{air}$ = moles of fresh air inducted
- $n_{EGR}$ = moles of EGR (mostly CO₂ and N₂)
- $n_f$ = moles of fuel (diesel ≈ C₁₂H₂₃)

The charge energy equation becomes:

$$
(n_{air} + n_{EGR} + n_f) \cdot \int_{T_{int}}^{T_{ad}} c_{p,mix}\,dT = n_f \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{comb}
$$

where $c_{p,mix}$ is the mean specific heat of the **mixed products + EGR inerts**.

### Step 3: Effect of EGR on $c_{p,mix}$

Adding CO₂ and N₂ raises the average specific heat of the products because:

$$
c_{p,CO_2} \approx 37\ \text{J/mol·K} \quad \text{vs} \quad c_{p,N_2} \approx 29\ \text{J/mol·K}
$$

So:

$$
c_{p,mix} = \frac{n_{CO_2} c_{p,CO_2} + n_{N_2} c_{p,N_2} + n_{H_2O} c_{p,H_2O} + n_{O_2} c_{p,O_2}}{\sum n_i}
$$

### Step 4: Solving for T_ad

Rearranging the energy balance:

$$
T_{ad} = T_{int} + \frac{n_f \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{comb}}{(n_{air} + n_{EGR} + n_f) \cdot c_{p,mix}}
$$

This shows that $T_{ad}$ is **inversely proportional** to the denominator — adding EGR (n_EGR) increases the denominator and reduces T_ad.

### Step 5: Quantitative result for typical diesel

- Without EGR: $T_{ad} \approx 2400\ \text{K}$
- With 25% EGR (cooled to 350 K): $T_{ad} \approx 2050\ \text{K}$ — a drop of **~350 K**.
- Result: NOx reduces by approximately **40–60%** because the Arrhenius term is exponential:

$$
\frac{[NOx]_{EGR}}{[NOx]_{0}} \approx e^{\frac{-E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ad,EGR}} - \frac{1}{T_{ad,0}}\right)}
$$

Plugging $E_a/R \approx 38{,}000\ \text{K}$ (Zeldovich):

$$
\frac{[NOx]_{EGR}}{[NOx]_0} \approx e^{38000 \times \left(\frac{1}{2050} - \frac{1}{2400}\right)} = e^{38000 \times 9.15 \times 10^{-5}} \approx e^{3.48} \approx 32.4
$$

Wait — this exponent sign needs careful handling. The Zeldovich rate is proportional to $e^{-E_a/(RT)}$, so a lower T gives a *lower* rate. The ratio of rates at the two temperatures is:

$$
\frac{r_{NOx,EGR}}{r_{NOx,0}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_0} - \frac{1}{T_{EGR}}\right)}
$$

$$
= e^{38000 \times \left(\frac{1}{2400} - \frac{1}{2050}\right)} = e^{38000 \times 4.07 \times 10^{-5}} = e^{1.55} \approx 4.7
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The *NOx formation rate* drops by a factor of ~4.7× with 25% cooled EGR, corresponding to a 78–80% reduction in raw NOx. In practice, lower peak temperature also reduces soot oxidation, so PM rises modestly (controlled by DPF). This is why EGR is the cornerstone of in-cylinder NOx control.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem (Model):** A 2.0 L, 4-cylinder common-rail diesel engine runs at 3000 rpm with brake torque 200 N·m and BSFC 240 g/kWh. Calculate the mass of fuel injected per cycle and estimate the blow-by mass flow assuming 1% blow-by fraction.

**Given:**
- $N = 3000\ \text{rpm}$
- $V_d = 2.0\ \text{L}$ (4-cyl, so per cylinder = 0.5 L)
- $T = 200\ \text{N·m}$
- $BSFC = 240\ \text{g/kWh}$

### Step 1: Brake Power

$$
P_b = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000 \times 200}{60} = 62{,}832\ \text{W} = 62.83\ \text{kW}
$$

### Step 2: Fuel flow rate

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{BSFC \times P_b}{1000} = \frac{240 \times 62.83}{1000} = 15.08\ \text{kg/h} = 4.19 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s}
$$

### Step 3: Fuel per cycle (4-stroke → 2 rev/cycle)

$$
\text{Cycles/s} = \frac{N}{2 \times 60} = 25\ \text{cycles/s (4-cyl total)}
$$

$$
m_{f,cycle} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{cycles/s} = \frac{4.19 \times 10^{-3}}{25} = 1.68 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/cycle}
$$

### Step 4: Blow-by mass flow

$$
\dot{m}_{bb} = 0.01 \times \dot{m}_f = 4.19 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/s} = 151\ \text{g/h}
$$

### Step 5: Annual blow-by (typical 1500 h running)

$$
m_{bb,annual} = 151 \times 1500 = 226{,}500\ \text{g} \approx 226.5\ \text{kg/yr}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that for a 4-stroke engine, the number of *firing cycles* per second per cylinder is N/(2×60), not N/60. This halves the per-cycle fuel mass.

---

## 3.3 EGR Rate — Worked Calculation

**Problem:** A diesel engine admits 12 kg/h of fresh air. An EGR valve routes 3 kg/h of exhaust back. Calculate the EGR rate.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{EGR Rate} = \frac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{EGR} + \dot{m}_{air}} \times 100\% = \frac{3}{3 + 12} \times 100\% = 20\%
$$

This 20% EGR falls within the typical medium-load diesel EGR window (15–25%).

---

## 3.4 Carbon Canister Adsorption Capacity (Evaporative Control)

**Problem:** A diesel tank holds 50 L. During a diurnal cycle, temperature swings from 25 °C to 40 °C. Estimate the diurnal HC loss if RVP-equivalent fuel gives saturated vapour pressures $P_{sat,25} = 30\ \text{kPa}$ and $P_{sat,40} = 50\ \text{kPa}$. Assume vapour behaves ideally and displaces out fully.

**Molar mass of vapour $M \approx 100\ \text{g/mol}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$.**

### Step 1: Vapour density at 25 °C (298 K)

$$
\rho_{vap,25} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.100}{8.314 \times 298} = 1.21\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

### Step 2: Vapour density at 40 °C (313 K)

$$
\rho_{vap,40} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 0.100}{8.314 \times 313} = 1.92\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Diurnal HC loss per m³ of headspace

$$
\Delta m = (1.92 - 1.21) \times 1\ \text{m}^3 = 0.71\ \text{kg/m}^3\ \text{of headspace}
$$

### Step 4: Realistic headspace (10% of 50 L tank = 5 L = 0.005 m³)

$$
m_{HC,diurnal} = 0.71 \times 0.005 = 3.55 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg} = 3.55\ \text{g}
$$

This is captured by a standard 250 g carbon canister (which can hold ~50 g of HC at breakthrough).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Integrated Diesel Emission Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Tank] -->|Liquid fuel| B[High Pressure Common Rail Pump]
    B -->|1800 to 2500 bar| C[Injector]
    C -->|Spray into cylinder| D[Combustion Chamber - DI or IDI]
    D -->|Exhaust gases| E[Turbocharger Turbine]
    E -->|High T exhaust| F[Cooled EGR Pipe]
    F -->|Recirculated exhaust| G[Charge Air Cooler]
    G -->|Cool dense charge| D
    E -->|Remaining exhaust| H[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst DOC]
    H -->|Reduces CO and HC| I[Diesel Particulate Filter DPF]
    I -->|Traps PM soot| J[Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR]
    J -->|AdBlue urea injection| K[NOx reduction to N2 and H2O]
    K --> L[Tailpipe]
    D -->|Blow by past rings| M[Crankcase]
    M -->|Oil separator| N[CCV Vent to Intake]
    N --> G
    A -->|Vapour| O[Activated Carbon Canister]
    O -->|Purge to intake| G
    A -->|Refuelling displaced vapour| O
```

## 4.2 EGR Loop Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Exhaust Manifold] -->|Hot exhaust| B[EGR Cooler]
    B -->|Cooled exhaust 100 to 200 C| C[EGR Control Valve ECU PWM]
    C -->|Modulated flow| D[Intake Manifold]
    D --> E[Combustion Chamber]
    E -->|Lower peak T| F[Reduced NOx]
```

## 4.3 Crankcase Blow-by Control (CCV) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Combustion Cylinder] -->|High P gas past rings| B[Crankcase]
    B -->|Oily mist| C[Oil Separator Centrifugal]
    C -->|Cleaned blow by gas| D[PCV Valve or Venturi]
    D -->|Vacuum suction| E[Intake Manifold]
    E --> F[Re combustion in cylinder]
    C -->|Drained oil| G[Oil Sump]
```

## 4.4 Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Tank] -->|Vapour displacement| B[Liquid-Vapour Separator]
    B -->|Vapour| C[Activated Carbon Canister]
    C -->|Adsorbed HC| D[Purge Solenoid PWM]
    D -->|During engine run| E[Intake Manifold]
    F[Tank Filler] -->|ORVR displaced vapour| C
```

## 4.5 NOx vs PM Trade-off Chart (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High EGR] -->|Down arrow NOx| B[NOx Low]
    A -->|Up arrow PM| C[PM High]
    D[Retarded Timing] -->|Down arrow NOx| B
    D -->|Up arrow PM| C
    E[DPF After Treatment] -->|Down arrow PM| C
    F[SCR AdBlue] -->|Down arrow NOx| B
    B --> G[BS VI Compliance]
    C --> G
```

---

## 4.6 Comparison Table — IDI vs DI Diesel Emissions (KTU Favourite Table)

| Parameter | Indirect Injection (IDI) | Direct Injection (DI) |
|---|---|---|
| Combustion Chamber | Pre / Swirl chamber | Piston bowl (M-type) |
| Peak Temperature | Lower | Higher |
| NOx | **Lower** | Higher |
| PM / Smoke | Higher (wall quench) | Lower |
| HC | Higher | **Lower** |
| CO | Higher | **Lower** |
| BSFC | Higher (5–8%) | **Lower** |
| Cold Start | Easier | Harder |
| Noise | **Quieter** | Louder (knock) |
| Fuel Injection Pressure | 100–300 bar | 1000–2500 bar |

---
