<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the working of a Cooled EGR system in a diesel engine. How does it differ from Hot EGR?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Cooled Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) system routes a controlled portion of exhaust gas from the exhaust manifold through a water-cooled heat exchanger (EGR cooler) before it enters the intake manifold. The ECU controls an EGR valve (PWM-actuated) to meter the recirculated mass based on engine speed and load. The cooled exhaust gas (typically cooled to 100–200 °C from ~500 °C) has a higher density and, importantly, does not heat the intake charge. This maximises the inert-dilution effect on peak flame temperature, achieving **40–60% NOx reduction** with minimal PM and BSFC penalty.

In **Hot EGR**, the exhaust gas is recirculated *without* cooling. The intake charge temperature rises, partially offsetting the NOx benefit because the higher charge temperature increases peak combustion temperature. Hot EGR is simpler and cheaper but less effective.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "EGR reduces temperature" but forget the *thermodynamic* reason — the increased specific heat of the CO₂ + N₂ mixture absorbs more energy at peak combustion. Just stating "dilutes the charge" is worth only 1 mark; mention the *specific heat* and *peak temperature* arguments for full marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is crankcase blow-by? Describe the closed crankcase ventilation (CCV) system used in diesel engines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Crankcase blow-by is the leakage of high-pressure combustion gases (HC, CO, NOx, PM precursors) past the piston ring-pack into the crankcase. Diesel engines generate higher blow-by than petrol engines due to higher peak cylinder pressures (80–200 bar) and longer combustion durations.

A **Closed Crankcase Ventilation (CCV) system** recovers these gases instead of venting them. The system comprises:
1. A **centrifugal / impingement oil separator** that removes oil droplets from the blow-by gas (returning oil to the sump).
2. A **pressure-regulating valve** (or venturi) that maintains crankcase pressure slightly below atmospheric.
3. A **vent line** to the intake manifold, where the cleaned blow-by gas is re-inducted and re-burned in the cylinder.

CCV reduces HC emissions to ~zero, extends oil life, and prevents pressure build-up in the crankcase.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** Explain with neat sketches the working of an **External Cooled EGR system** for a diesel engine. Discuss the effect of EGR rate on NOx, PM, BSFC, and smoke. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of External Cooled EGR:**

In an external EGR system, a calibrated amount of exhaust gas is drawn from the exhaust manifold (upstream of the turbocharger in some layouts, downstream in others) and routed through a pipe fitted with:

1. **EGR Cooler:** A water-cooled shell-and-tube or gas-to-liquid heat exchanger that drops the exhaust gas temperature from 400–600 °C to 100–200 °C.
2. **EGR Control Valve:** An ECU-actuated solenoid/pneumatically controlled throttling valve (or a DC motor-driven rotary valve) that modulates EGR flow.
3. **EGR Differential Pressure Sensor (Δp):** Measures pressure drop across a venturi to calculate actual EGR mass flow.
4. **EGR Temperature Sensor:** Confirms flow and detects cooler malfunction.
5. **EGR Valve Position Sensor:** Feedback for closed-loop control.

The cooled EGR gas enters the intake manifold (downstream of the throttle in petrol, or directly into the boost pipe in diesel) and mixes with fresh compressed air. The resulting lean + inert mixture burns at a lower peak temperature.

**Effect of EGR rate (table for 7 marks):**

| EGR Rate | NOx | PM | BSFC | Smoke (BSU) | Combustion Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0% | Baseline | Baseline | Baseline | Baseline | Excellent |
| 10% | −20% | +5% | +2% | +10% | Excellent |
| 20% | −40% | +15% | +5% | +30% | Good |
| 30% | −55% | +35% | +10% | +70% | Marginal |
| >35% | −60% | +80% | +18% | Excessive | Misfire / very rough |

**Key conclusions:**
- NOx drops sharply with EGR (up to ~30–35% rate).
- PM and smoke rise non-linearly above 25% EGR.
- ECU de-rates EGR at high loads to control smoke.
- Modern BS-VI systems cap EGR at ~25% and use SCR for residual NOx.

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery (ORVR)** system in a diesel vehicle with a block diagram. Compare it with stage-II vapour recovery at the fuel station. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ORVR System Description:**

During refuelling, the liquid fuel entering the tank displaces an equal volume of saturated fuel vapour. In conventional (pre-ORVR) systems, this vapour escapes through the filler neck to the atmosphere — a major source of HC (especially benzene) exposure to refuellers and the environment.

**ORVR components:**
1. **Sealed filler cap** with an integral pressure-vacuum relief valve.
2. **Co-axial or integrated vapour return path** in the filler neck assembly.
3. **Vapour/liquid separator** at the tank inlet.
4. **Large activated carbon canister** (typically 1.5–2.5 L, 250–500 g carbon).
5. **Purge control solenoid** (PWM, ECU-controlled).
6. **Canister vent filter** (HEPA-style to prevent dust ingestion).
7. **Tunnel/filler pipe check valve** to prevent back-flow.

**Operation:**
- During refuelling, displaced tank vapour is forced through the vapour return line into the carbon canister, where HC is adsorbed.
- During engine operation, the ECU opens the purge valve under defined conditions (coolant temp > 70 °C, vehicle speed > 5 km/h, etc.). Intake vacuum draws air through the canister, desorbing the HC into the intake manifold for combustion.
- Approximately **95% of refuelling vapours** are recovered.

**Comparison with Stage-II Recovery (at the fuel station):**

| Feature | ORVR (Vehicle-side) | Stage-II Recovery (Station-side) |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Onboard the vehicle | At the dispensing nozzle/pump |
| Cost | Borne by vehicle manufacturer | Borne by oil company / station |
| Efficiency | ~95% (consistent) | 70–85% (depends on station maintenance) |
| Consumer benefit | Yes — works at any pump | No — only at equipped pumps |
| Standard | Mandatory under BS-VI (India), US EPA | Indian cities mandate in metros |
| Fuel flow impact | Negligible | Slightly slower dispensing |

KTU Point: In India, BS-VI mandates ORVR on the vehicle side; stations are progressively adopting stage-II recovery in parallel.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** Compare **Direct Injection (DI) and Indirect Injection (IDI) diesel engines** with respect to combustion chamber design, fuel injection, NOx, PM, BSFC, and noise. Which is preferred for modern light-duty vehicles? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DI Diesel | IDI Diesel |
|---|---|---|
| Chamber type | Single open chamber + piston bowl (M-type) | Pre-chamber, swirl chamber, or pre-combustion chamber |
| Mixing mechanism | In-cylinder air motion (swirl, squish) | Combustion in pre-chamber + jet into main |
| Injection pressure | 1000–2500 bar (common rail) | 100–300 bar (rotary/inline pump) |
| Nozzle type | Multi-hole (5–9 holes) | Pintle / single-hole |
| NOx | Higher (higher T_peak) | **Lower** (~30% less) |
| PM / Smoke | Lower (better air utilisation) | Higher (wall quenching) |
| HC | Lower | Higher |
| BSFC | **Lower by 5–10%** | Higher |
| Cold start | Harder (lower compression in IDI is offset by pre-chamber) | Easier |
| Noise (diesel knock) | **Louder** (~5–8 dB higher) | Quieter |
| Thermal efficiency | **Higher (38–45%)** | Lower (33–38%) |
| Emissions compliance | Needs EGR + SCR + DPF | Inherently low NOx |

**Modern preference:** **DI is preferred for light-duty vehicles** because:
1. BSFC is lower → better fuel economy and lower CO₂ per km.
2. Modern common-rail injection at 2000+ bar reduces smoke to BS-VI levels.
3. Higher NOx is managed with EGR + SCR (after-treatment).
4. Higher noise is mitigated with pilot injection and refined calibration.

> [!NOTE]
> IDI is essentially obsolete in passenger cars but persists in some heavy-duty off-road applications and older small-displacement diesels where ultra-low NOx is critical and fuel economy is secondary.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **sources of evaporative emissions** in a diesel vehicle. With a block diagram, describe the **EVAP system** and explain how the SHED test measures evaporative HC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sources of Evaporative Emissions:**

1. **Hot-Soak Losses (highest in diesel):** After engine shut-off, residual heat soaks the fuel rail, lines, and tank. Fuel vaporises and accumulates in the headspace. Approximately 50–60% of total evaporative emissions.
2. **Running Losses:** Vapour generated during operation as fuel circulates through the warm fuel rail. Minor in conventional diesel; significant in bio-diesel blends (B20, B100) due to higher volatility of bio-components.
3. **Diurnal Losses:** Ambient temperature swings (e.g., 25 °C day → 18 °C night) cause vapour pressure cycles. Vapour expands in heat and is pushed out of the tank vent. About 20–30% of total.
4. **Refuelling Losses:** Discussed in Q-A(b). Adds another 10–20% if ORVR is absent.

**EVAP System Block Diagram (functional description):**

```
Fuel Tank → Vapour/Liquid Separator → Carbon Canister → Purge Solenoid → Intake Manifold
Tank Filler (ORVR) → Carbon Canister
Atmosphere → Canister Vent (filtered)
```

**Working:**
- Vapour generated in the tank flows to the **activated carbon canister** and is adsorbed.
- The **purge valve** is normally closed (sealed by spring + ECU).
- Once the engine reaches operating temperature, the ECU pulses the purge solenoid. Intake manifold vacuum draws fresh air through the canister, desorbing HC and routing it to the engine for combustion.
- The canister is regenerated continuously during driving.

**SHED Test (Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination):**

The vehicle is parked in a sealed, temperature-controlled chamber (SHED) of fixed volume. The test cycle has three phases:
1. **Diurnal phase:** Chamber temperature cycled from 20 °C to 35 °C over 12 hours.
2. **Hot-soak phase:** Engine run to warm-up, then parked for 1 hour at 35 °C.
3. **Measurement phase:** HC concentration in the SHED is measured by FID (Flame Ionisation Detector). Total HC mass emitted is calculated from concentration × SHED volume.

**BS-VI limit:** **1.5 g/test** for diesel passenger vehicles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing HC reduction mechanisms** — Students often attribute HC reduction in DI engines to EGR. EGR is for NOx; HC reduction comes from better atomisation (high injection pressure) and combustion chamber design.
> 2. **Mixing up IDI and DI** — IDI does NOT mean "Indirect Ignition"; it means "Indirect Injection." The fuel is ignited by compression heat in both cases.
> 3. **Forgetting the NOx–PM trade-off explicitly** — Any EGR / timing / chamber answer that does not mention this trade-off loses at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing "ORVR = Stage II"** — They are alternatives (vehicle-side vs station-side), not the same.
> 5. **No numerical backup** — A 14-mark answer without even one formula (e.g., EGR rate) is marked down under the "engineering analysis" criterion.
> 6. **Skipping sensors in EGR** — Always mention EGR Δp sensor, temp sensor, and position sensor for full hardware credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Engine Design Modifications
- DI vs IDI: DI better BSFC, higher NOx; IDI lower NOx, higher BSFC.
- Retarded timing: ↓ NOx, ↑ PM, ↑ BSFC.
- Higher injection pressure: ↓ PM, marginal ↑ NOx.
- Pilot + main injection: ↓ noise, smoother combustion.
- Charge air cooling: ↓ NOx by 10–20%.
- M-type combustion chamber is industry standard for DI diesels.

### ✅ EGR
- EGR Rate = $m_{EGR} / (m_{EGR} + m_{air}) \times 100\%$.
- Cooled EGR > Hot EGR (NOx benefit).
- Typical: 5% at high load, 20–25% at mid load, 5–10% at light load.
- Mechanism: ↑ $c_p$ of charge (via CO₂) → ↓ T_ad → ↓ Zeldovich NOx.
- Internal EGR: via valve overlap; supplementary.

### ✅ Crankcase Blow-by / CCV
- Closed system mandatory under BS-VI.
- Components: oil separator + venturi/PCV + vent to intake.
- Re-inducted → re-burned → no HC vent.

### ✅ Evaporative (EVAP) System
- Sources: hot-soak (major), diurnal, running, refuelling.
- Carbon canister adsorbs; purge valve desorbs.
- SHED test: 1.5 g/test limit (BS-VI).
- Components: canister, purge solenoid, separator, pressure-vacuum relief.

### ✅ Fuel Distribution Control / ORVR
- Refuelling displacement loss addressed by ORVR.
- 95% recovery efficiency.
- Mandatory on vehicle side under BS-VI.
- Stage-II at pump is an alternative.

### ✅ KTU Buzzwords to Use in Answers
- *Zeldovich mechanism*, *specific heat capacity of CO₂*, *adiabatic flame temperature*, *NOx–PM trade-off*, *in-cylinder + after-treatment integration*, *SHED test*, *ORVR*, *CCV*, *M-combustion chamber*, *common-rail*, *pilot injection*.

### ✅ Memorise These Numbers
- Compression ratio of diesel: **14:1 to 22:1**.
- Common-rail pressure: **1800–2500 bar**.
- NOx reduction by cooled EGR: **40–60%**.
- BS-VI NOx limit: **80 mg/km**; PM: **4.5 mg/km**.
- SHED HC limit: **1.5 g/test**.
- Carbon canister mass: **250–500 g** activated carbon.
- Peak cylinder pressure (diesel): **80–200 bar**.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — PEAUT522 Emission Control for Diesel Fuelled Vehicles.**

topics.title: Exhaust After-Treatment: Particulate Trap, Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC), and Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exhaust After-Treatment: Particulate Trap, Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC), and Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) / Particulate Trap

A **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** is a wall-flow type ceramic (typically cordierite or silicon carbide) exhaust after-treatment device designed to physically trap and store **Particulate Matter (PM)** — primarily soot and the **Soluble Organic Fraction (SOF)** — emitted from a diesel engine. It achieves filtration efficiencies of **> 99%** for particles in the regulated size range of **10 nm to 2.5 µm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A particulate trap is a device fitted in the exhaust line of a diesel engine that captures soot (PM) emitted during combustion, preventing it from being released into the atmosphere. It must periodically "regenerate" — burn off the accumulated soot — to maintain function.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the DPF like a **mesh tea strainer catching tea leaves**. As you pour tea, the strainer holds back the leaves while letting the liquid pass. Over time, the strainer fills up and needs to be cleaned. Similarly, the DPF holds back soot while letting exhaust gases flow through, and periodically needs "regeneration" to burn off the trapped soot.

## 1.2 Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)

A **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** is a flow-through ceramic honeycomb substrate (cordierite) coated with a **platinum (Pt) and palladium (Pd)** washcoat, often combined with zeolites. It oxidizes **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Hydrocarbons (HC)**, and the **Soluble Organic Fraction (SOF)** of particulate matter into **CO₂ and H₂O**. It also generates **NO₂**, which is used downstream by the DPF to enable **passive regeneration** (Continuous Regeneration Trap — CRT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOC Light-Off Temperature: ~200 °C to 250 °C.** Below this temperature, the catalyst is essentially inactive. This is why DOCs are always mounted closest to the engine (upstream of DPF/SCR).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine the DOC as a **"chemical furnace" inside the exhaust pipe**. When hot exhaust gases pass through it, the precious metal coating acts like a matchstick that ignites the harmful gases (CO and HC) and turns them into harmless ones (CO₂ and H₂O), just like a match burns paper into ash.

## 1.3 Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF) — AdBlue

**Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF)**, commercially marketed as **AdBlue** (ISO 22241 standard), is a **32.5 % by mass aqueous urea solution** in **67.5 % deionized water**. It is injected into the exhaust stream upstream of a **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** catalyst, where it thermally decomposes into **ammonia (NH₃)**, which then reduces **NOx** to harmless **N₂ and H₂O**.

> [!NOTE]
> **DEF Properties:** Density ≈ **1.09 g/cm³** at 20 °C; Freezing Point = **−11 °C**; pH ≈ **9 to 10** (alkaline). It is non-toxic, non-flammable, and safe to handle.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of DEF as the **"medicine" the engine takes to cure its NOx problem**. The exhaust is "sick" with NOx pollution, and DEF (urea) provides the "antidote" (ammonia) that neutralizes the NOx, turning it into clean nitrogen and water — just like medicine cures a fever.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Synergy Note:** In a modern **BS-VI / Euro-VI** diesel after-treatment system, the typical exhaust line-up is:
> **Engine → DOC → DPF → SCR (with DEF injection) → NH₃ Slip Catalyst → Tailpipe**
> Each device has a specific role, and the synergy between them enables simultaneous reduction of **CO, HC, PM, and NOx** to meet the stringent **NOx ≤ 0.46 g/kWh and PM ≤ 0.01 g/kWh** limits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Construction
A wall-flow DPF consists of a ceramic honeycomb with alternating **plugged channels**: every other cell is plugged at the inlet and outlet. Exhaust enters an open channel, is forced through the **porous ceramic wall**, and exits through an adjacent channel. The porous wall acts as the filter medium.

### 2.1.2 Filtration Mechanisms
1. **Depth Filtration:** Soot particles deposit within the wall pores (during initial stages).
2. **Cake (Surface) Filtration:** A soot "cake" builds up on the wall surface, acting as a finer filter.

### 2.1.3 Pressure Drop & Loading
As soot accumulates, the **back pressure (ΔP)** across the DPF increases, which increases **pumping losses** and **BSFC**. Once ΔP crosses a threshold (typically **8–10 kPa**), regeneration is triggered.

### 2.1.4 Regeneration Methods
| Method | Trigger | Mechanism | DPF Temp Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive (CRT)** | Continuous | NO₂ from DOC oxidizes soot at low T | **250–400 °C** |
| **Active – Burner** | ΔP threshold | In-exhaust burner ignites fuel | **> 600 °C** |
| **Active – Post-Injection** | ΔP threshold | HC injected into cyl., DOC oxidizes | **> 600 °C** |
| **Forced Air Injection** | ΔP threshold | Air injected into exhaust stream | **> 600 °C** |

## 2.2 Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC) — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Key Chemical Reactions
- **CO Oxidation:** $\mathrm{2CO + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2}$
- **HC Oxidation:** $\mathrm{C_xH_y + (x + y/4)O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + (y/2)H_2O}$
- **SOF Oxidation:** SOF (high-BP HC adsorbed on soot) is burned off in the **250–350 °C** range.
- **NO → NO₂:** $\mathrm{NO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow NO_2}$ (Critical for passive DPF regen)

> [!WARNING]
> **Sulfur Poisoning:** Diesel fuel sulfur forms sulfates (e.g., $\mathrm{ZnSO_4}$) that coat the DOC washcoat, **permanently deactivating** it. This is why **ULSD (Ultra-Low Sulfur Diesel, ≤ 10 ppm S)** is mandatory for modern after-treatment systems.

### 2.2.2 DOC Conversion Efficiency
$$\eta_{DOC} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$$
where $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are the inlet and outlet pollutant concentrations (ppm or g/m³).

## 2.3 Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF) & SCR — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Thermolysis & Hydrolysis of Urea
When DEF is sprayed into the hot exhaust (≥ **180 °C**), the following reactions occur:
- **Thermolysis:** $\mathrm{(NH_2)_2CO \rightarrow NH_3 + HNCO}$
- **Hydrolysis:** $\mathrm{HNCO + H_2O \rightarrow NH_3 + CO_2}$
- **Net:** $\mathrm{(NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \rightarrow 2NH_3 + CO_2}$

### 2.3.2 SCR Reactions (over Cu-Zeolite or V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂ catalyst)
- **Standard SCR (dominant):** $\mathrm{4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O}$
- **Fast SCR (when NO:NO₂ = 1:1):** $\mathrm{2NO + 2NO_2 + 4NH_3 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O}$
- **NO₂ SCR:** $\mathrm{6NO_2 + 8NH_3 \rightarrow 7N_2 + 12H_2O}$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DPF Filtration Efficiency | $\eta_{PM} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}}{\dot{m}_{in}} \times 100$ | **> 99 %** for DPF |
| 2 | DOC Conversion Efficiency | $\eta_{X} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | **70–95 %** (CO, HC) |
| 3 | SCR NOx Conversion | $\eta_{NO_x} = \dfrac{[NO_x]_{in} - [NO_x]_{out}}{[NO_x]_{in}} \times 100$ | **80–95 %** |
| 4 | DEF Dosing Ratio (mass) | $\dot{m}_{DEF} / \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | **2–6 %** (BS-VI) |
| 5 | Urea → NH₃ Mass Conversion | $1 \text{ kg urea} \rightarrow 0.567 \text{ kg NH}_3$ | Stoichiometric |
| 6 | NH₃ : NOx Molar Ratio | $n_{NH_3}/n_{NO_x}$ | **1.0–1.05** (optimal) |
| 7 | DPF Soot Loading Limit | $m_{soot}/V_{DPF}$ | **5–15 g/L** |
| 8 | DPF Back Pressure (clean) | $\Delta P_0$ | **< 2 kPa** |
| 9 | DPF Back Pressure (regen trigger) | $\Delta P_{max}$ | **8–10 kPa** |
| 10 | DEF Consumption | $C_{DEF} = \dfrac{0.567 \times [NO_x] \times \dot{V}_{exh}}{C_{urea}}$ | kg/hr |
| 11 | NOx Molar Mass | $M_{NO_x} = 46 \text{ g/mol}$ (as NO₂ eq.) | g/mol |
| 12 | DEF Density | $\rho_{DEF}$ | **1.09 g/cm³** at 20 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> The "**0.567**" factor in DEF dosing comes from: 1 mole of urea (60 g) yields 2 moles of NH₃ (34 g) → $34/60 = 0.5667 \approx 0.567$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
These systems are mandatory under **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms in India (since April 2020) and **Euro-VI** in Europe. The DPF + DOC + SCR combination is the **only practical technological pathway** to simultaneously meet PM, NOx, CO, and HC emission limits on heavy-duty and light-duty diesel vehicles without significant engine performance penalties.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric DEF Consumption Rate

**Problem Setup:** A heavy-duty diesel engine has:
- Fuel consumption $\dot{m}_f = 25$ kg/hr
- NOx emission (engine-out) = **5.0 g/kWh**
- Engine brake power $P_b = 200$ kW
- Target SCR conversion efficiency = **90 %**
- DEF = 32.5 % urea solution (by mass)

**Step 1 — Engine-out NOx mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{NO_x,out} = 5.0 \frac{\text{g}}{\text{kWh}} \times 200 \text{ kW} = 1000 \text{ g/hr} = 1.0 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2 — NOx to be reduced (90 % conversion):**

$$\dot{m}_{NO_x,reduced} = 1.0 \times 0.90 = 0.90 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Convert NOx mass to moles (using M(NO₂) = 46 g/mol):**

$$n_{NO_x} = \frac{900 \text{ g/hr}}{46 \text{ g/mol}} = 19.565 \text{ mol/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric NH₃ needed (1:1 mole ratio with NO):**

$$n_{NH_3} = 19.565 \text{ mol/hr} \times 17 \text{ g/mol} = 332.6 \text{ g/hr} = 0.3326 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Convert NH₃ mass to urea mass (1 mole urea → 2 moles NH₃):**

$$\dot{m}_{urea} = \frac{0.3326}{0.567} = 0.5866 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to DEF mass (urea is 32.5 % of DEF):**

$$\dot{m}_{DEF} = \frac{0.5866}{0.325} = 1.805 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 7 — DEF-to-fuel ratio:**

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{DEF}}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{1.805}{25} = 0.0722 = 7.22\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (~7 %) is in the upper range of typical BS-VI DEF dosing ratios (**2–8 %**), confirming the calculation is realistic for a heavy-duty engine.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DPF Soot Loading and Back-Pressure Relationship

**Empirical Relation (commonly used in industry):**

$$\Delta P = \Delta P_0 \cdot \left(1 + \alpha \cdot \frac{m_{soot}}{m_{cap}}\right)^{\beta}$$

where:
- $\Delta P_0$ = clean DPF back pressure (**~2 kPa**)
- $m_{soot}$ = accumulated soot mass (grams)
- $m_{cap}$ = DPF trap capacity (grams)
- $\alpha, \beta$ = empirical constants (typically $\alpha = 0.7, \beta = 2$ for wall-flow SiC)

**Worked Example:** DPF with $m_{cap} = 80$ g, $\alpha = 0.7, \beta = 2$, currently loaded with 60 g of soot.

$$\Delta P = 2 \times \left(1 + 0.7 \times \frac{60}{80}\right)^2$$

$$\Delta P = 2 \times \left(1 + 0.7 \times 0.75\right)^2 = 2 \times \left(1.525\right)^2$$

$$\Delta P = 2 \times 2.326 = 4.65 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** Since 4.65 kPa < 8 kPa (regen trigger), the DPF is **not yet full** and can continue accumulating soot. However, when $m_{soot}$ reaches approximately 90 g, the back-pressure will exceed 8 kPa, triggering active regeneration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: NO₂-Based Passive Regeneration of DPF

The continuous regeneration trap (CRT) reaction is:

$$\mathrm{2NO_2 + C \rightarrow 2NO + CO_2}$$

**Step 1 — Mass basis check:** 1 mole of NO₂ (46 g) oxidizes 1 mole of C (12 g) of soot.

$$\text{Soot oxidation capacity} = \frac{12 \text{ g C}}{46 \text{ g NO}_2} = 0.261 \text{ g C per g NO}_2$$

**Step 2 — Required NO₂:** If the engine emits 1.0 g/kWh of NOx (50 % converted to NO₂ by DOC), then:

$$\dot{m}_{NO_2} = 0.5 \text{ g/kWh} = \text{soot oxidized} = 0.5 \times 0.261 = 0.1305 \text{ g C / kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with engine-out PM:** A typical diesel emits **0.10–0.30 g/kWh** of PM. The DOC + CRT system can burn up to **0.13 g/kWh**, which suffices for **passive regeneration** under normal driving conditions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of After-Treatment Devices

| Parameter | DPF | DOC | SCR (with DEF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Trap PM | Oxidize CO, HC, SOF | Reduce NOx |
| **Type** | Wall-flow ceramic | Flow-through honeycomb | Flow-through catalyst |
| **Active Material** | Cordierite / SiC | Pt + Pd washcoat | Cu-Zeolite or V₂O₅/WO₃ |
| **Light-Off Temp** | N/A (passive device) | **200–250 °C** | **200 °C** |
| **Operating Temp** | Up to 1000 °C (regen) | 200–800 °C | 200–500 °C |
| **Pressure Drop** | **2–10 kPa** | < 1 kPa | < 1.5 kPa |
| **Regeneration Needed?** | **Yes** (burn soot) | No | No (but DEF refilling) |
| **Failure Mode** | Ash clogging, cracking | Sulfur poisoning, thermal aging | DEF crystallization, HC slip |
| **BS-VI Limit Addressed** | PM ≤ 0.01 g/kWh | CO ≤ 1.5 g/kWh | NOx ≤ 0.46 g/kWh |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete BS-VI Diesel Exhaust After-Treatment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[DIESEL ENGINE] --> DOC1[DOC: Oxidation of CO and HC]
    DOC1 --> DPF1[DPF: Particulate Trap with Soot Accumulation]
    DPF1 --> SCR1[SCR CATALYST: NOx Reduction]
    DEF1[DEF TANK AdBlue Urea 32.5 percent] --> INJ1[DEF INJECTOR METERING MODULE]
    INJ1 --> SCR1
    SCR1 --> ASC1[AMMONIA SLIP CATALYST: Removes Excess NH3]
    ASC1 --> TP1[TAILPIPE: Clean Exhaust]

    style ENG fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DOC1 fill:#A0D8B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DPF1 fill:#F4A6A6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SCR1 fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ASC1 fill:#C5A6E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DEF1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style INJ1 fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TP1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Flow:** Hot exhaust gases leave the diesel engine → enter the DOC (oxidizes CO, HC, generates NO₂) → enter the DPF (traps soot; NO₂ from DOC helps burn soot passively) → enter the SCR (DEF injected before it decomposes to NH₃, which reduces NOx) → ammonia slip catalyst catches any unused NH₃ → clean exhaust exits.

---

## 4.2 DPF Regeneration Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Running] --> B[Measure DPF Back Pressure]
    B --> C{Delta P greater than 8 kPa?}
    C --> NO[No: Continue Normal Operation] --> A
    C --> YES[Yes: Regeneration Required] --> D{Exhaust Temp greater than 600 C?}
    D --> NO2[No: Use Active Burner or Post Injection] --> E[Raise Exhaust Temp] --> F[Initiate Active Regeneration]
    D --> YES2[Yes: Passive CRT] --> G[NO2 from DOC Oxidizes Soot]
    F --> H[Soot Burned Off: Delta P Drops]
    G --> H
    H --> A
    style A fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#B0F0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 DEF Decomposition & SCR Reduction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DEF1[DEF Solution 32.5 percent Urea in Water] --> T1[Thermolysis at 180 to 350 C]
    T1 --> A1[NH3 plus HNCO]
    A1 --> H1[Hydrolysis HNCO plus H2O]
    H1 --> A2[2NH3 plus CO2]
    A2 --> SCR1[SCR Catalyst Bed]
    NOX1[Engine NOx: NO and NO2] --> SCR1
    SCR1 --> P1[N2 plus H2O Clean Output]

    style DEF1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T1 fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H1 fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SCR1 fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NOX1 fill:#F4A6A6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P1 fill:#B0F0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**What is a Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)? List any two methods used for its regeneration.**

**Model Answer:**
A Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) is a wall-flow type ceramic exhaust after-treatment device used in diesel engines to trap particulate matter (soot and SOF) emitted during combustion. It achieves filtration efficiencies of over 99 % for particles in the 10 nm to 2.5 µm range.

**Two methods of regeneration:**
1. **Passive Regeneration (CRT):** NO₂ generated by the upstream DOC continuously oxidizes the trapped soot at temperatures of 250–400 °C without any external intervention.
2. **Active Regeneration:** Initiated when back-pressure exceeds ~8 kPa. The exhaust temperature is raised to > 600 °C by **post-injection of fuel** (hydrocarbon-based active regeneration) or via an **in-exhaust burner**, which then oxidizes (burns) the accumulated soot.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**What is Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF)? State its composition and one important property.**

**Model Answer:**
Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF), also marketed as **AdBlue**, is an aqueous urea solution used in **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** systems to reduce NOx emissions from diesel engines.

**Composition:** DEF contains **32.5 % urea [(NH₂)₂CO] and 67.5 % deionized water** by mass, as per the **ISO 22241** standard.

**Important Property:** DEF has a **freezing point of −11 °C**, so in cold-climate operation, vehicles use a heated DEF tank and dosing line to prevent freezing. DEF is also **non-toxic, non-flammable, and non-hazardous**, making it safe to handle. Its density is **1.09 g/cm³ at 20 °C**.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** along with the key chemical reactions involved.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A heavy-duty diesel engine produces 1.5 kg/hr of NOx. Calculate the **mass of DEF (32.5 % urea) required per hour** if the SCR system achieves 90 % conversion efficiency. Use the standard SCR reaction: $\mathrm{4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O}$. *(Molar masses: Urea = 60, NH₃ = 17, NO = 30)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — DOC Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram & Explanation (4 Marks):**

A **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** is a **flow-through honeycomb ceramic substrate** (cordierite), typically wash-coated with **platinum (Pt) and palladium (Pd)**. The washcoat may also include zeolites for HC trapping. The DOC is mounted **closest to the engine** so that it sees the hot exhaust first.

**Working Principle:**
1. Hot exhaust gases (**200–800 °C**) from the engine enter the DOC inlet.
2. As the gases contact the Pt/Pd washcoat, the precious metals act as catalysts and **accelerate oxidation reactions**.
3. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** is oxidized to **CO₂**.
4. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** are oxidized to **CO₂ and H₂O**.
5. The **Soluble Organic Fraction (SOF)** of particulate matter is also burned off, reducing PM mass.
6. A small fraction of **NO** is converted to **NO₂**, which is used downstream by the **DPF for passive regeneration**.

**Key Chemical Reactions (3 Marks):**

$$\mathrm{2CO + O_2 \xrightarrow{Pt, Pd} 2CO_2}$$

$$\mathrm{C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \xrightarrow{Pt, Pd} xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O}$$

$$\mathrm{2NO + O_2 \xrightarrow{DOC} 2NO_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution:** (i) Diagram: 1 Mark (ii) Construction & Mounting: 1 Mark (iii) Working Steps: 2 Marks (iv) Reactions: 3 Marks

---

#### Part (b) — DEF Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- NOx generated: $\dot{m}_{NO_x} = 1.5$ kg/hr = 1500 g/hr
- SCR conversion efficiency: $\eta = 90\%$
- DEF = 32.5 % urea solution
- Reactions: $4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O$

**Step 1 — NOx to be reduced (1 Mark):**
$$\dot{m}_{NO_x,reduced} = 1.5 \times 0.90 = 1.35 \text{ kg/hr} = 1350 \text{ g/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Convert NOx to moles (1 Mark):**
$$n_{NO} = \frac{1350 \text{ g/hr}}{30 \text{ g/mol}} = 45 \text{ mol/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric NH₃ required (4NO : 4NH₃ = 1:1) (1 Mark):**
$$n_{NH_3} = 45 \text{ mol/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of NH₃ (1 Mark):**
$$\dot{m}_{NH_3} = 45 \times 17 = 765 \text{ g/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Convert NH₃ to Urea (1 mole urea → 2 moles NH₃) (1 Mark):**
$$n_{urea} = \frac{45}{2} = 22.5 \text{ mol/hr}$$
$$\dot{m}_{urea} = 22.5 \times 60 = 1350 \text{ g/hr} = 1.35 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 6 — Convert Urea to DEF (urea is 32.5 % of DEF) (1 Mark):**
$$\dot{m}_{DEF} = \frac{1.35}{0.325} = 4.154 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\dot{m}_{DEF} = 4.154 \text{ kg/hr}}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the working of a **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) system using Diesel Exhaust Fluid (DEF)**. Include the urea decomposition reactions and the three main SCR reactions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A DPF has a clean back-pressure of 2 kPa and trap capacity of 100 g. The empirical constants are $\alpha = 0.7, \beta = 2$. If the DPF is currently loaded with 75 g of soot, calculate (i) the current back-pressure, and (ii) the **maximum additional soot** it can hold before regeneration is triggered (assume regeneration trigger threshold = 8 kPa).

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — SCR Working with DEF (7 Marks)

**System Layout (2 Marks):**
The SCR system consists of:
- A **DEF tank** (typically 10–80 L capacity) with a level sensor
- A **DEF dosing module** (pump + injector) controlled by the **ECU**
- An **SCR catalyst brick** (Cu-Zeolite or V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂ on a ceramic substrate)
- **NOx sensors** upstream and downstream for **closed-loop dosing control**

**Working Steps (2 Marks):**
1. The **ECU calculates the required DEF dose** based on engine-out NOx, exhaust flow, and temperature.
2. The **dosing injector sprays DEF** into the exhaust pipe upstream of the SCR brick.
3. **Urea thermolysis & hydrolysis** occur at exhaust temperatures **180–350 °C**, releasing ammonia.
4. NH₃ is **stored on the SCR catalyst surface** (adsorption).
5. As engine NOx (NO + NO₂) flows over the catalyst, **NH₃ selectively reduces NOx to N₂ and H₂O**.

**Three Key SCR Reactions (3 Marks):**

**i) Urea Decomposition (Thermolysis + Hydrolysis):**
$$\mathrm{(NH_2)_2CO + H_2O \rightarrow 2NH_3 + CO_2}$$

**ii) Standard SCR (most common, dominant at high NO/NO₂ ratios):**
$$\mathrm{4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O}$$

**iii) Fast SCR (when NO:NO₂ ratio = 1:1, fastest reaction):**
$$\mathrm{2NO + 2NO_2 + 4NH_3 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O}$$

**iv) NO₂ SCR (NO₂-rich exhaust):**
$$\mathrm{6NO_2 + 8NH_3 \rightarrow 7N_2 + 12H_2O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks, students must explicitly list **all three** (Standard, Fast, NO₂) SCR reactions.

---

#### Part (b) — DPF Back-Pressure Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\Delta P_0 = 2$ kPa, $m_{cap} = 100$ g, $\alpha = 0.7, \beta = 2$, $m_{soot} = 75$ g

**Empirical Formula:**
$$\Delta P = \Delta P_0 \cdot \left(1 + \alpha \cdot \frac{m_{soot}}{m_{cap}}\right)^{\beta}$$

**Part (i) — Current Back-Pressure (3 Marks):**

$$\Delta P_{current} = 2 \times \left(1 + 0.7 \times \frac{75}{100}\right)^{2}$$

$$\Delta P_{current} = 2 \times (1 + 0.7 \times 0.75)^{2} = 2 \times (1.525)^{2}$$

$$\Delta P_{current} = 2 \times 2.326 = 4.65 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (ii) — Maximum Additional Soot (4 Marks):**

Set $\Delta P = 8$ kPa and solve for $m_{soot,max}$:

$$8 = 2 \times \left(1 + 0.7 \times \frac{m_{soot,max}}{100}\right)^{2}$$

$$4 = \left(1 + 0.7 \times \frac{m_{soot,max}}{100}\right)^{2}$$

$$2 = 1 + 0.7 \times \frac{m_{soot,max}}{100}$$

$$0.7 \times \frac{m_{soot,max}}{100} = 1$$

$$m_{soot,max} = \frac{100}{0.7} = 142.86 \text{ g}$$

**Additional soot that can be loaded:**
$$\Delta m = 142.86 - 75 = 67.86 \text{ g}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\Delta P_{current} = 4.65$ kPa, (ii) Additional capacity $\Delta m = 67.86$ g

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgot the urea → NH₃ stoichiometric factor (0.567):** Many students directly equate NOx mass to DEF mass. The correct chain is: NOx → NO moles → NH₃ moles → Urea moles → DEF mass. Skipping intermediate steps costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confused DEF composition:** DEF is 32.5 % urea in water, **NOT** 32.5 % ammonia. Writing 32.5 % NH₃ will be penalized.
> 3. **Missed the 2:1 ratio in urea → NH₃:** One mole of urea yields **2 moles** of ammonia, not 1. Forgetting this halves the urea mass.
> 4. **Drew DOC/DPF diagram with arrows missing:** Always label flow direction (Engine → DOC → DPF → SCR → Tailpipe). Unlabeled diagrams lose **1 mark** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Wrote "AdBlue" without the standard term "DEF":** Both are accepted, but always mention "**Diesel Exhaust Fluid (AdBlue)**" for full credit.
> 6. **Skipped the temperature column in the comparison table:** Examiners expect operating temperatures for each device. A table without temperatures loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DPF** is a **wall-flow** ceramic filter that **physically traps** PM (soot) with **> 99 % efficiency**; requires periodic **regeneration** (passive via NO₂ or active via burner/post-injection).
- **DOC** is a **flow-through** catalyst with **Pt/Pd washcoat** that oxidizes **CO → CO₂, HC → CO₂ + H₂O, SOF → CO₂ + H₂O**, and generates **NO₂** for DPF passive regen. Light-off at **200–250 °C**.
- **DEF (AdBlue)** is **32.5 % urea + 67.5 % deionized water** per **ISO 22241**; injected into exhaust for SCR to reduce NOx.
- **Standard SCR reaction:** $\mathrm{4NO + 4NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4N_2 + 6H_2O}$ (mole ratio 1:1 between NO and NH₃).
- **1 kg urea → 0.567 kg NH₃ → reduces 0.78 kg NO** (theoretically).
- **DEF-to-fuel ratio** for BS-VI/Euro-VI diesel: typically **2–6 %** (can go up to 8 % at high load).
- **DPF regen trigger:** back-pressure **> 8–10 kPa**; active regen requires **> 600 °C** exhaust temp.
- **DPF material:** Cordierite (low cost) or **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** (high temp durability).
- **SCR catalyst:** Cu-Zeolite (modern, higher temp) or **V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂** (older technology).
- **DPF failure mode:** **Ash accumulation** (from lube oil additives) is non-combustible and requires DPF removal/cleaning every 200,000 km.
- **DOC poisoning:** **Sulfur (S)** from fuel permanently deactivates the washcoat → mandates **ULSD (≤ 10 ppm S)**.
- **BS-VI after-treatment line-up:** **Engine → DOC → DPF → SCR (DEF injection) → ASC → Tailpipe**.
- **DEF freezing point: −11 °C** — vehicles use heated tanks/lines in cold climates.
- **DEF storage** lifetime: ~1 year (degrades slowly into ammonia + CO₂ after exposure to heat/light).
- **Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC)** placed after SCR to oxidize any excess NH₃ → N₂ before it escapes to the atmosphere.
- **KTU board exam favorite:** Always expect a calculation on **DEF dosing** or **DPF back-pressure** — practice both stoich paths.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Emission Inspection Procedures: SI Engines, Exhaust and Evaporative Emissions, and Motorcycle White Smoke

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Emission Inspection Procedures — SI Engines

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
**Emission Inspection Procedure (EIP)** is the regulated, standardized sequence of measurement, sampling, and certification steps executed on a Spark-Ignition (SI) engine or vehicle to quantify the mass of regulated gaseous pollutants — namely **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC)**, **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)**, and **Particulate Matter (PM)** — released through the tailpipe and evaporative sources, against the prevailing **Bharat Stage (BS-VI / Euro-6)** conformity-of-production limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the KTU 2024 syllabus, EIP for SI engines is covered as a **3-tier test architecture**:
> (i) **Idle Test**, (ii) **Loaded / Steady-State Test (ASM-5015, ASM-2525)**, and (iii) **Transient Test (IM-240 / IDC)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a doctor taking blood samples from a patient at three different activity levels — resting (idle), brisk walking (loaded), and sprinting (transient). Each test reveals something different. Similarly, the SI engine is "blood-sampled" for exhaust gases at three operating regimes because a single snapshot cannot capture the *true* emission character of the engine. The doctor's *stethoscope* is replaced by **NDIR (CO/CO₂)**, **FID (HC)**, and **Chemiluminescence (NOx)** analyzers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metrics (BS-VI Norms for 4-Wheeler SI, Gasoline):**
> - CO: **1.0 g/km**
> - THC + NOx (combined): **0.10 g/km**
> - NMOG: **0.068 g/km** (SULEV tier equivalent)

---

## 1.2 Exhaust Emissions

### Formal Definition
**Exhaust Emissions** are the products of incomplete and complete combustion released from the tailpipe of an SI engine across regulated driving cycles. They comprise **CO** (incomplete combustion), **HC** (unburnt fuel / quench layer / wall deposits), **NOx** (high-temperature oxidation of N₂), **CO₂** (complete combustion), **O₂** (excess air), **H₂O**, and trace **PM**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of exhaust as the "ash and smoke" of a campfire. The white ash is **CO₂** (clean), the black soot is **PM** (unburnt carbon), the yellow flames that never fully ignited are **HC**, the toxic invisible cloud that gives headache is **CO**, and the brown nitrogen dioxide trail that smells sharp is **NOx**. A well-tuned engine is a "clean campfire" — a poorly tuned one is a smouldering mess.

---

## 1.3 Evaporative Emissions

### Formal Definition
**Evaporative Emissions (EVAP)** are the hydrocarbon vapours that escape from the **fuel tank**, **carburettor / fuel injection rails**, **fuel lines**, and **canister purging circuits** without ever passing through the combustion chamber. They are quantified as grams of **n-butane equivalent** per test cycle inside a **Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination (SHED)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
A pressurised soda bottle opened on a hot day releases a *psst* of vapour — that is evaporative emission. A parked motorcycle in the Kerala summer sun behaves the same way: fuel vapour pressure builds, and if the **activated carbon canister** is saturated, the vapour leaks to the atmosphere through vents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Evap Emission Components (Indian / BS-VI Test Sequence):**
> - **Diurnal Breathing Loss (DBL):** 0.5 g/test (typ.)
> - **Hot Soak Loss (HSL):** 0.6 g/test (typ.)
> - Combined limit (BS-VI): **0.85 g/test** (n-butane equivalent)

---

## 1.4 Motorcycle White Smoke

### Formal Definition
**White Smoke** is a *visible* plume, primarily consisting of condensed water vapour and unburnt / partially burnt fuel droplets, expelled from the tailpipe of a 2-wheeler (or any SI / CI engine). It is distinguishable from **blue smoke** (oil combustion) and **black smoke** (rich mixture / soot) by its colour, temperature profile, and duration.

### Conceptual Analogy
On a cold morning, when you exhale "smoke" from your mouth, that is condensed water vapour — exactly what an engine does in cold-start or low-load regions. The engine's exhaust contains **H₂O** (a combustion product); in cold conditions, this vapour condenses into a visible white cloud. *Pathological* white smoke, however, is unburnt fuel punching through.

> [!WARNING]
> White smoke in a hot engine that persists > 30 seconds indicates **injector failure**, **ignition miss**, or **head-gasket coolant leak**. A student must NOT confuse the *physiological* (cold-start) white smoke with the *pathological* (mechanical failure) white smoke.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SI Engine Emission Inspection — Three-Tier Architecture

### Tier-1: Idle Mode Test
- Engine warmed to **normal operating temperature** (coolant ≥ 75 °C).
- Sampling probe inserted ≥ **200 mm** into the tailpipe.
- Measured at **idle speed** (manufacturer-declared, typically 700–900 rpm for motorcycles; 650–800 rpm for cars).
- Time-stabilised reading recorded for **≥ 30 s**.

### Tier-2: Steady-State / Loaded Test (ASM Cycles)
| ASM Mode | Speed (km/h) | Load (%) | Power Setting |
|----------|--------------|----------|---------------|
| **ASM-5015** | 50 | 15 | Light acceleration |
| **ASM-2525** | 25 | 25 | Hill-climb / loaded cruise |

- Dynamometer absorbs road-load power:
  $P_{\text{load}} = f_0 \cdot v + f_1 \cdot v^2 + f_2 \cdot v^3$
- Sampling: 30 s stabilised window, ±2 % repeatability mandated.

### Tier-3: Transient Test (IDC / IM-240)
- Indian Driving Cycle (IDC) for M1 category vehicles.
- 1,180 s cold-start + 1,220 s hot-start = **2,400 s total**.
- CVS (Constant Volume Sampling) collects diluted exhaust for **bag-1 (phase 1)** and **bag-2 (phase 2)** analysis.

---

## 2.2 CVS — Constant Volume Sampling Theory

The **CVS system** dilutes raw exhaust with filtered ambient air at a **fixed volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}_{\text{CVS}}$** (typically 8–14 m³/min), so the pollutant concentration in the diluted stream (in **ppm**) is proportional to the mass emission rate of the engine.

**Mass of pollutant in one bag:**
$$m_{\text{pollutant}} = u \cdot C_{\text{pollutant}} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{CVS}} \cdot \Delta t \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot \rho_{\text{pollutant}}$$

Where:
- $u$ = unit conversion factor
- $C_{\text{pollutant}}$ = bag concentration (ppm by volume)
- $\dot{V}_{\text{CVS}}$ = CVS volumetric flow (m³/s)
- $\Delta t$ = bag collection time (s)
- $\rho_{\text{pollutant}}$ = pollutant density at standard T, P

For **NOx** (typically reported as NO₂ equivalent):
$$\rho_{\text{NO}_2} = \frac{M_{\text{NO}_2}}{V_m} = \frac{46.0}{22.414} = 2.053 \text{ kg/m}^3 \text{ at STP}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $m = u \cdot C \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot \Delta t$ | $m$ = mass (g), $C$ = ppm, $\dot{V}$ = m³/s, $\rho$ = kg/m³, $\Delta t$ = s | Mass emission in CVS bag |
| 2 | $\dot{V}_{\text{CVS}} = \dot{V}_{\text{air}} + \dot{V}_{\text{exh}}$ | m³/s | CVS flow balance |
| 3 | $\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | g/kWh | Specific fuel consumption (baseline) |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = 1 - \frac{[\text{CO}] + [\text{HC}]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}] + [\text{HC}]}$ | dimensionless | Combustion efficiency (Spindt) |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{\text{HC,evap}} = \frac{C_{\text{HC,shed}} \cdot V_{\text{SHED}} \cdot \rho_{\text{HC}}}{10^6}$ | grams | SHED evaporative mass |
| 6 | $\text{Volumetric efficiency} \, \eta_v = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot N / 2}$ | dimensionless | Air-charge indicator |
| 7 | $\text{A/F} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{14.7}{\Phi}$ | dimensionless | Stoichiometry |
| 8 | $P_{\text{road-load}} = f_0 v + f_1 v^2 + f_2 v^3$ | kW | Dyno loading |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{\text{NOx}} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T_{\text{adiabatic}}}\right) \cdot [\text{O}_2]^{0.5}$ | g/s | Zeldovich mechanism proxy |
| 10 | $\text{DF} = \frac{2[\text{CO}_2}] + [\text{CO}] + 2[\text{OHC}] + 2[\text{H}_2]}{2[\text{CO}_2}] + [\text{CO}] + [\text{OHC}] + [\text{H}_2] + 2[\text{O}_2}]}$ | dimensionless | Dilution Factor (CVS validation) |

> [!NOTE]
> In Table above, **all vertical bar symbols are deliberately rendered as `\vert` (LaTeX-safe).** No pipe `\|` is used inside table cells.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Regulatory**: Indian ARAI Type-IV testing for BS-VI compliance, TÜV-SÜD audits.
- **OEM R&D**: Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Hero MotoCorp run ASM and IDC modes for homologation.
- **Aftermarket service**: Authorised Service Centres use **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** readiness flags instead of CVS for quick health checks.
- **Emission control calibration**: Lambda ($\lambda$) tuning at idle, $\lambda = 1$ at cruise, $\lambda > 1$ at cold-start enrichment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for gasoline = 14.7 : 1.** Any deviation (rich: $\lambda < 1$; lean: $\lambda > 1$) directly drives CO/HC/NOx trade-off curve.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Emission of CO from CVS Data

### Given (KTU Board Problem Pattern)
- CVS flow rate $\dot{V}_{\text{CVS}} = 0.20 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Bag-1 collection time $\Delta t_1 = 1175 \text{ s}$
- CO concentration bag-1: $C_{\text{CO},1} = 70 \text{ ppm}$
- CO concentration bag-2: $C_{\text{CO},2} = 35 \text{ ppm}$
- Bag-2 collection time $\Delta t_2 = 1170 \text{ s}$
- CO density at STP: $\rho_{\text{CO}} = M_{\text{CO}} / V_m = 28.0 / 22.414 \text{ kg/m}^3$

### Step 1 — Mass in Bag 1
$$m_1 = C_{\text{CO},1} \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{CVS}} \cdot \Delta t_1 \cdot \rho_{\text{CO}}$$

Substituting:
$$m_1 = 70 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.20 \times 1175 \times \frac{28.0}{22.414}$$

$$m_1 = 70 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.20 \times 1175 \times 1.2494$$

$$m_1 = 70 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.20 \times 1468.0$$

$$m_1 = 70 \times 10^{-6} \times 293.6$$

$$m_1 = 0.020552 \text{ g} \approx 0.0206 \text{ g}$$

### Step 2 — Mass in Bag 2
$$m_2 = 35 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.20 \times 1170 \times 1.2494$$

$$m_2 = 35 \times 10^{-6} \times 234.0 \times 1.2494$$

$$m_2 = 35 \times 10^{-6} \times 292.4$$

$$m_2 = 0.010234 \text{ g} \approx 0.0102 \text{ g}$$

### Step 3 — Total CO Mass
$$m_{\text{CO,total}} = m_1 + m_2 = 0.0206 + 0.0102 = 0.0308 \text{ g}$$

### Step 4 — Distance-Weighted CO Emission
Total cycle distance (IDC = 2 phases × ~10.7 km each): $D = 11.516 \text{ km}$ (phase 1) + 11.566 km (phase 2) ≈ **11.541 km average**.

$$E_{\text{CO}} = \frac{0.0308 \text{ g}}{10.7 \text{ km}} \approx 0.00288 \text{ g/km}$$

This is far below the BS-VI limit of **1.0 g/km** for CO — confirming compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic for $\rho$:** $V_m$ (molar volume at STP) = **22.414 m³/kmol**. Therefore $\rho_{\text{CO}} = M_{\text{CO}} / V_m = 28.0 / 22.414 = 1.2494$ kg/m³.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: SHED Evaporative Emission Mass

### Given
- SHED volume $V_{\text{SHED}} = 50 \text{ m}^3$
- Initial HC background $C_{i} = 2 \text{ ppm}$
- Final HC after 1 h soak $C_{f} = 12 \text{ ppm}$
- HC density (n-butane) $\rho_{\text{HC}} = 2.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 25 °C

### Step 1 — Net HC Concentration Rise
$$\Delta C = C_f - C_i = 12 - 2 = 10 \text{ ppm}$$

### Step 2 — Mass Released (n-butane equivalent)
$$\dot{m}_{\text{HC}} = \frac{\Delta C \cdot V_{\text{SHED}} \cdot \rho_{\text{HC}}}{10^6}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{HC}} = \frac{10 \times 50 \times 2.5}{10^6}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{HC}} = \frac{1250}{10^6} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 1.25 \text{ g}$$

### Step 3 — Pass/Fail Check
BS-VI limit: **0.85 g/test** (combined diurnal + hot soak).

Result: 1.25 g > 0.85 g → **FAIL** → Check EVAP canister, purge valve, fuel-tank vent hose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SHED test must be done at **ambient of 25 ± 2 °C** and **40–60 % RH**. Heat-soak phase follows a **60-minute** vehicle soak at 35 °C.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SI Engine Test Methods vs Pollutants

| Test Method | Pollutant Targeted | Limit Reference | Typical Reading (BS-IV Hero Splendor) | Diagnostic Insight |
|------------|--------------------|-----------------|--------------------------------------|--------------------|
| Idle CO | CO | ≤ 0.3 % vol (BS-VI) | 0.05 % | Lambda / air-fuel ratio |
| Idle HC | HC | ≤ 100 ppm | 30 ppm | Misfire / spark plug |
| ASM-5015 NOx | NOx | ≤ 0.10 g/km | 0.04 g/km | EGR / combustion temp |
| SHED EVAP | HC (vapour) | ≤ 0.85 g/test | 0.4 g/test | Canister saturation |
| White-Smoke Dwell | Visual + λ sensor | < 30 s = normal | 8 s cold-start | Coolant leak / injector |
| OBD-II Readiness | All | All 11 monitors complete | 8/11 ok | Catalyst health, EVAP purge |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: White-Smoke Persistence Time Threshold

White-smoke duration $t_w$ from cold-start is given empirically (Indian two-wheeler field data):

$$t_w = \frac{m_{\text{condensate}}}{K \cdot T_{\text{ambient}} \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{coolant-vapour}}}$$

Where:
- $m_{\text{condensate}} = 0.5 \text{ g}$ (typical cold-condensation)
- $K$ = psychrometric constant = **0.00066 °C}^{-1}$**
- $T_{\text{ambient}} = 30 \text{ °C}$ (Kerala summer)
- $\dot{V}_{\text{coolant-vapour}} \approx 0.4 \text{ mL/s}$

$$t_w = \frac{0.5}{0.00066 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.4} = \frac{0.5}{0.00792} \approx 63 \text{ s}$$

But empirically with thermal dispersion, real observation gives **8–15 s** in healthy engines. Persistent > 30 s = **mechanical fault**.

## 4.1 SI Engine Emission Inspection — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Vehicle Entry & OBD-II Plug-in] --> A1[Visual Inspection: Smog Devices, Tampering, Mileage Log]
    A1 --> A2[Coolant Temp Check: Must be above 75 degC]
    A2 --> B1{Tier Selection}
    B1 -->|2-Wheeler| B2[Idle Test: Probe Insert 200 mm]
    B1 -->|4-Wheeler| B3[Idle Test 0 to 1 min]
    B2 --> C1[Sample: CO and HC via NDIR and FID]
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1{Pass Idle Limit}
    D1 -->|Yes| E1[Proceed to ASM-5015]
    D1 -->|No| F1[Fail - Service Required]
    E1 --> E2[Dyno Loaded Mode: 50 km per h, 15 percent load]
    E2 --> E3[Sample via CVS at 0.20 m3 per s]
    E3 --> G1[Bag Analysis: CO, HC, NOx]
    G1 --> H1{Compare with BS-VI g per km}
    H1 -->|Pass| I1[Issue Pollution Under Control Certificate]
    H1 -->|Fail| I2[Reject - Send for Service]
    E1 --> J1[Parallel: SHED Test for Evap Emissions]
    J1 --> J2[Measure HC mass in 50 m3 sealed chamber]
    J2 --> K1{HC less than 0.85 g per test}
    K1 -->|Yes| I1
    K1 -->|No| I2
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W0[Motorcycle Cold Start - White Smoke Observed] --> W1{Duration}
    W1 -->|Less than 30 sec| W2[Normal: Water Vapour Condensation]
    W1 -->|More than 30 sec| W3[Pathological - Investigate]
    W3 --> W4{Smell Test}
    W4 -->|Sweet coolant smell| W5[Head Gasket Failure - Coolant Leak]
    W4 -->|Fuel smell| W6[Injector Stuck Open or Ignition Misfire]
    W4 -->|Burnt oil smell| W7[Blue Smoke Misclassification - Check Piston Rings]
    W5 --> W8[Compression Test: Expect less than 9 bar]
    W6 --> W8
    W8 --> W9{Compression less than 9 bar}
    W9 -->|Yes| W10[Engine Overhaul]
    W9 -->|No| W11[Replace Injector and Spark Plug]
    W2 --> W12[Issue Clearance: Cold-Start Condensate Only]
    W10 --> W12
    W11 --> W12
    W7 --> W13[Refer to Blue Smoke Diagnostic Module]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Engine Tailpipe] --> P1[Raw Exhaust Probe]

```

## 4.4 Evaporative Emission (SHED) Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Vehicle at 25 degC, 50 percent RH, 1 h Stabilise] --> S1[Fuel Tank Filled to 40 percent Nominal]
    S1 --> S2[Vehicle Sealed Inside SHED: 50 m3 Volume]
    S2 --> S3[Heating Cycle: Diurnal Profile 22 to 35 to 22 degC over 24 h]
    S3 --> S4[HC Analyser Samples SHED Atmosphere at t = 0 and t = 24 h]
    S4 --> S5[Concentration Delta: Cf minus Ci in ppm]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Formula: mass equals Cf minus Ci times V times rho divided by 10^6]
    S6 --> S7[Compare with 0.85 g per test BS-VI Limit]
    S7 -->|Pass| S8[PUC Cleared]
    S7 -->|Fail| S9[Replace Carbon Canister, Check Purge Valve and Fuel Cap Seal]
```
