# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`

**Define Inspection Frequency. List any two factors that determine the inspection frequency of an in-use motor vehicle in India.**

**Model Answer:**

**Inspection Frequency** is the pre-defined periodic interval at which an in-use vehicle must undergo an exhaust emission compliance test at CPCB-authorised testing stations, as mandated under the Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989, to ensure that the vehicle continues to meet the applicable emission standard throughout its operational life.

**Two determining factors:**

1. **Age of the vehicle** — Older vehicles (e.g., > 15 years in Delhi-NCR) are tested every 3 months, while newer vehicles (BS-VI) are tested annually.
2. **Vehicle category** — Commercial / public transport vehicles face a stricter combined fitness + emission test cycle (every 1–2 years), whereas private vehicles are typically annual.

*(Examiner's note: A 3-mark answer expects 1 definition + 2 factors with brief justification. 1 + 1 + 1 distribution.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**What is a Portable Emissions Measurement System (PEMS)? Mention its role in roadside inspection.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Portable Emissions Measurement System (PEMS)** is a compact, mobile gas analysis unit that can be temporarily installed on a moving or stationary vehicle to continuously measure real-time concentrations of **CO, CO₂, HC, NOₓ, and PM** in the exhaust, as per **AIS-052 (rev. 2017)** and **EU Regulation 2016/427**.

**Role in Roadside Inspection:**
- Allows **on-the-spot, real-world** emission measurement under actual driving (or free acceleration) conditions, unlike laboratory-type approval tests.
- Detects **super-emitters** and tampered vehicles (defeat devices, removed catalytic converter) that may pass a routine PUC test but pollute in real operation.
- Provides data for **in-use compliance enforcement** and is the standard tool for **Real Driving Emissions (RDE)** verification.

*(Examiner's note: Definition (1.5) + Role (1.5) = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Inspection and Maintenance (I/M) Program** for in-use vehicles. Discuss the standard test procedure adopted at an authorised PUC centre in India for both petrol and diesel vehicles, including the applicable emission limits as per BS-IV norms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A roadside smoke opacity test on a BS-IV diesel vehicle gave the following data:
- Measured absorption coefficient: $k_{meas} = 1.8 \text{ m}^{-1}$
- Ambient temperature: $40 \, °C$
- Atmospheric pressure: $98.5 \text{ kPa}$
- Optical path length of the opacimeter: $L = 0.3$ m

Reference conditions: $T_{ref} = 20 \, °C = 293.15$ K, $P_{ref} = 101.325$ kPa.

**Compute:**
(i) The corrected absorption coefficient $k_{NTP}$.
(ii) The % opacity.
(iii) Verify whether the vehicle passes the BS-IV free acceleration limit of **25 HSU** (corresponding to $k \approx 1.62$ m⁻¹).

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

### Part (a) — I/M Program (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** An **Inspection and Maintenance (I/M) Program** is a regulatory framework mandating that every in-use vehicle undergo periodic emission testing and necessary maintenance to ensure continued compliance with type-approval emission limits throughout its service life.

**Why I/M is needed (2 marks):**
- A vehicle's emission performance **degrades** with age (catalyst poisoning, oil consumption, EGR clogging, DPF ash loading).
- **10% of fleet (super-emitters)** can contribute 50% of fleet emissions.
- Type-approval alone is insufficient; in-use compliance is essential.

**Test procedure at an authorised PUC centre (3 marks):**

**For Petrol (BS-IV):**
1. Visual inspection for tampering (no cat converter, no ECU blanking).
2. Engine warm-up to normal operating temperature (~80 °C coolant).
3. **Idle CO** measured with NDIR analyser — sample probe inserted in tailpipe. Limit: **≤ 0.3 % vol** (4-stroke BS-IV).
4. **Idle HC** measured simultaneously. Limit: **≤ 200 ppm**.
5. **OBD-II scan** (BS-IV and above) — verify no MIL ON, no critical DTCs.

**For Diesel (BS-IV):**
1. Visual inspection (DPF, EGR, intake).
2. Engine warm-up.
3. **Free acceleration smoke test** — accelerator rapidly snapped from idle to cut-off rpm (typically 2500–3000 rpm) for 3–4 cycles. Peak opacity recorded.
4. Measured using **Hartridge opacimeter** or **full-flow smoke meter** as per **IS 14624**.
5. Limit: **≤ 25 HSU** (≈ 1.62 m⁻¹, ≈ 30 % opacity) for BS-IV.

**Documentation (1 mark):** Pass → PUC certificate issued; uploaded to VAHAN database. Fail → Vehicle sent for repair and retest within stipulated time.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $k_{meas} = 1.8$ m⁻¹, $T_{amb} = 40 \, °C = 313.15$ K, $P_{amb} = 98.5$ kPa, $L = 0.3$ m, $T_{ref} = 293.15$ K, $P_{ref} = 101.325$ kPa.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Convert temperatures.

$$ T_{meas} = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K} $$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Apply atmospheric correction formula.

$$ k_{NTP} = k_{meas} \cdot \dfrac{P_{meas} \cdot T_{ref}}{P_{ref} \cdot T_{meas}} $$

$$ k_{NTP} = 1.8 \cdot \dfrac{98.5 \cdot 293.15}{101.325 \cdot 313.15} $$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Numerator = $98.5 \times 293.15 = 28{,}875.275$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Denominator = $101.325 \times 313.15 = 31{,}729.93$

**Step 5 (0.5 marks):** Ratio = $28{,}875.275 / 31{,}729.93 = 0.9100$

**Step 6 (0.5 marks):** $k_{NTP} = 1.8 \times 0.9100 = \mathbf{1.638}$ **m⁻¹**

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Compute % opacity.

$$ \%O = 100 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k_{NTP} \cdot L}\right) = 100 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-1.638 \cdot 0.3}\right) $$

$$ k_{NTP} \cdot L = 0.4914 $$

$$ e^{-0.4914} = 0.6117 $$

$$ \%O = 100 \cdot (1 - 0.6117) = \mathbf{38.83 \, \%} $$

**Step 8 (1 mark):** Compliance check.

The BS-IV free-acceleration limit is $k \leq 1.62$ m⁻¹.

- Computed $k_{NTP} = 1.638$ m⁻¹ **exceeds** 1.62 m⁻¹.
- **Decision: Vehicle FAILS** the BS-IV emission norm.
- Action: On-the-spot challan under MV (Amendment) Act, 2019; vehicle to be repaired and re-tested within stipulated period.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Roadside Inspection (RSI)** procedure for in-use vehicles. List the equipment used and discuss the legal framework (with penalty structure) under the Indian Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019, for non-compliance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A study of 5000 randomly stopped vehicles in a city found that 12 % were super-emitters (exceeding the in-use CO limit by ≥ 2×). If a daily RSI drive tests 25 vehicles:

(i) Calculate the probability of detecting **at least one** super-emitter in a single day.
(ii) If the city's fleet is 2 million and 12 % are super-emitters, estimate the **absolute number** of super-emitters, and comment on why I/M + RSI must be combined to address the pollution load.
(iii) Discuss the role of **OBD-II scanning** in identifying tampered BS-IV and above vehicles.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

### Part (a) — RSI Procedure (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Roadside Inspection is the random or targeted on-street testing of vehicle emissions, OBD-II status, and roadworthiness by enforcement authorities using portable equipment.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Selection** — Random stopping of vehicles OR targeted selection of high-emitter candidates (older diesel, visible smoke, two-stroke 2-wheelers).
2. **Document check** — PUC certificate, fitness certificate, registration, insurance.
3. **Test execution:**
   - **Petrol:** Insert NDIR probe; measure idle CO (and HC if equipped).
   - **Diesel:** Connect portable opacimeter; perform 3–4 free acceleration snap cycles from idle to cut-off rpm.
   - **BS-IV and above (petrol & diesel):** OBD-II scan to check MIL status and DTCs.
4. **Decision** — Pass (vehicle released) or Fail (challan issued; repair and re-test within 7–30 days).
5. **Data upload** — Results uploaded to CPCB/State Pollution Control Board server.

**Equipment used (1.5 marks):** PEMS, portable smoke opacimeter, handheld OBD-II scanner, NDIR gas analyser, tachometer, ambient P/T probe.

**Legal framework (1.5 marks):** Under the **Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019**:
- **Section 190(2)** — Driving without valid PUC: **₹ 10,000 fine** (first offence) and **₹ 25,000** (repeat).
- **Section 200** — Use of vehicle in unsafe condition: up to **₹ 5,000 – 10,000** fine.
- The vehicle can be **detained** under Section 207 until compliance is proven.

---

### Part (b) — Statistical & Conceptual (7 Marks)

**(i) Probability of detecting at least one super-emitter (2 marks):**

$$ P = 1 - (1 - f)^n = 1 - (1 - 0.12)^{25} $$

$$ (0.88)^{25} = e^{25 \cdot \ln(0.88)} = e^{25 \cdot (-0.12783)} = e^{-3.1958} = 0.0409 $$

$$ P = 1 - 0.0409 = \mathbf{0.9591 \approx 95.9 \, \%} $$

**Interpretation:** With 25 random samples per day, there is a 95.9% chance of catching at least one super-emitter — statistically very powerful.

**(ii) Absolute number of super-emitters (2 marks):**

$$ N_{super} = 0.12 \times 2{,}000{,}000 = \mathbf{240{,}000 \text{ vehicles}} $$

**Commentary (1 mark):** 240,000 super-emitters, though only 12% of the fleet, contribute disproportionately (≈ 50%) to total fleet emissions. **I/M programs alone** (annual PUC) cannot catch vehicles that are *intentionally* misbehaving on non-test days — they require tampering, which is reversed just before testing. **RSI provides the deterrence**: it tests the vehicle "in the wild" without notice. Together, I/M + RSI create a continuous, dual-layer enforcement that is essential for sustainable urban air quality.

**(iii) Role of OBD-II (2 marks):**
- OBD-II continuously monitors critical emission components (catalyst efficiency, O₂ sensors, EVAP system, EGR, DPF, SCR).
- On BS-IV and above, **MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp)** illuminates if any monitored system fails. A roadside OBD-II scan instantly reveals tampering — e.g., disconnected O₂ sensor, removed cat (post-cat sensor signals non-catalyst performance), EGR blanking plate.
- OBD-II readiness monitors (e.g., catalyst monitor, EVAP monitor) when "not ready" indicate recent **battery disconnection / ECU reset** — a classic sign of post-repair fraud.
- This makes OBD-II scanning a **non-invasive, electronic equivalent of the tailpipe test** for modern vehicles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** in atmospheric correction (°C vs K) cost 1–2 marks easily. Always convert to Kelvin: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
> 2. **Using the wrong form of the formula** ($k_{NTP} = k_{meas} \cdot T_{ref}/T_{meas}$ vs the alternate) can give a wrong answer even with correct numbers. The KTU-preferred form (IS 14624) is $k_{NTP} = k_{meas} \cdot (P_{meas} \cdot T_{ref}) / (P_{ref} \cdot T_{meas})$.
> 3. **Mixing up HSU and % opacity** in diesel norms — HSU is a Hartridge-specific empirical unit; the underlying physics uses $k$ (m⁻¹) and % opacity. Limit values: **HSU 25 ≈ k 1.62 m⁻¹ ≈ 30% opacity** for BS-IV.
> 4. **Forgetting the OBD-II concept** in answers about BS-IV and above vehicles — modern emission enforcement is increasingly OBD-based, and KTU expects this awareness.
> 5. **In numericals, showing no intermediate steps** leads to partial-marks loss. Always show: given data, formula, substitution, intermediate arithmetic, final answer with units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 — Module 4 : Inspection Frequency & Roadside Inspection — Quick Recall List**

### 🔹 Inspection Frequency
- ⏰ Default PUC cycle (India, private): **Annual**; for **> 15 yrs in NCR: every 3 months**; commercial vehicles: **annual + fitness (every 2 yrs)**.
- 📄 Legal document: **PUC Certificate** (cloud-uploaded to **VAHAN**).
- 💰 Fine (MV Amendment Act 2019): **₹ 10,000** first / **₹ 25,000** repeat.
- 🔬 Petrol limits (BS-IV, idle): **CO ≤ 0.3 % vol**, **HC ≤ 200 ppm**.
- 💨 Diesel limits (BS-IV, free accel.): **HSU ≤ 25** (≈ $k$ 1.62 m⁻¹ ≈ 30 % opacity).
- 🧪 CNG / LPG (idle): **CO ≤ 0.5 % vol**.

### 🔹 Roadside Inspection (RSI / RRTI)
- 🎯 Purpose: deterrence + real-world compliance + super-emitter detection.
- 🧰 Key tools: **PEMS** (real-time, AIS-052), **portable opacimeter** (IS 14624), **OBD-II scanner** (SAE J1962), **handheld NDIR**.
- 🛣️ Test cycle: visual → warm-up → tailpipe / free accel → OBD scan → pass / fail + challan.
- 🔢 Atmospheric correction of $k$: $k_{NTP} = k_{meas} \cdot (P_{meas} \cdot T_{ref}) / (P_{ref} \cdot T_{meas})$; **NTP = 20 °C, 101.325 kPa**.
- 📊 Super-emitter detection probability: $P = 1 - (1 - f)^n$.
- 💀 10% of fleet (super-emitters) → ~50% of fleet emissions (Pareto principle).
- 🛑 MIL ON / readiness "not ready" / major DTCs ⇒ OBD-II fail ⇒ vehicle fail.

### 🔹 KTU Exam Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Forget unit conversion (°C → K) in $k$ correction → lose 1–2 marks.
- ❌ Confuse **HSU** with **% opacity** — they are related but not identical.
- ❌ Skip OBD-II discussion in BS-IV and above answers — KTU expects awareness.
- ❌ Apply petrol test limits to diesel (or vice versa) — test procedures differ.
- ❌ Forget to cite the **standard / act** (IS 14624, AIS-052, MV Amendment Act 2019).

### 🔹 Cross-Reference within Module 4
- RSI directly feeds into **air quality monitoring** (Module 4 — measurement of air pollutants).
- PEMS-based RDE connects to **type-approval** (Module 2/3) by validating that in-use vehicles still meet their certified standards.
- I/M programs complement **end-of-life vehicle (ELV) scrappage** policy by flagging chronically non-compliant vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Replacement and Retrofit Programs: Scrap Age, Relocation, Replacement, and Retrofit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT522 - AUTOMOTIVE POLLUTION AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Replacement and Retrofit Programs: Scrap Age, Relocation, Replacement, and Retrofit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vehicle End-of-Life (EOL) Management — The Four Pillars

Vehicle End-of-Life management is the structured process through which a motor vehicle, after completing its useful operational phase, is dealt with to minimise environmental damage, recover material value, and reduce urban air pollution. The four interconnected strategies discussed under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT522, Module 4) are **Scrap Age Policy, Relocation, Replacement, and Retrofit**.

---

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition Block — Read Twice, Retain Forever**

**1. Scrap Age (Scrap Life / End-of-Life Age)**
Scrap age is the chronological age of a vehicle (in years or kilometres) at which the vehicle becomes economically and environmentally unfit for further roadworthy service, and is therefore mandatorily derecognised from registration for scrappage or recycling. It is a **policy-driven threshold**, not a purely mechanical one.

**2. Relocation (Cascading / Re-deployment)**
Relocation is the process of transferring an in-use but older/polluting vehicle from a **high-emission-sensitive zone** (typically a metropolitan/urban area) to a **low-sensitivity zone** (rural or non-attainment city) so that the cumulative pollution load in sensitive zones is reduced without immediate scrappage.

**3. Replacement (Modernisation / Fleet Renewal)**
Replacement is the direct substitution of an aged, high-emission vehicle with a new vehicle conforming to the prevailing **Bharat Stage (BS) emission norm** (currently BS-VI Phase 2 in India), usually accompanied by an **incentive/scrappage certificate** issued by the Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility (RVSF).

**4. Retrofit (Retrofits / After-Treatment Add-On)**
Retrofit is the engineering practice of **installing an additional emission-control device** (e.g., catalytic converter, DPF, CNG kit, EGR) on an in-service vehicle to bring its tailpipe emissions closer to a specified norm **without replacing the entire vehicle**.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Scrap Age** | Your old pair of running shoes that has crossed 800 km — you stop wearing them not because they tore one day, but because running in them now injures your knees. The km mark is a *policy of your body*. | A vehicle's scrap age is set by policy when repair costs, emission load, and safety risk jointly outweigh utility. |
| **Relocation** | A school teacher being transferred from a city school to a rural school — the person is the same, but the urban pressure is relieved. | A polluting older bus shifted to a rural route reduces urban load without scrapping. |
| **Replacement** | Throwing out the old shoes and buying a new, lighter, more cushioned pair. | A new BS-VI car replaces a BS-II/III car; net emissions drop dramatically. |
| **Retrofit** | Adding a medical-grade **insole** to the old shoes — they perform almost like new, but at a fraction of the cost of new shoes. | A DPF fitted on an old diesel truck captures soot cheaply without scrapping the truck. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 4 explicitly tests the student's ability to **distinguish** between these four strategies and to apply **cost-emission mathematics** to each. Confusing "retrofit" with "replacement" is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in the KTU valuation.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in India (Memorise These)

- **Petrol vehicle scrap age:** **20 years** (Personal); **15 years** (Commercial)
- **Diesel vehicle scrap age:** **15 years** (Personal); **10–12 years** (Commercial heavy-duty)
- **Fitness test interval:** Every **5 years after 15 years** of age
- **NGT bench mark (older order):** **15 years** for all diesel vehicles in Delhi-NCR
- **BS-VI effective:** **1 April 2020** for all categories
- **Number of registered vehicles in India (≈ 2024):** > **350 million**
- **In-use BS-IV and older share (urban centres):** ≈ **60–70%** of total fleet — the primary retrofit/replacement target pool

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind Each Strategy

#### 2.1.1 Scrap Age — *Why a fixed age?*
- Older vehicles emit **disproportionately high pollutants** (pre-BS era vehicles can emit **20–40× more PM and NOx** than BS-VI vehicles).
- A fixed scrap age avoids the **subjective fitness-test malpractice** that historically allowed 25-year-old trucks to ply on forged fitness certificates.
- It creates a **predictable, demand-driven scrap market** for the steel industry (every tonne of recycled steel saves ≈ **1.5 tonnes of CO₂**).
- It enforces **environmental equity** — old, polluting vehicles are not allowed to externalise pollution costs on society.

#### 2.1.2 Relocation — *Why move instead of scrap?*
- Economic: owner avoids a complete write-off; buyer (rural operator) gets a cheap working vehicle.
- Logical: pollution damage is **location-specific** (dense urban airshed is far more sensitive than rural).
- Limitation: only **defers** the problem; the vehicle still ages, still pollutes, just elsewhere — hence most modern KTU/GoI policies **discourage** relocation as a primary strategy.

#### 2.1.3 Replacement — *Why incentivise?*
- New BS-VI vehicles cut tailpipe emissions by ≈ **70% NOx and 80% PM** versus BS-IV.
- **India's Vehicle Scrapping Policy 2021** offers:
  - **Road-tax rebate of up to 25%** for new vehicle purchase against scrap certificate.
  - **Discount of up to 5%** from OEM on new vehicle.
  - **Registration-fee waiver** for new vehicle.
  - Scrap value of **≈ 4–6% of ex-showroom price** of the old vehicle.

#### 2.1.4 Retrofit — *Why not just scrap?*
- **Cost-effectiveness:** retrofitting a DPF costs ≈ ₹40,000–60,000 versus buying a new truck at ₹20,00,000+.
- **Material conservation:** retains ≈ **80% of embodied energy** of the vehicle.
- **Targeted pollution control:** a DPF cuts PM by **70–90%** on an old diesel bus at one-tenth the replacement cost.
- **Time-effective:** fleet operators do not have to halt operations for long.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Engineering Logic for Replacement Decision

To decide *whether* to scrap or keep, the KTU Board expects the student to compare the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** of running the old vehicle vs the EUAC of replacing it.

**Logic Flow:**
1. Compute present value of all operating, maintenance, and repair costs of the *existing* vehicle over its next **n** years.
2. Subtract present value of salvage value at year **n**.
3. Convert to EUAC using the capital-recovery factor **CRF(i, n)**.
4. Repeat for a *new* replacement vehicle.
5. **Decision rule:** Replace when `EUAC(new) + Δ scrap-cost penalty < EUAC(old)`.

### 2.3 Retrofit Reduction Mathematics

If baseline tailpipe emission of pollutant **j** is `E_j^0` (in g/km), and the retrofit device has **removal efficiency** `η_j` (dimensionless, 0–1), the post-retrofit emission is:

$$E_{j}^{\text{post}} = E_{j}^{0} \,\bigl(1 - \eta_{j}\bigr)$$

**Cost-effectiveness** of the retrofit (in ₹ per kg of pollutant removed over vehicle life) is:

$$CE_{j} = \frac{C_{\text{retrofit}} + n_{\text{yr}} \cdot C_{\text{O\&M}}}{VKT \cdot \bigl(E_{j}^{0} - E_{j}^{\text{post}}\bigr) \cdot 365}$$

where $VKT$ is vehicle-km travelled per day.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' favourite — memorise this table verbatim. The superscript `*` marks questions that have appeared in KTU past papers.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{t} = P + \sum_{k=1}^{t}\,(O_{k}+M_{k})\,(1+i)^{-k} - S\,(1+i)^{-t}$ | Total discounted cost of keeping vehicle up to year $t$ | ₹ |
| 2 | $CRF(i,n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor (i = interest, n = years) | yr$^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $EUAC(t) = \bigl[C_{t}\bigr] \cdot CRF(i,t)$ | Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost | ₹/yr |
| 4 | $E_{j}^{\text{post}} = E_{j}^{0}\,(1-\eta_{j})$ | Post-retrofit pollutant emission | g/km |
| 5 | $\eta_{j} = \dfrac{E_{j}^{0}-E_{j}^{\text{post}}}{E_{j}^{0}}$ | Removal efficiency of retrofit device | dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 6 | $CE_{j} = \dfrac{C_{\text{retro}} + n_{\text{yr}} \cdot C_{\text{O\&M}}}{VKT \cdot \eta_{j} \cdot E_{j}^{0} \cdot 365}$ | Cost-effectiveness of retrofit | ₹ / kg pollutant removed |
| 7 | $\Delta E_{j}^{\text{fleet}} = N_{f} \cdot VKT \cdot \eta_{j} \cdot E_{j}^{0}$ | Total annual fleet emission reduction | kg/yr |
| 8 | $LCO = \dfrac{PV(\text{all costs})}{PV(\text{all km travelled})}$ | Levelised Cost of Ownership | ₹/km |
| 9 | $S_{t} = S_{0}\,(1-d)^{t}$ | Salvage value decay (declining-balance) | ₹ |
| 10 | $N\!P\!V = \sum_{t=0}^{T}\dfrac{B_{t}-C_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | Net Present Value of a replacement decision | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In any numerical problem, the discount rate `i` is generally given as **10% p.a.** (unless specified otherwise) and `r` in NPV problems as **12% p.a.** Use these defaults in the exam to avoid ambiguity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These four strategies collectively form the **Demand-side Management (DSM) layer** of an urban air-quality plan. They sit **on top of** supply-side measures (better fuels, tighter BS norms) and **parallel to** Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS). A typical **Comprehensive Air-Quality Plan** in any Indian smart-city (Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru) deploys:
- **BS-VI fuel** + tighter mass-emission norms (supply-side).
- **Replacement incentives + scrappage policy** (DSM – replacement).
- **Retrofit mandate** on commercial fleet (DSM – retrofit).
- **Odd-Even / Zonal restriction** during peak pollution (DSM – relocation).
- **Fitness test tightening** to enforce scrap age (DSM – scrap age).

The KTU paper typically presents a numerical case and asks the student to **pick the most cost-effective strategy** — meaning both cost AND emission reduction are weighed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Scrap Age (Using EUAC Minimisation) ***

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU Board favourite — practice till you can derive it on a blank sheet in under 4 minutes.**

**Statement:** Determine the optimal scrap age `t*` at which the Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost of owning and operating a vehicle is minimised.

#### Step 1 — Write the cost components
- $P$ = Present purchase price (₹)
- $O_k$ = Operating cost in year $k$ (fuel, insurance)
- $M_k$ = Maintenance + repair cost in year $k$
- $S_t$ = Salvage value at end of year $t$
- $i$ = discount / interest rate per year

#### Step 2 — Discounted cost of ownership till year $t$
Convert every future cost to present value (year 0):

$$C_{t} \;=\; P \;+\; \sum_{k=1}^{t}\dfrac{O_{k}+M_{k}}{(1+i)^{k}} \;-\; \dfrac{S_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Sum of present value of all future operating + maintenance costs, plus initial capital, minus present value of salvage recovered at year `t`.

#### Step 3 — Convert PV to Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)
Multiply by the Capital Recovery Factor:

$$EUAC(t) \;=\; C_{t} \cdot CRF(i,t)$$

$$EUAC(t) \;=\; \biggl[\,P + \sum_{k=1}^{t}\dfrac{O_{k}+M_{k}}{(1+i)^{k}} - \dfrac{S_{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}\,\biggr] \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{t}}{(1+i)^{t}-1}$$

**Conversion logic:** EUAC is the *flat yearly payment* that, if made every year for `t` years, would exactly repay the present-valued total cost. Hence the multiplication by CRF.

#### Step 4 — Differentiate and set to zero to find the minimum
For a **continuous approximation** (smooth operating and maintenance cost growth), treat `t` as continuous:

$$\dfrac{d\,EUAC}{dt} \;=\; 0$$

Let operating + maintenance cost grow at an average rate $g$ per year, so $O_{k}+M_{k} = (O_{1}+M_{1})\,(1+g)^{k-1}$. And salvage decays: $S_{t} = S_{0}\,(1-d)^{t}$.

After algebraic simplification (a routine but tedious KTU exam step), the optimal `t*` satisfies:

$$\bigl(P - \dfrac{S_{0}}{(1+i)^{t^{*}}}\bigr) \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{t^{*}}}{\bigl((1+i)^{t^{*}}-1\bigr)^{2}} \cdot \ln(1+i) \;\approx\; (O_{1}+M_{1})\,(1+g)^{t^{*}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Left side is the marginal cost of postponing the *replacement* by one more year; right side is the marginal benefit (saving) of postponing the *replacement*. Set them equal — the classic *Economic Replacement* condition. The optimal `t*` is solved numerically (in the exam, `t*` is usually obtained by tabulation, see Section 3.4 below).

#### Step 5 — Practical KTU short-cut: Tabulation Method
Compute `EUAC(t)` for `t = 1, 2, 3, ..., 10` (or up to policy cap) and pick the `t` with the **lowest EUAC**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Cost-Effectiveness of a Retrofit Device

#### Step 1 — Define total cost of retrofit
$$C_{\text{total}}^{\text{retro}} = C_{\text{retro}} + n_{\text{yr}} \cdot C_{\text{O\&M}}$$

where $C_{\text{retro}}$ is capital cost, $C_{\text{O\&M}}$ is annual operation & maintenance cost, $n_{\text{yr}}$ is life in years.

#### Step 2 — Define annual emission reduction
A vehicle travels $VKT$ km/day. Per km emission drops from $E_{j}^{0}$ to $E_{j}^{0}(1-\eta_{j})$, so per km removal is $\eta_{j}\cdot E_{j}^{0}$ grams.

$$\Delta m_{j} \;=\; VKT \cdot \eta_{j}\cdot E_{j}^{0} \cdot 365 \quad \text{(grams per year)}$$

Convert to kg:

$$\Delta m_{j}^{\text{kg}} \;=\; \dfrac{VKT \cdot \eta_{j}\cdot E_{j}^{0} \cdot 365}{1000}$$

#### Step 3 — Form cost-effectiveness ratio

$$CE_{j} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{\text{retro}} + n_{\text{yr}}\cdot C_{\text{O\&M}}}{\Delta m_{j}^{\text{kg}}} \quad \text{(₹ per kg pollutant removed)}$$

#### Step 4 — Decision rule
Retrofit is **economically justified** if $CE_{j} \le$ societal damage cost of 1 kg of pollutant $j$ (e.g., Delhi EPA values PM2.5 at ≈ ₹**1,50,000/kg** for health costs; NOx ≈ ₹**25,000/kg**).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick exam trick:** If a retrofit cuts PM by 90% at ₹50,000 capital cost, with VKT 100 km/day, life 7 years, baseline PM 0.5 g/km, then the numerator is approximately ₹50,000 + 7×(₹2,000) = ₹64,000, and the denominator is 100×0.9×0.5×365/1000 ≈ 16.4 kg/yr → CE ≈ **₹557/kg-PM removed** — easily cheaper than ₹1,50,000 societal cost. **Retrofit justified.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Optimal Scrap Age by Tabulation

A diesel LCV is bought for ₹8,00,000. Operating + maintenance cost grows linearly. Salvage value declines at 10% p.a. Discount rate $i$ = 10% p.a. Tabulate EUAC for years 1 to 6.

| Year $t$ | O+M cost (₹) | Salvage $S_t$ (₹) | PV(O+M) cum. (₹) | $C_t$ (₹) | CRF(10%,t) | EUAC (₹/yr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80,000 | 7,20,000 | 72,727 | 1,25,227 | 1.1000 | 1,37,750 |
| 2 | 1,00,000 | 6,48,000 | 1,55,372 | 2,51,765 | 0.5762 | 1,45,083 |
| 3 | 1,20,000 | 5,83,200 | 2,46,053 | 3,88,719 | 0.4021 | 1,56,294 |
| 4 | 1,40,000 | 5,24,880 | 3,43,108 | 5,35,772 | 0.3155 | 1,69,037 |
| 5 | 1,60,000 | 4,72,392 | 4,45,517 | 6,89,073 | 0.2638 | 1,81,801 |
| 6 | 1,80,000 | 4,25,153 | 5,52,164 | 8,48,541 | 0.2296 | 1,94,825 |

**Interpretation:** EUAC is **minimum at $t = 1$ year** under these numbers — meaning the operating cost growth is so steep that the optimal *economic* scrap age is much earlier than the *policy* scrap age of 15 years. In the real world, policy scrap age is fixed; **the engineering exercise is to find the cost-optimal *replacement cycle* within the policy window** (often 5–8 years for commercial diesel vehicles).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Strategy

| Situation | Best KTU Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Vehicle age > policy scrap age, structural damage | **Replacement** | Legally forced; no other option |
| Urban bus pre-BS-III, operator cost-constrained | **Retrofit (DPF + SCR)** | 70% PM cut at 10% of new-bus cost |
| Truck going from NCR to Bihar for goods | **Relocation** | Cheap, immediate load-shift from NCR airshed |
| Old 2-wheeler, owner cannot afford new | **Retrofit** (limited — usually no retrofit available) | Most 2-wheelers go to scrap; retrofit is rare |
| Government fleet under Central scheme | **Replacement + scrap certificate** | Government is policy-maker; leads by example |
| Pre-2000 diesel car, daily VKT > 50 km | **Retrofit catalytic converter** | Cheapest PM/HC cut per kg |
| New vehicle under BS-VI | **None** (already compliant) | Retrofit unnecessary |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    I -- YES --> G
    I -- NO --> J[RELOCATE to Non-Attainment Zone]

    J --> K
    F --> K
```

### 4.2 Retrofit Decision Tree — Technology Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Existing Vehicle]) --> T{Fuel Type?}
    T -- PETROL --> P1{Need CO HC Cut?}
    P1 -- YES --> P2[Install TWC Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    P1 -- NO --> P3[No Retrofit]
    T -- DIESEL --> D1{Primary Pollutant?}
    D1 -- PM --> D2[Install DPF Diesel Particulate Filter]
    D1 -- NOx --> D3[Install SCR with DEF AdBlue]
    D1 -- Both PM and NOx --> D4[Install DPF plus DOC plus SCR]
    T -- CNG --> C1[No Retrofit Typically Required]
    T -- LPG --> L1{Need HC Cut?}
    L1 -- YES --> L2[Install Oxidation Catalyst]
    L1 -- NO --> L3[No Retrofit]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG([Engine]) --> EXH[Exhaust Manifold]
    EXH --> DOC[DOC Oxidation Catalyst]

    DPF --> SCR[SCR with DEF Injection]

```

flowchart TD

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]
**"Differentiate between Vehicle Replacement and Vehicle Retrofit. State two advantages of each with respect to urban air quality."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Replacement | Retrofit |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Discarding old vehicle and buying a new one | Fitting emission-control device on existing vehicle |
| **Cost** | Very high (new vehicle price) | Low to medium (device cost) |
| **Emission cut** | Very high (up to 95%+) | Medium (50–90% per pollutant) |
| **Material use** | New raw material consumed | Re-uses 80% of existing vehicle |

**Advantages of Replacement:** (i) Brings vehicle to the latest BS norm in one step; (ii) Restores full efficiency and safety. (1 mark each)
**Advantages of Retrofit:** (i) Cost-effective — only 10–20% of replacement cost; (ii) Faster implementation, no fleet downtime. (1 mark each)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]
**"Define scrap age. As per the Government of India's Vehicle Scrapping Policy 2021, what is the scrap age prescribed for (a) petrol cars and (b) diesel commercial vehicles?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Scrap age definition (1 mark):** The chronological age, expressed in years (or km), at which a vehicle is legally de-recognised for road use and must be sent to a Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility (RVSF), based on policy, emission load, and safety criteria.
- **Petrol cars:** **20 years** (1 mark)
- **Diesel commercial vehicles:** **15 years** (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (READ THIS)**
> - Students often **write a generic answer on "pollution control"** and lose 4–5 marks. The examiner is specifically marking for **scrap-age mathematics OR retrofit efficiency computation**. Show the formula, substitute values, and **box the final answer**.
> - Always state the **assumed interest rate** (use **10% p.a.** if not given).
> - Always state **units** in every line of the final answer table.
> - For retrofit questions, students forget to **convert grams to kilograms** — the most common single-mark loss.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set C]

**(a)** Explain the **four end-of-life vehicle management strategies** — Scrap Age, Relocation, Replacement, and Retrofit — with the rationale and policy framework of India's Vehicle Scrapping Policy 2021. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diesel LCV purchased at ₹6,50,000 has the following operating + maintenance costs and salvage values. Use a discount rate of 10% p.a. and the **EUAC method** to determine the **optimal replacement age** by tabulation. **(7 marks)**

| Year | O+M (₹) | Salvage (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 70,000 | 5,85,000 |
| 2 | 90,000 | 5,26,500 |
| 3 | 1,10,000 | 4,73,850 |
| 4 | 1,30,000 | 4,26,465 |
| 5 | 1,50,000 | 3,83,818 |

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**
1. **Scrap Age (1.5 marks):** Legal age threshold. India 2021: 20 yr petrol, 15 yr diesel commercial, 15 yr NGT for NCR diesel. Triggers fitness test every 5 yr after 15 yr.
2. **Relocation (1.5 marks):** Transfer of old vehicle from high-pollution zone to low-sensitivity zone. Merits: cheap, immediate; demerit: defers not solves.
3. **Replacement (2 marks):** Scrapping old, buying BS-VI. Incentives: 25% road-tax rebate, 5% OEM discount, registration-fee waiver, 4–6% scrap value.
4. **Retrofit (2 marks):** Add-on devices — TWC, DPF, SCR, EGR. Cuts PM 70–90%, NOx 50–70% at 10% of new-vehicle cost.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

CRF formula: $CRF(0.10, t) = \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^{t}}{(1.10)^{t}-1}$

$$C_{t} = P + \sum_{k=1}^{t}\dfrac{O_{k}+M_{k}}{(1.10)^{k}} - \dfrac{S_{t}}{(1.10)^{t}}$$

| $t$ | $\sum \frac{O+M}{(1.10)^k}$ (₹) | $\frac{S_t}{(1.10)^t}$ (₹) | $C_t$ (₹) | $CRF(10\%,t)$ | $EUAC$ (₹/yr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 63,636 | 5,31,818 | 1,81,818 | 1.1000 | 2,00,000 |
| 2 | 1,38,017 | 4,35,455 | 3,52,562 | 0.5762 | 2,03,139 |
| 3 | 2,19,535 | 3,55,949 | 5,13,586 | 0.4021 | 2,06,517 |
| 4 | 3,06,212 | 2,91,140 | 6,65,072 | 0.3155 | 2,09,830 |
| 5 | 3,96,471 | 2,37,985 | 8,08,486 | 0.2638 | 2,13,275 |

**Optimal replacement age = 1 year (minimum EUAC = ₹2,00,000).** (However, note that policy-driven scrap age is **15 years**; the engineering optimum is the *replacement cycle* of 5–8 years for commercial LCVs in practice — students should comment on this contradiction for full marks.) **Final answer boxed.** ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A] *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **working of a Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** as a retrofit device. List the emission reduction achieved and the operational challenges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An old diesel bus has a baseline PM emission of **0.60 g/km**. After retrofitting with a DPF of removal efficiency 85%, the bus travels **120 km/day** for **300 days/year** over **7 years**. The DPF costs **₹50,000** and the annual O&M is **₹3,000**. Calculate the **cost-effectiveness (₹ per kg PM removed)** and state whether retrofit is justified if the societal cost of PM2.5 is **₹1,50,000/kg**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**
1. **Working (3 marks):** Exhaust gas with PM enters porous DPF substrate (SiC or cordierite). PM trapped on pore walls via **deep-bed filtration** and **cake filtration**. Periodic **regeneration** burns off accumulated soot using heat from post-injection (active) or engine load (passive).
2. **Reduction achieved (1.5 marks):** **70–90% PM mass reduction**; CO and HC also cut by 50–70% by oxidation.
3. **Challenges (2.5 marks):** Back-pressure increase (≈ 50–100 mbar), **regeneration failure** in stop-go duty, ash accumulation requiring service every 1,00,000 km, expensive replacement substrate, **low-load operation** in city buses can clog the filter.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

Step 1: Annual km travelled per year = $VKT_{\text{yr}} = 120 \times 300 = 36,000 \text{ km/yr}$

Step 2: Per km PM removed (post-retrofit)
$$\Delta E_{\text{PM}} = \eta \cdot E^{0}_{\text{PM}} = 0.85 \times 0.60 = 0.51 \text{ g/km}$$

Step 3: Annual PM removed
$$\Delta m_{\text{yr}} = 36,000 \times 0.51 = 18,360 \text{ g/yr} = 18.36 \text{ kg/yr}$$

Step 4: Total cost over 7-year life
$$C_{\text{total}} = 50,000 + (7 \times 3,000) = 50,000 + 21,000 = 71,000 \text{ ₹}$$

Step 5: Total PM removed over 7 years
$$\Delta m_{\text{life}} = 18.36 \times 7 = 128.52 \text{ kg}$$

Step 6: Cost-effectiveness
$$CE_{\text{PM}} = \dfrac{71,000}{128.52} = 552.4 \text{ ₹/kg-PM removed}$$

Step 7: Decision
Since $CE_{\text{PM}} = 552.4 \text{ ₹/kg}$ is **far less** than the societal cost of ₹1,50,000/kg, the DPF retrofit is **highly justified**. **Net benefit ≈ ₹1,49,447/kg PM removed** (or 271× return on cost). **Final answer boxed.** ✅

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this list once before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- [ ] **Scrap age** = *legal/policy* threshold; **Optimal replacement age** = *economic/EUAC* threshold — **do not confuse the two**.
- [ ] India's Vehicle Scrapping Policy 2021 scrap ages: **Petrol = 20 yr (personal)**, **Diesel = 15 yr (commercial)**.
- [ ] NGT directive for NCR: **15 years for diesel vehicles** (all categories).
- [ ] **EUAC** is *the* mathematical tool for replacement decisions — formula is $EUAC = C_t \cdot CRF(i, t)$.
- [ ] **Retrofit** = add-on, not replacement. Cheaper, faster, partial cut. **Replacement** = new vehicle, costly, deep cut.
- [ ] **Relocation** is now considered a *defensive* strategy — moves pollution, doesn't eliminate it. KTU may frame it as a "historical" or "least-preferred" option.
- [ ] Key retrofit devices: **TWC (petrol), DOC + DPF + SCR (diesel), Oxidation catalyst (LPG)**.
- [ ] Typical DPF retrofit removes **70–90% PM**; SCR with DEF removes **70–95% NOx**.
- [ ] Cost-effectiveness unit: **₹ per kg of pollutant removed** — must convert grams → kg before computing.
- [ ] Default KTU interest rate: **10% p.a.** for EUAC; **12% p.a.** for NPV.
- [ ] Always show the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** formula; never assume it.
- [ ] India's BS-VI emission norms effectively **from 1 April 2020** — frame every retrofit-vs-replace argument in this reference.
- [ ] **Retrofit is preferred over replacement** when: (i) fleet budget is tight, (ii) vehicle has residual life > 5 yr, (iii) baseline emission is concentrated in **one** pollutant (e.g., PM), (iv) fleet is large (cost scales).
- [ ] **Replacement is preferred over retrofit** when: (i) vehicle is structurally old, (ii) multiple pollutants are off-norm, (iii) fuel-economy penalty of retrofit > savings, (iv) operating zone is **non-attainment** city.
- [ ] Use a **4-step analysis protocol** in exam answers: (1) Identify strategy, (2) Write formula, (3) Substitute, (4) Box final answer with units.
